- **Assembly-line balancing** — assigning tasks to stations to meet takt time.
- **Inventory and supply chain** — $(s, S)$ policy simulation.
- **Flexible Manufacturing System (FMS)** — automated tool-change and pallet routing.

**Step 3 — Why simulation (not analytics)?** Closed-form queuing theory fails when there are *multiple part-types* with *different routings* and *machine failures*. Simulation captures this without approximation.

**Step 4 — Typical metrics.**
- **Throughput** $TH$ (jobs/hr).
- **Machine utilisation** $\rho_i = B_i / T$, where $B_i$ is busy time.
- **Work-In-Progress** (WIP) and **cycle time**.
- **Cost of overtime** ₹/day.

**Step 5 — Example.** A Bosch plant uses Arena to simulate an engine-assembly line with 8 stations; the model reduced average WIP by 18% and increased throughput by 12%.

**(b)** *Explain how simulation is applied in **health-care management** for hospital patient-flow optimisation. Mention at least two key metrics and one software tool.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Context.** Hospitals are complex service systems with **multi-stage patient flow** — registration → triage → consultation → diagnostics → pharmacy → discharge. Bottlenecks occur unpredictably due to emergencies.

**Step 2 — Sub-applications.**
- **Emergency Room (ER)** patient flow.
- **Operating Theatre (OT)** scheduling.
- **Inpatient bed management** and **discharge planning**.
- **Ambulance dispatch and location**.

**Step 3 — Modelling approach.** Each patient is an *entity*; consultations, lab tests, and bed allocation are *resources* with stochastic service times. A DES model is built and run for 1 simulated year, with 1000+ replications to get statistical confidence.

**Step 4 — Key metrics.**
- **Average waiting time** $W_q$ per patient.
- **Bed occupancy rate** $\rho$ (target 0.80–0.85 for safety).
- **Length of stay (LOS)** in days.
- **Staff-to-patient ratio**.

**Step 5 — Software.** **MedModel** (by ProModel), **Arena**, or **AnyLogic** are widely used. Apollo Hospitals and AIIMS Delhi have published case studies using such tools.

**Step 6 — Outcome.** Typical interventions tested in-silico: adding one more triage nurse reduces average waiting time by ~22%; opening a fast-track lane for minor cases reduces ER congestion by 30%.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often write generic advantages of simulation and **forget the domain-specific metric**. In a 7-mark sub-question, **at least two numerical metrics with units** are mandatory. Without them, you lose 2 marks even if the prose is good.

---

### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** *Explain the application of simulation in **transportation and traffic engineering**. List three specific use-cases and state the simulation technique used in each.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Domain definition.** Transportation simulation models the movement of vehicles, pedestrians, and public-transport units across road, rail, and air networks.

**Step 2 — Use-cases.**
1. **Signalised intersection design** — DES or **microsimulation** (e.g., VISSIM) to optimise green-signal phase durations.
2. **Highway traffic-flow** — car-following models (Gipps, Wiedemann) calibrated with real-loop-detector data; used by NHAI for expressway planning.
3. **Metro/rail scheduling** — agent-based simulation of train dwell times at stations; Mumbai Metro uses such models.
4. **Airport runway/taxiway** — DES for aircraft pushback and gate assignment to reduce ground-holding time.

**Step 3 — Techniques.** Microsimulation (VISSIM, SUMO) for vehicular flow; System Dynamics for long-term demand forecasting; Agent-Based Modelling (ABM) for pedestrian dynamics in mass-evacuation studies.

**Step 4 — Output metrics.** Average travel time (min), average delay (s/veh), level of service (LOS) grade A–F, queue spillback frequency, fuel consumption (L/km).

**(b)** *With the help of a single-server M/M/1 queueing model, derive the steady-state probability $P_n$ and the average number in queue $L_q$. Assume $\lambda = 40$/hr and $\mu = 50$/hr and find $P_0$, $L_q$, and $W_q$ for a bank teller application.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model assumptions.** Poisson arrivals at rate $\lambda = 40$/hr, exponential service at rate $\mu = 50$/hr, single server, FCFS discipline, infinite queue capacity.

**Step 2 — Birth-death balance.** As derived in **Section 3.1** of these notes:

$$
P_n = (1-\rho)\,\rho^n
$$

**Step 3 — Compute traffic intensity.**

$$
\rho = \frac{\lambda}{\mu} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.8
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of empty system.**

$$
P_0 = 1 - \rho = 1 - 0.8 = 0.2
$$

**Step 5 — Average queue length.**

$$
L_q = \frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho} = \frac{(0.8)^2}{1-0.8} = \frac{0.64}{0.2} = 3.2 \text{ customers}
$$

**Step 6 — Average waiting time (Little's Law).**

$$
W_q = \frac{L_q}{\lambda} = \frac{3.2}{40} = 0.08 \text{ hr} = 4.8 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 7 — Verification.**

$$
L = L_q + \rho = 3.2 + 0.8 = 4.0 \text{ customers}
$$

$$
W = \frac{L}{\lambda} = \frac{4.0}{40} = 0.1 \text{ hr} = 6 \text{ min}
$$

Sanity check: $W = W_q + 1/\mu = 4.8 + 1.2 = 6.0$ min ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (Examiners' Pet Loss):** A shocking 40% of KTU scripts forget to state the **stability condition $\rho < 1$**. It is *worth 1 mark by itself*. Always include the line: *"For a steady state to exist, we require $\rho < 1$."*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Save this list. It covers **90% of the marks** that can be asked on "Areas of Application of Simulation".

- ☐ **Definition of simulation** — model + experiment + analysis over time, *not* the real system.
- ☐ Simulation is preferred when: **complex, stochastic, dangerous, or expensive** systems are involved.
- ☐ The **12 application areas** to memorise: Manufacturing, Health care, Transportation, Networks, Finance, Aerospace, Power, Project Mgmt, Environment, Education, Queueing services, Supply chain.
- ☐ **Discrete-Event Simulation (DES)** is the most common technique across these areas.
- ☐ **Monte Carlo Simulation** is dominant in finance, reliability, and PERT.
- ☐ **System Dynamics** dominates environmental, climate, and long-term policy models.
- ☐ **Agent-Based Modelling (ABM)** is used for supply chains, pedestrian flow, epidemic spread.
- ☐ **Kendall's M/M/1 formulas** are *the* mathematical backbone — must be derivable from scratch.
- ☐ Key metrics to recall cold: $\rho = \lambda/\mu$, $L_q = \rho^2/(1-\rho)$, $L = \lambda W$ (Little's Law), $P_0 = 1-\rho$.
- ☐ **Stability condition:** $\rho < 1$ — must be stated in every queueing derivation.
- ☐ **Eight phases of a simulation study** (formulation → data → model → V&V → design → run → analyse → decide) — frequently asked as a 7-mark question.
- ☐ Common software: **Arena, AnyLogic, FlexSim, ns-3, VISSIM, STELLA, MATLAB/Simulink, ProModel**.
- ☐ Local-relevance examples win bonus marks: **CIAL, KSEB, KSRTC, ISRO, Apollo Hospitals**.
- ☐ Always close a numerical answer with a **sanity check** (e.g., $W = W_q + 1/\mu$).
- ☐ Avoid Python code in this paper unless explicitly a CS-lab elective — answer in **prose + formula + diagram**.

---

> **END OF NOTES — MODULE 1: AREAS OF APPLICATION OF SIMULATION**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steps in Discrete Event Simulation Study
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Steps in Discrete Event Simulation Study

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrete Event Simulation (DES)** is a modelling paradigm in which the state of the system changes only at discrete points in time, triggered by the occurrence of **events**. The set of chronological, methodology-driven activities undertaken to define, build, validate, and use such a simulation model is collectively referred to as the **Steps in a Discrete Event Simulation Study**.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme framing of PCIET601 (System Modelling and Simulation), every DES study follows a structured, iterative workflow that converts a **real-world problem** into a **verified, validated, and analysed simulation model** whose output informs managerial or engineering decisions. The classical reference framework (Banks, Carson, Nelson & Nicol) decomposes this workflow into **nine canonical steps**, starting from problem formulation and terminating with documentation and implementation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Simulation" Intuition

Imagine a hospital administrator who wants to know whether adding a second MRI machine will reduce patient waiting time. She cannot afford to physically buy the machine and experiment. Instead, she builds a **computer replica** of the hospital — patients arrive, queue, get scanned, leave. By *running* this replica thousands of times, she observes the system.

> [!NOTE]
> **The DES Workflow = The Hospital Administrator's To-Do List**
> 1. *Define the problem* — "Will 2 MRI machines reduce wait below 30 min?"
> 2. *Collect data* — patient arrival rates, scan durations.
> 3. *Build a flowchart of the process* — check-in → scan → discharge.
> 4. *Translate to a software model* — code it in Arena / SIMUL8 / Python-SimPy.
> 5. *Debug the model* — did the code do what the flowchart said?
> 6. *Verify with reality* — does the model behave like the real hospital?
> 7. *Design experiments* — run with 1 machine, 2 machines, 3 machines.
> 8. *Run, analyse, present results.*
> 9. *Document and implement* the recommended change.

That nine-step to-do list, formalised and academically rigorous, is what KTU expects you to master for Module 1.

### 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning in DES Context |
|---|---|
| **System** | The real-world entity under study (e.g., bank, hospital, network). |
| **Entity** | The dynamic objects flowing through the system (patients, packets, jobs). |
| **Attribute** | A property of an entity (priority, age, type). |
| **Activity** | A time-consuming process (scan, service, transmission). |
| **Event** | An instantaneous change in system state (arrival, departure). |
| **State Variable** | A descriptor of the system's current condition (queue length, server status). |
| **Clock** | The simulation's internal time counter. |
| **Experiment** | A single run of the model with a specified input configuration. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** The words *event*, *activity*, *process* and *entity* are routinely interchanged loosely in textbooks. In an ESE answer, use them **precisely** to score the examiner's confidence marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Nine Canonical Steps — Logic Decomposition

Below is the academically rigorous, KTU-aligned breakdown of every step in a DES study.

#### **Step 1 — Problem Formulation**
- **What:** Translate the real-world managerial/engineering problem into a clearly defined simulation problem statement.
- **Why:** Without a crisp problem, the entire study drifts.
- **How:** Identify objectives (e.g., minimise cost, maximise throughput), performance metrics, scope, and constraints.
- **Output:** A written *Problem Statement Document* with explicit performance measures such as **average waiting time**, **server utilisation $\rho$**, or **maximum queue length $L_{\max}$**.

#### **Step 2 — Data Collection & Analysis**
- **What:** Gather all numerical/qualitative data needed to drive the model.
- **Why:** A DES model is only as accurate as its input distributions.
- **How:** Collect inter-arrival times, service times, routing probabilities, failure/repair rates, etc., and fit them to theoretical distributions (Exponential, Normal, Erlang, Empirical).
- **Tools used:** Chi-square goodness-of-fit test, Kolmogorov–Smirnov test, histograms, Q-Q plots.

#### **Step 3 — Conceptual Model Development**
- **What:** Build an abstract, often flowchart-based, representation of the system.
- **Why:** Bridges the gap between the verbal problem and the executable code.
- **How:** Define entities, attributes, resources, queues, routing logic, and activity durations. Construct an *Activity Cycle Diagram* or *Process Flow Diagram*.

#### **Step 4 — Model Translation (Coding)**
- **What:** Convert the conceptual model into executable simulation code.
- **Why:** Computational execution is required to generate output.
- **How:** Implement in a general-purpose language (C++, Java, Python-SimPy) or a specialised DES environment (Arena, FlexSim, AnyLogic, SIMUL8).

#### **Step 5 — Verification**
- **What:** Confirm that the *computer program* correctly implements the *conceptual model*.
- **Why:** A syntactically correct program can still contain logic bugs.
- **How:** Use structured walkthroughs, code inspection, trace logs, animated run visualisation, and degenerate tests (e.g., a single server, single customer, deterministic service).

#### **Step 6 — Validation**
- **What:** Confirm that the *conceptual model* is a faithful representation of the *real system*.
- **Why:** Even a bug-free program that mimics the wrong system is useless.
- **How:** Compare model output (e.g., mean waiting time) with real-system historical data using statistical tests (t-test on means, confidence interval overlap).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU distinguishes Verification from Validation explicitly:**
> - **Verification** = *Are we building the model right?* (code vs. design)
> - **Validation** = *Are we building the right model?* (model vs. reality)

#### **Step 7 — Experimental Design**
- **What:** Plan the input factor combinations and the number of replications to be run.
- **Why:** A poorly designed experiment yields noisy, biased, or under-powered conclusions.
- **How:** Choose between *terminating* (finite-horizon) and *steady-state* (infinite-horizon) analysis; pick factors and levels; decide replication count $R$.

#### **Step 8 — Production Runs & Output Analysis**
- **What:** Execute the designed experiments and statistically analyse the responses.
- **Why:** Simulation output is **stochastic**, so a single run is meaningless.
- **How:** Compute sample means, construct confidence intervals (CI), perform sensitivity analysis, and test hypotheses.

#### **Step 9 — Implementation, Documentation & Iteration**
- **What:** Translate findings into actionable recommendations and document the study.
- **Why:** A model never used is a wasted study.
- **How:** Write a final report, present to stakeholders, archive the model, and, if the real system later changes, re-enter the cycle from Step 1.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are the **statistical backbone** of Step 8. KTU routinely embeds them in 7- and 14-mark sub-parts.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sample Mean** | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{R} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{R} X_i$ | Average of $R$ replication outputs. |
| 2 | **Sample Variance** | $S^2 = \dfrac{1}{R-1} \displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{R}(X_i-\bar{X})^2$ | Unbiased estimator. |
| 3 | **100$(1-\alpha)\%$ Confidence Interval (CI)** | $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,R-1}\,\dfrac{S}{\sqrt{R}}$ | Used when $R$ is small. |
| 4 | **Large-sample CI ($R \ge 30$)** | $\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\dfrac{S}{\sqrt{R}}$ | Replace $t$ with $z$. |
| 5 | **Required Replications for CI Half-Width $h$** | $R \ge \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\,S}{h} \right)^{2}$ | Round up to next integer. |
| 6 | **Steady-state Truncation Rule (Welch)** | $d = 0.5\,$warm-up based on $\bar{X}(m)$ plateau | For infinite-horizon analysis. |
| 7 | **Server Utilisation** | $\rho = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu}$ | $\lambda$ = arrival rate, $\mu$ = service rate (single server, M/M/1). |
| 8 | **Mean Inter-Arrival Time** | $\bar{A} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Used while fitting exponential distribution. |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

DES is the **workhorse of operational analytics** in:

- **Manufacturing** — assembly-line bottleneck analysis, AGV dispatch.
- **Healthcare** — patient-flow optimisation, ER staffing.
- **Logistics** — port terminal operations, warehouse layout.
- **Computer networks** — packet routing, queueing analysis.
- **Defence & Aerospace** — mission rehearsal, radar-satellite scheduling.

The structured 9-step workflow is what separates an ad-hoc "script" from a **publishable, defensible simulation study**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

Because this topic is **methodological** rather than purely mathematical, Section 3 provides an **exhaustive walkthrough** of how each step is executed, followed by an **analytical comparison matrix** that KTU examiners love to test in 7-mark sub-questions.

### 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough of Each Step

#### **Step 1 — Problem Formulation (Detailed)**

1. Identify the **decision-maker** and their information need.
2. State the **objective function** (e.g., minimise $\bar{W}_q$ where $W_q$ is waiting time in queue).
3. Define **performance measures**: $E[T]$, $\rho$, $L_q$, throughput, cost.
4. State the **scope** (one department? one shift? one plant?).
5. List **assumptions**: stationarity, exponential inter-arrivals, FIFO discipline, single-class entities, etc.
6. Define the **time horizon**: terminating (e.g., 8-hour shift) vs. non-terminating (24/7).
7. Identify **constraints**: budget, server count, buffer capacity.

#### **Step 2 — Data Collection & Analysis (Detailed)**

1. Collect raw observations: arrival timestamps, service timestamps, queue counts.
2. Compute **summary statistics**: mean, variance, min, max, mode, percentiles.
3. Test for **independence** and **stationarity** (run plots, lag plots).
4. Form **frequency distribution** and plot histogram.
5. Apply **goodness-of-fit tests**:
   - **Chi-square $\chi^2$ test** for categorical/discretised data.
   - **Kolmogorov–Smirnov test** for continuous data.
6. Estimate parameters (e.g., for exponential: $\hat{\lambda} = 1/\bar{X}$).
7. **Validate** the fitted distribution by overlaying empirical vs. theoretical PDF/CDF plots.

#### **Step 3 — Conceptual Model (Detailed)**

1. List all **entities** and their **attributes**.
2. List all **resources** (machines, doctors, tellers) and their capacities.
3. For each **activity**, specify duration distribution and resource requirements.
4. Define **queues** (capacity, discipline — FIFO/LIFO/priority).
5. Define **routing logic** (probabilistic vs. deterministic).
6. Draw an **Activity Cycle Diagram (ACD)** or **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)**.
7. Specify the **initial conditions** and **termination conditions**.

#### **Step 4 — Model Translation (Detailed)**

1. Choose a **simulation environment** (Arena, AnyLogic, SimPy, MATLAB-SimEvents).
2. Map each conceptual element to a **module/block** in the environment.
3. Populate input parameters with **fitted distributions** from Step 2.
4. Configure **replication controls**: run length, warm-up period, number of replications $R$.
5. Set **output collection points** (statistics on each queue, resource, system).

#### **Step 5 — Verification (Detailed)**

1. Apply **structured walkthroughs** with the development team.
2. Use **debug mode / single-step execution** to trace entity flow.
3. **Animation check** — visually confirm the process flow matches expectations.
4. Run **degenerate tests** (e.g., no arrivals, single deterministic service, no queue capacity limit).
5. Use **trace reports** to confirm event sequencing.

#### **Step 6 — Validation (Detailed)**

1. Compare **model outputs** with **historical system data**:
   - $H_0: \mu_{\text{model}} = \mu_{\text{real}}$ vs. $H_1: \mu_{\text{model}} \ne \mu_{\text{real}}$.
2. Use a **two-sample t-test** or **two-sample CI** for the mean.
3. Apply **Theil's inequality coefficient** for trend comparison:

$$
U = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(M_i - A_i)^2}}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} M_i^2} + \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i^2}}
$$

where $M_i$ = model output, $A_i$ = actual output. $U \approx 0$ → high accuracy; $U \approx 1$ → poor.

4. Conduct **face validation** with domain experts.
5. If validation fails, **return to Step 1** or **Step 3** and iterate.

#### **Step 7 — Experimental Design (Detailed)**

1. Identify **input factors** and their **levels** (e.g., number of servers $\in \{1, 2, 3\}$).
2. Choose **design type**: full factorial, fractional factorial, response surface, or one-factor-at-a-time.
3. Choose **output analysis approach**:
   - **Terminating analysis** — use independent replications; compute CI using $t$-distribution.
   - **Steady-state analysis** — use batch means, regenerative method, or spectral method.
4. Set **desired CI half-width** $h$ and **confidence level** $1-\alpha$.
5. Compute required $R$ using Formula 5 above (pilot run → refined $R$).

#### **Step 8 — Production Runs & Output Analysis (Detailed)**

1. Execute all $R$ replications.
2. Collect **time-series output** if steady-state.
3. Discard **warm-up data** for steady-state.
4. Compute $\bar{X}, S$ per design point.
5. Construct **CIs** (Formula 3).
6. Perform **hypothesis tests** if comparing alternative designs.
7. Run **sensitivity analysis** (tornado plots, ANOVA).
8. Generate **graphical summaries** (box plots, line charts, dashboards).

#### **Step 9 — Implementation & Documentation (Detailed)**

1. Prepare a **final report** with problem, methodology, results, conclusions, recommendations.
2. Include **assumptions, limitations, and caveats**.
3. Present to **stakeholders** and obtain sign-off.
4. Archive the **model file, data, and report** for future re-use.
5. **Monitor** the real system post-implementation; trigger re-study if conditions change.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

This matrix is a **favourite KTU question** ("Compare verification and validation" or "Distinguish between terminating and steady-state simulation").

| Aspect | **Verification** | **Validation** |
|---|---|---|
| Question answered | *Are we building the model right?* | *Are we building the right model?* |
| Compares | Code ↔ Conceptual model | Model ↔ Real system |
| Performed by | Model developer | Developer + domain expert |
| Techniques | Walkthroughs, debugging, trace, animation | Face validity, statistical tests, Theil's $U$ |
| Failure implies | Bug in the code | Flaw in the conceptual model |
| Timing | After Step 4 | After Step 5 / Step 8 |

| Aspect | **Terminating Simulation** | **Steady-State Simulation** |
|---|---|---|
| Time horizon | Finite (e.g., one shift, one day) | Infinite / long-run |
| Initial conditions | Matter and are set explicitly | Erased via warm-up |
| Output statistic | Mean over fixed run length | Long-run mean (steady-state) |
| Analysis method | Independent replications, $t$-CI | Batch means, regenerative method |
| Example | Bank 9 AM – 5 PM | Data centre 24/7 traffic |

| Aspect | **Conceptual Model** | **Computer Model** |
|---|---|---|
| Form | Flowchart, ACD, equations | Source code, simulation blocks |
| Readability | High for humans | High for machines |
| Tools | Pen & paper, Visio, draw.io | Arena, AnyLogic, SimPy, MATLAB |
| Output of | Step 3 | Step 4 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Nine-Step DES Workflow — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[step1Problem] --> B[step2Data]
    B --> C[step3Conceptual]
    C --> D[step4Translate]
    D --> E[step5Verify]
    E --> F{step6Validate}
    F -- No --> C
    F -- Yes --> G[step7Experiment]
    G --> H[step8RunAnalyse]
    H --> I[step9Document]
    I --> J[step10Implement]
    J --> K[step11Monitor]
    K -- System Changed --> A
    E -.Debug.- D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **solid arrows** show the forward sequence (Steps 1 → 9).
- The **dashed feedback loop** from Step 5 (Verification) to Step 4 (Translation) represents *debugging iterations*.
- The **green-coded validation gate** routes the workflow back to Step 3 if the model fails to match reality.
- The **outer loop** from Step 11 (Monitor) back to Step 1 represents *post-implementation re-study*.

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Where Each Step Produces Artifacts

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Stakeholder Need] --> step1A[Problem Statement Doc]
    step1A --> step2A[Raw Data + Fitted Distributions]
    step2A --> step3A[ACD PFD Diagram]
    step3A --> step4A[Executable Model File]
    step4A --> step5A[Verification Report]
    step5A --> step6A[Validation Report with U]
    step6A --> step7A[Experimental Design Table]
    step7A --> step8A[Output Data + CIs]
    step8A --> step9A[Final Report and Recommendations]
    step9A --> outputA[Implemented Decision in Real System]
```

### 4.3 Iterative Loop Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start Study]) --> plan[Plan Problem Formulation]
    plan --> model[Build Model]
    model --> test[Verify and Validate]
    test --> decide{Model Acceptable}
    decide -- No --> model
    decide -- Yes --> exp[Design Experiments]
    exp --> run[Run and Analyse]
    run --> recommend[Document and Recommend]
    recommend --> stop([End of Current Study])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "draw the simulation study flowchart", reproduce the **solid-flow + feedback-loop** structure above. Examiners specifically look for the *two feedback loops*: one within the modelling block (verify ↔ translate) and one at the end (monitor ↔ problem formulation).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PCIET601]`

**List any six steps in a Discrete Event Simulation (DES) study and explain the purpose of the *validation* step.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six steps are:
1. Problem formulation
2. Data collection and analysis
3. Conceptual model development
4. Model translation (coding)
5. Verification
6. Validation
*(+ Experimental design, Production runs, Documentation & implementation as additional steps.)*

**Purpose of validation (key scoring point):** Validation confirms that the conceptual model is an **accurate representation of the real system**. It answers the question *"Are we building the right model?"* by statistically comparing model output (e.g., mean waiting time) with historical data from the actual system, often using a two-sample t-test or **Theil's inequality coefficient $U$**. If validation fails, the modeller returns to Step 1 or Step 3 and refines the model.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PCIET601]`

**Distinguish between *verification* and *validation* in a simulation study. Give one technique for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Criterion | Verification | Validation |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Confirming the program correctly implements the conceptual model | Confirming the model faithfully represents the real system |
| Question | *Are we building the model right?* | *Are we building the right model?* |
| Technique | Structured walkthrough / trace log / animation debugging | Face validity / t-test on means / Theil's $U$ statistic |

*(1 mark for correct definitions, 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for one technique each.)*

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCIET601, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **nine canonical steps** in a Discrete Event Simulation study with a suitable real-world example. Highlight the *outputs* of each step.

**(b) [7 marks]** A single-server bank teller system is modelled. The modeller runs **5 pilot replications** of length 8 hours each and obtains the following average customer waiting times (in minutes): 4.2, 5.1, 3.8, 4.9, 5.5. Using a 95% confidence interval, determine how many **additional replications** are required so that the half-width of the CI does not exceed **0.4 minutes**. State the final CI.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PCIET601, Module 1]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast **terminating** and **steady-state** simulation analysis. Under what scenarios would you prefer each? Describe the **replication method** for terminating analysis with its CI formula.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss the role of **experimental design** and **output analysis** in a DES study. Explain with formulae how the number of replications is determined for a desired CI half-width.

---

### **MODEL SOLUTIONS — Question A**

#### **Solution A(a) — 7 marks**

> The nine steps (each step must be clearly named + output stated; awarding 0.5–1 mark per step):

| # | Step | Purpose | Output / Artifact |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Problem Formulation** | Define the question, objectives, performance measures, scope. | Written *Problem Statement Document*. |
| 2 | **Data Collection & Analysis** | Collect input data, fit distributions. | Fitted distributions + parameters (e.g., $\hat{\lambda} = 0.2$/min). |
| 3 | **Conceptual Model** | Abstract flowchart of the system. | Activity Cycle Diagram (ACD). |
| 4 | **Model Translation** | Code the conceptual model. | Executable simulation file (e.g., `bank_model.doe`). |
| 5 | **Verification** | Ensure code matches the conceptual model. | Verification report / animation log. |
| 6 | **Validation** | Ensure model matches real system. | Validation report with Theil's $U$ or t-test result. |
| 7 | **Experimental Design** | Plan runs, factors, replication count. | Experimental design table. |
| 8 | **Production Runs & Output Analysis** | Execute and statistically analyse. | Means, variances, confidence intervals. |
| 9 | **Implementation & Documentation** | Deliver recommendations, archive. | Final report, stakeholder presentation. |

**Real-world example:** Modelling the patient flow of an **Out-Patient Department (OPD)** to reduce average waiting time below 20 minutes. The problem is formulated (Step 1), patient inter-arrival and consultation-time data are collected (Step 2), a flowchart of *registration → triage → consultation → pharmacy* is drawn (Step 3), the model is built in **Arena** (Step 4), debugged (Step 5), validated against last month's historical wait times (Step 6), experiments with 1, 2, 3 doctors are designed (Step 7), runs are executed (Step 8), and a recommendation to add one doctor during peak hours is delivered (Step 9).

#### **Solution A(b) — 7 marks**

> **Given:** $n = 5$ pilot replications; $X_i = \{4.2,\,5.1,\,3.8,\,4.9,\,5.5\}$; confidence level $= 95\%$; desired half-width $h = 0.4$ min.

**Step 1 — Compute the sample mean $\bar{X}$:**

$$
\bar{X} = \dfrac{4.2 + 5.1 + 3.8 + 4.9 + 5.5}{5}
$$

$$
\bar{X} = \dfrac{23.5}{5} = 4.70\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sample variance $S^2$:**

$$
S^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(X_i - \bar{X})^2
$$

Computing each squared deviation:

- $(4.2 - 4.7)^2 = (-0.5)^2 = 0.25$
- $(5.1 - 4.7)^2 = (0.4)^2 = 0.16$
- $(3.8 - 4.7)^2 = (-0.9)^2 = 0.81$
- $(4.9 - 4.7)^2 = (0.2)^2 = 0.04$
- $(5.5 - 4.7)^2 = (0.8)^2 = 0.64$

Sum $= 0.25 + 0.16 + 0.81 + 0.04 + 0.64 = 1.90$

$$
S^2 = \dfrac{1.90}{5-1} = \dfrac{1.90}{4} = 0.475
$$

$$
S = \sqrt{0.475} \approx 0.6892
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required number of replications $R$:**

For 95% confidence, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$ (using the normal approximation since we are computing the *required* future sample size).

$$
R \ge \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\,S}{h} \right)^{2} = \left( \dfrac{1.96 \times 0.6892}{0.4} \right)^{2}
$$

$$
R \ge \left( \dfrac{1.3509}{0.4} \right)^{2} = (3.3772)^{2} \approx 11.41
$$

Round up: $R_{\text{req}} = 12$ replications **in total**.

**Additional replications needed** $= 12 - 5 = \mathbf{7}$.

**Step 4 — Construct the final 95% CI using the new $R = 12$:**

Using $t_{0.025,\,11} = 2.201$ (from t-table with $df = 11$):

$$
h_{\text{new}} = t_{0.025,\,11}\,\dfrac{S}{\sqrt{12}} = 2.201 \times \dfrac{0.6892}{\sqrt{12}}
$$

$$
h_{\text{new}} = 2.201 \times \dfrac{0.6892}{3.4641} = 2.201 \times 0.1990 \approx 0.438
$$

*(Note: In practice the modeller would re-estimate $S$ from the 12 replications; for the exam, the formula is the expected deliverable.)*

**Final CI (after 7 more replications):**

$$
\bar{X} \pm h_{\text{new}} = 4.70 \pm 0.44\ \text{minutes}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{CI}_{95\%} = [4.26,\ 5.14]\ \text{minutes}}
$$

---

### **MODEL SOLUTIONS — Question B**

#### **Solution B(a) — 7 marks**

> **Terminating vs. Steady-State simulation** (use the comparison matrix from Section 3.2 as backbone):

| Basis | Terminating | Steady-State |
|---|---|---|
| Horizon | Finite, fixed | Infinite / long-run |
| Initial conditions | Set explicitly; matter | Erased by warm-up |
| System state at end | Specific (e.g., "bank closed") | Irrelevant |
| Output measure | Mean over the run | Long-run (steady) mean |
| Replication | Independent; same setup, different random streams | Independent replications with warm-up truncation |
| CI formula | $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,R-1}\,S/\sqrt{R}$ | $\bar{Y} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,m-1}\,S_B/\sqrt{m}$ (batch means) |
| Example | Bank 9–5, factory one shift | Power plant 24/7, server farm |

**Scenarios for terminating:** Bank branch operating hours; emergency-room day; airport peak-hour check-in. Initial conditions (e.g., empty queues at opening) matter.

**Scenarios for steady-state:** Telecom core router; data-centre cooling system; highway traffic outside rush hours. Long-run throughput matters; initial transients must be discarded.

**Replication method for terminating analysis:** Each replication uses an independent random stream with identical initial conditions and run length. The replications are i.i.d., so the standard $t$-based CI applies directly. The half-width formula in Formula 5 is used to determine the required number of replications.

#### **Solution B(b) — 7 marks**

**Role of experimental design (3 marks):** It determines *what* to vary, *how many* runs to make, and *which statistical technique* to use for inference. A well-designed experiment maximises information per simulation run (especially since runs are expensive). Common designs: full factorial, fractional factorial, one-factor-at-a-time, and response-surface methodology.

**Role of output analysis (3 marks):** It converts raw simulation traces into **defensible, statistically valid conclusions** — point estimates, confidence intervals, hypothesis tests, and sensitivity rankings. Since simulation output is stochastic, naive averaging without CIs misleads the decision-maker.

**Formula for required replications (1 mark):** Given a desired CI half-width $h$ and confidence level $1-\alpha$, the number of replications needed (after a pilot run to estimate $S$) is:

$$
R \ge \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\,S}{h} \right)^{2}
$$

Round up to the next integer. Iterative refinement: after the new replications, recompute $S$ and check if $h$ is now below target; if not, run more.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often **confuse verification with validation** — memorise the one-line mantra: *Verification = building the model right; Validation = building the right model.*
> 2. In $R$-calculation, **forgetting to round up** (using 11 instead of 12) loses 1 mark.
> 3. In CI construction, students use $z = 1.96$ when $R$ is small and $t$ is required. Use $z$ only when $R \ge 30$ (or in the $R$-estimation formula as a planning approximation).
> 4. Students often write the **nine steps in the wrong order** — verification comes *after* translation, *before* validation. Order matters.
> 5. When asked for a "flowchart", students draw a *program flowchart* (if-else, loops) instead of a *study-workflow flowchart* (Steps 1–9 with feedback loops).

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- ✅ **The nine steps** are: (1) Problem Formulation → (2) Data Collection → (3) Conceptual Model → (4) Model Translation → (5) Verification → (6) Validation → (7) Experimental Design → (8) Production Runs & Analysis → (9) Implementation & Documentation.
- ✅ **Verification** = code ↔ conceptual model. **Validation** = conceptual model ↔ real system.
- ✅ **Verification techniques:** walkthrough, trace, animation, degenerate tests.
- ✅ **Validation techniques:** face validity, t-test, Theil's $U$ statistic, $U \approx 0$ means excellent fit.
- ✅ **Terminating analysis** uses independent replications + $t$-CI; **steady-state** uses batch means, regenerative, or spectral methods after discarding warm-up data.
- ✅ **CI formula:** $\bar{X} \pm t_{\alpha/2,\,R-1}\,S/\sqrt{R}$.
- ✅ **Required replications:** $R \ge \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\,S}{h} \right)^{2}$ — always round **up**.
- ✅ The DES study is **iterative**: feedback loops exist within modelling (verify ↔ translate) and at study level (monitor ↔ Step 1).
- ✅ For 95% CI: $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $t_{0.025,\,4} = 2.776$, $t_{0.025,\,11} = 2.201$ — keep these in your memory.
- ✅ The **output of every step is an artifact** (document, diagram, code, report). Examiners love asking for the *output* of a step.
- ✅ Real-world DES applications span manufacturing, healthcare, logistics, networks, and defence.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Properties of Random Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Random Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **System Modelling and Simulation**, a **Random Number** is a numerical value drawn from a defined probability distribution such that every admissible value in the sample space has a known (and ideally equal) chance of being selected. In Monte Carlo and discrete-event simulation, the term *Random Number* strictly refers to a real number $R \in [0,1]$ drawn from a **Uniform $[0,1]$** distribution, denoted as $R \sim U(0,1)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Examiner Standard):**
> A *Random Number Sequence* is a sequence of digits (or real numbers) in which each element is statistically independent of all others, and the sequence as a whole exhibits the properties of **Uniformity** and **Independence** required to faithfully emulate the stochastic behaviour of real-world systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are rolling a **perfectly fair six-sided die** infinitely many times in a soundproof room.

- Each face (1 through 6) has an exactly equal probability of $\frac{1}{6}$ — this is **Uniformity**.
- The outcome of the 47th roll has absolutely no memory of the 46th roll that came before it — this is **Independence**.
- You can document the die's physical imperfections (slightly heavier corner) and reproduce the *exact same sequence* if you start from the same throw — this is **Reproducibility**.

A *Random Number Generator* (RNG) used in KTU-grade simulation software is simply a deterministic mathematical mechanism that *emulates* this perfectly fair die, generating numbers between **0** and **1** with uniform density.

### 1.3 Why Random Numbers are the "Engine" of Simulation

In simulation, deterministic models cannot capture the *randomness* inherent in real systems:

- Customer inter-arrival times at a bank
- Machine breakdown intervals in a manufacturing plant
- Demand fluctuations in a supply chain

**Random Number Streams** (sequences of pseudo-random numbers) provide the underlying stochastic fuel that drives these stochastic processes. The fidelity of the entire simulation rests squarely on the **statistical quality** of these numbers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:**
> - *True Random Numbers* — generated by physical processes (atmospheric noise, radioactive decay).
> - *Pseudo-Random Numbers* — generated by deterministic algorithms (LCG, Mid-square, Multiplicative Congruential) on a computer. These are *reproducible* (a critical requirement for debugging simulations) but must *pass* statistical randomness tests.
> 
> KTU examinations overwhelmingly test **pseudo-random number properties**, since they are the practical foundation of all simulation software.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Notations

- **Range:** $R_i \in [0, 1]$ or specifically $(0, 1)$ excluding endpoints.
- **Sequence Length / Period:** The number of values generated before the pattern repeats, denoted as $m$.
- **Mean (Expected):** $\mu = 0.5$
- **Variance (Expected):** $\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{12} \approx 0.08333$
- **Standard Deviation (Expected):** $\sigma \approx 0.28868$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Desirable Properties of Random Numbers

KTU 2024 scheme prescribes the following six canonical properties. Each must be tested for every Random Number Generator (RNG) before it is deployed in a simulation study.

#### Property 1 — Uniformity (Uniform Distribution)
The generated numbers $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ must be **uniformly distributed** over the interval $[0, 1]$. The empirical probability density function (PDF) must closely match the theoretical Uniform PDF:

$$
f(x) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \le x \le 1 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

- **Why it matters:** Most real-world random variates (e.g., exponential, Poisson) are *derived* by transforming uniform numbers. If uniformity fails, the entire downstream distribution is corrupted.

#### Property 2 — Independence
The values $R_i$ and $R_j$ for $i \neq j$ must be **statistically independent**. That is, knowledge of $R_i$ must give **zero information** about the value of $R_j$.

- **Why it matters:** Independence ensures that the random inputs to a simulation model (e.g., arrivals and service times) are not artificially correlated, which would distort output statistics.

#### Property 3 — Efficiency
The RNG must be **computationally fast** and use **minimal memory**. A simulation may require millions of random numbers; a slow algorithm is unacceptable.

- **Why it matters:** In real-time and large-scale Monte Carlo simulations, execution time is a critical constraint.

#### Property 4 — Reproducibility (Cyclical)
Given the same initial seed and algorithm parameters, the RNG must produce the **identical sequence** every time it is run.

- **Why it matters:** Reproducibility is essential for **debugging** and **common random number (CRN)** variance reduction techniques.

#### Property 5 — Long Period (Non-Repetition over a Useful Range)
The sequence must not repeat for a **sufficiently long period** $m$, typically $m \ge 2^{31}$ for 32-bit systems. All pseudo-RNGs are eventually periodic.

- **Why it matters:** Short periods cause cyclic artefacts in simulation output, especially in long runs.

#### Property 6 — Portability
The same RNG must yield the same sequence on **different hardware platforms and compilers** when given the same seed.

- **Why it matters:** Ensures results can be verified across institutions (a key KTU lab evaluation requirement).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Property** | **Test Name** | **Test Statistic Formula** | **Degrees of Freedom** | **Critical Value at $\alpha = 0.05$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Uniformity | Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Test | $\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$ | $k - 1$ | $\chi^2_{0.05, \, k-1}$ |
| Uniformity | Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) Test | $D^+ = \max \left(\frac{i}{n} - F(X_i)\right)$, $D^- = \max \left(F(X_i) - \frac{i-1}{n}\right)$, $D = \max(D^+, D^-)$ | Not applicable (non-parametric) | $D_{\alpha, n}$ from K-S table |
| Independence | Runs Test (Above/Below Mean) | $Z_0 = \frac{R - \mu_R}{\sigma_R}$ | Not applicable (normal approx.) | $\pm 1.96$ for $\alpha = 0.05$ |
| Independence | Auto-Correlation Test | $Z_0 = \frac{\rho_{lm} - \mu_{\rho}}{\sigma_{\rho}}$ | Not applicable (normal approx.) | $\pm 1.96$ for $\alpha = 0.05$ |
| Periodicity | Theoretical Cycle Length | $m$ (algorithm-dependent) | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where to use $\vert$ vs $\mid$ in markdown tables:** I have used `max()` notation instead of the vertical bar to maintain markdown table integrity. In your written KTU answer scripts, the standard notation $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value is acceptable.

### 2.3 Where the Expected Mean and Variance Come From

For the theoretical Uniform $[0,1]$ distribution:

$$
\mu = E[R] = \int_0^1 x \cdot f(x) \, dx = \int_0^1 x \cdot 1 \, dx = \left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^1 = \frac{1}{2}
$$

$$
\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(R) = \int_0^1 (x - \mu)^2 f(x) \, dx = \int_0^1 (x - 0.5)^2 \, dx = \frac{1}{12}
$$

$$
\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{12}} \approx 0.28868
$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| **Engineering Field** | **Use of Random Numbers** |
|---|---|
| Telecommunications | Modelling packet arrival jitter and noise in 5G/6G channel simulation. |
| Manufacturing | Simulating machine failure times and queue lengths in production lines. |
| Finance | Monte Carlo pricing of exotic options and Value-at-Risk (VaR) computation. |
| Civil Engineering | Stochastic finite element analysis of structural loads. |
| Computer Networks | Discrete-event simulation of routers, congestion, and traffic. |
| Operations Research | Inventory simulation, reliability engineering, queuing theory. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Chi-Square ($\chi^2$) Uniformity Test Statistic

The Chi-Square test partitions the interval $[0, 1]$ into $k$ equal-width sub-intervals (cells) and counts the number of random numbers falling into each cell. The test statistic measures the *squared deviation* between observed and expected frequencies, normalised by the expected frequency.

**Step 1 — Define the partition.**
Sub-divide $[0, 1]$ into $k$ equal cells of width $\Delta = \frac{1}{k}$. For $n$ random numbers, the expected frequency in each cell under perfect uniformity is:

$$
E_i = \frac{n}{k}, \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, k
$$

**Step 2 — Define the observed frequency.**
Let $O_i$ denote the number of random numbers that fall into the $i$-th cell. The observed frequency is a discrete count.

**Step 3 — Construct the test statistic.**
The Chi-Square statistic aggregates the squared deviations, weighted by the inverse of the expected frequency (to prevent bias):

$$
\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Degrees of freedom.**
The degrees of freedom for the test are:

$$
\text{d.o.f.} = k - 1
$$

(One degree is lost because the constraint $\sum_{i=1}^{k} O_i = n$ ties the cell counts together.)

**Step 5 — Decision rule.**
Compare $\chi^2_0$ against the critical value $\chi^2_{\alpha, \, k-1}$ from the Chi-Square distribution table.

- If $\chi^2_0 \le \chi^2_{\alpha, \, k-1}$: **Accept $H_0$** (uniformity cannot be rejected).
- If $\chi^2_0 > \chi^2_{\alpha, \, k-1}$: **Reject $H_0$** (numbers are *not* uniform).

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb for KTU:** For valid Chi-Square application, ensure $E_i \ge 5$ for every cell. If not, merge adjacent cells until the condition is met.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) Test Statistic

Unlike the Chi-Square test (which is binned), the K-S test directly compares the **empirical cumulative distribution function (ECDF)** of the random numbers with the theoretical Uniform CDF.

**Step 1 — Sort the random numbers.**
Arrange the $n$ random numbers in ascending order: $R_{(1)} \le R_{(2)} \le \ldots \le R_{(n)}$.

**Step 2 — Define the theoretical CDF.**
For Uniform $[0,1]$, the theoretical CDF is:

$$
F(x) = x, \quad 0 \le x \le 1
$$

**Step 3 — Construct the empirical CDF.**
The ECDF at any point $R_{(i)}$ is:

$$
F_n(x) = \frac{\text{number of } R_j \le x}{n}
$$

For the sorted sequence, $F_n(R_{(i)}) = \frac{i}{n}$.

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum deviations (one-sided).**

$$
D^+ = \max_{1 \le i \le n} \left( \frac{i}{n} - R_{(i)} \right)
$$

$$
D^- = \max_{1 \le i \le n} \left( R_{(i)} - \frac{i-1}{n} \right)
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the two-sided K-S statistic.**

$$
D = \max(D^+, D^-)
$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule.**
Compare $D$ with the critical value $D_{\alpha, n}$ from the K-S table. Accept $H_0$ (uniformity) if $D \le D_{\alpha, n}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why K-S is preferred for small samples:** K-S uses the raw data without binning, making it more powerful for small $n$ (typically $n < 50$). Chi-Square requires binning, which loses information.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Runs Test (Independence)

The Runs Test (also called the "Above and Below the Mean" test) checks **independence** by counting the number of *runs* — consecutive subsequences of numbers that lie on the same side of the mean.

**Step 1 — Compute the sample mean.**

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i
$$

**Step 2 — Classify each number.**
Define a binary indicator:

$$
a_i = \begin{cases} +1, & \text{if } R_i \ge \bar{R} \\ -1, & \text{if } R_i < \bar{R} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Count the runs.**
A *run* is a maximal sequence of consecutive identical $a_i$ values. Let $R$ be the total number of runs in the sequence.

**Step 4 — Compute the expected mean and variance of $R$ under $H_0$ (independence).**

$$
\mu_R = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{n} + 1
$$

$$
\sigma_R^2 = \frac{2 n_1 n_2 (2 n_1 n_2 - n)}{n^2 (n - 1)}
$$

where $n_1$ = number of $R_i \ge \bar{R}$ and $n_2$ = number of $R_i < \bar{R}$, with $n = n_1 + n_2$.

**Step 5 — Compute the normalised Z-statistic.**

$$
Z_0 = \frac{R - \mu_R}{\sigma_R}
$$

**Step 6 — Decision rule.**
For $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed):

- If $-1.96 \le Z_0 \le 1.96$: Accept $H_0$ (numbers are independent).
- Otherwise: Reject $H_0$ (numbers exhibit dependence).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting the $+1$ in $\mu_R$. This shifts the expected count of runs by exactly one and inflates the test statistic, leading to false rejection of valid sequences.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Auto-Correlation Test (Independence)

This test directly measures the **lag-l serial correlation** between $R_i$ and $R_{i+l}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the mean and variance of the sample.**

$$
\bar{R} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} R_i, \quad s^2 = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (R_i - \bar{R})^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the lag-$l$ auto-correlation coefficient.**

$$
\rho_{l} = \frac{\frac{1}{n-l} \sum_{i=1}^{n-l} (R_i - \bar{R})(R_{i+l} - \bar{R})}{s^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the expected value and variance of $\rho_l$ under $H_0$.**

$$
\mu_{\rho} = 0, \quad \sigma_{\rho}^2 = \frac{1}{n - 1}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Z-statistic.**

$$
Z_0 = \frac{\rho_l - 0}{\sigma_{\rho}} = \rho_l \sqrt{n - 1}
$$

**Step 5 — Decision rule.**
Reject independence if $\vert Z_0 \vert > 1.96$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. Repeat for several lags (e.g., $l = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, 10$) to capture different dependence structures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The Auto-correlation test is more *diagnostic* than the Runs test. While Runs test tells you *whether* dependence exists, Auto-correlation tells you *at what lag* the dependence is strongest. Both are required for a complete independence evaluation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Map: Properties of Random Numbers

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the hierarchical relationship between the six desirable properties of random numbers and their associated statistical tests.

```mermaid
graph TD
    RNS["Random Number Stream R1, R2, R3 ... Rn"]
    UNI["Property 1: Uniformity"]
    IND["Property 2: Independence"]
    EFF["Property 3: Efficiency"]
    REP["Property 4: Reproducibility"]
    PER["Property 5: Long Period"]
    POR["Property 6: Portability"]
    CHI["Chi-Square Frequency Test"]
    KS["Kolmogorov-Smirnov Test"]
    RUN["Runs Test Above and Below Mean"]
    AUT["Auto-Correlation Test at Lag L"]
    ALG["Algorithm Design: LCG, Mid-Square"]
    SEED["Fixed Seed Input"]
    PLAT["Cross Platform Compilation"]

    RNS --> UNI
    RNS --> IND
    RNS --> EFF
    RNS --> REP
    RNS --> PER
    RNS --> POR
    UNI --> CHI
    UNI --> KS
    IND --> RUN
    IND --> AUT
    REP --> SEED
    EFF --> ALG
    POR --> PLAT
    PER --> ALG
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture: Statistical Testing Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **operational workflow** of validating a pseudo-random number generator against the two most critical properties: Uniformity and Independence.

```mermaid
graph LR
    STAGE1["STAGE 1: GENERATE"]:::stageClass
    STAGE2["STAGE 2: UNIFORMITY TEST"]:::stageClass
    STAGE3["STAGE 3: INDEPENDENCE TEST"]:::stageClass
    STAGE4["STAGE 4: VALIDATION"]:::stageClass
    DEC1{"Accept H0?"}:::decisionClass
    DEC2{"Accept H0?"}:::decisionClass
    REJ1["Reject RNG: Not Uniform"]:::rejectClass
    REJ2["Reject RNG: Dependent"]:::rejectClass
    PASS["RNG Certified for Simulation Use"]:::passClass

    STAGE1 --> STAGE2
    STAGE2 --> DEC1
    DEC1 -- "Yes" --> STAGE3
    DEC1 -- "No" --> REJ1
    STAGE3 --> DEC2
    DEC2 -- "Yes" --> STAGE4
    DEC2 -- "No" --> REJ2
    STAGE4 --> PASS

    classDef stageClass fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#0d3b66,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef decisionClass fill:#ffbb33,stroke:#b37700,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef rejectClass fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a0e0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef passClass fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#0d5c0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Decision Flow for Each Statistical Test

```mermaid
graph TD
    HYP["Formulate Hypotheses"]:::hyp
    H0["H0: Numbers are Uniform and Independent"]:::nullH
    H1["H1: Numbers are NOT Uniform or Dependent"]:::altH
    CALC["Compute Test Statistic"]:::calc
    CRIT["Lookup Critical Value at alpha equals 0.05"]:::crit
    COMPARE{"Statistic vs Critical Value"}:::comp
    ACC["Accept H0: RNG Passes Test"]:::accept
    REJ["Reject H0: RNG Fails Test"]:::reject

    HYP --> H0
    HYP --> H1
    H0 --> CALC
    H1 --> CALC
    CALC --> CRIT
    CRIT --> COMPARE
    COMPARE -- "Within bounds" --> ACC
    COMPARE -- "Exceeds bounds" --> REJ

    classDef hyp fill:#9467bd,stroke:#4a2c75,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef nullH fill:#17becf,stroke:#0a6673,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef altH fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#5e600d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef calc fill:#e377c2,stroke:#732a5c,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef crit fill:#7f7f7f,stroke:#404040,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef comp fill:#ffbb33,stroke:#b37700,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef accept fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#0d5c0d,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef reject fill:#d62728,stroke:#7a0e0e,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Note on Diagrams:** A well-labelled block diagram in your answer script (whether hand-drawn or using flowchart conventions) is worth 1-2 bonus marks. The Mermaid-style architecture is rendered above; in your exam, replicate the *functional stages* (Generate → Test Uniformity → Test Independence → Validate) as a flow chart.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1.a]**

**Q1.** *Define a Random Number. List any four desirable properties that a sequence of pseudo-random numbers must satisfy to be used in simulation studies.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A Random Number is a numerical value drawn from a probability distribution such that each value in the sample space has a known probability of occurrence. In simulation, it typically refers to a real number $R \in [0, 1]$ drawn from a Uniform $[0,1]$ distribution.

The four desirable properties are:
1. **Uniformity** — numbers must be uniformly distributed in $[0, 1]$.
2. **Independence** — no statistical correlation between successive numbers.
3. **Efficiency** — fast generation with low memory overhead.
4. **Reproducibility** — same seed must produce the same sequence.

*(Marks: 1 for definition, 0.5 each × 4 for properties = 3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sample Paper Q1.b]**

**Q2.** *Distinguish between the Chi-Square test and the Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) test for testing the uniformity of random numbers. Which is preferred for small sample sizes, and why?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Chi-Square Test** | **K-S Test** |
|---|---|---|
| Data handling | Requires binning into discrete cells | Uses raw (unbinned) data |
| Sample size | Better for large $n \ge 50$ | Better for small $n < 50$ |
| Test type | Non-parametric, discrete | Non-parametric, continuous |
| Power | Less powerful for small samples | More powerful for small samples |

**K-S test is preferred for small samples** because it does not require arbitrary cell-width selection, thus preserving more statistical information and avoiding loss of power due to binning. *(3 marks: 1.5 for distinction table, 1.5 for justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Paper Q2 (Module 1) — Choice A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **Kolmogorov-Smirnov (K-S) test** for testing the uniformity of random numbers. State the hypotheses, derive the test statistic, and describe the decision rule with appropriate critical value.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A pseudo-random number generator produces the following sorted sequence of $n = 10$ numbers. Apply the K-S test at significance level $\alpha = 0.05$ to determine if the sequence is uniform. The critical value $D_{0.05, 10} = 0.409$.

Data: $0.05, 0.14, 0.22, 0.34, 0.41, 0.55, 0.63, 0.74, 0.86, 0.97$

**Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: The random numbers follow Uniform $[0, 1]$ distribution.
- $H_1$: The random numbers do *not* follow Uniform $[0, 1]$ distribution.

**Step 2 — Procedure:**
Sort the $n$ random numbers in ascending order: $R_{(1)} \le R_{(2)} \le \ldots \le R_{(n)}$. The theoretical CDF for Uniform $[0,1]$ is $F(R) = R$.

**Step 3 — Compute the test statistics:**

$$
D^+ = \max_{1 \le i \le n} \left( \frac{i}{n} - R_{(i)} \right)
$$

$$
D^- = \max_{1 \le i \le n} \left( R_{(i)} - \frac{i-1}{n} \right)
$$

$$
D = \max(D^+, D^-)
$$

**Step 4 — Decision Rule:**
- If $D \le D_{\alpha, n}$: **Accept $H_0$** (uniformity holds).
- If $D > D_{\alpha, n}$: **Reject $H_0$** (uniformity violated).

*(Marks: 1 for hypotheses, 3 for procedure, 2 for test statistic derivation, 1 for decision rule)*

**Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Tabulate the calculations:**

| $i$ | $R_{(i)}$ | $i/n$ | $(i-1)/n$ | $i/n - R_{(i)}$ | $R_{(i)} - (i-1)/n$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.05 | 0.10 | 0.00 | 0.05 | 0.05 |
| 2 | 0.14 | 0.20 | 0.10 | 0.06 | 0.04 |
| 3 | 0.22 | 0.30 | 0.20 | 0.08 | 0.02 |
| 4 | 0.34 | 0.40 | 0.30 | 0.06 | 0.04 |
| 5 | 0.41 | 0.50 | 0.40 | 0.09 | 0.01 |
| 6 | 0.55 | 0.60 | 0.50 | 0.05 | 0.05 |
| 7 | 0.63 | 0.70 | 0.60 | 0.07 | 0.03 |
| 8 | 0.74 | 0.80 | 0.70 | 0.06 | 0.04 |
| 9 | 0.86 | 0.90 | 0.80 | 0.04 | 0.06 |
| 10 | 0.97 | 1.00 | 0.90 | 0.03 | 0.07 |

**Step 2 — Identify the maximum deviations:**

$$
D^+ = \max(0.05, 0.06, 0.08, 0.06, 0.09, 0.05, 0.07, 0.06, 0.04, 0.03) = 0.09
$$

$$
D^- = \max(0.05, 0.04, 0.02, 0.04, 0.01, 0.05, 0.03, 0.04, 0.06, 0.07) = 0.07
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the K-S statistic:**

$$
D = \max(D^+, D^-) = \max(0.09, 0.07) = 0.09
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**
Since $D = 0.09 \le D_{0.05, 10} = 0.409$, we **accept $H_0$**. The sequence is consistent with a Uniform $[0, 1]$ distribution at the 5% significance level. *(7 marks: 3 for table, 2 for $D^+$ and $D^-$, 1 for $D$, 1 for conclusion)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Main Paper Q2 (Module 1) — Choice B]**

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Runs Test (Above and Below the Mean)** for testing the independence of random numbers. Derive the expressions for the mean and variance of the number of runs.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sequence of 20 random numbers has a sample mean of 0.5. Out of 20 numbers, 12 lie above or equal to the mean and 8 lie below the mean. The total number of runs observed is 8. Apply the Runs Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ to test for independence. Given: $Z_{0.025} = 1.96$.

**Model Solution for (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Concept of a Run:**
A *run* is a maximal sequence of consecutive random numbers that all lie on the same side of the sample mean $\bar{R}$. Independence implies a "balanced" number of runs — too few indicate strong correlation; too many indicate oscillation (also undesirable).

**Step 2 — Classification:**
Replace each $R_i$ with a binary symbol $A$ (if $R_i \ge \bar{R}$) or $B$ (if $R_i < \bar{R}$). The sequence becomes a string of $A$s and $B$s. Count the number of transitions; the number of runs $R$ equals the number of transitions + 1.

**Step 3 — Hypotheses:**
- $H_0$: The numbers are independent.
- $H_1$: The numbers are not independent.

**Step 4 — Mean and Variance of Runs under $H_0$:**

For $n_1$ values $\ge \bar{R}$ and $n_2$ values $< \bar{R}$ (with $n = n_1 + n_2$):

$$
\mu_R = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{n} + 1
$$

$$
\sigma_R^2 = \frac{2 n_1 n_2 (2 n_1 n_2 - n)}{n^2 (n - 1)}
$$

**Step 5 — Test Statistic and Decision Rule:**

$$
Z_0 = \frac{R - \mu_R}{\sigma_R}
$$

For $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-tailed): Accept $H_0$ if $-1.96 \le Z_0 \le 1.96$. *(7 marks: 1 for concept, 1 for classification, 1 for hypotheses, 2 for mean and variance derivations, 2 for test statistic and decision rule)*

**Model Solution for (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Step 1 — Given data:**
$n = 20$, $n_1 = 12$, $n_2 = 8$, $R = 8$.

**Step 2 — Compute $\mu_R$:**

$$
\mu_R = \frac{2 \times 12 \times 8}{20} + 1 = \frac{192}{20} + 1 = 9.6 + 1 = 10.6
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\sigma_R^2$:**

$$
\sigma_R^2 = \frac{2 \times 12 \times 8 \times (2 \times 12 \times 8 - 20)}{20^2 \times (20 - 1)} = \frac{192 \times (192 - 20)}{400 \times 19} = \frac{192 \times 172}{7600}
$$

$$
\sigma_R^2 = \frac{33024}{7600} = 4.3453
$$

$$
\sigma_R = \sqrt{4.3453} \approx 2.0845
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $Z_0$:**

$$
Z_0 = \frac{R - \mu_R}{\sigma_R} = \frac{8 - 10.6}{2.0845} = \frac{-2.6}{2.0845} \approx -1.247
$$

**Step 5 — Decision:**
Since $-1.96 \le Z_0 = -1.247 \le 1.96$, we **accept $H_0$** at the 5% significance level. The random numbers are **independent**. *(7 marks: 1 for identification, 2 for $\mu_R$, 2 for $\sigma_R$, 1 for $Z_0$, 1 for conclusion)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in "Properties of Random Numbers" (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing the Two Properties (3-mark loss):** Students frequently mix up *Uniformity* and *Independence*. Memorise: **Uniformity = how the numbers are *distributed***; **Independence = how the numbers are *related***.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting the $+1$ in Runs Test (2-mark loss):** The expected number of runs is $\mu_R = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{n} + 1$. The trailing $+1$ comes from the convention that a single number is also a run. Omitting it shifts $Z_0$ by exactly $\frac{1}{\sigma_R}$, often flipping the decision.
> 
> 3. **Wrong Degrees of Freedom in Chi-Square (2-mark loss):** The d.o.f. is $k - 1$ (not $k$ or $k - 2$). One degree is lost due to the constraint $\sum O_i = n$.
> 
> 4. **Using the Wrong Critical Value (2-mark loss):** For K-S, you must look up $D_{\alpha, n}$ — the critical value depends on BOTH $\alpha$ AND $n$. Students often quote a single "magic number" like 0.05, which is wrong.
> 
> 5. **Not Stating Hypotheses (1-2 mark loss):** Always write $H_0$ and $H_1$ explicitly before the calculation. Examiners are mandated to deduct marks for missing hypotheses in the KTU 2024 OBE pattern.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Properties of Random Numbers"**

- [x] A **Random Number** in simulation is a value from Uniform $[0,1]$, denoted $R \sim U(0,1)$.
- [x] Theoretical **mean** $= 0.5$; **variance** $= \frac{1}{12}$; **standard deviation** $\approx 0.2887$.
- [x] The **six desirable properties** are: Uniformity, Independence, Efficiency, Reproducibility, Long Period, Portability.
- [x] **Uniformity** is tested by: Chi-Square test (binned) or Kolmogorov-Smirnov test (unbinned).
- [x] **Chi-Square statistic:** $\chi^2_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}$, with d.o.f. $= k - 1$.
- [x] **K-S statistic:** $D = \max(D^+, D^-)$ where $D^+ = \max \left( \frac{i}{n} - R_{(i)} \right)$ and $D^- = \max \left( R_{(i)} - \frac{i-1}{n} \right)$.
- [x] **Independence** is tested by: Runs Test (above/below mean) or Auto-correlation test (at lag $l$).
- [x] **Runs Test statistic:** $Z_0 = \frac{R - \mu_R}{\sigma_R}$ with $\mu_R = \frac{2 n_1 n_2}{n} + 1$ and $\sigma_R^2 = \frac{2 n_1 n_2 (2 n_1 n_2 - n)}{n^2 (n - 1)}$.
- [x] **Auto-correlation statistic:** $Z_0 = \rho_l \sqrt{n - 1}$ where $\rho_l$ is the lag-$l$ correlation coefficient.
- [x] **Decision rule** at $\alpha = 0.05$: accept $H_0$ if test statistic $\le$ critical value; reject otherwise.
- [x] K-S is preferred for **small samples** ($n < 50$); Chi-Square is preferred for **large samples** ($n \ge 50$).
- [x] **Always state hypotheses** ($H_0$ and $H_1$) explicitly before any test calculation.
- [x] Random number streams are **reproducible** (a feature, not a bug) — this is essential for debugging simulation runs.
- [x] Period length $m$ should be $\ge 2^{31}$ for production-quality 32-bit RNGs.
- [x] Uniform $[0,1]$ random variates are the **building blocks** for all other distributions (exponential, normal, Poisson, etc.) via the **Inverse Transform Method**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Generation of Pseudo-Random Numbers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Generation of Pseudo-Random Numbers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pseudo-Random Number (PRN)** is a sequence of numerical values generated by a fully deterministic mathematical algorithm that exhibits statistical properties indistinguishable from those of a truly random sequence drawn from a uniform distribution $U(0, 1)$. The pseudo-random number generator (PRNG) is initialized using a starting value known as the **seed**, which makes the entire sequence **reproducible** — a critical property for scientific simulation under the KTU 2024 scheme.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for Examiners:**
> - **Pseudo-Random** → Deterministic, periodic, reproducible (computer generated)
> - **Quasi-Random** → Low-discrepancy sequences (e.g., Sobol, Halton) — *NOT covered in KTU Module 1*
> - **Cryptographically Secure PRNG** → Unpredictable to attackers — *Out of Module 1 scope*

### 1.2 Properties of a "Good" PRNG (KTU Mandatory Highlights)

A generator used in discrete-event simulation must satisfy the following requirements:

| Property | Definition | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Uniformity** | Each $R_i \sim U(0,1)$ with equal density $f(r) = 1$ | Ensures fair sampling of input space |
| **Independence** | $\text{Cov}(R_i, R_j) = 0$ for $i \neq j$ | Prevents hidden correlation biasing output |
| **Long Period** | Cycle length $\to \infty$ | Avoids repetition artifacts in long runs |
| **Reproducibility** | Same seed $\Rightarrow$ same stream | Required for debugging and variance reduction |
| **Computational Speed** | Minimal CPU cycles per number | Required for million-iteration Monte Carlo runs |
| **Portability** | Identical output across hardware | Allows replicated studies globally |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Mathematical Slot Machine"**
> Imagine a slot machine that *looks* random but is secretly powered by a perfectly memorized recipe. You feed it a starting recipe number (the **seed**), and it rolls out a deterministic sequence of "wins." If you ever use the same recipe (seed), it will produce the *exact same wins* in the exact same order. That is pseudo-randomness — deterministic from the outside, statistically random from the inside.

In simulation, this is **desirable**, not a flaw. We *want* a researcher in Thiruvananthapuram and a researcher in Kochi to obtain identical experimental results when simulating the same queuing system. True randomness (e.g., atmospheric noise) makes this impossible.

### 1.4 Random Number Streams and Substreams

A **stream** is the full output sequence of one PRNG. A **substreams** are non-overlapping sub-blocks of a single stream, indexed by a starting seed value. KTU Module 1 expects you to know that stream independence is achieved by using **different seeds** (or for combined generators, different prime moduli).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of PRNGs (Module 1 Syllabus Map)

The KTU 2024 PCIET601 Module 1 mandates study of **four** core generation techniques:

1. **Middle-Square Method** (von Neumann, 1946) — historical
2. **Linear Congruential Generator (LCG)** — most heavily tested
3. **Combined Linear Congruential Generator (CLCG)** — extension
4. **Random Number Tables** — reference source

### 2.2 The Linear Congruential Generator (LCG) — The Heart of the Module

The LCG is governed by the recurrence:

$$
X_{i+1} = (a \, X_i + c) \pmod{m}
$$

where every symbol has strict KTU-mandated meaning:

- $X_0$ → **seed** (initial value), with $0 \le X_0 < m$
- $a$ → **multiplier** (also called the *constant of multiplication*)
- $c$ → **increment** (also called the *additive constant*)
- $m$ → **modulus** (also called the *prime modulus* in mixed LCGs)
- The output **pseudo-random integer** is $X_{i+1}$
- The **uniform PRN** is obtained by: $R_{i+1} = \dfrac{X_{i+1}}{m}$, so $R \in (0, 1)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Bookmark — LCG Variants:**
> - **Mixed (Additive) LCG** → $c \neq 0$
> - **Multiplicative LCG** → $c = 0$, i.e., $X_{i+1} = a X_i \pmod{m}$
> - The multiplicative LCG is the form almost universally used in commercial simulators (e.g., MATLAB `rng('twister')` family, SIMAN, Arena).

### 2.3 Hull-Dobell Theorem — Full Period Conditions (KTU High-Yield)

A mixed LCG achieves its **maximum possible period $m$** if and only if **all three** Hull-Dobell conditions are satisfied:

1. $\gcd(c, m) = 1$ (i.e., $c$ and $m$ are relatively prime)
2. $a - 1$ is divisible by every prime factor of $m$
3. $a - 1$ is divisible by **4** if $m$ is divisible by **4**

For the **multiplicative LCG** ($c=0$), the maximum achievable period is $m - 1$, and $m$ must be a prime number with $a$ a *primitive root modulo $m$*.

### 2.4 The Middle-Square Method (von Neumann)

Operational steps:
1. Start with a 4-digit seed $X_0$ (e.g., $X_0 = 5731$).
2. Square it: $X_0^2 = 32850361$.
3. Extract the **middle** 4 digits: $X_1 = 8503$.
4. Pad with leading zeros if necessary.
5. $R_1 = X_1 / 10000 = 0.8503$.
6. Repeat indefinitely.

> [!WARNING]
> **Why Middle-Square is not used in modern simulation:** It collapses to zero quickly (the "zero trap") and exhibits poor statistical independence. KTU tests it for **historical context only**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

$$
\begin{array}{|l|l|l|}
\hline
\textbf{Generator} & \textbf{Recurrence Formula} & \textbf{KTU Condition} \\
\hline
\text{Mixed LCG} & X_{i+1} = (a X_i + c) \bmod m & \text{Full period } m \text{ via Hull-Dobell} \\
\hline
\text{Multiplicative LCG} & X_{i+1} = a X_i \bmod m & m \text{ prime; } a \text{ primitive root} \\
\hline
\text{Combined LCG} & X_{i,n+1} = (X_{1,n+1} - X_{2,n+1}) \bmod m_1 & m_1, m_2 \text{ distinct primes} \\
\hline
\text{PRN Normalization} & R_i = X_i / m & R_i \in [0, 1) \\
\hline
\text{PRN Range Scaling} & S_i = a + (b-a) R_i & S_i \in [a, b) \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility — Why LCGs Dominate Industrial Simulation**
> LCGs are used in **Arena, Simul8, FlexSim, AnyLogic, MATLAB/Simulink, R, Python (legacy `numpy.random`)**, and nearly every commercial discrete-event simulator. Their dominance stems from O(1) generation, seed-reproducibility, and trivially validatable periods — perfect for Monte Carlo, queuing, and reliability simulations.

### 2.6 Random Number Tables

Pre-computed tables (e.g., the A Million Random Digits book by RAND Corporation, 1955) provide digits $0$–$9$ uniformly. Grouping digits yields PRNs: e.g., $5$-digit groups divided by $100000$ produce $R_i \in [0,1)$. KTU question banks occasionally ask for table-based normalization; the formula is $R = 0.d_1 d_2 d_3 \dots d_k$ for $k$ chosen digits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Mixed LCG (KTU Dec 2023 Pattern)

**Problem:** Given $X_0 = 17$, $a = 21$, $c = 35$, $m = 100$, generate the first **six** pseudo-random integers and the corresponding $R_i$ values.

**Step 1 — Validate Hull-Dobell (Examiner's first check):**
- $\gcd(35, 100) = 5 \neq 1$ → **FAIL** (Full period not achieved, but $X_i$ values are still well-defined for the first few iterations.)

> [!NOTE]
> For pedagogical KTU problems, examiners *intentionally* violate Hull-Dobell to test whether you can mechanically compute the sequence. You must compute regardless.

**Step 2 — Compute $X_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= (a X_0 + c) \bmod m \\
    &= (21 \times 17 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= (357 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= 392 \bmod 100 \\
    &= 92
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_1$:**

$$
R_1 = \frac{X_1}{m} = \frac{92}{100} = 0.92
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $X_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_2 &= (21 \times 92 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= (1932 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= 1967 \bmod 100 \\
    &= 67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $R_2$:**

$$
R_2 = \frac{67}{100} = 0.67
$$

**Step 6 — Iterate through $X_6$:** Applying the same recurrence mechanically:

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_3 &= (21 \times 67 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= (1407 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= 1442 \bmod 100 = 42 \\
R_3 &= 0.42
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_4 &= (21 \times 42 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= (882 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= 917 \bmod 100 = 17 \\
R_4 &= 0.17
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cycle Detection Insight:** $X_4 = 17 = X_0$. The cycle has closed! The period of this LCG is exactly **4**. This visually demonstrates the failure of Hull-Dobell's first condition — you should always observe the period in your answer table.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_5 &= (21 \times 17 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= 392 \bmod 100 = 92 = X_1 \\
R_5 &= 0.92
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_6 &= (21 \times 92 + 35) \bmod 100 \\
    &= 1967 \bmod 100 = 67 = X_2 \\
R_6 &= 0.67
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical Matrix — Final Answer Table:**

| $i$ | $X_{i-1}$ | $a X_{i-1} + c$ | $X_i = (aX_{i-1}+c) \bmod 100$ | $R_i = X_i / 100$ |
|:---:|:---------:|:---------------:|:-------------------------------:|:-----------------:|
| 1 | 17 | 392 | 92 | 0.92 |
| 2 | 92 | 1967 | 67 | 0.67 |
| 3 | 67 | 1442 | 42 | 0.42 |
| 4 | 42 | 917 | **17** | 0.17 |
| 5 | 17 | 392 | 92 | 0.92 |
| 6 | 92 | 1967 | 67 | 0.67 |

### 3.2 Worked Example — Multiplicative LCG

**Problem:** $X_0 = 5$, $a = 11$, $c = 0$, $m = 1000$. Generate $X_1 \dots X_5$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
X_1 &= (11 \times 5) \bmod 1000 = 55 \\
X_2 &= (11 \times 55) \bmod 1000 = 605 \\
X_3 &= (11 \times 605) \bmod 1000 = 6655 \bmod 1000 = 655 \\
X_4 &= (11 \times 655) \bmod 1000 = 7205 \bmod 1000 = 205 \\
X_5 &= (11 \times 205) \bmod 1000 = 2255 \bmod 1000 = 255
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example — Random Number Range Mapping

**Problem:** Generate five exponentially distributed service times with mean $\beta = 2$ minutes from the $R_i$ values $[0.92,\ 0.67,\ 0.42,\ 0.17,\ 0.92]$ using the inverse-transform method.

The exponential inverse CDF is $S_i = -\beta \ln(1 - R_i)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= -2 \ln(1 - 0.92) = -2 \ln(0.08) = 5.105 \text{ min} \\
S_2 &= -2 \ln(1 - 0.67) = -2 \ln(0.33) = 2.218 \text{ min} \\
S_3 &= -2 \ln(1 - 0.42) = -2 \ln(0.58) = 1.089 \text{ min} \\
S_4 &= -2 \ln(1 - 0.17) = -2 \ln(0.83) = 0.373 \text{ min} \\
S_5 &= -2 \ln(1 - 0.92) = 5.105 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This example shows the *entire purpose* of pseudo-random number generation: producing reproducible input samples for any distribution needed in a simulation model.

### 3.4 Combined LCG — Derivation Sketch

The Combined LCG combines two multiplicative LCGs of distinct prime moduli $m_1$ and $m_2$ to produce a sequence with period equal to the **least common multiple** of the individual periods, typically achieving full-period independence even when neither generator alone would.

$$
X_{i,n+1} = (X_{1,n+1} - X_{2,n+1}) \bmod m_1
$$

with $m_2 = m_1 - 1$. The combined sequence $X_i$ has period $\approx m_1 m_2$ and statistically superior independence.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LCG Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    seedIn["Seed X0"] --> mul1["Multiply by a"]
    mul1 --> add1["Add c"]
    add1 --> mod1["Modulo m"]
    mod1 --> out1["New X(i+1)"]
    out1 --> split{Split Output}
    split --> norm["Normalize X(i+1) / m"]
    split --> loopback["Feedback as X(i+1)"]
    loopback --> mul1
    norm --> prn["Uniform PRN R(i+1) in 0 to 1"]
    prn --> transform["Distribution Transform Step"]
    transform --> sample["Final Simulated Sample"]
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of PRNGs (Module 1 Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Pseudo Random Number Generators"] --> B["Deterministic Algebraic Methods"]
    A --> C["Table Lookup Methods"]
    B --> D["Middle Square Method"]
    B --> E["Linear Congruential Generator"]
    E --> F["Mixed LCG with c nonzero"]
    E --> G["Multiplicative LCG with c equals zero"]
    B --> H["Combined Linear Congruential Generator"]
    C --> I["RAND Corporation Tables"]
    C --> J["Digit Concatenation Methods"]
```

### 4.3 PRNG Validation and Testing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Generated PRN Sequence R1 to Rn"] --> T1["Frequency Test Chi Square"]
    P --> T2["Runs Test Up Down Independence"]
    P --> T3["Autocorrelation Test Lag k"]
    P --> T4["Gap Test and Poker Test"]
    T1 --> D{"All Tests Pass"}
    T2 --> D
    T3 --> D
    T4 --> D
    D -->|Yes| Accept["Accept Generator for Simulation Use"]
    D -->|No| Reject["Reject and Reselect Parameters"]
```

### 4.4 LCG Period and Quality Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["Choose m a X0 c"] --> C1{"gcd of c and m equals 1"}
    C1 -->|No| F1["Period less than m"]
    C1 -->|Yes| C2{"Every prime factor of m divides a minus 1"}
    C2 -->|No| F2["Period less than m"]
    C2 -->|Yes| C3{"If m divisible by 4 then a minus 1 divisible by 4"}
    C3 -->|No| F3["Period less than m"]
    C3 -->|Yes| PASS["Full Period m Achieved Hull Dobell Satisfied"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
