1. **Define the Problem as an Expectation:** Express the desired quantity $I$ in the form $I = E[f(X)]$ where $X$ is a random variable with known pdf $p(x)$. This is the most *cerebral* step.
2. **Generate Random Inputs:** Use a PRNG (e.g., Linear Congruential Generator, Mersenne Twister) to produce samples $X_i \sim p(x)$.
3. **Evaluate the Function:** For each sample, compute the corresponding $Y_i = f(X_i)$.
4. **Aggregate the Sample Average:** Compute $\hat{I}_N = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} Y_i$.
5. **Estimate the Error:** Use the sample variance to construct a confidence interval of the form $\hat{I}_N \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Theoretical Pillars

| Pillar | Statement | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Law of Large Numbers (LLN)** | $\lim_{N \to \infty} P(\vert \hat{I}_N - I \vert < \epsilon) = 1$ | Guarantees convergence as sample size grows. |
| **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** | $\sqrt{N}(\hat{I}_N - I) \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ | Justifies the Gaussian confidence interval. |
| **Strong Law of Large Numbers** | $\hat{I}_N \to I$ *almost surely* | Provides deterministic-like convergence. |
| **Chebyshev's Inequality** | $P(\vert \hat{I}_N - I \vert \geq \epsilon) \leq \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{N\epsilon^2}$ | First-moment error bound (loose but universal). |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination).

| Formula / Identity | Mathematical Form | Units / Domain | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Monte Carlo Estimator | $\hat{I}_N = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} f(X_i)$ | dimensionless | Generic expectation estimation |
| Monte Carlo Integration in $[a,b]$ | $I \approx \frac{(b-a)}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} f(X_i)$ | units of $f$ | Numerical integration in 1-D |
| Multi-Dim Integration | $I \approx \frac{V}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} f(\mathbf{X}_i)$ | units of $f$ | Multi-dimensional integration |
| Standard Error (SE) | $\text{SE} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}$ | units of $\hat{I}_N$ | Confidence interval half-width |
| Sample Variance | $s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(Y_i - \hat{I}_N)^2$ | squared units of $Y$ | Estimator precision |
| Confidence Interval (95%) | $\hat{I}_N \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}}$ | units of $\hat{I}_N$ | KTU-favoured bound |
| Chebyshev Bound | $P(\vert \hat{I}_N - I \vert \geq \epsilon) \leq \frac{s^2}{N\epsilon^2}$ | dimensionless | Universal (weak) bound |
| Convergence Rate | $\mathcal{O}(N^{-1/2})$ | dimensionless | Universal (independent of dim.) |
| Buffon's Needle (π est.) | $\pi \approx \frac{2L \cdot N}{D \cdot n_h}$ | dimensionless | Classic geometric probability |
| Hit-or-Miss Estimator | $\hat{p} = \frac{N_h}{N}$ | dimensionless | Estimating probabilities |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Monte Carlo methods are **the workhorse** of modern engineering simulation:

- **Nuclear Engineering:** Neutron transport simulation (the original Manhattan Project application by Ulam, von Neumann, Metropolis, 1940s).
- **Financial Engineering:** Option pricing, Value-at-Risk (VaR), portfolio optimization.
- **Computer Graphics:** Global illumination via path tracing (rays bounce randomly).
- **Reliability Engineering:** Estimating system failure probability in complex networks.
- **Computational Physics:** Quantum many-body problems, statistical mechanics, lattice gauge theory.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Monte Carlo Excels in High Dimensions:**
> Unlike deterministic quadrature (Trapezoidal, Simpson's) whose error grows exponentially with dimension $d$, Monte Carlo's error remains $\mathcal{O}(N^{-1/2})$ **regardless of $d$**. For $d \geq 4$, Monte Carlo is unbeatable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Deterministic Integral to Monte Carlo Estimator

**Goal:** Estimate the integral $I = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$.

**Step 1 — Probabilistic Reformulation:**
Let $X \sim \text{Uniform}(a, b)$. The pdf of $X$ is $p(x) = \frac{1}{b-a}$ for $x \in [a, b]$. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx = \int_{a}^{b} (b-a) \cdot f(x) \cdot \frac{1}{b-a} \, dx \\[6pt]
&= (b-a) \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, p(x) \, dx \\[6pt]
&= (b-a) \, E[f(X)]
\end{aligned}
$$

*Explanation:* We multiplied and divided by $(b-a)$ and recognized the integrand as $(b-a)f(x)p(x)$, where $p(x)$ is the uniform pdf. The integral of any $g(x) p(x)$ over its support is, by definition, $E[g(X)]$.

**Step 2 — Sample Average Approximation:**
By the **Strong Law of Large Numbers**, for i.i.d. samples $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_N \sim \text{Uniform}(a,b)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[f(X)] &= \lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(X_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Finite-Sample Estimator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{I}_N &= (b-a) \cdot \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(X_i) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(b-a)}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(X_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **standard 1-D Monte Carlo integration formula**. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Error Bound and Confidence Interval

**Step 1 — Variance of the Estimator:**
Since the $X_i$ are independent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(\hat{I}_N) &= \text{Var}\left( \frac{(b-a)}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(X_i) \right) \\[6pt]
&= \left(\frac{b-a}{N}\right)^2 \sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{Var}(f(X_i)) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(b-a)^2}{N^2} \cdot N \cdot \sigma^2 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{(b-a)^2 \sigma^2}{N}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(f(X))$. Hence the **standard error** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SE}(\hat{I}_N) &= \sqrt{\text{Var}(\hat{I}_N)} = \frac{(b-a)\,\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Applying the Central Limit Theorem (CLT):**
By the CLT, for large $N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{I}_N \sim \mathcal{N}\left(I, \frac{(b-a)^2 \sigma^2}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Constructing the 95% Confidence Interval:**
For a standard normal distribution, $P(-1.96 \leq Z \leq 1.96) = 0.95$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P\left( I - 1.96 \cdot \frac{(b-a)\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \leq \hat{I}_N \leq I + 1.96 \cdot \frac{(b-a)\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \right) \approx 0.95
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $I$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{I}_N - 1.96 \cdot \frac{(b-a)\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \leq I \leq \hat{I}_N + 1.96 \cdot \frac{(b-a)\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\sigma$ is unknown, we substitute the **sample standard deviation** $s$, yielding the final practical form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &\approx \hat{I}_N \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{(b-a)\,s}{\sqrt{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $s^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (Y_i - \hat{I}_N)^2$ and $Y_i = f(X_i)$. $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem:** Estimate $I = \int_{0}^{1} e^{x} \, dx$ using $N = 5$ uniform random samples. (True value: $e - 1 \approx 1.7183$.)

**Step 1 — Draw Random Samples:**
Suppose the PRNG produces $X_1 = 0.41, X_2 = 0.78, X_3 = 0.13, X_4 = 0.95, X_5 = 0.62$.

**Step 2 — Evaluate $f(X) = e^{X}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= e^{0.41} = 1.5068 \\
Y_2 &= e^{0.78} = 2.1815 \\
Y_3 &= e^{0.13} = 1.1388 \\
Y_4 &= e^{0.95} = 2.5857 \\
Y_5 &= e^{0.62} = 1.8589
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Average (note $(b-a)=1$ here):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{I}_5 &= \frac{1}{5}(1.5068 + 2.1815 + 1.1388 + 2.5857 + 1.8589) \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1}{5}(9.2717) \\[6pt]
&= 1.8543
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Error and Confidence Interval:**
Sample variance: $s^2 = \frac{1}{4}\sum (Y_i - 1.8543)^2 = 0.2917$, so $s = 0.5401$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SE} &= \frac{s}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{0.5401}{2.2361} = 0.2415
\end{aligned}
$$

95% CI: $1.8543 \pm 1.96 \times 0.2415 = 1.8543 \pm 0.4734 \Rightarrow [1.3809,\ 2.3277]$. True value $1.7183$ lies inside — verified!

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Monte Carlo Simulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Problem as Expectation I = E f X] --> B[Select Probability Distribution p x]
    B --> C[Generate Pseudo-Random Samples Xi using PRNG]
    C --> D[Evaluate Function Yi = f Xi for each sample]
    D --> E[Compute Sample Average I_N]
    E --> F[Compute Sample Variance s squared]
    F --> G{Is Error Acceptable?}
    G -- No --> H[Increase Sample Size N]
    H --> C
    G -- Yes --> I[Output Final Estimate with Confidence Interval]
    I --> J[Report Mean plus minus z half alpha times s over sqrt N]
```

*Figure 1: End-to-end Monte Carlo simulation pipeline. The loop on the right represents the iterative refinement of the estimator until the desired precision is achieved.*

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer[INPUT GENERATION LAYER]
        A1[PRNG Core - Mersenne Twister] --> A2[Uniform 0 1]
        A2 --> A3[Inverse Transform Method]
        A3 --> A4[Target Distribution p x]
    end

    subgraph ComputeLayer[COMPUTATION LAYER]
        A4 --> B1[Sample Xi]
        B1 --> B2[Function Evaluator f Xi]
        B2 --> B3[Yi Storage Buffer]
        B3 --> B4[Running Sum and Sum of Squares]
    end

    subgraph OutputLayer[STATISTICAL OUTPUT LAYER]
        B4 --> C1[Mean Estimator I hat N]
        B4 --> C2[Variance Estimator s squared]
        C2 --> C3[Standard Error SE]
        C1 --> C4[Confidence Interval Builder]
        C3 --> C4
        C4 --> C5[Final Report - Mean plus minus CI]
    end
```

*Figure 2: Block-level architecture of a Monte Carlo engine. The three layers are decoupled for modularity — this is the standard structure implemented in production simulation frameworks.*

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Convergence Schematic — Error vs. Sample Size

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Convergence Behaviour of Monte Carlo] --> Q[Convergence Rate is O of N to power minus 1 over 2]
    P --> R[To Halve the Error we must Quadruple N]
    P --> S[To Decuple Precision we need 100x more samples]
    Q --> T[Compare with Deterministic Quadrature]
    T --> U[Trapezoidal Rule - O of N to power minus 2 in 1D]
    T --> V[Simpsons Rule - O of N to power minus 4 in 1D]
    T --> W[But in d dimensions cost becomes exponential]
    W --> X[Monte Carlo wins in d greater than or equal 4]
```

*Figure 3: Comparative convergence schematic illustrating why Monte Carlo dominates in high-dimensional problems.*

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> **Define the Monte Carlo Method. State any two applications of it in engineering.**

**Model Answer:**
The Monte Carlo Method is a computational technique that uses repeated random sampling and the Law of Large Numbers to obtain numerical estimates for mathematical or physical problems that are difficult to solve analytically.

*(3 Marks — Definition: 2 marks; Applications: 0.5 + 0.5 marks)*

**Two Applications:**
1. **Numerical Integration:** Estimating high-dimensional integrals where deterministic quadrature fails (the "Curse of Dimensionality").
2. **Reliability Engineering:** Estimating the probability of failure in complex systems with stochastic component lifetimes.
3. *(Optional third for extra credit)* **Finance:** Option pricing using stochastic simulation (Black-Scholes extension).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> **State and explain the Law of Large Numbers as applied to Monte Carlo simulation.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Weak Law of Large Numbers (WLLN)** states that for i.i.d. random variables $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_N$ with finite mean $\mu$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{N \to \infty} P\left( \left\vert \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} X_i - \mu \right\vert < \epsilon \right) = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

for any $\epsilon > 0$.

**Application to Monte Carlo:** The sample mean $\hat{I}_N = \frac{1}{N}\sum f(X_i)$ converges (in probability) to the true expected value $I = E[f(X)]$ as $N \to \infty$. This theorem is the mathematical guarantee that running more simulations produces more accurate estimates.

*(3 Marks — Statement: 2 marks; Application explanation: 1 mark)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the Monte Carlo estimator for the integral $I = \int_{a}^{b} f(x)\, dx$ using uniform random sampling. Show all steps clearly. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Estimate $I = \int_{0}^{2} x^2 \, dx$ using Monte Carlo method with the following $N=4$ uniform samples in $[0,2]$: $X_1 = 0.5, X_2 = 1.2, X_3 = 1.8, X_4 = 0.9$. Compute the 95% confidence interval. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Let $X \sim \text{Uniform}(a,b)$ with pdf $p(x) = \frac{1}{b-a}$.

**Step 2:** Express the integral as an expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \cdot \frac{1}{p(x)} \cdot p(x) \, dx = (b-a) \int_{a}^{b} \frac{f(x)}{1} \cdot p(x) \, dx = (b-a) \, E[f(X)]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** By the **Strong Law of Large Numbers**, for i.i.d. samples:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[f(X)] &\approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(X_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{I}_N &= \frac{b-a}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} f(X_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Error analysis via CLT:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SE} &= \frac{(b-a)\,\sigma}{\sqrt{N}} \approx \frac{(b-a)\,s}{\sqrt{N}}
\end{aligned}
$$

CI: $\hat{I}_N \pm 1.96 \cdot \frac{(b-a) s}{\sqrt{N}}$.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Given $f(x) = x^2$, $a=0, b=2, N=4$.

**Step 1 — Evaluate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1 &= (0.5)^2 = 0.25 \\
Y_2 &= (1.2)^2 = 1.44 \\
Y_3 &= (1.8)^2 = 3.24 \\
Y_4 &= (0.9)^2 = 0.81
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum and Mean:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum Y_i &= 0.25 + 1.44 + 3.24 + 0.81 = 5.74 \\
\hat{I}_4 &= \frac{2 - 0}{4} \times 5.74 = \frac{1}{2} \times 5.74 = 2.87
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sample Variance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s^2 &= \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(Y_i - \bar{Y})^2 = \frac{1}{3}\left[(0.25 - 1.435)^2 + (1.44 - 1.435)^2 + (3.24 - 1.435)^2 + (0.81 - 1.435)^2\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{3}[1.4032 + 0.000025 + 3.2550 + 0.3906] \\
&= \frac{1}{3}(5.0489) = 1.6830
\end{aligned}
$$

So $s = \sqrt{1.6830} = 1.2973$.

**Step 4 — Standard Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SE} &= \frac{(b-a) \cdot s}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{2 \times 1.2973}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{2.5946}{2} = 1.2973
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — 95% Confidence Interval:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &\approx 2.87 \pm 1.96 \times 1.2973 = 2.87 \pm 2.5427 \\
&\Rightarrow [0.3273,\ 5.4127]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** True value $= \int_0^2 x^2 dx = \frac{8}{3} \approx 2.667$, which lies inside the interval. The wide interval reflects the small sample size $N=4$.

---

> **Question B [14 Marks] — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the Central Limit Theorem and its role in establishing the confidence interval for a Monte Carlo estimator. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A researcher estimates the reliability of a system by simulating $N = 100$ trials. The sample mean time-to-failure is $\hat{\mu} = 850$ hours, and the sample standard deviation is $s = 120$ hours. Construct a 99% confidence interval for the true mean time-to-failure. ($z_{0.005} = 2.576$) **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CLT and Confidence Interval (7 Marks):**

The **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)** states that for i.i.d. random variables $X_i$ with mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_N &= \frac{\bar{X}_N - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{N}} \xrightarrow{d} \mathcal{N}(0,1) \text{ as } N \to \infty
\end{aligned}
$$

**Role in Monte Carlo:**
- The sample mean $\hat{I}_N$ is asymptotically Gaussian, regardless of the underlying distribution of $X$.
- This permits use of standard normal quantiles ($z_{\alpha/2}$) to construct confidence intervals.
- The width of the CI is $\propto 1/\sqrt{N}$, giving the celebrated Monte Carlo convergence rate of $\mathcal{O}(N^{-1/2})$.

**CI Construction:** $P(-z_{\alpha/2} \leq Z_N \leq z_{\alpha/2}) = 1 - \alpha$. Rearranging:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P\left( \hat{I}_N - z_{\alpha/2}\frac{s}{\sqrt{N}} \leq \mu \leq \hat{I}_N + z_{\alpha/2}\frac{s}{\sqrt{N}} \right) = 1 - \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

Given: $N = 100$, $\hat{\mu} = 850$ hours, $s = 120$ hours, $z_{0.005} = 2.576$.

**Step 1 — Standard Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SE} &= \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}} = \frac{120}{\sqrt{100}} = \frac{120}{10} = 12 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Margin of Error:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \text{SE} = 2.576 \times 12 = 30.912 \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — 99% Confidence Interval:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu &\approx 850 \pm 30.912 \\
&\Rightarrow [819.088,\ 880.912] \text{ hours}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** We are 99% confident that the true mean time-to-failure of the system lies between **819.09 hours** and **880.91 hours**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor $(b-a)$:** Students often write $\hat{I}_N = \frac{1}{N}\sum f(X_i)$ without scaling by interval length — this loses **2 marks** in derivations.
> 2. **Using $N$ instead of $N-1$ in sample variance:** The unbiased estimator requires $N-1$ in the denominator. Using $N$ is a frequent error in KTU boards — deduct **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing 95% $z$-value (1.96) with 99% (2.576):** Always confirm the confidence level before picking the quantile. Wrong value = **2 marks lost**.
> 4. **Skipping units in the final answer:** KTU examiners reward dimensional consistency. Always write "hours," "metres," etc.
> 5. **Not citing LLN/CLT by name:** In derivations, naming these theorems explicitly earns full methodology marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1 (Monte Carlo Method):**

- [ ] **Definition:** Monte Carlo = repeated random sampling + sample averaging to estimate $E[f(X)]$.
- [ ] **Two Pillars:** Law of Large Numbers (convergence) + Central Limit Theorem (Gaussian CI).
- [ ] **Standard 1-D Estimator:** $\hat{I}_N = \frac{b-a}{N}\sum f(X_i)$, with $X_i \sim U(a,b)$.
- [ ] **Standard Error Formula:** $\text{SE} = \frac{(b-a) \cdot s}{\sqrt{N}}$.
- [ ] **CI Half-Width:** $E = z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \text{SE}$. Know $z = 1.96$ (95%), $2.576$ (99%).
- [ ] **Convergence Rate:** $\mathcal{O}(N^{-1/2})$ — dimension-independent. **Golden fact for KTU.**
- [ ] **Unbiased Variance:** Always use $N-1$ in the denominator of sample variance.
- [ ] **Advantage in High Dimensions:** MC error is **independent** of dimension $d$; quadrature error is $\mathcal{O}(N^{-k/d})$.
- [ ] **Applications to Remember:** Nuclear transport, finance (option pricing), reliability, computer graphics (ray tracing).
- [ ] **Variance Reduction Techniques:** Importance Sampling, Antithetic Variates, Control Variates, Stratified Sampling — *bonus points if mentioned*.
- [ ] **Classic Toy Problem:** Buffon's Needle for $\pi$ estimation; Hit-or-Miss for area estimation.
- [ ] **Common Mistake:** Confusing estimator formula with crude Monte Carlo integration (factor of $(b-a)$ is critical).
- [ ] **Sample Path Vocabulary:** One "run" = one realization; $N$ "runs" = $N$ samples for averaging.
- [ ] **Pseudo vs. True Random:** Always use PRNG (deterministic seed) for reproducibility — examiners love this nuance.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Transformation Method for Exponential, Uniform, Weibull, Triangular, and Discrete Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Transformation Method for Exponential, Uniform, Weibull, Triangular, and Discrete Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: Inverse Transformation Method (ITM)

The **Inverse Transformation Method (ITM)** is a fundamental **pseudo-random variate generation technique** used in Monte Carlo simulation to draw samples from any continuous probability distribution whose **Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF)** is known in closed form and is strictly invertible.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **Probability Integral Transformation Theorem (PIT)**
> If $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$, then the random variable $X = F^{-1}(U)$ has the Cumulative Distribution Function $F_X(x)$, provided $F$ is continuous and strictly monotonic.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — The "Dial and Read" Method

Imagine you have a **horizontal sliding scale (slider)** marked from **0 to 1**, and a **vertically stacked bar chart** where each bar represents a probability slice of a distribution.

1. You generate a random number $U$ between 0 and 1 — like spinning a perfectly fair roulette wheel.
2. You look horizontally from the value $U$ on the scale until you hit the **CDF curve** $F(x)$.
3. You drop a perpendicular down to the x-axis to read out $X$.

This $X$ value is your **simulated sample** drawn from the target distribution. The denser the CDF (steeper slope), the more frequently you will sample from that region — this is precisely how probability mass behaves!

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Inverse"?** We are *inverting* the CDF $F(x)$ to get $x = F^{-1}(u)$. The CDF maps $X \to U$, but we need the reverse map $U \to X$.

### 1.3 Pre-requisites Checklist for Applying ITM

| Requirement | Reason |
|---|---|
| CDF $F(x)$ must be **known analytically** | We need a closed-form equation to invert |
| $F(x)$ must be **continuous and strictly increasing** | Guarantees a unique inverse |
| Range of $F(x)$ must be **$[0, 1]$** | Matches the output of a $\text{Uniform}(0,1)$ RNG |
| $F^{-1}(u)$ must be computable in **closed form** | Numerical root-finding is slow for large simulations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Course Outcome Mapping (KTU CO):** This topic maps to **CO2** — *Apply random variate generation techniques to model stochastic systems.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Algorithm (The "How")

The ITM follows a **deterministic 3-step pipeline**:

1. **Generate** $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$ using the computer's RNG (e.g., LCG, Mersenne Twister).
2. **Compute** $X = F^{-1}(U)$ by plugging the random number into the inverted CDF formula.
3. **Return** $X$ as one observation drawn from the target distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why"**: Because $U$ is uniformly distributed on $[0,1]$, every sub-interval of equal width is **equally likely** to contain $U$. Since $F$ stretches and compresses the x-axis non-uniformly (reflecting the PDF shape), the resulting $X$ values inherit the exact probability structure of $F$.

## 2.2 Mathematical Justification (Theorem Statement)

Let $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$ and $X = F^{-1}(U)$. Then:

$$F_X(x) = P(X \leq x) = P(F^{-1}(U) \leq x) = P(U \leq F(x)) = F(x)$$

since $F$ is monotonic, and $P(U \leq u) = u$ for $u \in [0,1]$. QED.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Distribution | PDF $f(x)$ | CDF $F(x)$ | Inverse CDF $X = F^{-1}(U)$ | Valid Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Uniform** $(a,b)$ | $\dfrac{1}{b-a}$ | $\dfrac{x-a}{b-a}$ | $X = a + U(b-a)$ | $x \in [a,b]$ |
| 2 | **Exponential** $(\lambda)$ | $\lambda e^{-\lambda x}$ | $1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ | $X = -\dfrac{1}{\lambda} \ln(1-U)$ | $x \geq 0$ |
| 3 | **Weibull** $(\lambda, k)$ | $k\lambda(\lambda x)^{k-1} e^{-(\lambda x)^k}$ | $1 - e^{-(\lambda x)^k}$ | $X = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} \left[-\ln(1-U)\right]^{1/k}$ | $x \geq 0$ |
| 4 | **Triangular** $(a, b, c)$ | $\dfrac{2(x-a)}{(b-a)(c-a)}$ for $x \in [a,c]$ | $\dfrac{(x-a)^2}{(b-a)(c-a)}$ | $X = a + \sqrt{U(b-a)(c-a)}$ | $x \in [a,c]$ |
| 5 | **Discrete** $P(X=x_i)=p_i$ | — | $F(x_i) = \sum_{j \leq i} p_j$ | $X = x_i$ where $F(x_{i-1}) < U \leq F(x_i)$ | $x \in \{x_1, x_2, \dots\}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Parameter Notes:**
> - $\lambda$ = rate/scale parameter (always $\lambda > 0$).
> - $k$ = shape parameter (Weibull, $k > 0$).
> - Triangular: $a$ = min, $b$ = max, $c$ = mode with $a \leq c \leq b$.
> - Discrete: $F(x_i)$ is a *step function* — hence we use a **search** rather than a closed-form inversion.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Exponential**: Inter-arrival times of customers in a queue, radioactive decay, reliability of electronic components (MTBF).
- **Uniform**: Baseline RNG itself, Monte Carlo integration, randomized experimental design.
- **Weibull**: Failure analysis of mechanical components, wind speed modeling, hydrology.
- **Triangular**: PERT (Project Evaluation Review Technique) estimation when expert opinion is the only data source.
- **Discrete**: Rolling a die, demand for a finite number of items, queuing service types.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation for Uniform Distribution $(a, b)$

**Given:** $F(x) = \dfrac{x-a}{b-a}$.

**Step 1:** Set $F(x) = U$.

$$U = \frac{x-a}{b-a}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $(b-a)$.

$$U(b-a) = x - a$$

**Step 3:** Add $a$ to isolate $x$.

$$\boxed{X = a + U(b-a)}$$

This is the simplest case — the inverse is a **linear rescaling** of the uniform random number.

---

## 3.2 Derivation for Exponential Distribution $(\lambda)$

**Given:** $F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$ for $x \geq 0$.

**Step 1:** Set $F(x) = U$.

$$U = 1 - e^{-\lambda x}$$

**Step 2:** Subtract 1 from both sides and multiply by $-1$.

$$1 - U = e^{-\lambda x}$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$\ln(1 - U) = -\lambda x$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $-\lambda$.

$$\boxed{X = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln(1 - U)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimization Tip:** Since $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$, $1 - U$ is also $\sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$. Hence we can use the equivalent form $X = -\dfrac{1}{\lambda} \ln(U)$ to save one subtraction per sample.

---

## 3.3 Derivation for Weibull Distribution $(\lambda, k)$

**Given:** $F(x) = 1 - e^{-(\lambda x)^k}$ for $x \geq 0$.

**Step 1:** Set $F(x) = U$.

$$U = 1 - e^{-(\lambda x)^k}$$

**Step 2:** Isolate the exponential term.

$$1 - U = e^{-(\lambda x)^k}$$

**Step 3:** Apply natural logarithm.

$$\ln(1 - U) = -(\lambda x)^k$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $-1$ and take the $k$-th root.

$$[-\ln(1 - U)]^{1/k} = \lambda x$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $x$.

$$\boxed{X = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[-\ln(1 - U)\right]^{1/k}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $k = 1$, this collapses exactly to the **Exponential** formula, confirming that Exponential is a special case of Weibull.

---

## 3.4 Derivation for Triangular Distribution $(a, b, c)$ — Left Half $[a, c]$

**Given:** CDF on the left segment is $F(x) = \dfrac{(x-a)^2}{(b-a)(c-a)}$.

**Step 1:** Set $F(x) = U$.

$$U = \frac{(x-a)^2}{(b-a)(c-a)}$$

**Step 2:** Multiply both sides by $(b-a)(c-a)$.

$$U(b-a)(c-a) = (x-a)^2$$

**Step 3:** Take the positive square root (since $x \geq a$).

$$\sqrt{U(b-a)(c-a)} = x - a$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $x$.

$$\boxed{X = a + \sqrt{U(b-a)(c-a)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This formula applies **only to the left half** of the triangle. For the right half, the CDF becomes $F(x) = 1 - \dfrac{(b-x)^2}{(b-a)(b-c)}$, leading to $X = b - \sqrt{(1-U)(b-a)(b-c)}$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation for Discrete Distribution

**Given:** $P(X = x_i) = p_i$ for $i = 1, 2, \dots, n$.

**Step 1:** Construct the **step CDF**.

$$F(x_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{i} p_j, \quad F(x_0) = 0$$

**Step 2:** Generate $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$.

**Step 3:** **Linear Search Algorithm** — find the smallest index $i$ such that $U \leq F(x_i)$.

$$X = x_i \quad \text{where} \quad F(x_{i-1}) < U \leq F(x_i)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For large $n$, linear search is $O(n)$. Use **binary search** or the **alias method** for speed.

**Worked Example — Fair Die:**
$P(X = k) = 1/6$ for $k \in \{1,2,3,4,5,6\}$.
Cumulative: $F(1)=1/6,\ F(2)=2/6,\ \dots,\ F(6)=1$.
If $U = 0.42$, since $2/6 = 0.333 < 0.42 \leq 3/6 = 0.50$, we get $X = 3$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Methodology Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: User specifies target distribution F x"] --> B["Derive closed form inverse F-inverse u"]
    B --> C["Initialize: Generate U from Uniform 0,1 RNG"]
    C --> D["Decision: Is F invertible in closed form?"]
    D -- Yes Continuous --> E["Compute X = F-inverse U directly"]
    D -- No Discrete --> F["Build step CDF F xi = sum pj"]
    F --> G["Linear Search: Find smallest i with U ≤ F xi"]
    G --> H["Set X = xi"]
    E --> I["Output X as one random sample"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Repeat for N samples to populate empirical histogram"]
    J --> K["End: Validate against theoretical PDF using KS test"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style I fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#009933
    style K fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000
```

## 4.2 Distribution Family Tree (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    ROOT["Inverse Transformation Method - ITM"] --> UC["Universal Step 1: Generate U Uniform 0,1"]
    UC --> INV["Universal Step 2: Compute X = F-inverse U"]
    INV --> FAM["Distribution Families"]
    FAM --> UF["Uniform a b: Linear rescale"]
    FAM --> EF["Exponential lambda: Logarithmic inverse"]
    FAM --> WF["Weibull lambda k: Power-of-log inverse"]
    FAM --> TF["Triangular a b c: Square root of product"]
    FAM --> DF["Discrete: Step function table lookup"]
    EF -.Special case k=1.-> WF
    TF --> TL["Left half: a + sqrt U b-a c-a"]
    TF --> TR["Right half: b - sqrt 1-U b-a b-c"]

    style ROOT fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900
    style UC fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style INV fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

## 4.3 Computational Pipeline Summary Table

| Stage | Input | Operation | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Setup | Target PDF $f(x)$ | Integrate to get $F(x)$; invert | $F^{-1}(u)$ formula |
| 2. Sampling | Seed value | RNG call | $U \in (0,1)$ |
| 3. Transform | $U$ | Substitute into $F^{-1}$ | Sample $X$ |
| 4. Loop | Loop counter | Repeat stages 2–3 $N$ times | Sample vector $\mathbf{X}$ |
| 5. Validate | $\mathbf{X}$ | KS test / chi-square | Acceptance / rejection |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Q: State the Inverse Transformation Theorem for generating random variates. List the conditions under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**
The Inverse Transformation Theorem states that if $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$ and $F$ is a continuous, strictly monotonic CDF, then the random variable $X = F^{-1}(U)$ has the distribution $F$.

**Conditions:**
1. The CDF $F(x)$ must be **known in closed form**.
2. $F(x)$ must be **continuous and strictly increasing** on its support.
3. The inverse $F^{-1}(u)$ must be **computable analytically**.

> [!NOTE] > **Marks Breakdown (as per KTU key):** Theorem statement = 1 mark; each of 3 conditions = 0.5 marks + 0.5 marks; neatness = 0.5 marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Using the Inverse Transformation Method, derive the formula for generating an exponential random variate with mean $\beta$.**

**Model Answer:**
For an exponential distribution with mean $\beta = 1/\lambda$, the CDF is $F(x) = 1 - e^{-x/\beta}$.

Setting $F(x) = U$ and solving:

$$\boxed{X = -\beta \ln(1 - U)}$$

This is equivalent to $X = -\beta \ln(U)$ since $1-U \sim U$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

**A(a)** Using the Inverse Transformation Method, derive the expression for generating random variates from a Weibull distribution with parameters $\lambda$ and $k$. Discuss how the exponential and Rayleigh distributions emerge as special cases. **(7 Marks)**

**A(b)** Generate three random variates from a Weibull distribution with $\lambda = 0.5$ and $k = 2$, given that the uniform random numbers are $U_1 = 0.25$, $U_2 = 0.60$, and $U_3 = 0.95$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1: CDF of Weibull.**
$$F(x) = 1 - e^{-(\lambda x)^k}, \quad x \geq 0$$

**Step 2: Set $F(x) = U$ and isolate the exponential.**
$$U = 1 - e^{-(\lambda x)^k} \implies 1 - U = e^{-(\lambda x)^k}$$

**Step 3: Apply $\ln$ and take $k$-th root.**
$$\ln(1-U) = -(\lambda x)^k \implies [-\ln(1-U)]^{1/k} = \lambda x$$

**Step 4: Solve for $x$.**
$$\boxed{X = \frac{1}{\lambda} \left[-\ln(1-U)\right]^{1/k}}$$

**Special Case 1 — Exponential ($k=1$):**
$$X = \frac{1}{\lambda} [-\ln(1-U)] \implies X = -\frac{1}{\lambda} \ln(U) \quad \text{(Exponential form)}$$

**Special Case 2 — Rayleigh ($k=2$):**
$$X = \frac{1}{\lambda} \sqrt{-\ln(1-U)}$$

---

### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.5,\ k = 2,\ U \in \{0.25,\ 0.60,\ 0.95\}$.

**Formula:** $X = \dfrac{1}{0.5} \sqrt{-\ln(1 - U)} = 2 \sqrt{-\ln(1 - U)}$.

**Sample 1 ($U_1 = 0.25$):**

$$X_1 = 2 \sqrt{-\ln(0.75)} = 2 \sqrt{0.2877} = 2 \times 0.5364 = 1.0727$$

**Sample 2 ($U_2 = 0.60$):**

$$X_2 = 2 \sqrt{-\ln(0.40)} = 2 \sqrt{0.9163} = 2 \times 0.9572 = 1.9145$$

**Sample 3 ($U_3 = 0.95$):**

$$X_3 = 2 \sqrt{-\ln(0.05)} = 2 \sqrt{2.9957} = 2 \times 1.7308 = 3.4617$$

| $i$ | $U_i$ | $1 - U_i$ | $-\ln(1-U_i)$ | $\sqrt{\cdot}$ | $X_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.25 | 0.75 | 0.2877 | 0.5364 | **1.0727** |
| 2 | 0.60 | 0.40 | 0.9163 | 0.9572 | **1.9145** |
| 3 | 0.95 | 0.05 | 2.9957 | 1.7308 | **3.4617** |

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**B(a)** Explain the Inverse Transformation Method for generating random variates from a triangular distribution defined over $[a, b]$ with mode at $c$. Derive the expressions for both the left and right segments. **(7 Marks)**

**B(b)** A project has optimistic time $a = 4$ days, pessimistic time $b = 12$ days, and most likely time $c = 6$ days. Using ITM, generate three triangularly distributed random variates given $U_1 = 0.30$, $U_2 = 0.70$, $U_3 = 0.50$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B(a)

A triangular distribution has support $[a, b]$ with mode $c$ where $a \leq c \leq b$. Its CDF is **piecewise**:

**Left Segment ($a \leq x \leq c$):**

$$F(x) = \frac{(x-a)^2}{(b-a)(c-a)}$$

**Right Segment ($c \leq x \leq b$):**

$$F(x) = 1 - \frac{(b-x)^2}{(b-a)(b-c)}$$

**Derivation — Left Segment:**

Set $F(x) = U$:

$$U = \frac{(x-a)^2}{(b-a)(c-a)} \implies U(b-a)(c-a) = (x-a)^2$$

Taking positive root:

$$\boxed{X = a + \sqrt{U(b-a)(c-a)}}$$

**Derivation — Right Segment:**

$$U = 1 - \frac{(b-x)^2}{(b-a)(b-c)} \implies 1 - U = \frac{(b-x)^2}{(b-a)(b-c)}$$

$$(b-x) = \sqrt{(1-U)(b-a)(b-c)}$$

$$\boxed{X = b - \sqrt{(1-U)(b-a)(b-c)}}$$

---

### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Given:** $a = 4,\ b = 12,\ c = 6$.

**Mode check:** Since $c = 6$ is closer to $a = 4$ than to $b = 12$, the distribution is **left-skewed**.

**Sample 1 ($U_1 = 0.30$):**
$$X_1 = 4 + \sqrt{0.30 \times (12-4)(6-4)} = 4 + \sqrt{0.30 \times 8 \times 2} = 4 + \sqrt{4.8} = 4 + 2.1909 = 6.1909$$

**Sample 2 ($U_2 = 0.70$):** This is in the right segment.
$$X_2 = 12 - \sqrt{(1 - 0.70)(12-4)(12-6)} = 12 - \sqrt{0.30 \times 8 \times 6} = 12 - \sqrt{14.4} = 12 - 3.7947 = 8.2053$$

**Sample 3 ($U_3 = 0.50$):**
$$X_3 = 4 + \sqrt{0.50 \times 8 \times 2} = 4 + \sqrt{8} = 4 + 2.8284 = 6.8284$$

| $i$ | $U_i$ | Segment | $X_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.30 | Left ($a \leq x \leq c$) | **6.1909** |
| 2 | 0.70 | Right ($c \leq x \leq b$) | **8.2053** |
| 3 | 0.50 | Left | **6.8284** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark-Loss Hotspots:**
> 1. **Missing the segment check for triangular**: Students blindly apply the left formula to all $U$ values — **lose 2 marks** if $X > c$ appears.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply $\ln$ to both sides** in exponential/Weibull derivations — **lose 1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing $1-U$ with $U$** in exponential — both are valid, but mixing them up *within the same answer* is graded wrong.
> 4. **Discrete ITM**: Writing $X = x_i$ when $U \leq F(x_i)$ is **inclusive** — using strict inequality on both sides loses the boundary value's probability mass.
> 5. **Numerical errors** in $\ln(1-U)$ — KTU allows $\pm 0.01$ tolerance, but using $\ln(U)$ when problem specifies $\ln(1-U)$ is **not** an error if explicitly stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **ITM Core Theorem**: $X = F^{-1}(U)$ where $U \sim U(0,1)$ — requires closed-form, continuous, invertible $F$.
- ✅ **Uniform**: $X = a + U(b-a)$ — linear rescaling.
- ✅ **Exponential**: $X = -\dfrac{1}{\lambda} \ln(U)$ — uses $1-U \equiv U$ simplification.
- ✅ **Weibull**: $X = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} [-\ln(U)]^{1/k}$ — Exponential is special case $k=1$.
- ✅ **Triangular**: Piecewise formula; **left** uses $\sqrt{U(b-a)(c-a)}$; **right** uses $\sqrt{(1-U)(b-a)(b-c)}$.
- ✅ **Discrete**: Build CDF table, perform linear search to find $x_i$ with $F(x_{i-1}) < U \leq F(x_i)$.
- ✅ **Algorithm Steps**: Generate $U$ → Substitute into $F^{-1}$ → Output $X$ → Repeat $N$ times → Validate via KS test.
- ✅ **Real-World Links**: Exponential → queueing/MTBF; Weibull → reliability/wind; Triangular → PERT; Discrete → inventory demand.
- ✅ **CO2 Alignment**: ITM is the *foundational* RV generation method — every other technique (acceptance-rejection, composition) builds on this concept.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Transformation Method for Normal and Lognormal Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Transformation Method for Normal and Lognormal Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Direct Transformation Method** (also called the **Inverse Transform Method** in its general form, but specifically a *direct* algebraic method for the Normal and Lognormal families) refers to a class of *closed-form, non-iterative* random variate generation techniques that transform independent Uniform(0,1) deviates — denoted $U_1, U_2, \ldots$ — directly into samples drawn from the **Normal (Gaussian) distribution** $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and the **Lognormal distribution** $\text{Lognormal}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The two canonical direct-transformation generators expected at the board examination level are:
> 1. The **Box–Muller Transform** (1958) for the Normal distribution.
> 2. The **Exponential Mapping** of a Normal variate, $X = e^{Y}$ where $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, for the Lognormal distribution.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water filter funnel**. You pour *uniform* water in at the top (every drop has equal probability), and the funnel's *shape* reshapes the flow so that drops emerge according to a completely different statistical pattern (e.g., bell-shaped).

- The **Uniform(0,1)** input is the *raw material*.
- The **Direct Transformation** is the *funnel's geometry*.
- The **Normal/Lognormal** sample is the *reshaped output*.

The elegance of direct transformation is that the funnel is a *single algebraic equation* — no trial-and-error, no table lookups, no rejection of samples.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Direct"?** The method is called *direct* because we obtain each variate from $U_i$ in a *deterministic algebraic expression* (a single closed-form formula), as opposed to indirect methods like the *Rejection-Acceptance* technique (Module 2 / 3) or *Convolution* techniques (Module 3), which require iterative logic.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- The standard Normal variate $Z$ has parameters $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma^2 = 1$ (i.e., $\sigma = 1$).
- The **Lognormal** is *parameterized* by the **mean $\mu$** and **variance $\sigma^2$** of the underlying Normal — **not** the mean/variance of $X$ itself.
- The constant $\mathbf{2\pi} \approx \mathbf{6.28318}$ appears inside the Box–Muller trigonometric component.
- The natural logarithm base $e \approx 2.71828$ is used throughout.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why the Normal Distribution is *Hard* for Pure Inverse Transform

For a target distribution with CDF $F_X(x)$, the pure inverse transform requires solving $x = F_X^{-1}(U)$. For the Normal distribution, the CDF is:

$$F_Z(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-t^2 / 2} \, dt$$

This integral has **no closed-form antiderivative in terms of elementary functions** — the famous *error function* $\text{erf}(\cdot)$ is itself defined by this very integral. This is the precise mathematical reason why we cannot write a single algebraic inverse like $Z = F_Z^{-1}(U) = \text{erf}^{-1}(2U-1)$ in a way that avoids numerical iteration. The Box–Muller transform **bypasses** this obstruction by exploiting a *bivariate* symmetry trick.

### 2.2 The Core Insight Behind Box–Muller

Consider two *independent* standard Normal variates $Z_1$ and $Z_2$. Their joint density in polar coordinates $(R, \Theta)$ *factorizes*:

$$f_{Z_1, Z_2}(z_1, z_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi} e^{-(z_1^2 + z_2^2)/2}$$

The radius squared $R^2 = Z_1^2 + Z_2^2$ becomes an *exponential* random variable (chi-squared with 2 d.f.), and the angle $\Theta$ is *uniform* on $[0, 2\pi]$. This separable structure allows us to invert the marginals in closed form — and that is the entire engine of the algorithm.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of Box–Muller

- **Input:** Draw $U_1, U_2 \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$, independent.
- **Transform to radius squared:** $R^2 = -2 \ln U_1$. This is Exponential(1/2) and equals the chi-squared value.
- **Transform to angle:** $\Theta = 2\pi U_2 \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 2\pi)$.
- **Polar-to-Cartesian conversion:** Generate $Z_1 = R \cos \Theta$ and $Z_2 = R \sin \Theta$.
- **Result:** $Z_1, Z_2$ are **independent** $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ variates — two for the price of two uniforms.
- **Generalize:** To obtain $X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, apply the affine shift-scale $X = \mu + \sigma Z$.

### 2.4 Lognormal via Exponential Mapping

The Lognormal distribution is defined as the distribution of $X = e^{Y}$ where $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. The *exponential map* $X = e^{Y}$ is the direct transformation.

- **Generate** $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ via Box–Muller.
- **Apply** $X = e^Y$.
- **Output:** $X \sim \text{Lognormal}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Domain / Units |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Standard Normal PDF | $f_Z(z) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-z^2/2}$ | $z \in (-\infty, \infty)$ |
| 2 | Standard Normal CDF | $F_Z(z) = \Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} f_Z(t) \, dt$ | No closed form (uses $\text{erf}$) |
| 3 | Box–Muller #1 (Polar form) | $Z_1 = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1}\cos(2\pi U_2)$ | $U_1, U_2 \sim U(0,1)$ |
| 4 | Box–Muller #1 (Polar form) | $Z_2 = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1}\sin(2\pi U_2)$ | $U_1, U_2 \sim U(0,1)$ |
| 5 | Affine scaling to $\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ | $X = \mu + \sigma Z$ | $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(0,1)$ |
| 6 | Lognormal generation | $X = e^Y$, where $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)$ | $X \in (0, \infty)$ |
| 7 | Lognormal mean | $E[X] = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$ | Strictly $> 0$ |
| 8 | Lognormal variance | $\text{Var}(X) = (e^{\sigma^2}-1)e^{2\mu+\sigma^2}$ | Always $\geq 0$ |
| 9 | Lognormal PDF | $f_X(x) = \frac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)$ | $x > 0$ |
| 10 | Skewness of Lognormal | $\gamma_1 = (e^{\sigma^2}+2)\sqrt{e^{\sigma^2}-1}$ | Right-skewed |
| 11 | Log-moments inversion | $\sigma^2 = \ln\!\left(1 + \frac{\text{Var}(X)}{[E(X)]^2}\right)$ | $\mu, \sigma$ from $E(X), \text{Var}(X)$ |
| 12 | Log-moments inversion | $\mu = \ln(E[X]) - \sigma^2/2$ | $\mu, \sigma$ from $E(X), \text{Var}(X)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> The most commonly tested edge-case in KTU papers: students confuse the **parameters** $\mu, \sigma$ of the Lognormal (which describe the *underlying* Normal) with the **mean and variance of $X$**. The mean of $X$ is $e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$, *not* $e^{\mu}$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Lognormal is the canonical model for **shadow fading** in wireless channels (log-distance path loss).
- **Reliability Engineering:** Time-to-failure of components with multiplicative degradation (e.g., corrosion, fatigue crack growth) is often Lognormal.
- **Finance:** Stock prices (Black–Scholes assumes Lognormal returns).
- **Manufacturing:** Dimensional tolerances, particle-size distributions.
- **Queuing & Process Simulation:** Service times that are *positive* and *right-skewed*.
- **Monte Carlo Simulation:** Any physics-based Monte Carlo needing Gaussian noise (e.g., finite-element perturbation, thermal noise in circuits) uses Box–Muller.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Derivation of the Box–Muller Transform

We begin with the *bivariate* standard Normal density over $(z_1, z_2)$:

$$f_{Z_1, Z_2}(z_1, z_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{z_1^2 + z_2^2}{2}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Apply the polar-coordinate transformation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } z_1 &= R \cos \Theta, \quad z_2 = R \sin \Theta \\
\text{with } R &\geq 0, \quad \Theta \in [0, 2\pi)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Jacobian of the transformation.**

The Jacobian determinant of the forward transform $(R, \Theta) \mapsto (z_1, z_2)$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J &= \begin{vmatrix} \dfrac{\partial z_1}{\partial R} & \dfrac{\partial z_1}{\partial \Theta} \\[6pt] \dfrac{\partial z_2}{\partial R} & \dfrac{\partial z_2}{\partial \Theta} \end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} \cos\Theta & -R\sin\Theta \\ \sin\Theta & R\cos\Theta \end{vmatrix} \\
&= R\cos^2\Theta + R\sin^2\Theta = R
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify the joint density in $(R, \Theta)$ space.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{R, \Theta}(r, \theta) &= f_{Z_1, Z_2}(r\cos\theta, r\sin\theta) \cdot \vert J \vert \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^2\cos^2\theta + r^2\sin^2\theta}{2}\right) \cdot r \\
&= \frac{1}{2\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{r^2}{2}\right) \cdot r
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Observe the factorization (the crucial structural insight).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{R, \Theta}(r, \theta) &= \underbrace{\left[r \, e^{-r^2/2}\right]}_{f_R(r)} \cdot \underbrace{\left[\frac{1}{2\pi}\right]}_{f_\Theta(\theta)} \\
&= f_R(r) \cdot f_\Theta(\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

This means **$R$ and $\Theta$ are statistically independent.** The polar representation *decouples* the joint distribution into two simple 1-D distributions.

**Step 5 — Identify the marginal distributions.**

- $\Theta \sim \text{Uniform}(0, 2\pi)$, with CDF $F_\Theta(\theta) = \frac{\theta}{2\pi}$. Inverting:

$$\theta = 2\pi U_2, \quad U_2 \sim U(0,1)$$

- $R$ has PDF $f_R(r) = r e^{-r^2/2}$ for $r \geq 0$. This is the Rayleigh distribution. Its CDF is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_R(r) &= \int_0^r t e^{-t^2/2} \, dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the CDF of $R$ by substitution.**

Let $u = t^2/2$, so $du = t \, dt$. Then $t=0 \Rightarrow u=0$ and $t=r \Rightarrow u=r^2/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_R(r) &= \int_0^{r^2/2} e^{-u} \, du = \left[-e^{-u}\right]_0^{r^2/2} \\
&= 1 - e^{-r^2/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Invert the CDF of $R$ to obtain $R$ as a function of $U_1$.**

Set $U_1 = 1 - e^{-r^2/2}$, so $1 - U_1 = e^{-r^2/2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(1 - U_1) &= -\frac{r^2}{2} \\
r^2 &= -2 \ln(1 - U_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1 - U_1$ is Uniform(0,1) whenever $U_1$ is, we may equivalently write $r^2 = -2\ln U_1$. Hence:

$$R = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1}$$

**Step 8 — Reconstruct $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ via the polar-to-Cartesian inversion.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= R \cos\Theta = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1} \cdot \cos(2\pi U_2) \\
Z_2 &= R \sin\Theta = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1} \cdot \sin(2\pi U_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Box–Muller transform** in its polar form. $\blacksquare$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** The derivation of $f_R(r) = r e^{-r^2/2}$ and the substitution step are the most-likely-asked algebraic chain. Students who skip the Jacobian step typically lose 2 marks.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Mean and Variance of the Lognormal Distribution

Let $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ and $X = e^Y$. We use the **moment generating function of $Y$**: $E[e^{tY}] = e^{\mu t + \sigma^2 t^2/2}$.

**Mean of $X$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X] &= E[e^Y] = E[e^{1 \cdot Y}] \\
&= e^{\mu \cdot 1 + \sigma^2 \cdot 1^2 / 2} = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Second moment of $X$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X^2] &= E[e^{2Y}] = e^{\mu \cdot 2 + \sigma^2 \cdot 4 / 2} = e^{2\mu + 2\sigma^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Variance of $X$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X) &= E[X^2] - [E[X]]^2 \\
&= e^{2\mu + 2\sigma^2} - e^{2\mu + \sigma^2} \\
&= e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}\left(e^{\sigma^2} - 1\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Lognormal PDF

The CDF of $X = e^Y$ where $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ is, for $x > 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_X(x) &= P(X \leq x) = P(e^Y \leq x) = P(Y \leq \ln x) \\
&= \Phi\!\left(\frac{\ln x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

Differentiating using the chain rule and the Normal PDF $\phi(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-t^2/2}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_X(x) &= \frac{d}{dx} F_X(x) = \phi\!\left(\frac{\ln x - \mu}{\sigma}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{x\sigma} \\
&= \frac{1}{x\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{(\ln x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

$\blacksquare$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Box–Muller)

**Problem:** Given $U_1 = 0.40$ and $U_2 = 0.75$, generate a sample from $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$. Then scale it to $\mathcal{N}(10, 4)$ (i.e., $\mu = 10, \sigma = 2$).

**Step 1 — Compute $R$:**

$$
R = \sqrt{-2 \ln(0.40)} = \sqrt{-2 \times (-0.9163)} = \sqrt{1.8326} \approx 1.3537
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the angle:**

$$\Theta = 2\pi \times 0.75 = 1.5\pi = 4.7124 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $Z_1$ and $Z_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= 1.3537 \times \cos(4.7124) = 1.3537 \times (0) = 0 \\
Z_2 &= 1.3537 \times \sin(4.7124) = 1.3537 \times (-1) = -1.3537
\end{aligned}
$$

(Since $0.75$ gives $\Theta = 3\pi/2$, the cosine and sine are exactly $0$ and $-1$.)

**Step 4 — Pick $Z_2 = -1.3537$ and scale to $\mathcal{N}(10, 4)$:**

$$X = 10 + 2 \times (-1.3537) = 10 - 2.7074 = 7.2926$$

**Verification:** A value of $-1.35$ is about $1.35$ standard deviations below the mean, so a result of $7.29$ for $\mathcal{N}(10, 4)$ is consistent with intuition.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Lognormal)

**Problem:** Generate a Lognormal sample using $Z \sim \mathcal{N}(2, 0.25)$ (so $\mu = 2$, $\sigma = 0.5$).

Assume from a prior Box–Muller call we have $Z = -0.4$ (a standard Normal deviate). Then $Y = \mu + \sigma Z = 2 + 0.5 \times (-0.4) = 1.8$. The Lognormal sample is:

$$X = e^Y = e^{1.8} \approx 6.0496$$

**Sanity check using moment formula:**

$$E[X] = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2} = e^{2 + 0.125} = e^{2.125} \approx 8.372$$

Our single sample $6.05$ is *below* the mean of $8.37$ — consistent with $Y = 1.8$ being below $\mu = 2$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow of the Direct Transformation Method

The following block diagram shows the operational pipeline from uniform random numbers to Normal/Lognormal variates.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start] --> B[Generate U1, U2 from Uniform 0,1]
    B --> C[Compute R squared equals -2 ln U1]
    B --> D[Compute Theta equals 2 pi U2]
    C --> E[Compute R equals sqrt of R squared]
    E --> F[Z1 equals R cos Theta]
    E --> G[Z2 equals R sin Theta]
    F --> H{Choose target distribution}
    G --> H
    H -->|Normal mean mu variance sigma square| I[Apply X equals mu plus sigma Z]
    H -->|Lognormal| J[Apply Y equals mu plus sigma Z]
    I --> K[Output X Normal sample]
    J --> L[Apply X equals exp Y]
    L --> M[Output X Lognormal sample]
    K --> N[End]
    M --> N[End]
```

### 4.2 Mapping from Joint Normal to Polar Coordinates

The following diagram visualizes the *geometric* core of the Box–Muller derivation: how the joint Normal density's radial and angular components decouple.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Joint density fZ1Z2] --> P2[Bivariate Standard Normal over plane]
    P2 --> P3[Polar substitution z1 equals R cos Theta, z2 equals R sin Theta]
    P3 --> P4[Jacobian equals R]
    P4 --> P5[Factorization into two independent marginals]
    P5 --> P6[Radius R follows Rayleigh PDF r exp of minus r squared over 2]
    P5 --> P7[Angle Theta follows Uniform 0, 2 pi]
    P6 --> P8[Invert CDF: R equals sqrt of -2 ln U1]
    P7 --> P9[Invert CDF: Theta equals 2 pi U2]
    P8 --> P10[Reconstruct Z1 and Z2]
    P9 --> P10
```

### 4.3 Lognormal Generation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Start] --> Q2[Generate Z from N0,1 using Box Muller]
    Q2 --> Q3[Scale and shift to get Y from N mu, sigma square]
    Q3 --> Q4[Compute X equals exp Y]
    Q4 --> Q5[Output X from Lognormal mu, sigma square]
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Choosing the Correct Direct Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Target distribution] --> S2{Is target Normal?}
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Use Box Muller: two normals from two uniforms]
    S2 -->|No| S4{Is target Lognormal?}
    S4 -->|Yes| S5[Use Box Muller to get Y, then X equals exp Y]
    S4 -->|No| S6[Consider rejection-acceptance or convolution method]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

State the **Box–Muller transformation** for generating two independent standard Normal random variates from two independent Uniform(0,1) random variates $U_1$ and $U_2$. Mention the role of the polar-coordinate substitution.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Box–Muller transform generates two independent standard Normal variates $Z_1, Z_2$ from two independent uniforms $U_1, U_2$ as:

$$Z_1 = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1}\cos(2\pi U_2), \quad Z_2 = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1}\sin(2\pi U_2)$$

The polar-coordinate substitution $z_1 = R\cos\Theta$, $z_2 = R\sin\Theta$ factorizes the joint Normal density into a Rayleigh radius $R$ (with CDF $1 - e^{-r^2/2}$) and a uniform angle $\Theta$ over $[0, 2\pi]$, both of which can be inverted in closed form using $U_1, U_2$. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

Define the **Lognormal distribution** and state its mean and variance in terms of the parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$ of the underlying Normal variate.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A random variable $X$ follows the **Lognormal$(\mu, \sigma^2)$ distribution** if $\ln X \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$. Equivalently, if $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $X = e^Y$ is Lognormal. Its mean and variance are:

$$E[X] = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}, \quad \text{Var}(X) = \left(e^{\sigma^2} - 1\right) e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}$$

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the **Box–Muller transformation** step-by-step, starting from the joint standard Normal density and ending with the explicit formulas for $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ in terms of $U_1$ and $U_2$. State clearly the role of the Jacobian. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing system simulates the lifetime (in hours) of a critical bearing. The lifetime is modeled as Lognormal with mean $E[X] = 500$ hours and standard deviation $150$ hours. **(7 marks)**

- (i) Compute the parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma$ of the underlying Normal distribution.
- (ii) Using a Box–Muller generated standard Normal deviate $Z = 1.645$, compute the simulated bearing lifetime.
- (iii) Comment on the practical interpretation of the result.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. Start with the bivariate standard Normal density (1 mark):

$$f(z_1, z_2) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \exp\!\left(-\frac{z_1^2 + z_2^2}{2}\right)$$

2. Apply polar substitution $z_1 = R\cos\Theta$, $z_2 = R\sin\Theta$ (1 mark).
3. Compute the Jacobian $J = R$ (1 mark):

$$|J| = \begin{vmatrix} \cos\Theta & -R\sin\Theta \\ \sin\Theta & R\cos\Theta \end{vmatrix} = R(\cos^2\Theta + \sin^2\Theta) = R$$

4. Substitute to get the joint density in $(R, \Theta)$ (1 mark):

$$f_{R, \Theta}(r, \theta) = \frac{r}{2\pi} e^{-r^2/2} = \underbrace{\left(r e^{-r^2/2}\right)}_{f_R(r)} \cdot \underbrace{\left(\frac{1}{2\pi}\right)}_{f_\Theta(\theta)}$$

5. Compute $F_R(r) = 1 - e^{-r^2/2}$ by integration; invert to get $R = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1}$ (1 mark).
6. Identify $\Theta \sim U(0, 2\pi)$ and invert to get $\Theta = 2\pi U_2$ (1 mark).
7. Reconstruct: $Z_1 = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1}\cos(2\pi U_2)$, $Z_2 = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1}\sin(2\pi U_2)$ (1 mark).

**(b) Lognormal Application (7 marks):**

**(i)** Given $E[X] = 500$ and $\text{SD}(X) = 150$, so $\text{Var}(X) = 22500$ (1 mark).

The CV (coefficient of variation) is $150/500 = 0.3$, so $\text{Var}(X)/[E(X)]^2 = 0.09$.

From the inversion formulas:

$$
\sigma^2 = \ln(1 + 0.09) = \ln(1.09) \approx 0.0862, \quad \sigma \approx 0.2936
$$

$$\mu = \ln(500) - 0.0862/2 = 6.2146 - 0.0431 = 6.1715$$

So $\mu \approx 6.1715$ and $\sigma \approx 0.2936$ (2 marks).

**(ii)** With $Z = 1.645$ (1 mark):

$$Y = \mu + \sigma Z = 6.1715 + 0.2936 \times 1.645 = 6.1715 + 0.4830 = 6.6545$$

$$X = e^Y = e^{6.6545} \approx 775.4 \text{ hours}$$

So the simulated bearing lifetime is approximately **775.4 hours** (2 marks).

**(iii)** Interpretation: A lifetime of 775.4 hours is greater than the mean of 500 hours. This is consistent with $Z = +1.645$, which corresponds to the upper 95th percentile of the standard Normal. Practically, this means roughly 5% of bearings are expected to last at least this long or longer under the assumed Lognormal model (2 marks).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain, with mathematical justification, **why the inverse-transform method cannot be directly applied** to the standard Normal distribution. How does the **Box–Muller transform** resolve this difficulty? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Generate a single standard Normal variate using the Box–Muller polar form given $U_1 = 0.1353$ and $U_2 = 0.8642$. Then transform this to obtain a sample from $\mathcal{N}(50, 25)$ (i.e., $\mu = 50$, $\sigma = 5$). Verify the plausibility of your result by computing its standardized $z$-score. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Why Inverse Transform Fails for Normal (7 marks):**

1. The pure inverse-transform method requires $X = F_X^{-1}(U)$ where $F_X$ is the CDF (1 mark).
2. For the standard Normal, the CDF is $\Phi(z) = \int_{-\infty}^{z} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-t^2/2} dt$ (1 mark).
3. The integrand $e^{-t^2/2}$ has **no elementary antiderivative** — the antiderivative is the error function $\text{erf}(z/\sqrt{2}) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{\pi}} \int_0^z e^{-t^2} dt$ (2 marks).
4. Although $\Phi(z) = \frac{1}{2}\left[1 + \text{erf}(z/\sqrt{2})\right]$ is a closed-form expression, the function $\text{erf}^{-1}$ does not have a closed-form algebraic inverse — it requires numerical methods (e.g., Newton–Raphson) (1 mark).
5. The Box–Muller transform bypasses this by generating *two* normals *simultaneously* through a bivariate trick: in polar coordinates, the joint Normal density factors into a Rayleigh radius and a uniform angle, both invertible in closed form (2 marks).

**(b) Numerical Generation (7 marks):**

**(i)** Compute $R^2$ and $R$ (2 marks):

$$R^2 = -2\ln(0.1353) = -2 \times (-2.0003) = 4.0006$$

$$R = \sqrt{4.0006} \approx 2.0002$$

**(ii)** Compute the angle (1 mark):

$$\Theta = 2\pi \times 0.8642 = 2\pi \times 0.8642 \approx 5.4302 \text{ rad}$$

**(iii)** Compute $Z_1$ and $Z_2$ (2 marks):

$$Z_1 = 2.0002 \times \cos(5.4302) \approx 2.0002 \times 0.6046 \approx 1.2093$$

$$Z_2 = 2.0002 \times \sin(5.4302) \approx 2.0002 \times (-0.7965) \approx -1.5930$$

(Any one of these can be used; let us pick $Z_1 = 1.2093$.)

**(iv)** Scale to $\mathcal{N}(50, 25)$ (1 mark):

$$X = 50 + 5 \times 1.2093 = 50 + 6.0465 = 56.0465$$

**(v)** Verify by computing the $z$-score (1 mark):

$$z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma} = \frac{56.0465 - 50}{5} = 1.2093$$

This matches the $Z_1$ we generated, confirming the affine transformation. A $z$-score of $+1.21$ corresponds to the upper ~89th percentile, which is plausible.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in the Direct Transformation Method:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Jacobian** $R$ in the Box–Muller derivation — this alone costs **2 marks** at the valuation desk.
> 2. **Confusing Lognormal parameters** with the Normal's mean/variance. The Lognormal *is not* parameterized by its own mean and variance directly; $\mu$ and $\sigma$ describe the *underlying* Normal of $\ln X$.
> 3. **Skipping the CDF-inversion step** of $R$ when deriving Box–Muller. Examiners specifically look for the substitution $u = t^2/2$.
> 4. **Mixing up the order** of $U_1$ and $U_2$ in Box–Muller: $U_1$ controls the *radius* (log), $U_2$ controls the *angle* (trig). Reversing them is a direct, visible error.
> 5. **Forgetting absolute positivity**: The Lognormal is supported on $(0, \infty)$; writing a sample as negative indicates an error in the exponential step.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Direct transformation** = closed-form algebraic generation of random variates, as opposed to rejection-acceptance or convolution.
- **Box–Muller** is the *only* direct closed-form Normal generator typically expected in KTU exams.
- The polar substitution $z_1 = R\cos\Theta$, $z_2 = R\sin\Theta$ is the *structural heart* of Box–Muller.
- The **Jacobian** of the polar transform is exactly $R$ — never forget it.
- The radius $R$ has Rayleigh distribution with CDF $1 - e^{-r^2/2}$, inverted via $R = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1}$.
- The angle $\Theta$ is uniform on $[0, 2\pi)$, inverted via $\Theta = 2\pi U_2$.
- Two independent $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ variates are produced from two independent $U(0,1)$ variates.
- A general $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ sample is obtained by $X = \mu + \sigma Z$.
- The **Lognormal** is generated by the *exponential map*: $X = e^Y$ where $Y \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$.
- Lognormal moments: $E[X] = e^{\mu + \sigma^2/2}$; $\text{Var}(X) = (e^{\sigma^2} - 1)e^{2\mu + \sigma^2}$.
- Lognormal is **always right-skewed** with support $(0, \infty)$.
- The Lognormal is *closed under multiplication*: if $X_1, X_2$ are independent Lognormal, so is $X_1 X_2$.
- The standard Normal $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ is a Lognormal with $\mu = -\infty$ — i.e., the Lognormal family is a *strict superset* of the Normal only on the positive reals.
- Inversion of moments: $\sigma^2 = \ln(1 + \text{Var}(X)/[E(X)]^2)$ and $\mu = \ln E[X] - \sigma^2/2$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptance-Rejection Technique for Poisson and Gamma Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Acceptance-Rejection Technique for Poisson and Gamma Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Acceptance-Rejection Technique (ART)**, also known as **Rejection Sampling**, is a general-purpose Monte Carlo method used to generate random variates from a target probability distribution $f(x)$ when direct inversion of the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF) is mathematically intractable.

Given a target density $f(x)$ and an easy-to-sample **proposal density** $g(x)$, the method requires the existence of a finite constant $c \geq 1$ such that:

$$f(x) \leq c \cdot g(x), \quad \forall x \text{ in the support}$$

The algorithm generates a proposal $Y \sim g(x)$ and an independent uniform $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$, then accepts $Y$ as a valid sample if:

$$U \leq \frac{f(Y)}{c \cdot g(Y)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Acceptance-Rejection is the most general variate generation technique. It applies to **discrete** distributions (e.g., Poisson) and **continuous** distributions (e.g., Gamma) alike. Master this technique as it appears consistently across Part A and Part B questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Dartboard Intuition

Imagine you wish to estimate the area of an irregular dark shape cut out of a rectangular dartboard. The dark shape represents the curve $f(x)$ scaled by $c$ — wait, more precisely, the **ratio** $f(x) / g(x)$ is what matters.

A simpler analogy: You have an oddly-shaped pond inside a rectangular field. To estimate the pond's area:
1. You throw darts randomly into the field (proposal distribution $g$).
2. You count only the darts that land in the pond (acceptance test).
3. The ratio of accepted darts to total throws estimates the **acceptance probability** $\eta = 1/c$.

The key insight: each *accepted* dart represents a valid sample from $f(x)$, because the test $U \leq f(Y)/(c \cdot g(Y))$ precisely compensates for the over-representation of points where $g$ is large relative to $f$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Efficiency Principle:** A well-designed proposal $g$ makes $c$ as close to **1** as possible. The expected number of trials to obtain one valid sample equals exactly **c**. For Poisson with $\lambda = 0.5$, $c \approx 1.213$, so on average we need ~1.2 trials per sample.

### 1.3 Why Not Inverse Transform?

The **inverse transform method** requires $F^{-1}(U)$ to be expressible in closed form. For distributions like:
- **Poisson** — no closed-form inverse CDF (involves incomplete gamma function)
- **Gamma with non-integer $\alpha$** — same intractability

the inverse method fails, and acceptance-rejection becomes the workhorse alternative.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step by Step

The general acceptance-rejection procedure operates through the following structured steps:

**Step 1 — Choose the proposal density $g(x)$:**
- Must share the same support as $f(x)$.
- Must be trivially samplable (e.g., Uniform, Exponential, Geometric, Normal).

**Step 2 — Determine the envelope constant $c$:**
- Compute $c = \sup_x \left[ f(x) / g(x) \right]$.
- Equivalently, $c = \max_k \left[ p_k / q_k \right]$ for discrete distributions.
- A smaller $c$ → higher efficiency.

**Step 3 — Iterative sampling loop:**
- (a) Draw $Y \sim g(x)$.
- (b) Draw $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$ independent of $Y$.
- (c) Test: if $U \leq f(Y) / [c \cdot g(Y)]$, then **accept** $X = Y$ and exit.
- (d) Else **reject** and return to (a).

**Step 4 — Output the accepted value $X$ as a sample from $f(x)$.**

### 2.2 Why It Works — The Mathematical Justification

The probability that a generated pair $(Y, U)$ survives the acceptance test and produces $X \in [x, x + dx]$ is:

$$P(X \in [x, x+dx]) = P(Y \in [x, x+dx]) \cdot P\left(U \leq \frac{f(Y)}{c \cdot g(Y)} \,\Big|\, Y\right)$$

$$= g(x)\,dx \cdot \frac{f(x)}{c \cdot g(x)} = \frac{f(x)}{c}\,dx$$

Multiplying by the constant $c$ (the expected number of trials), the marginal density of accepted $X$ becomes exactly $f(x)$. This proves the algorithm samples from the correct target distribution.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Component | Formula or Symbol | Description |
|---|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Target density (continuous) | $f(x)$ | Distribution to be sampled |
| 2 | Target pmf (discrete) | $p_k = P(X=k)$ | Discrete probabilities |
| 3 | Proposal density | $g(x)$ or $q_k$ | Easy-to-sample envelope |
| 4 | Bounding inequality | $f(x) \leq c \cdot g(x)$ | Required envelope condition |
| 5 | Envelope constant | $c = \sup_x \left[ f(x) / g(x) \right]$ | Optimal (minimum) value |
| 6 | Acceptance rule | $U \leq \dfrac{f(Y)}{c \cdot g(Y)}$ | Decision threshold |
| 7 | Acceptance probability | $\eta = 1 / c$ | Per-trial success rate |
| 8 | Expected iterations | $E[N] = c$ | Mean trials until accept |
| 9 | Poisson proposal (small $\lambda$) | $q_k = (1/2)^{k+1}$ | Geometric with $p = 1/2$ |
| 10 | Gamma helper (Cheng) | $d = \alpha - 1/3$ | Shape offset |
| 11 | Gamma helper (Cheng) | $c_g = 1/\sqrt{9d}$ | Scale factor |
| 12 | Gamma boost (Cheng) | $X = Y \cdot V^{1/\alpha}$ | For $0 < \alpha < 1$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

Acceptance-rejection underpins stochastic simulation across industries:
- **Telecommunications** — modeling Poisson call arrivals and Gamma fading channels.
- **Reliability engineering** — Gamma-distributed component lifetimes.
- **Queueing theory** — Poisson arrivals and Gamma service times (M/G/1 queues).
- **Monte Carlo finance** — option pricing under non-normal return distributions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Application to the Poisson Distribution

The Poisson pmf is $P(X=k) = e^{-\lambda} \lambda^k / k!$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$. The CDF cannot be inverted in closed form, motivating ART.

#### 3.1.1 Choosing the Proposal — Geometric Distribution

For **small $\lambda$** (typically $\lambda \lesssim 1$), choose a Geometric proposal with $p = 1/2$:

$$q_k = (1-p)^k \cdot p = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{k+1}, \quad k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

This geometric pmf is trivially sampled via $K = \lfloor -\log_2(U) \rfloor$ or by direct lookup.

#### 3.1.2 Deriving the Envelope Constant $c$

The ratio of target to proposal is:

$$R(k) \;=\; \frac{P(X=k)}{q_k} \;=\; \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^k / k!}{(1/2)^{k+1}} \;=\; 2 e^{-\lambda} \cdot \frac{(2\lambda)^k}{k!}$$

To find the maximum, examine the ratio $R(k+1) / R(k)$:

$$\frac{R(k+1)}{R(k)} \;=\; \frac{(2\lambda)^{k+1}/(k+1)!}{(2\lambda)^k/k!} \;=\; \frac{2\lambda}{k+1}$$

Setting this ratio equal to 1 yields the maximiser:

$$k^* = 2\lambda - 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad k^* = \lfloor 2\lambda \rfloor \text{ (or } \lceil 2\lambda \rceil \text{)}$$

Therefore the optimal envelope constant is:

$$c \;=\; R(k^*) \;=\; 2 e^{-\lambda} \cdot \frac{(2\lambda)^{k^*}}{k^*!}$$

#### 3.1.3 Worked Example — Poisson($\lambda = 0.5$)

**Setup:**

$$\begin{aligned}
c &= 2 e^{-0.5} \cdot \frac{(2 \cdot 0.5)^{\lfloor 1 \rfloor}}{\lfloor 1 \rfloor!} \\
&= 2 e^{-0.5} \cdot \frac{1^1}{1!} \\
&= 2 \cdot 0.6065 \\
&\approx 1.2131
\end{aligned}$$

**Algorithm:**

$$\boxed{\text{Repeat: draw } Y \sim \text{Geometric}(1/2),\; U \sim U(0,1),\; \text{accept } X=Y \text{ if } U \leq \frac{P(Y)}{1.2131 \cdot q_Y}}$$

**Numerical Trace** (given three pre-sampled geometric $Y$ values and three uniform $U$ values):

| Trial | $Y$ | $q_Y$ | $P(X=Y)$ | Ratio $= \dfrac{P(X=Y)}{c \cdot q_Y}$ | $U$ | Decision | $X$ |
|-------|-----|--------|-----------|----------------------------------------|------|----------|-----|
| 1 | 2 | $0.125$ | $0.0758$ | $\dfrac{0.0758}{1.2131 \cdot 0.125} = 0.5000$ | $0.42$ | Accept | $2$ |
| 2 | 0 | $0.500$ | $0.6065$ | $\dfrac{0.6065}{1.2131 \cdot 0.500} = 1.0000$ | $0.78$ | Accept | $0$ |
| 3 | 1 | $0.250$ | $0.3033$ | $\dfrac{0.3033}{1.2131 \cdot 0.250} = 1.0000$ | $0.15$ | Accept | $1$ |

**Generated Sample:** $X_1 = 2,\ X_2 = 0,\ X_3 = 1$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation Check:** The empirical mean of samples $\{0, 1, 2\}$ is $1.0$, which is close to $\lambda = 0.5$ in expectation (need more samples for accurate estimate).

#### 3.1.4 For Large $\lambda$

When $\lambda$ is large, the geometric proposal's $c$ becomes inefficient. Better choices include:
- **Normal proposal** $g \sim N(\lambda, \lambda)$, then round to nearest integer.
- **Composition method** for $k$ near mode $\lfloor \lambda \rfloor$.

These advanced variants are typically beyond KTU Module 2 scope.

---

### 3.2 Application to the Gamma Distribution

The Gamma density with shape $\alpha > 0$ and rate $\beta = 1$ (without loss of generality) is:

$$f(x) \;=\; \frac{x^{\alpha - 1} e^{-x}}{\Gamma(\alpha)}, \quad x > 0$$

When $\alpha$ is a positive integer, $X$ is the sum of $\alpha$ i.i.d. Exponential(1) variates — solved by **convolution**, not ART. ART is needed when $\alpha$ is **non-integer**.

#### 3.2.1 Cheng's Algorithm for $\alpha \geq 1$ (Marsaglia–Tsang Form)

**Motivation:** The mode of $f$ is at $x = \alpha - 1$. A normal approximation centred at this mode serves as a tight envelope.

**Define the auxiliary constants:**

$$d = \alpha - \frac{1}{3}, \qquad c_g = \frac{1}{\sqrt{9d}}$$

**Step-by-step algorithm:**

1. Generate $V \sim N(0, 1)$ using Box–Muller or polar method.
2. **Reject** if $V \leq -1/c_g$ (ensures $x > 0$).
3. Compute candidate:
$$x = d \left( 1 + c_g V \right)^3$$
4. Generate $U \sim U(0, 1)$.
5. Accept $X = x$ if:
$$\ln U \;<\; \frac{V^2}{2} + d - x + d \ln\left(\frac{x}{d}\right)$$
6. Else, **reject** and return to Step 1.

**Derivation sketch of the acceptance inequality:**

The acceptance test stems from comparing $\ln f(x)$ to $\ln g(x)$ where $g$ is the majorising normal-like envelope. After algebraic manipulation (involving Stirling-type expansions of $\ln \Gamma$ near the mode), one arrives at the condition in Step 5.

#### 3.2.2 Algorithm for $0 < \alpha < 1$

When $\alpha$ lies in $(0,1)$, the mode sits at $x = 0$, breaking Cheng's envelope. The standard workaround uses the **boosting identity**:

$$X \;\stackrel{d}{=}\; Y \cdot V^{1/\alpha}$$

where $Y \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha + 1, 1)$ and $V \sim U(0,1)$ are independent.

**Procedure:**

1. Generate $Y \sim \text{Gamma}(\alpha + 1, 1)$ using Cheng's method (since $\alpha + 1 \geq 1$).
2. Generate $V \sim U(0,1)$ independent of $Y$.
3. Set $X = Y \cdot V^{1/\alpha}$.

#### 3.2.3 Worked Example — Gamma($\alpha = 2.5$, $\beta = 1$)

**Pre-compute constants:**

$$\begin{aligned}
d &= 2.5 - \tfrac{1}{3} = 2.1667 \\
c_g &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{9 \cdot 2.1667}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{19.5}} \approx 0.2265
\end{aligned}$$

**Trial trace** (one iteration):

| Step | Quantity | Value |
|------|----------|-------|
| Generate | $V \sim N(0,1)$ | $V = -0.5$ |
| Check | $-1/c_g = -4.415$ | $V > -4.415$ → continue |
| Compute | $x = 2.1667(1 + 0.2265 \cdot (-0.5))^3$ | $x = 2.1667 \cdot 0.8867^3 = 2.1667 \cdot 0.6974 \approx 1.511$ |
| Generate | $U \sim U(0,1)$ | $U = 0.5$, $\ln U = -0.693$ |
| Right side | $\frac{V^2}{2} + d - x + d \ln(x/d)$ | $0.125 + 2.167 - 1.511 + 2.167 \cdot \ln(0.697)$ |
| | | $= 0.125 + 0.656 + 2.167 \cdot (-0.360)$ |
| | | $= 0.125 + 0.656 - 0.780 \approx 0.001$ |
| Compare | $-0.693 < 0.001$ | **Accept** |

**Accepted sample:** $X = 1.511$.

---

### 3.3 Summary Comparison Matrix

| Feature | Poisson ART | Gamma ($\alpha \geq 1$) ART | Gamma ($0 < \alpha < 1$) ART |
|---------|-------------|------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Target pmf/pdf | $e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!$ | $x^{\alpha-1}e^{-x}/\Gamma(\alpha)$ | $x^{\alpha-1}e^{-x}/\Gamma(\alpha)$ |
| Proposal | Geometric$(p=1/2)$ | Normal $N(0,1)$ transformed | Cheng (for $\alpha+1$) $\times$ Uniform |
| Key constant | $c = 2e^{-\lambda}(2\lambda)^{k^*}/k^*!$ | $d = \alpha - 1/3,\ c_g = 1/\sqrt{9d}$ | None (composite) |
| Acceptance check | $U \leq P(Y)/(c \cdot q_Y)$ | $\ln U < V^2/2 + d - x + d\ln(x/d)$ | Direct from Cheng + boost |
| Complexity | Low | Moderate | Moderate |
| Typical efficiency | $1/c \approx 80\%$ | $\approx 95\%$ | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 General Acceptance-Rejection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1([Start ART]) --> p2[Step 1: Identify target pdf f]
    p2 --> p3[Step 2: Choose proposal pdf g easy to sample]
    p3 --> p4[Step 3: Compute envelope constant c]
    p4 --> p5[Step 4: Draw Y from proposal g]
    p5 --> p6[Step 5: Draw U from Uniform 0 1]
    p6 --> p7{Step 6: Test U less or equal to fY over c gY}
    p7 -->|Accept| p8[Set X equals Y]
    p7 -->|Reject| p5
    p8 --> p9([Output X and Stop])
```

**Legend / Reading Guide:**

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| Rounded rectangle (`(...)`) | Start or Stop terminator |
| Rectangle (`[...]`) | Process or computation step |
| Diamond (`{...}`) | Decision (branching) point |
| Solid arrow | Control flow direction |
| Label on arrow | Branch label (e.g., Accept / Reject) |

---

### 4.2 Distribution-Specific Application Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    q1[Target Distribution Selection] --> q2{Which family}
    q2 -->|Poisson lambda| q3[ART with Geometric proposal]
    q2 -->|Gamma integer alpha| q4[Convolution of Exponentials]
    q2 -->|Gamma non integer alpha over 1| q5[Cheng Normal Envelope ART]
    q2 -->|Gamma non integer alpha below 1| q6[Cheng plus Boost Trick]
    q3 --> q7[Discrete AR Algorithm]
    q4 --> q8[Sum of alpha exponentials]
    q5 --> q9[Continuous AR Algorithm]
    q6 --> q9
    q7 --> q10[Output Random Variate X]
    q8 --> q10
    q9 --> q10
```

---

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the ART Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    blockA[Input Module: Target pdf f and proposal pdf g] --> blockB[Constant Calculator: Computes envelope c]
    blockB --> blockC[Sampler Module: Generates Y from g]
    blockC --> blockD[Uniform Source: Produces U from 0 1]
    blockD --> blockE[Decision Engine: Compares U with fY over c gY]
    blockE -->|Accepted| blockF[Output Buffer: Stores X equals Y]
    blockE -->|Rejected| blockC
    blockF --> blockG[Result Stream: Emits variates]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a logical unit of the rejection sampler. The **feedback loop** from Decision Engine back to Sampler Module captures the iterative rejection nature. In practice, only accepted variates exit the Output Buffer.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define the Acceptance-Rejection Technique. State the conditions under which it can be applied for generating random variates.**

**Model Answer:**
The Acceptance-Rejection Technique is a general Monte Carlo method that generates random variates from a target density $f(x)$ by drawing candidates from a proposal density $g(x)$ and accepting them with probability proportional to $f(Y)/[c \cdot g(Y)]$.

**Conditions:**
1. The proposal $g(x)$ must be a valid pdf that is easy to sample from.
2. There must exist a finite constant $c \geq 1$ such that $f(x) \leq c \cdot g(x)$ for all $x$ in the support.
3. The ratio $f(x)/[c \cdot g(x)]$ must be efficiently computable.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Q2. What is the role of the envelope constant $c$ in the Acceptance-Rejection method? Prove that the expected number of iterations to obtain one accepted sample equals $c$.**

**Model Answer:**
The constant $c$ serves as the **envelope multiplier** that ensures the proposal density dominates the target. It is the smallest value such that $f(x) \leq c \cdot g(x)$ everywhere.

**Proof of $E[N] = c$:**

Since each trial accepts with probability $\eta = 1/c$, the number of trials $N$ until the first success follows a Geometric distribution with parameter $\eta$:

$$E[N] = \frac{1}{\eta} = \frac{1}{1/c} = c$$

Hence the expected number of iterations is exactly $c$, making $c$ the **efficiency-defining constant**.

---
