```mermaid
        e3 -->|exits| e4[System Exit]
    end
    subgraph INF["INFORMATION FLOW (Abstract)"]
        i1[Triage reads vitals] -->|data packet| i2[Doctor decides]
        i2 -->|prescription| i3[Pharmacy processes]
        i3 -->|status update| i4[EMR record]
    end
    e1 -.triggers.-> i1
    e4 -.updates.-> i4
    style ENT fill:#fff,stroke:#666
    style INF fill:#e7f3ff,stroke:#0066cc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The dotted arrows (`.triggers.` / `.updates.`) are the **cross-coupling points** where entity-flow and information-flow intersect. In real systems, 60–80 % of total delay originates from these coupling nodes — a frequent 14-mark design question topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between entity flow and information flow in a discrete-event simulation model. Give one example of each from a manufacturing system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Entity Flow | Information Flow |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Movement of *physical* objects | Movement of *abstract data/signals* |
| **Consumes Resources?** | Yes (time, space, energy) | No (logical) |
| **Measured in** | Units (jobs, packets, kg) | Bits / messages |
| **Example** | A *job moving* from Machine-1 to Machine-2 | The *completion signal* sent from Machine-1 to the Central Scheduler |

> *Awarding scheme: 1 mark for clear distinction (entity vs abstract), 1 mark for correct attribute, 1 mark for relevant manufacturing example.*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define "information gathering" as a sub-activity in the Activity-Scan world view. List any two state variables typically gathered.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Information gathering is the **first sub-flow** in the Activity-Scan logic where the model *acquires* the current values of system state variables, entity attributes, and resource statuses necessary to evaluate activity conditions.

**Two state variables:**
1. $L(t)$ — number of busy servers at time $t$.
2. $Q(t)$ — number of entities waiting in queue at time $t$.

*(Any two from: $N(t)$ = jobs in system, $W_q(t)$ = waiting time, $\rho(t)$ = utilisation.)*

> *Awarding scheme: 1 mark for definition, ½ mark × 2 for each state variable with notation.*

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently **omit the units** in numerical answers → **−0.5 to −1 mark** per instance.
> - Confusing *Shannon bits* with *information events* is the **#1 mark-killer** in this module.
> - In 14-mark design questions, students must include **at least one schematic diagram** (mermaid/hand-drawn) — absence costs **2 marks** outright.
> - Always state **assumptions and boundary conditions** explicitly; KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for unjustified assumptions.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **three pillars of information flow** in a discrete-event simulation system. Discuss how each pillar contributes to the overall model fidelity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing cell has two machines in series. Jobs arrive at $\lambda = 5$ jobs/hour (Poisson). Machine-1 has exponential service rate $\mu_1 = 8$ jobs/hour; Machine-2 has $\mu_2 = 10$ jobs/hour. Compute the **average information-decision delay** if the scheduling rule updates every $T_s = 0.05$ hours, assuming each scheduling update behaves like an M/M/1 server with effective info-processing rate $\mu_{info} = 60$ decisions/hour. **(7 marks)**

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

The three pillars of information flow, derived from the *Activity-Scan World View* (Banks et al., 5th Ed.), are:

**Pillar I — Information Gathering (2 marks)**
- Acquires state vector $S(t) = \{L(t), Q(t), \rho(t), \dots\}$.
- Reads entity attributes (job priority, due-date).
- Polls resource status (machine uptime, operator availability).

**Pillar II — Information Processing (2 marks)**
- Applies decision rule $D(t) = \mathcal{F}(S(t), R(t), \mathcal{P})$.
- Computes routing probabilities, scheduling priorities.
- Performs statistical aggregation (running averages, $L$-statistics).

**Pillar III — Information Dissemination (2 marks)**
- Communicates decisions to downstream activities.
- Updates entity attributes ($status \to "complete"$).
- Writes to output accumulators and post-run reports.

**Contribution to fidelity (1 mark):** Without explicit modelling of all three pillars, simulation *underestimates real-world latency* by 20–60 %, leading to over-promising system capacity. Including them yields *digital-twin-grade* accuracy.

*Examiner's note: Full 7 marks awarded if a labelled block diagram is included and each pillar has a one-sentence "why it matters".*

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Information-decision processing: M/M/1 model with $\lambda_{info} = T_s^{-1} = 1/0.05 = 20$ decisions/hour (decision triggers).
- Effective info-processing rate: $\mu_{info} = 60$ decisions/hour.

**Step 1 — Compute traffic intensity:**
$$\rho_{info} = \frac{\lambda_{info}}{\mu_{info}} = \frac{20}{60} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.333$$

**Step 2 — Verify stability:**
Since $\rho_{info} < 1$, the system is **stable**. ✔

**Step 3 — Apply M/M/1 average delay formula:**
$$\bar{D}_{info} = \frac{1}{\mu_{info} - \lambda_{info}} = \frac{1}{60 - 20} = \frac{1}{40} \text{ hour}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to seconds:**
$$\bar{D}_{info} = 0.025 \text{ hour} \times 3600 \text{ s/hour} = 90 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with material flow delay:**

Machine-1 M/M/1 delay:
$$\bar{D}_1 = \frac{1}{\mu_1 - \lambda} = \frac{1}{8 - 5} = \frac{1}{3} \text{ hour} = 1200 \text{ s}$$

Information delay fraction:
$$\frac{\bar{D}_{info}}{\bar{D}_1} = \frac{90}{1200} = 7.5\%$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Information-decision delay is **90 seconds**, contributing ~7.5 % of Machine-1's material-flow delay. For high-throughput cells, even modest info-delay is significant and must be modelled explicitly.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Define **Shannon entropy** and **mutual information**. A binary sensor reports "fault" with probability $p = 0.2$ and "no-fault" with probability $0.8$. Compute the **entropy per observation** and the **information content** (in bits) when the sensor fires an alarm. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An industrial IoT gateway transmits 16-byte telemetry packets every 100 ms over an AWGN channel of bandwidth $B = 20$ kHz and SNR $= 31$ (linear). Compute the **(i) channel capacity**, **(ii) information rate offered**, and **(iii) channel utilisation**. Comment on whether the channel can additionally support a *voice channel* requiring 64 kbps. **(7 marks)**

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Shannon Entropy** $H(X)$: average information (in bits) provided by source $X$ per symbol.
- **Mutual Information** $I(X;Y)$: reduction in uncertainty of $X$ given knowledge of $Y$.

**Step 1 — Apply entropy formula (2 marks):**
$$H(X) = -\bigl[p \log_2 p + (1-p)\log_2(1-p)\bigr]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $p = 0.2$ (2 marks):**
$$H(X) = -\bigl[0.2 \log_2 0.2 + 0.8 \log_2 0.8\bigr]$$
$$= -\bigl[0.2 \cdot (-2.3219) + 0.8 \cdot (-0.3219)\bigr]$$
$$= 0.4644 + 0.2575 = 0.7219 \text{ bits/observation}$$

**Step 3 — Information content on alarm (1 mark):**
Each "fault" event carries $-\log_2(0.2) = 2.3219$ bits of information — *more informative* than the average because fault events are rarer.

#### 🔑 Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- Packet size = 16 bytes = 128 bits.
- Rate = 1 packet per 100 ms → 10 packets/s.
- $B = 20{,}000$ Hz, SNR = 31.

**(i) Channel capacity (2 marks):**
$$C = 20{,}000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 31) = 20{,}000 \cdot \log_2(32) = 20{,}000 \cdot 5 = 100{,}000 \text{ bits/s} = 100 \text{ kbps}$$

**(ii) Information rate offered (2 marks):**
$$R = 128 \cdot 10 = 1280 \text{ bits/s} = 1.28 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iii) Channel utilisation (2 marks):**
$$\eta = \frac{R}{C} = \frac{1280}{100{,}000} = 0.0128 = 1.28\%$$

**Voice-channel feasibility (1 mark):**
Required for voice = 64 kbps. Residual capacity after telemetry = $100 - 1.28 = 98.72$ kbps. Since $64 < 98.72$, **YES**, the channel can additionally support the voice channel with ~34.72 kbps margin.

---

## 5.3 ✅ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> **Read this section 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- ☐ Information flows = **abstract** data movement (bits/messages), *not* physical entities.
- ☐ Three pillars: **Gather → Process → Disseminate** (Activity-Scan World View).
- ☐ Hierarchical levels: **L1 (intra-activity)**, **L2 (inter-activity)**, **L3 (inter-process)**.
- ☐ **Shannon Entropy**: $H(X) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$; max = 1 bit for balanced binary source.
- ☐ **Channel Capacity**: $C = B\log_2(1 + S/N)$ — theoretical ceiling, AWGN.
- ☐ **M/M/1 Info-Packet Delay**: $\bar{D} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$, valid iff $\rho < 1$.
- ☐ Always **separate entity flow (physical)** from **information flow (logical)** in diagrams.
- ☐ **Cross-coupling nodes** (where entity ↔ information intersect) are the dominant bottleneck.
- ☐ In numerical answers: **state units, state assumptions, show stability check**.
- ☐ Use **block diagrams with three pillars** for full marks in design questions.
- ☐ Common units: **bits/s**, **nats**, **messages/s**, **packets/s** — never mix.
- ☐ KTU frequently asks: *channel utilisation gap* — always compute $\eta = R/C$ and comment.
- ☐ Always justify why your chosen delay/throughput model (M/M/1, M/G/1, etc.) is appropriate.
- ☐ Examiner deductions: missing units (−0.5), no diagram (−2), no assumptions (−1), wrong bit/event (−1).

---

> **End of Module 3 — Information Flows | PCIET601 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
> *— Prepared under KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10, aligned with NEP 2020 OBE framework —*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Level and Rate Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Level and Rate Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **System Dynamics** (a methodology founded by Jay W. Forrester at MIT in the 1950s for modelling the behaviour of complex systems over time), the universe of variables is fundamentally divided into two mathematically distinct categories:

1. **Level Variables (L):** Also referred to as *Stock Variables* or *State Variables*. A level variable is an **accumulated quantity** that exists at a specific instant in time. It is the integral of the difference between its inflow and outflow rates. Formally, a level variable has *units of inventory* (e.g., litres, rupees, people, kilograms) and can never be negative in a physically meaningful system.

2. **Rate Variables (R):** Also referred to as *Flow Variables* or *Auxiliary-to-Level variables*. A rate variable describes the **speed** at which a level variable changes, measured in units of *quantity per unit time* (e.g., litres/second, rupees/year). The rate variable is the derivative with respect to time of a level variable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Must Learn Verbatim):**
> *"A level variable represents the accumulation or stock of a quantity within a system boundary at a point in time, while a rate variable represents the flow into or out of that level over a time interval. The rate of change of a level variable is determined by the net rate variable."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bathtub Model

The universally accepted intuition for understanding this concept is the **Bathtub / Water Tank Analogy**.

Imagine a real-world water tank:
- The **water level** (height of water in metres, or volume in litres) inside the tank is a **Level Variable**. If you stop everything else, the water in the tank *stays* where it is. It has memory. It is the *result of past actions*.
- The **water tap (inflow)** and the **drain pipe (outflow)** are **Rate Variables**. The tap is the *inflow rate* (litres/min), and the drain is the *outflow rate* (litres/min).
- The *net rate* of change of the water level equals (Inflow rate) − (Outflow rate).

If you suddenly close both the tap and the drain, the water **level** remains constant at its current value — but the **rate** becomes zero. The level has *memory*; the rate has *no memory* of past values.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memoryless vs. Memory Property:** A Rate variable's value at time *t* does NOT depend on its value at time *(t − Δt)*. A Level variable's value at time *t* DOES depend on all past rates. This is the cornerstone of System Dynamics modelling.

### 1.3 Why Two Variables? The Engineering Motivation

In real engineering systems — inventory control, population dynamics, signal processing, fluid networks, and even software architecture — we must distinguish between:
- **What is accumulated** (the *state* of the system at this instant), and
- **What is flowing** (the *activity* happening right now).

Without this distinction, we cannot model feedback, delays, or accumulation correctly. A bank balance is a **level**; a monthly salary credit/debit is a **rate**. A room temperature is a **level**; heat flow through a wall is a **rate**.

### 1.4 Units and Dimensions

- **Level Variable Units:** [Quantity] — e.g., kg, m³, ₹, persons, joules
- **Rate Variable Units:** [Quantity / Time] — e.g., kg/s, m³/hr, ₹/month
- The standard **time unit** must be consistent throughout the model (e.g., all in **seconds**, or all in **hours**). Mixing units is the **#1 cause of validation failure** in KTU lab simulations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Fundamental Differential Equation

Every system dynamics model in KTU syllabus is built upon one foundational equation that links a level variable to its rate variable:

$$L(t) = L(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \left[ R_{in}(\tau) - R_{out}(\tau) \right] d\tau$$

Where:
- $L(t)$ is the level at current time $t$
- $L(t_0)$ is the initial level at the start of the simulation
- $R_{in}(\tau)$ is the inflow rate at any past time $\tau$
- $R_{out}(\tau)$ is the outflow rate at any past time $\tau$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$ yields the rate equation:

$$\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)$$

This is a first-order ordinary differential equation (ODE) — the most commonly tested form in KTU Part B questions.

### 2.2 Discrete-Time (Simulation) Form

Because digital computers cannot handle continuous integration exactly, KTU simulations are run using **discrete time-steps**, often denoted $\Delta t$. The most common discretization method tested in the syllabus is the **Euler Integration** method:

$$L(t + \Delta t) = L(t) + \left[ R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t) \right] \cdot \Delta t$$

This is the workhorse equation for hand-simulation problems in the KTU ESE (End Semester Exam).

### 2.3 Forrester Notation (Standard KTU Diagram Notation)

In KTU System Dynamics diagrams (also called **causal loop** or **stock-flow diagrams**), a strict graphical convention is used:

| Symbol | Meaning | KTU Drawing Convention |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rectangle / Box | Level Variable | Inside a rectangular tank symbol |
| Valve symbol (bow-tie) | Rate Variable | A bow-tie shape with an arrow |
| Thick arrow (→) | Flow of material (real) | Connects level to rate |
| Thin arrow (→) | Flow of information | Provides a rate with its parameter |
| Cloud (source/sink) | Boundary of universe | No level exists here |

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Formula Bank — Memorize These:**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Used For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\frac{dL}{dt} = R_{in} - R_{out}$ | Net flow equals rate of change of level | Every level-rate problem |
| 2 | $L(t) = L(0) + \int_{0}^{t} R_{net}(\tau)\, d\tau$ | Continuous integration form | Derivation questions |
| 3 | $L_{n+1} = L_{n} + R_{net}(t_n) \cdot \Delta t$ | Euler forward integration | Hand simulation |
| 4 | $L_{n+1} = L_{n} + \frac{\Delta t}{2}\bigl[R_{net}(t_n) + R_{net}(t_{n+1})\bigr]$ | Trapezoidal (Improved Euler) | Numerical accuracy proofs |
| 5 | $\text{Units of Rate} = \frac{\text{Units of Level}}{\text{Units of Time}}$ | Dimensional consistency check | 3-mark verification questions |
| 6 | $R(t) = k \cdot f(\text{parameters}, L, t)$ | Rate is a function of levels + parameters | Defining auxiliary equations |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Initial Condition $L(t_0)$ is mandatory.** A level variable without an initial value is a physically undefined system. The KTU examiner always deducts marks if the initial condition is missing.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Inventory Management:** Level = warehouse stock; Rate = procurement / dispatch
- **Chemical Engineering:** Level = tank volume; Rate = inflow from pump, outflow to reactor
- **Population Dynamics:** Level = population; Rate = birth rate − death rate
- **Queueing Theory:** Level = queue length; Rate = arrival rate − service rate
- **Software Systems:** Level = memory buffer; Rate = packet inflow − packet processing rate
- **Finance:** Level = account balance; Rate = credit − debit per day

In every one of these, the *level* answers the question *"How much do we have now?"* and the *rate* answers *"How fast is it changing right now?"*
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Level Equation

We begin from first principles. Let $L(t)$ denote the quantity accumulated in a system at time $t$. Consider an infinitesimally small time interval $dt$.

**Step 1 — Statement of Conservation:**
The change in the level over the interval $dt$ equals the net quantity that flowed in during $dt$.

$$L(t + dt) - L(t) = \bigl[R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)\bigr] \cdot dt$$

**Step 2 — Rearrangement:**
Bring $L(t)$ to the left-hand side and divide both sides by $dt$:

$$\frac{L(t + dt) - L(t)}{dt} = R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Take the Limit as $dt \to 0$:**
The left side becomes the formal definition of the derivative:

$$\lim_{dt \to 0} \frac{L(t + dt) - L(t)}{dt} = \frac{dL(t)}{dt} = R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)$$

This completes the derivation. The level's instantaneous rate of change is governed entirely by the net rate at the same instant.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Discrete Euler Form

Digital simulation replaces the continuous derivative with a finite-difference approximation:

**Step 1 — Definition of Finite Difference:**
For small $\Delta t > 0$:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} \approx \frac{L(t + \Delta t) - L(t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitution into the Continuous Equation:**

$$\frac{L(t + \Delta t) - L(t)}{\Delta t} \approx R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by $\Delta t$:**

$$L(t + \Delta t) - L(t) = \bigl[R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)\bigr] \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Final Euler Form:**

$$L(t + \Delta t) = L(t) + \bigl[R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)\bigr] \cdot \Delta t$$

In compact indexing notation, where $n$ represents the $n$-th simulation step:

$$L_{n+1} = L_{n} + R_{net}(n) \cdot \Delta t$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the form used in nearly every KTU Part B 14-mark hand-simulation question.

### 3.3 Worked Example: Water Tank with Constant Inflow

A cylindrical water tank initially contains $L(0) = 100$ litres. The tap flows in at a constant $R_{in} = 20$ L/min. The drain flows out at $R_{out} = 5$ L/min. Use $\Delta t = 10$ min and compute the level for the first 4 time steps.

**Analytical (Exact) Solution:**
The net rate is constant: $R_{net} = 20 - 5 = 15$ L/min. So:

$$L(t) = 100 + 15t$$

**Numerical (Euler) Solution (Matrix Table):**

| Step $n$ | Time $t$ (min) | $L_n$ (L) | $R_{in}$ (L/min) | $R_{out}$ (L/min) | $R_{net}$ (L/min) | $L_{n+1} = L_n + R_{net} \cdot \Delta t$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | 100 | 20 | 5 | 15 | $100 + 15(10) = 250$ |
| 1 | 10 | 250 | 20 | 5 | 15 | $250 + 150 = 400$ |
| 2 | 20 | 400 | 20 | 5 | 15 | $400 + 150 = 550$ |
| 3 | 30 | 550 | 20 | 5 | 15 | $550 + 150 = 700$ |
| 4 | 40 | 700 | — | — | — | — |

**Verification using analytical formula** at $t = 40$ min:
$L(40) = 100 + 15(40) = 700$ L. ✔ **The Euler solution matches exactly** because the net rate is constant. For variable rates, the Euler method introduces a small *truncation error* proportional to $\Delta t$.

### 3.4 Truncation Error Analysis (Trapezoidal Improvement)

The local truncation error in the Euler scheme is $O(\Delta t^2)$, leading to global error $O(\Delta t)$. To improve accuracy, the KTU syllabus introduces the **Trapezoidal Rule** (also called Heun's method or Improved Euler):

**Step 1 — Predictor (Euler step):**

$$\tilde{L}_{n+1} = L_n + R_{net}(n) \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 2 — Compute predicted rate at new time:**

$$\tilde{R}_{net}(n+1) = R_{in}(n+1) - R_{out}(n+1) \quad \text{evaluated using } \tilde{L}_{n+1}$$

**Step 3 — Corrector (trapezoidal average):**

$$L_{n+1} = L_n + \frac{\Delta t}{2}\bigl[R_{net}(n) + \tilde{R}_{net}(n+1)\bigr]$$

This raises the global accuracy to $O(\Delta t^2)$, a frequent KTU Part B sub-question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Conceptual Stock–Flow Architecture (Forrester Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **canonical level–rate system** as taught in KTU Module 3, mapping the data dependencies between the two variable types.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0[Level L initial L0] -- initialization --> L1[Level L at time t]
    L1 -- "current value" --> RV[Rate Variable R]
    P[Parameter / Auxiliary] -- "influences" --> RV
    RV -- "R net dt" --> L1
    L1 -- "feedback to next step" --> L1
    SRC([Source - Inflow R_in]) -- "inflow" --> L1
    L1 -- "outflow" --> SNK([Sink - Outflow R_out])
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The `Source` (cloud) pumps material into the `Level` $L(t)$.
- The `Level` `L1` sends its current value (a thin information arrow) into the `Rate Variable` `RV`.
- The `Rate Variable` computes $R_{net}$ and feeds the result back to update `L1` at the next time step.
- The `Sink` `SNK` collects the outflow leaving `L1`.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram of Euler Hand-Simulation Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Start: n = 0] --> stepB[Read Initial Level L_n]
    stepB --> stepC[Read Current Time t_n]
    stepC --> stepD[Compute R_in t_n and R_out t_n]
    stepD --> stepE[Compute R_net = R_in - R_out]
    stepE --> stepF[Compute L_new = L_n + R_net times dt]
    stepF --> stepG[Update: L_n = L_new, t_n = t_n + dt]
    stepG --> stepH{Is t_n less than T_max?}
    stepH -- "Yes" --> stepD
    stepH -- "No" --> stepI[Print Final L_T_max]
    stepI --> stepJ[End of Simulation]
```

This is the **algorithmic skeleton** of every KTU hand-simulation question. The student should be able to draw this loop from memory for full marks in a 7-mark sub-part.

### 4.3 Water-Tank Physical Analogy (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TAP[Inflow Tap] -- "R_in" --> TANK[Tank - Holds Water Level L]
    TANK -- "Water Out" --> DRAIN[Outflow Drain R_out]
    TANK -- "Level reading feedback" --> CTRL[Rate Controller]
    CTRL -- "Adjusts valve position" --> TAP
    CTRL -- "Adjusts drain valve" --> DRAIN
    styleTANK[Tank - Level Variable]
    styleTAP[Rate In - R_in]
    styleDRAIN[Rate Out - R_out]
    styleCTRL[Auxiliary - Rate Logic]
```

**Physical Mapping Table:**

| Physical Element | System-Dynamics Variable |
| :--- | :--- |
| Water inside the tank | Level $L(t)$ |
| Tap flow rate | Inflow Rate $R_{in}(t)$ |
| Drain flow rate | Outflow Rate $R_{out}(t)$ |
| Net change in water level | $R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)$ |
| Volume reading on the gauge | Information link (no units of material) |

### 4.4 Causal Information Flow vs. Material Flow

In KTU diagrams, a critical distinction (worth **2 marks** in a 3-mark question) is the difference between:
- **Material flow** (drawn as a thick arrow): carries the *quantity* (e.g., water from tap to tank).
- **Information flow** (drawn as a thin arrow): carries only *numeric values* used for calculation, no physical material.

A parameter (e.g., the valve coefficient) sent to a rate variable is *information*, not material.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a Level variable and a Rate variable in the context of System Dynamics. Provide one engineering example for each.**

**Model Answer (Mark-Wise Allocation):**

| Mark | Content Expected |
| :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Level variable: An *accumulated quantity* at a point in time; has *memory* of past inflows/outflows; unit is [Quantity] |
| 1 | Rate variable: A *flow per unit time*; *memoryless*; unit is [Quantity / Time] |
| 1 | Example: Level = litres of fuel in a tank; Rate = fuel consumption rate of engine (litres/hour) |

> [!NOTE]
> The examiner awards 1 mark for each of: (a) definition, (b) property difference, (c) example. Missing the example = 2/3 marks.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**For a level variable $L(t)$, state and explain the fundamental differential equation that relates it to its rate variables. What happens to $L(t)$ if both inflow and outflow rates become zero at $t = t_0$?**

**Model Answer:**

The fundamental relation is:
$$\frac{dL(t)}{dt} = R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)$$

**Explanation (1 mark):** The instantaneous rate of change of the level is equal to the net rate (inflow minus outflow). This is a first-order ODE with no explicit time-dependence unless the rates are time-dependent.

**Consequence (1 mark):** If both $R_{in}(t_0) = 0$ and $R_{out}(t_0) = 0$ at $t = t_0$, then $\frac{dL}{dt} = 0$, so the level *remains frozen* at its current value $L(t_0)$. The level exhibits inertia/memory, and this property is the foundation of all delay and feedback effects in dynamic systems (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **bathtub model** analogy. Explain how it is used to model a **fuel inventory system** in a manufacturing plant. Identify the level variable, inflow rate, and outflow rate in this example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cooling tank initially contains $L(0) = 500$ litres of water at $80^\circ C$. Cold water enters at $R_{in} = 30$ L/min. Hot water is drained at $R_{out} = 25$ L/min. The temperature of inflow is $20^\circ C$, and the contents are perfectly mixed. Use $\Delta t = 5$ min and the **Euler integration** method to compute the volume of water in the tank for the first **3 time-steps**. Show all calculations in a table. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Solution (a) — Bathtub Model & Fuel Inventory (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Diagram (3 marks):** The student should draw a tank with an inflow tap and outflow drain, similar to the architecture in Section 4.3. A neat diagram with proper labels (`Inflow`, `Outflow`, `Level`) carries full marks.

**Step 2 — Mapping to Fuel Inventory (2 marks):**

| Bathtub Element | Fuel Inventory Equivalent |
| :--- | :--- |
| Water in tank (Level) | Litres of fuel in storage tank |
| Tap (Inflow Rate) | Fuel delivery rate from supplier (L/day) |
| Drain (Outflow Rate) | Fuel consumption rate by production (L/day) |
| Net change | Daily fuel build-up or depletion |

**Step 3 — Governing Equation (2 marks):**

$$\frac{dL_{fuel}}{dt} = R_{supplier}(t) - R_{production}(t)$$

If $R_{supplier} > R_{production}$ → tank fills up. If $R_{supplier} < R_{production}$ → tank depletes. The level *cannot go negative* (you cannot consume what you do not have), a fact noted in **1 mark**.

---

**Solution (b) — Euler Hand Simulation (7 marks):**

Net rate is constant:
$$R_{net} = R_{in} - R_{out} = 30 - 25 = 5 \text{ L/min}$$

Euler update equation:
$$L_{n+1} = L_n + 5 \cdot \Delta t = L_n + 5 \cdot 5 = L_n + 25$$

**Step-by-step table (5 marks for the table, 2 marks for the final answer and interpretation):**

| Step $n$ | Time $t$ (min) | $L_n$ (L) | $R_{in}$ (L/min) | $R_{out}$ (L/min) | $R_{net}$ (L/min) | $L_{n+1} = L_n + 25$ (L) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | 500 | 30 | 25 | 5 | 525 |
| 1 | 5 | 525 | 30 | 25 | 5 | 550 |
| 2 | 10 | 550 | 30 | 25 | 5 | 575 |
| 3 | 15 | 575 | 30 | 25 | 5 | 600 |

**Final Interpretation (2 marks):** The tank volume increases linearly by 25 L every 5 min. At $t = 15$ min, the volume is 600 L. The level is *monotonically increasing* because net rate is positive.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define **Euler's method** of numerical integration. Derive the discrete-time update equation for a level variable $L(t)$. State **one advantage** and **one disadvantage** of the method. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a population system where the level $P(t)$ is the number of people in a city. The birth rate is $R_b = 0.02 \cdot P(t)$ people/year, and the death rate is $R_d = 0.005 \cdot P(t)$ people/year. Initial population $P(0) = 10000$. Using $\Delta t = 1$ year, compute the population for the first **3 time-steps** using the Euler method. State the assumption made. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Solution (a) — Euler Method Derivation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Euler's method is a first-order numerical procedure for solving ODEs of the form $\frac{dL}{dt} = f(t, L)$ by approximating the derivative with a forward finite-difference.

**Derivation (3 marks):** From the fundamental relation:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = R_{in} - R_{out}$$

The forward finite-difference approximation:

$$\frac{L(t + \Delta t) - L(t)}{\Delta t} \approx R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)$$

Solving for $L(t + \Delta t)$:

$$L(t + \Delta t) = L(t) + \bigl[R_{in}(t) - R_{out}(t)\bigr] \cdot \Delta t$$

**Advantage (1 mark):** Simplicity — only the current value of the rate is needed; computationally cheap.

**Disadvantage (1 mark):** Truncation error grows with $\Delta t$ — large step-sizes lead to inaccurate or unstable simulations, especially when rates are non-linear in $L$.

---

**Solution (b) — Population Simulation (7 marks):**

**Governing Equation (1 mark):**
$$\frac{dP}{dt} = R_b - R_d = 0.02P(t) - 0.005P(t) = 0.015P(t)$$

**Assumption (1 mark):** Birth and death rates are **proportional to the current population** and remain constant in their coefficients over the simulation horizon.

**Euler update (1 mark):**
$$P_{n+1} = P_n + 0.015 P_n \cdot \Delta t = P_n + 0.015 P_n \cdot 1 = 1.015 P_n$$

**Step-by-step computation (4 marks):**

| Step $n$ | Time $t$ (year) | $P_n$ | $R_b = 0.02 P_n$ | $R_d = 0.005 P_n$ | $R_{net} = 0.015 P_n$ | $P_{n+1} = P_n + R_{net} \cdot 1$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | 10000 | 200 | 50 | 150 | 10150 |
| 1 | 1 | 10150 | 203 | 50.75 | 152.25 | 10302.25 |
| 2 | 2 | 10302.25 | 206.05 | 51.51 | 154.53 | 10456.78 |
| 3 | 3 | 10456.78 | — | — | — | — |

**Final Answer:** $P(3) \approx 10457$ people.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the initial condition $L(0)$** → 1 mark deduction per occurrence.
> 2. **Mixing time units** (e.g., $R_{in}$ in L/min, $t$ in seconds) → full mark cancellation in dimensional questions.
> 3. **Confusing information flow with material flow** in diagrams → 0.5 mark deduction per wrong arrow.
> 4. **Writing the Euler equation as $L_{n+1} = L_n \cdot R_{net}$** (forgetting $\Delta t$) → 1 mark deduction.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention that level variables cannot be negative** in a physical system → 0.5 mark deduction.
> 6. **Computing the rate first but applying the rate at the *next* time step** (this is Euler-forward — only correct if rates are evaluated at $t_n$, not $t_{n+1}$).
> 7. **Rounding intermediate results** instead of carrying full precision to the next step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision — Must Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ✔ **Level Variable** = Stock, State, Accumulation. **Unit:** [Quantity]. **Property:** Has memory; *cannot* be negative.
- ✔ **Rate Variable** = Flow, Activity. **Unit:** [Quantity / Time]. **Property:** Memoryless; *depends on* levels + parameters.
- ✔ **Master Equation:** $\frac{dL}{dt} = R_{in} - R_{out}$
- ✔ **Integral Form:** $L(t) = L(0) + \int_0^t R_{net}(\tau)\, d\tau$
- ✔ **Euler Discrete Form:** $L_{n+1} = L_n + R_{net}(n) \cdot \Delta t$
- ✔ **Trapezoidal (Improved) Form:** $L_{n+1} = L_n + \frac{\Delta t}{2}\bigl[R_{net}(n) + R_{net}(n+1)\bigr]$
- ✔ **Forrester Notation:** Rectangle = Level; Valve (bow-tie) = Rate; Thick arrow = Material; Thin arrow = Information.
- ✔ **Initial condition $L(0)$ is mandatory** — no level is defined without it.
- ✔ **Time unit consistency** is the single most common source of numerical error.
- ✔ **Euler's method is first-order accurate** (error $\sim \Delta t$); **Trapezoidal is second-order** (error $\sim \Delta t^2$).
- ✔ **The bathtub model** is the universal analogy for any level–rate system (water tank, fuel, money, people, queue, memory buffer).
- ✔ **Material flow ≠ Information flow** in KTU diagrams — the difference is a guaranteed 2-mark question.
- ✔ **Levels integrate; Rates differentiate.** This is the one-line summary of the entire module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Delays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Delay (Simulation Definition):** A *delay* is a span of simulated time during which the state of the system experiences no event-driven change, but the simulation clock is advanced by a specified (deterministic or stochastic) duration. It represents the time an entity spends in a particular activity such as service, travel, processing, or waiting.

In the formal **event-scheduling worldview** of discrete-event simulation (DES), a delay is implemented by scheduling a *future event* (the delay-completion event) into the **Future Events List (FEL)** at time $t + D$, where $t$ is the current simulation clock value and $D$ is the delay duration. Until that completion event is popped from the FEL, the entity is considered *in-service* or *in-transit*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Bank-Teller Analogy:** Imagine you walk into a bank. You take a token, walk to the counter, and hand it to the teller. From that instant, *you enter a delay* — you stand there for, say, 3 to 7 minutes while the teller processes your transaction. During this 3-7 minutes, **nothing else happens in your personal state** (your "turn" is fixed, your place is held), but the **bank's clock (simulation clock) keeps moving forward**. The instant the teller says "Thank you, next!", your delay *completes* — a new event fires — and you walk out, freeing the teller's resource.

This simple analogy captures every property of a delay:
- **Pre-delay state** (you reach the counter)
- **Time advancement without event activity** (the 3-7 minutes)
- **Resource held** (the teller is busy with you)
- **Completion event** (the next customer is called)
- **Resource release** (the teller becomes free)

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern delay analysis in any KTU-evaluated queuing/simulation problem:

- **Service Rate ($\mu$):** Average number of entities served per unit time. Units = *customers/time*.
- **Arrival Rate ($\lambda$):** Average number of entities arriving per unit time. Units = *customers/time*.
- **Traffic Intensity ($\rho$):** Ratio $\lambda / \mu$. Dimensionless. For stability: $\rho < 1$.
- **Mean Delay ($E[D]$):** Expected time of a single delay. Units = *time*.
- **Variance of Delay ($Var[D]$):** Squared time units. Quantifies delay uncertainty.

### 1.4 Why Delays Are Central to Module 3

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Delays are the **temporal heart** of every discrete-event simulation. Whether modelling a manufacturing line, a hospital triage, an airport runway, or a packet-switched network — every activity between two consecutive events IS a delay. Mastery of delay modelling is the single highest-weight topic for Module 3 in the KTU 2024 ESE pattern.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Decomposition of a Delay

Every delay in a DES model, regardless of the application, can be decomposed into four operational phases:

1. **Entry (Seize Phase):** The entity arrives at a station and *seizes* the required resources. If resources are unavailable, the entity either *balks* (leaves immediately) or *joins a queue* (passive delay). This phase generates a *resource-request* event.
2. **Active Occupancy (Delay Body):** The simulation clock is advanced by a sample $D$ drawn from the delay's probability distribution. No state-changing events occur to the entity. Resources remain *held*.
3. **Completion (Release Phase):** The delay-completion event fires. The entity becomes eligible to seize the next station's resources or to release the currently held ones.
4. **Exit (Routing):** The entity departs the delay station and is routed to the next block, or terminated.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Delay Modelling

- **Why model delays stochastically?** Real-world service times (humans, machines, networks) are inherently variable. Using a single average hides critical tail behaviour and produces over-optimistic system performance estimates.
- **How are delays represented?** Via a **probability distribution** (Exponential, Normal, Uniform, Triangular, Erlang, Lognormal, etc.) whose parameters are estimated from observed data.
- **How is a delay implemented in code?** Via a *function-call wrapper* around a random-variate generator that schedules a future event $D$ time units ahead.

### 2.3 Classification of Delays

| Class | Sub-Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **By Variability** | Deterministic | $D$ is a constant $c$ | CNC machine cycle (fixed program) |
| | Stochastic | $D$ sampled from a distribution | Human teller service time |
| **By Entity State** | Active (Work) | Entity is being processed, resource held | Surgery in operation theatre |
| | Passive (Wait) | Entity is in queue, no resource held | Patients in waiting room |
| **By Interruptibility** | Non-Preemptable | Delay must run to completion | Aircraft landing rollout |
| | Preemptable | Delay can be suspended and resumed | Server failure mid-job |
| **By Reneging** | Patient | Entity waits indefinitely | Caller on hold |
| | Reneging | Entity leaves after timeout $T_r$ | Customer abandons queue after 5 min |
| | Balking | Entity refuses to enter at all | Shopper sees long queue, leaves |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Sheet for Delays (M/M/1 reference unless noted).** Master these — they appear in 80%+ of KTU Part B numericals on Module 3.

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E[D] = 1/\mu$ | Mean exponential service (delay) time | time, $\mu>0$ |
| 2 | $Var[D] = 1/\mu^2$ | Variance of exponential delay | time² |
| 3 | $f_D(t) = \mu\, e^{-\mu t}$ | Exponential PDF of delay | $t \geq 0$ |
| 4 | $F_D(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$ | Exponential CDF of delay | $t \geq 0$ |
| 5 | $\rho = \lambda / \mu$ | Traffic intensity (utilization) | $\rho < 1$ for stability |
| 6 | $W_q = \dfrac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda} = \dfrac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu-\lambda)}$ | Mean waiting time in queue | time |
| 7 | $W = W_q + 1/\mu = \dfrac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$ | Mean total time in system | time |
| 8 | $L = \lambda W = \dfrac{\rho}{1-\rho}$ | Mean number in system (Little's Law) | entities |
| 9 | $L_q = \lambda W_q = \dfrac{\rho^2}{1-\rho}$ | Mean number in queue | entities |
| 10 | $P(T_{\text{delay}} > t) = e^{-\mu t}$ | Memoryless property of exponential delay | dimensionless |
| 11 | $CV = \sigma_D / \mu_D$ | Coefficient of variation of delay | dimensionless |
| 12 | $T_{\text{response}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (W_{q,i} + 1/\mu_i)$ | Total delay in tandem (series) of $n$ stations | time |

> **Note on notation:** the symbol $\vert$ is avoided in the table; absolute values are written as $\lvert x \rvert$ in prose.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Manufacturing:** Delay distributions for machine cycle times drive **bottleneck identification** and **buffer sizing**.
- **Healthcare:** Patient delay times in OPDs feed into **staff scheduling** and **OPD capacity planning**.
- **Telecommunications:** Packet delay modelling in routers underpins **QoS engineering** and **buffer-management algorithms**.
- **Logistics:** Loading/unloading delays at docks drive **truck turnaround** and **warehouse layout** decisions.
- **Computer Architecture:** Instruction-pipeline stage delays determine **clock-period** and **IPC** (instructions per cycle).

### 2.6 The SEIZE–DELAY–RELEASE Pattern (The Heart of Module 3)

The **SEIZE–DELAY–RELEASE** pattern is the canonical three-step transaction flow used in every DES language (GPSS, Arena, Simio, AnyLogic). It is the single most-tested sub-topic on delays in the KTU ESE.

- **SEIZE:** The entity requests and acquires the resource(s) it needs. If unavailable, the entity enters a passive wait.
- **DELAY:** The entity occupies the resource for a duration $D$ drawn from the service-time distribution.
- **RELEASE:** The entity frees the resource, making it available for the next waiting entity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In **GPSS World**, this is implemented as:
>
> ```gpss
>      SEIZE    TELLER
>      ADVANCE  4,2          ; Mean=4, Half-range=2 -> Uniform(2,6)
>      RELEASE  TELLER
> ```
>
> The `ADVANCE A,B` block models a **deterministic-cum-uniform delay** of mean $A$ and half-range $B$ — i.e., $D \sim \text{Uniform}(A-B, A+B)$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mean Delay in an M/M/1 Queue (From First Principles)

**Problem Setup:** Entities arrive according to a Poisson process with rate $\lambda$, and service (delay) times are i.i.d. exponentials with rate $\mu$. Find the mean *total* delay $E[W]$ (queue + service).

**Step 1 — Steady-state balance equations.**

Let $p_n$ be the steady-state probability that there are $n$ entities in the system. The birth-death balance gives:

$$\lambda\, p_n = \mu\, p_{n+1} \quad \text{for } n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

**Step 2 — Solve the recurrence.**

Dividing both sides and telescoping:

$$p_{n+1} = \left(\frac{\lambda}{\mu}\right) p_n = \rho\, p_n$$

Hence by induction:

$$p_n = \rho^n\, p_0$$

**Step 3 — Apply the normalization condition $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} p_n = 1$.**

$$p_0 \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \rho^n = p_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1-\rho} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_0 = 1 - \rho$$

Therefore:

$$p_n = (1 - \rho)\,\rho^n, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$

**Step 4 — Compute $E[N]$, the mean number of entities in the system.**

$$E[N] = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\, p_n = (1-\rho) \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n\, \rho^n$$

Using the standard series identity $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} n x^n = x / (1-x)^2$ for $\lvert x \rvert < 1$:

$$E[N] = (1-\rho) \cdot \frac{\rho}{(1-\rho)^2} = \frac{\rho}{1-\rho}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Little's Law $E[N] = \lambda\, E[W]$ to obtain the mean total delay.**

$$E[W] = \frac{E[N]}{\lambda} = \frac{\rho}{\lambda(1-\rho)} = \frac{\lambda/\mu}{\lambda(1-\lambda/\mu)} = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda}$$

**Final Result:**

$$\boxed{\,E[W] = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} = \frac{1}{\mu}\cdot\frac{1}{1-\rho}\,}$$

**Verification:** As $\rho \to 0$, $E[W] \to 1/\mu$ (the mean service time alone — no queueing). As $\rho \to 1^-$, $E[W] \to \infty$ (queue explodes). Both limits are physically consistent. $\blacksquare$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Mean Waiting Time in Queue, $E[W_q]$

Subtract the mean service time from $E[W]$:

$$E[W_q] = E[W] - E[\text{Service}] = \frac{1}{\mu - \lambda} - \frac{1}{\mu}$$

Bring to a common denominator:

$$E[W_q] = \frac{\mu - (\mu - \lambda)}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)} = \frac{\lambda}{\mu(\mu - \lambda)}$$

Equivalent form using $\rho = \lambda/\mu$:

$$E[W_q] = \frac{\rho}{\mu(1 - \rho)} = \frac{\rho}{\mu - \lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-tested formula** in the KTU Module-3 question bank. Commit it to memory along with $L_q = \lambda W_q = \rho^2 / (1-\rho)$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Inverse-Transform Sampling of an Exponential Delay

**Why this matters:** Simulation cannot use the analytic formula $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\mu t}$ directly to *generate* a delay. We need to *invert* it and draw $D$ from $U(0,1)$.

**Step 1 — Set $F_D(D) = U$ where $U \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$.**

$$U = 1 - e^{-\mu D}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $D$.**

$$1 - U = e^{-\mu D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(1 - U) = -\mu D$$

$$D = -\frac{1}{\mu}\ln(1 - U)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the symmetry property of the uniform distribution.**

Since $1 - U$ and $U$ have the same distribution:

$$\boxed{\,D = -\frac{1}{\mu}\ln(U)\,}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of mean.**

Taking expectation over $U \sim U(0,1)$, using $E[-\ln U] = 1$:

$$E[D] = \frac{1}{\mu} E[-\ln U] = \frac{1}{\mu} \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{\mu} \quad \checkmark$$

**Algorithm (Universal, for any KTU exam):**

1. Draw $U \sim U(0,1)$ using a pseudo-random generator.
2. Compute $D = -\ln(U) / \mu$.
3. Use $D$ as the delay sample for the current entity.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Mean Total Delay in a Tandem (Series) Queue

Consider two M/M/1 stations in series, with rates $\mu_1$ and $\mu_2$, fed by a Poisson arrival of rate $\lambda < \min(\mu_1, \mu_2)$. The mean total time in the system is the sum of the mean times at each station, *provided the system is in equilibrium and the departure process of station 1 remains Poisson* (a property known as **Burke's theorem** for M/M/1):

$$E[W_{\text{total}}] = E[W_1] + E[W_2] = \frac{1}{\mu_1 - \lambda} + \frac{1}{\mu_2 - \lambda}$$

**Generalization to $n$ stations in series:**

$$E[W_{\text{total}}] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{\mu_i - \lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Burke's theorem** is implicitly assumed in the KTU textbook treatment of tandem queues. A more advanced analysis (Jackson networks) relaxes this assumption and is **out of syllabus** for Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic Delay Station

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the **functional state-machine** of an entity traversing a delay station, from arrival to departure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Entity Arrival] --> B{Resource Available?}
    B -- Yes --> C[SEIZE Resource]
    B -- No --> D[Enter Queue and Wait]
    D --> E{Resource Free?}
    E -- Yes --> C
    E -- No Timeout --> F[Reneging Exit]
    C --> G[Begin DELAY: Sample D from Distribution]
    G --> H[Hold Resource for Duration D]
    H --> I[Delay Completion Event Fires]
    I --> J[RELEASE Resource]
    J --> K[Route to Next Station]
    F --> L[Entity Lost from System]
    K --> M[Departure]
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 SEIZE – DELAY – RELEASE Transaction Flow (GPSS Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[SEIZE Block] --> S2[ADVANCE Block]
    S2 --> S3[RELEASE Block]
    S3 --> S4[Next Station]
    S1 -.Holds.-> R1[(Resource Pool)]
    S2 -.Occupies.-> R1
    S3 -.Frees.-> R1
```

### 4.3 Time-Advance Mechanism: How a Delay Jumps the Clock

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLK as Simulation Clock
    participant FEL as Future Events List
    participant ENT as Entity X
    CLK->>FEL: Pop next event at t=10
    FEL->>CLK: Event: "Begin Delay for X"
    CLK->>ENT: Set X status = IN-SERVICE
    ENT->>FEL: Schedule "Delay Complete" at t=10+5=15
    Note over CLK: Clock idles between t=10 and t=15
    CLK->>FEL: Pop next event at t=15
    FEL->>CLK: Event: "Delay Complete for X"
    CLK->>ENT: Set X status = FREE
    ENT->>ENT: Proceed to next station
```

### 4.4 Delay Classification Tree (Hierarchical Overview)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D[Delays in DES]
    D --> V[By Variability]
    V --> V1[Deterministic: D = c]
    V --> V2[Stochastic: D ~ F]
    D --> S[By Entity State]
    S --> S1[Active Work: resource held]
    S --> S2[Passive Wait: resource not held]
    D --> I[By Interruptibility]
    I --> I1[Non-Preemptable]
    I --> I2[Preemptable]
    D --> R[By Reneging Behaviour]
    R --> R1[Patient: no timeout]
    R --> R2[Balking: refuses to enter]
    R --> R3[Reneging: leaves after timeout]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Define a *delay* in the context of discrete-event simulation. List any two of its classifications with one real-world example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **delay** in discrete-event simulation is a span of simulated time during which an entity occupies one or more resources (active delay) or waits for a resource to become free (passive delay), with no state-changing event occurring to the entity during this interval. The simulation clock advances by the delay duration $D$, and a "delay-completion" event is scheduled in the Future Events List at time $t + D$.

Two classifications with examples:
- **Deterministic vs. Stochastic:** A CNC machine executing a fixed G-code cycle is a *deterministic delay* (constant $D$); a human teller servicing customers is a *stochastic delay* (typically exponential, $D \sim \text{Exp}(\mu)$).
- **Active vs. Passive:** A patient undergoing surgery is in an *active delay* (the surgeon and operation theatre are held); the same patient waiting in the pre-op ward is in a *passive delay* (no resource held).

#### Question A2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Differentiate between *balking* and *reneging* in queueing systems. State one engineering consequence of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Balking:** An arriving entity *refuses to enter* a queue upon seeing its current length or waiting time. *Consequence:* in a call-centre simulation, balking reduces offered load — the system throughput $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$ falls below the nominal arrival rate, masking true demand and causing under-provisioning of agents.
- **Reneging:** An entity *enters* a queue but *abandons* it after a maximum waiting time $T_r$ elapses. *Consequence:* in an emergency-room model, reneging due to long wait directly translates to clinical risk (patient deterioration), forcing capacity planners to add a safety margin to staffing.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a) Derive the mean total delay $E[W]$ in an M/M/1 queueing system from first principles, and hence obtain the mean queue delay $E[W_q]$ and the mean number of entities $E[N]$ in the system. State all assumptions. (7 marks)**

**(b) With the help of a block-level diagram, explain the SEIZE–DELAY–RELEASE transaction flow as implemented in GPSS World. Use the syntax `ADVANCE A, B` and clearly state what distribution of delay it represents. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *See the full derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes.* For board-exam conciseness, the expected solution steps are:

1. State assumptions: Poisson arrivals with rate $\lambda$; i.i.d. exponential service times with rate $\mu$; single server; FCFS discipline; infinite queue capacity; steady-state $\rho = \lambda/\mu < 1$.
2. Write the birth-death balance $\lambda p_n = \mu p_{n+1}$.
3. Solve to obtain $p_n = (1-\rho)\rho^n$.
4. Use $\sum p_n = 1$ to verify $p_0 = 1 - \rho$.
5. Compute $E[N] = \sum n p_n = \rho/(1-\rho)$.
6. Apply Little's Law: $E[W] = E[N]/\lambda = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$.
7. Subtract service time: $E[W_q] = E[W] - 1/\mu = \lambda/[\mu(\mu - \lambda)]$.

**Mark split:** Assumptions (1), balance equations (1), solution of $p_n$ (1), normalization (1), $E[N]$ (1), Little's Law and $E[W_q]$ (2) = 7 marks.

**(b)** *See the SEIZE–DELAY–RELEASE diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.* The expected answer:

1. **SEIZE TELLER** — Entity requests the resource named `TELLER`. If the resource is busy, the entity is placed in a FIFO queue attached to that resource. (2 marks)
2. **ADVANCE 4, 2** — The entity enters the delay block; a sample is drawn from $\text{Uniform}(4-2, 4+2) = \text{Uniform}(2, 6)$. The simulation clock advances by this sample. The entity holds the `TELLER` resource. (3 marks)
3. **RELEASE TELLER** — On delay completion, the entity frees `TELLER`. If a queue exists, the next entity automatically seizes the resource. (2 marks)

**Mark split:** Block identification (1), explanation of SEIZE (2), explanation of ADVANCE + uniform distribution (3), explanation of RELEASE (1) = 7 marks.

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) Enumerate any four common probability distributions used to model stochastic delays in simulation, and for each state: its PDF, its mean, its variance, and one real-world application. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the concepts of *preemption* and *reneging* in delay modelling, with diagrams. Why is the exponential distribution's memoryless property particularly useful for the former? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Four common delay distributions:

1. **Exponential, $D \sim \text{Exp}(\mu)$:** PDF $f(t) = \mu e^{-\mu t}$, mean $1/\mu$, variance $1/\mu^2$. *Application:* inter-arrival and service times in classic M/M/c models. (2 marks)
2. **Uniform, $D \sim U(a,b)$:** PDF $f(t) = 1/(b-a)$ on $[a,b]$, mean $(a+b)/2$, variance $(b-a)^2/12$. *Application:* GPSS `ADVANCE A,B` block; bounded random loads. (2 marks)
3. **Normal, $D \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$:** PDF $f(t) = \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-(t-\mu)^2/2\sigma^2}$, mean $\mu$, variance $\sigma^2$. *Application:* machining time when central-limit-theorem aggregation dominates. (1.5 marks)
4. **Triangular, $D \sim \text{Tri}(a,b,c)$ with $a \le b \le c$:** mean $(a+b+c)/3$, variance $(a^2+b^2+c^2 - ab - ac - bc)/18$. *Application:* expert-elicited estimates when empirical data is scarce. (1.5 marks)

**(b)** *Preemption* is the mechanism by which a delay in progress is *temporarily suspended* to allow a higher-priority entity to seize the same resource, after which the original entity *resumes* its delay. Two sub-types:
- **Preemptive-resume:** The interrupted entity resumes from where it was paused. Most realistic for CPU scheduling.
- **Preemptive-restart:** The interrupted entity begins its delay anew from time zero. Common in manufacturing retooling.

*Reneging* is the mechanism by which an entity *voluntarily abandons* a queue (or an active service) after waiting longer than a tolerance time $T_r$. The abandonment reduces the effective queue length and, in service systems, often forces the entity to leave the system unserved.

**Why exponential helps preemption:** The exponential distribution is the **unique continuous distribution with the memoryless property**:

$$P(D > t + s \mid D > t) = P(D > s) = e^{-\mu s}$$

Hence, when a preemption suspends an exponential delay at elapsed time $t$, the **remaining** delay is *statistically identical* in distribution to a fresh exponential sample of rate $\mu$. This makes the math of preemptive-resume analysis tractable, since the resumed delay does not depend on how long the entity had already waited. (4 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the steady-state condition $\rho < 1$:** Marks are docked if you write $E[W] = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$ without first establishing that the system is stable.
> 2. **Confusing $E[W]$ (system time) with $E[W_q]$ (queue time):** A common 1–2 mark loss. Remember: $E[W] = E[W_q] + 1/\mu$.
> 3. **Mixing up active and passive delays:** A *passive* delay does **not** hold a resource — claiming otherwise is a 1-mark error.
> 4. **In GPSS `ADVANCE A, B`:** Many students write the range as $(A, B)$ instead of $(A-B, A+B)$. This costs a full mark.
> 5. **In inverse-transform derivation:** The step $D = -\ln(1-U)/\mu$ must be simplified to $-\ln(U)/\mu$ using the symmetry of $U(0,1)$. Skipping the justification loses 1 mark.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Delays in DES**

- **Definition:** A delay is a clock-advance interval with no internal events; it is scheduled as a future event in the FEL at time $t + D$.
- **Active vs. Passive:** Active = resource held (service); Passive = resource not held (queue).
- **Deterministic vs. Stochastic:** Constant $D$ vs. $D$ sampled from a PDF.
- **SEIZE–DELAY–RELEASE:** The canonical 3-step transaction pattern. In GPSS: `SEIZE R`, `ADVANCE A,B` (uniform on $(A-B, A+B)$), `RELEASE R`.
- **Preemption:** Suspending a delay in favour of a higher-priority entity. Exponential delay is mathematically convenient due to memorylessness.
- **Reneging:** Entity abandons queue after timeout $T_r$. Reduces effective load.
- **Balking:** Entity refuses to enter at all.
- **M/M/1 Results (the "Big Five"):** $E[N] = \rho/(1-\rho)$; $E[W] = 1/(\mu - \lambda)$; $E[W_q] = \lambda/[\mu(\mu-\lambda)]$; $L = \lambda W$; $L_q = \lambda W_q$.
- **Stability requirement:** $\rho = \lambda/\mu < 1$ (single server).
- **Tandem queues (Burke's theorem):** $E[W_{\text{total}}] = \sum_i 1/(\mu_i - \lambda)$ for $n$ M/M/1 stations in series.
- **Inverse-transform method for exponential delay:** $D = -\ln(U)/\mu$, where $U \sim U(0,1)$.
- **Memoryless property:** $P(D > t + s \mid D > t) = e^{-\mu s}$ — *unique* to the exponential among continuous distributions.
- **GPSS `ADVANCE A,B` syntax:** Uniform on $(A-B, A+B)$, mean $A$, half-range $B$.
- **Variance of exponential delay:** $Var[D] = 1/\mu^2$ — equal to the *square* of the mean. This is why exponential delays are highly variable.
- **Coefficient of variation:** $CV = 1$ for exponential (high variability); $CV = 0$ for deterministic (no variability).
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Always subtract the service time correctly when computing queue-only metrics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Information Smoothing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Information Smoothing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Information Smoothing** is a class of estimation techniques used in System Modelling and Simulation to refine noisy, stochastic, or incomplete time-series data by suppressing random fluctuations while preserving the underlying systematic trend, structural pattern, or true signal of the system.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PCIET601**, information smoothing is broadly categorized into three operational regimes:

1. **Filtering** – Estimating the present state using observations up to the present (causal).
2. **Prediction / Extrapolation** – Estimating a future state using past and present observations.
3. **Smoothing** – Estimating a **past** state using observations up to the present (non-causal, retrospective).

Mathematically, given observations $Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_k$, the **smoothed estimate** of the state at time $j < k$ is denoted:

$$
\hat{X}_{j \mid k} = E[X_j \mid Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_k]
$$

where $\hat{X}_{j \mid k}$ is the optimal estimate of the state $X_j$ conditioned on the entire observation record up to time $k$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Smoothing uses *more* information than filtering because it incorporates **future observations** relative to the estimated time point. This results in lower mean-square error compared to filtering.

### 1.2 Types of Smoothing (KTU Syllabus Highlight)

| Smoothing Type | Definition | Common Algorithm |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed-Interval Smoothing** | All observations over a fixed time window are available | Rauch–Tung–Striebel (RTS) |
| **Fixed-Point Smoothing** | Estimate a specific past time $j$ using growing observation set | Mayne-Fraser Smoother |
| **Fixed-Lag Smoothing** | Estimate state at time $k - \Delta$ with constant lag $\Delta$ | Two-filter formula |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are listening to a **scratchy vinyl record** of a classical music concert. The music plays in real-time (that's *filtering*), and you can hear the notes. Now imagine that the entire record has finished playing, and you are **playing it back again** in your mind, knowing how the symphony ends. This retrospective understanding of how the music resolves allows you to mentally "clean up" the earlier scratches — you can now better appreciate the violin's true melody in the middle of the piece because you know the resolution at the end. **That retrospective clarity is exactly what information smoothing does** — it uses future data to enhance the estimate of past states.

### 1.4 Physical / Engineering Constants

- **Standard Smoothing Constant ($\alpha$):** Typically $\alpha \in (0, 1)$, with $0.1 \le \alpha \le 0.3$ for slow-changing trends.
- **Process Noise Covariance ($Q$):** Represents uncertainty in the system dynamics.
- **Measurement Noise Covariance ($R$):** Represents uncertainty in sensor observations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

Information smoothing operates on the foundational assumption that observed data $Z_k$ is a corrupted version of a true signal $X_k$:

$$
Z_k = X_k + V_k
$$

where $V_k$ is **measurement noise** (typically zero-mean, white Gaussian: $V_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$).

The smoothing problem decomposes into **three sequential steps**:

**Step 1 — Forward Pass (Kalman Filter):**
Process the data forward from $k = 1$ to $k = N$, computing predicted and updated state estimates. This is the standard Kalman filter recursion.

**Step 2 — Backward Pass (Smoother Recursion):**
Process the data backward from $k = N$ to $k = 1$, using future observations to refine past estimates. This is the **Rauch–Tung–Striebel (RTS)** recursion.

**Step 3 — Combine Forward and Backward Estimates:**
The optimal smoothed estimate combines both passes via a weighted fusion.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Smoothing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Smoothing is **non-causal** — it cannot be used in real-time control. Its primary engineering utility lies in:
> - **Offline data analysis** (post-processing sensor logs)
> - **Parameter estimation** in system identification
> - **State initialization** for subsequent simulation runs
> - **Fault diagnosis** where past events must be reconstructed precisely

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the RTS smoother equations and the exponential smoothing family. These appear repeatedly in KTU Previous Year Papers.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Z_k = X_k + V_k$ | Observation model | Measurement |
| 2 | $X_k = \Phi X_{k-1} + W_k$ | State transition model | System dynamics |
| 3 | $\hat{X}_{k\mid k} = \hat{X}_{k\mid k-1} + K_k(Z_k - H\hat{X}_{k\mid k-1})$ | Kalman Filter Update | Filtering |
| 4 | $K_k = P_{k\mid k-1} H^T (H P_{k\mid k-1} H^T + R)^{-1}$ | Kalman Gain | Filtering |
| 5 | $\hat{X}_{k\mid N} = \hat{X}_{k\mid k} + A_k (\hat{X}_{k+1\mid N} - \hat{X}_{k+1\mid k})$ | RTS Smoother (state) | Smoothing |
| 6 | $A_k = P_{k\mid k} \Phi^T P_{k+1\mid k}^{-1}$ | RTS Smoother Gain | Smoothing |
| 7 | $P_{k\mid N} = P_{k\mid k} + A_k(P_{k+1\mid N} - P_{k+1\mid k})A_k^T$ | RTS Smoother (covariance) | Smoothing |
| 8 | $S_t = \alpha X_t + (1-\alpha) S_{t-1}$ | Simple Exponential Smoothing | Forecasting |
| 9 | $S_t = \alpha X_t + (1-\alpha)(S_{t-1} + b_{t-1})$ | Holt's Linear Trend | Trended Data |
| 10 | $b_t = \beta(S_t - S_{t-1}) + (1-\beta)b_{t-1}$ | Holt's Trend Update | Trended Data |

> **Critical Notation Warning:** The vertical bar `$\vert$` in $\hat{X}_{k \mid k}$ means "estimated at time $k$ given observations up to time $k$". **Do not confuse with absolute value.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Post-flight trajectory reconstruction for missile guidance systems.
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG signal denoising for accurate diagnosis.
- **Economics:** GDP time-series smoothing for policy analysis.
- **Manufacturing:** Sensor data refinement for predictive maintenance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Simple Exponential Smoother

We begin with the **first-order recursive exponential smoother**. Let $S_t$ be the smoothed value at time $t$ and $X_t$ be the raw observation.

**Assumption:** The new smoothed estimate is a weighted average of the current observation and the previous smoothed estimate.

$$
S_t = \alpha X_t + (1 - \alpha) S_{t-1}
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the recursion backward:**

$$
S_t = \alpha X_t + (1-\alpha)[\alpha X_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) S_{t-2}]
$$

$$
S_t = \alpha X_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) X_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)^2 S_{t-2}
$$

**Step 2 — Continue expansion to initial value $S_0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_t &= \alpha X_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) X_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 X_{t-2} \\
    &\quad + \alpha(1-\alpha)^3 X_{t-3} + \cdots + (1-\alpha)^t S_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret as exponentially decaying weights:**

$$
S_t = \sum_{i=0}^{t} \alpha(1-\alpha)^i X_{t-i} + (1-\alpha)^t S_0
$$

This shows that the smoother is essentially an **infinite impulse response (IIR) filter** with exponentially decaying weights summing to unity.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Rauch–Tung–Striebel (RTS) Fixed-Interval Smoother

**Given:**
- Linear discrete-time system: $X_k = \Phi X_{k-1} + W_k$, where $W_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, Q)$
- Observation model: $Z_k = H X_k + V_k$, where $V_k \sim \mathcal{N}(0, R)$
- Kalman filter has produced forward estimates $\hat{X}_{k\mid k}$ and covariances $P_{k\mid k}$.

**Step 1 — Define the smoothing gain matrix $A_k$:**

We seek the relationship between the smoothed estimate $\hat{X}_{k\mid N}$ and the filtered estimate $\hat{X}_{k\mid k}$:

$$
\hat{X}_{k\mid N} = \hat{X}_{k\mid k} + A_k (\hat{X}_{k+1\mid N} - \hat{X}_{k+1\mid k})
$$

where $A_k$ is the smoother gain to be determined.

**Step 2 — Substitute state equation:**

From the model: $\hat{X}_{k+1\mid k} = \Phi \hat{X}_{k\mid k}$, hence:

$$
X_{k+1} = \Phi X_k + W_k
$$

**Step 3 — Compute cross-covariance $P_{k, k+1 \mid N}$:**

Using the orthogonality principle and minimizing the mean-square error, we derive:

$$
A_k = P_{k\mid k} \Phi^T P_{k+1\mid k}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Final smoothed covariance update:**

$$
P_{k\mid N} = P_{k\mid k} + A_k (P_{k+1\mid N} - P_{k+1\mid k}) A_k^T
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The RTS smoother is initialized at $k = N$ with $\hat{X}_{N\mid N}$ (the final Kalman filter estimate) and $P_{N\mid N}$, then run **backwards** to $k = 1$.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Filtering | Smoothing | Prediction |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Causality** | Causal (real-time) | Non-causal (offline) | Causal |
| **Data Used** | $Z_1 \ldots Z_k$ | $Z_1 \ldots Z_N$ (full set) | $Z_1 \ldots Z_k$ |
| **Error** | Higher | **Lowest** | Highest |
| **Algorithm** | Kalman Filter | RTS / Two-Filter | Kalman Predictor |
| **Application** | Real-time control | Post-processing | Forecasting |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Simple Exponential Smoothing

**Problem:** Given observations $X = [10, 12, 13, 11, 14, 15]$ and $\alpha = 0.3$, $S_0 = 10$, compute the smoothed series.

**Solution:**

Using $S_t = \alpha X_t + (1-\alpha) S_{t-1}$:

| $t$ | $X_t$ | Calculation | $S_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | $0.3(10) + 0.7(10)$ | **10.00** |
| 2 | 12 | $0.3(12) + 0.7(10)$ | **10.60** |
| 3 | 13 | $0.3(13) + 0.7(10.6)$ | **11.32** |
| 4 | 11 | $0.3(11) + 0.7(11.32)$ | **11.22** |
| 5 | 14 | $0.3(14) + 0.7(11.22)$ | **12.05** |
| 6 | 15 | $0.3(15) + 0.7(12.05)$ | **12.94** |

The smoothed series $[10.00, 10.60, 11.32, 11.22, 12.05, 12.94]$ exhibits reduced variance compared to the raw series, confirming the noise-suppression effect.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Information Smoothing — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Observation Stream Z1 Z2 ... ZN] --> B[Forward Pass Kalman Filter]
    B --> C[Predicted State Xk given k minus 1]
    B --> D[Updated State Xk given k]
    B --> E[Error Covariance Pk given k]
    F[Backward Pass RTS Smoother] --> G[Smoothed State Xk given N]
    F --> H[Smoothed Covariance Pk given N]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    C --> F
    G --> I[Optimal Smoothed Output]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Application Layer Post processing Control Diagnosis]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Smoothing Taxonomy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Information Smoothing] --> B[Fixed Interval]
    A --> C[Fixed Point]
    A --> D[Fixed Lag]
    B --> E[Rauch Tung Striebel]
    B --> F[Two Filter Formula]
    C --> G[Mayne Fraser]
    D --> H[Constant Lag Estimator]
    E --> I[Batch Mode Offline]
    G --> J[Recursive Growth]
    H --> K[Streaming with Delay]
    style A fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Three-Mode Estimation Hierarchy (Conceptual Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Time Axis t] --> B[Past t less than k minus 1]
    A --> C[Present t equals k]
    A --> D[Future t greater than k plus 1]
    B --> E[Smoothing uses all observations]
    C --> F[Filtering uses observations up to k]
    D --> G[Prediction uses observations up to k minus 1]
    E --> H[Highest Accuracy]
    F --> I[Medium Accuracy]
    G --> J[Lowest Accuracy]
    style E fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#f1948a,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Define information smoothing. List its three main types.**

**Model Answer:**
Information smoothing is a retrospective estimation technique used in system modelling to estimate the state of a system at a past time $j < k$ using observations available up to time $k$. It provides higher accuracy than filtering because it incorporates future observations.

The three main types are:
1. **Fixed-Interval Smoothing** — fixed observation window.
2. **Fixed-Point Smoothing** — fixed time point of interest.
3. **Fixed-Lag Smoothing** — constant time delay.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Write the recursive equation for simple exponential smoothing and state the role of the smoothing constant $\alpha$.**

**Model Answer:**
The recursive equation is:

$$
S_t = \alpha X_t + (1 - \alpha) S_{t-1}
$$

where $S_t$ is the smoothed value, $X_t$ is the current observation, and $S_{t-1}$ is the previous smoothed value.

**Role of $\alpha$:**
- $\alpha \in (0, 1)$ controls the trade-off between responsiveness and stability.
- **High $\alpha$** (close to 1): More responsive to recent changes, less smoothing.
- **Low $\alpha$** (close to 0): More smoothing, slower response to trends.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Part (a) (7 Marks):** Explain the Rauch–Tung–Striebel (RTS) fixed-interval smoothing algorithm with the necessary equations. State its initial conditions clearly.

**Model Answer:**

The RTS smoother is a backward-recursive algorithm that refines the Kalman filter estimates using future observations.

**Step 1 — Forward Pass:** Run the standard Kalman filter from $k = 1$ to $k = N$, storing $\hat{X}_{k\mid k}$ and $P_{k\mid k}$ at each step.

**Step 2 — Initialization (Backward Pass):**

$$
\hat{X}_{N\mid N} = \text{final Kalman estimate}, \quad P_{N\mid N} = \text{final Kalman covariance}
$$

**Step 3 — Backward Recursion (for $k = N-1, N-2, \ldots, 1$):**

Smoother gain:

$$
A_k = P_{k\mid k} \Phi^T P_{k+1\mid k}^{-1}
$$

Smoothed state:

$$
\hat{X}_{k\mid N} = \hat{X}_{k\mid k} + A_k \left(\hat{X}_{k+1\mid N} - \hat{X}_{k+1\mid k}\right)
$$

Smoothed covariance:

$$
P_{k\mid N} = P_{k\mid k} + A_k \left(P_{k+1\mid N} - P_{k+1\mid k}\right) A_k^T
$$

**Significance:** The RTS smoother provides the minimum mean-square error (MMSE) estimate of past states by leveraging the full observation record.

---

**Part (b) (7 Marks):** A system has observations $[20, 22, 21, 23, 25]$ with smoothing constant $\alpha = 0.4$ and $S_0 = 20$. Compute the exponentially smoothed series and comment on the result.

**Model Answer:**

Using $S_t = 0.4 X_t + 0.6 S_{t-1}$:

| $t$ | $X_t$ | Computation | $S_t$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 20 | $0.4(20) + 0.6(20)$ | **20.00** |
| 2 | 22 | $0.4(22) + 0.6(20)$ | **20.80** |
| 3 | 21 | $0.4(21) + 0.6(20.8)$ | **20.88** |
| 4 | 23 | $0.4(23) + 0.6(20.88)$ | **21.73** |
| 5 | 25 | $0.4(25) + 0.6(21.73)$ | **23.04** |

**Variance Reduction:**
- Raw variance $\approx 3.5$
- Smoothed variance $\approx 1.4$

**Comment:** The smoothed series exhibits significantly lower variance, demonstrating effective noise suppression. The increasing trend in raw data is preserved in the smoothed series, confirming that the smoother retains essential signal characteristics while filtering out fluctuations.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Part (a) (7 Marks):** Compare and contrast filtering, smoothing, and prediction in the context of system modelling. Provide a suitable mathematical formulation for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Mode | Mathematical Formulation | Information Used | Causality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prediction** | $\hat{X}_{k+1\mid k} = E[X_{k+1} \mid Z_1, \ldots, Z_k]$ | Past & present | Causal |
| **Filtering** | $\hat{X}_{k\mid k} = E[X_k \mid Z_1, \ldots, Z_k]$ | Past & present | Causal |
| **Smoothing** | $\hat{X}_{j\mid k} = E[X_j \mid Z_1, \ldots, Z_k]$, $j < k$ | Past, present, future | Non-causal |

**Discussion:**
- **Prediction** projects the state forward in time; useful for forecasting.
- **Filtering** estimates the current state in real-time; the foundation of control theory.
- **Smoothing** refines past estimates using later data; yields the **lowest error** among the three because it exploits the complete observation record.

**Engineering Example:** In a satellite trajectory, filtering provides real-time position estimates during flight, while smoothing is used post-mission to reconstruct the exact flight path for analysis.

---

**Part (b) (7 Marks):** Derive the recursive form of simple exponential smoothing and show that it acts as a low-pass filter with exponentially decaying weights.

**Model Answer:**

**Starting Equation:**

$$
S_t = \alpha X_t + (1 - \alpha) S_{t-1}
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the recursion backward:**

$$
S_t = \alpha X_t + (1-\alpha)[\alpha X_{t-1} + (1-\alpha) S_{t-2}]
$$

**Step 2 — Continue to initial condition $S_0$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_t &= \alpha X_t + \alpha(1-\alpha) X_{t-1} + \alpha(1-\alpha)^2 X_{t-2} \\
    &\quad + \cdots + (1-\alpha)^t S_0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the structure:**

$$
S_t = \sum_{i=0}^{t} w_i X_{t-i} + (1-\alpha)^t S_0
$$

where the weights are $w_i = \alpha(1-\alpha)^i$.

**Step 4 — Verify weight properties:**

Sum of weights:

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \alpha(1-\alpha)^i = \frac{\alpha}{1-(1-\alpha)} = 1
$$

**Conclusion:** The smoother is a **low-pass IIR filter** with weights decaying geometrically. Low-frequency components (the underlying trend) pass through with unity gain, while high-frequency noise is attenuated by the exponentially decaying weights.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing smoothing with filtering** — students frequently write filter equations when asked about smoothing, losing 3–4 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting initialization** — the RTS smoother must be initialized at $k = N$, not $k = 0$.
> 3. **Misinterpreting $\alpha$** — $\alpha$ close to 0 gives *more* smoothing, *not less*. Reversed logic is a common error.
> 4. **Skipping the backward pass** — students describe only the forward Kalman filter, losing 5 marks.
> 5. **Wrong covariance notation** — writing $P_{k\mid k}$ where $P_{k\mid N}$ is required.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Smoothing is retrospective** — it uses future observations to estimate past states.
- ✅ **RTS smoother** is the standard fixed-interval smoother for linear Gaussian systems.
- ✅ **Three smoothing types:** Fixed-Interval, Fixed-Point, Fixed-Lag.
- ✅ **Exponential smoothing** is the simplest form: $S_t = \alpha X_t + (1-\alpha) S_{t-1}$.
- ✅ **$\alpha$ interpretation:** High $\alpha$ = responsive; Low $\alpha$ = heavy smoothing.
- ✅ **Smoothing always yields lower MSE** than filtering for the same time point.
- ✅ **Non-causality:** Smoothing cannot be used for real-time control — only offline analysis.
- ✅ **Two-pass structure:** Forward Kalman + Backward RTS = Optimal smoothed estimate.
- ✅ **Holt's method** extends exponential smoothing to handle trends.
- ✅ **Initialization of RTS:** Set $\hat{X}_{N\mid N}$ equal to final Kalman estimate.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Table Functions and Table Function Multipliers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Table Functions and Table Function Multipliers
