Then: $$\dot{x}_1 = x_2$$ $$\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{k}{m}x_1 = -100\, x_1$$ With $m = 1$, $k = 100$, $\omega_n = 10$ rad/s. **Euler Method Update:** $$x_{1,n+1} = x_{1,n} + h\, x_{2,n}$$ $$x_{2,n+1} = x_{2,n} + h(-100\, x_{1,n})$$ **RK4 Method Update:** For each stage: $$k_{1a} = h\, x_{2,n}, \quad k_{1b} = h(-100\, x_{1,n})$$ $$k_{2a} = h(x_{2,n} + k_{1b}/2), \quad k_{2b} = h(-100)(x_{1,n} + k_{1a}/2)$$ $$k_{3a} = h(x_{2,n} + k_{2b}/2), \quad k_{3b} = h(-100)(x_{1,n} + k_{2a}/2)$$ $$k_{4a} = h(x_{2,n} + k_{3b}), \quad k_{4b} = h(-100)(x_{1,n} + k_{3a})$$ $$x_{1,n+1} = x_{1,n} + \frac{1}{6}(k_{1a} + 2k_{2a} + 2k_{3a} + k_{4a})$$ $$x_{2,n+1} = x_{2,n} + \frac{1}{6}(k_{1b} + 2k_{2b} + 2k_{3b} + k_{4b})$$ **Step 2 (3 marks):** Sample numerical computation. **Analytical solution:** $x(t) = \cos(10t)$, so $x(2\pi) = \cos(20\pi) = 1$ m, period $T = 2\pi/10 \approx 0.628$ s. **Euler with $h = 0.1$:** Apply first step from $x_1 = 1, x_2 = 0$: $$x_1(0.1) = 1 + 0.1 \times 0 = 1$$ $$x_2(0.1) = 0 + 0.1 \times (-100)(1) = -10$$ After 62.83 steps (approx), the Euler amplitude grows as $(1 + h^2\omega_n^2)^{n/2} = (1 + 1)^{31.4} = 2^{31.4} \approx 2.7 \times 10^9$ — **catastrophic numerical explosion**. **RK4 with $h = 0.1$:** Since $h\omega_n = 0.1 \times 10 = 1 < 2\sqrt{2} \approx 2.83$, the method is **stable**. The amplitude at $t = 2\pi$ will be very close to 1 (within ~0.1% error), and the period error is of order $O(h^4) \approx 10^{-4}$ s, negligible. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Stability comment. - **Euler method** is **unstable** for the harmonic oscillator because $\vert\lambda\vert^2 = 1 + h^2\omega_n^2 > 1$ for any $h > 0$. Energy is artificially injected, causing amplitude to grow without bound. - **RK4 method** is **stable** as long as $h\omega_n \leq 2\sqrt{2}$. It conserves energy well and produces accurate amplitude/period for moderate $h$. This makes RK4 the preferred choice for simulating oscillatory systems in KTU lab work. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Oscillation Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Always state rad/s, seconds, N, etc. (–0.5 marks) > 2. **Confusing $\omega_n$ with $\omega_d$** — $\omega_n$ is the undamped natural frequency; $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ is the damped frequency. (–1 mark) > 3. **Wrong sign in the characteristic equation** — A sign error propagates through the entire solution. (–2 marks) > 4. **Not specifying the damping regime** — For $\zeta \geq 1$, the system does NOT oscillate; writing sinusoidal solutions loses marks. (–1 mark) > 5. **Skipping the derivation of constants A, B from initial conditions** — Always apply $x(0)$ and $\dot{x}(0)$ explicitly. (–1 mark) > 6. **In numerical methods, forgetting to convert to first-order system** — A 2nd-order ODE must be written as a coupled 1st-order system before applying Euler/RK4. (–2 marks) > 7. **Step-size issues** — Failing to verify the stability condition $h\omega_n \leq 2\sqrt{2}$ for RK4. (–1 mark) --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Oscillation (Module 4)** - **Standard Form:** Always write the ODE as $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = F(t)/m$ before solving. - **Three Master Parameters:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$, $\zeta = c/(2m\omega_n)$, $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$. - **Damping Regimes:** $\zeta = 0$ (undamped), $0 < \zeta < 1$ (underdamped—OSCILLATES), $\zeta = 1$ (critical), $\zeta > 1$ (overdamped). - **Solution Forms:** Free undamped: $A\cos\omega_n t + B\sin\omega_n t$. Free underdamped: $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}[A\cos\omega_d t + B\sin\omega_d t]$. - **Forced Response:** Steady-state amplitude $X = F_0/\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}$. - **Resonance Frequency:** $\omega_r = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ (exists only for $\zeta < 1/\sqrt{2}$). - **Logarithmic Decrement:** $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ — used to find $\zeta$ from measured peaks. - **Numerical Methods:** - Euler: Simple but **unstable** for oscillations ($\vert\lambda\vert^2 = 1 + h^2\omega_n^2 > 1$). - RK4: **Stable** when $h\omega_n \leq 2\sqrt{2}$; fourth-order accuracy, energy-conserving for moderate $h$. - **Transmissibility:** For $r = \omega/\omega_n > \sqrt{2}$, vibration isolation occurs. - **Quality Factor:** $Q = 1/(2\zeta)$ — high $Q$ means sharp resonance, low $Q$ means broad/damped. - **KTU Favourite Questions:** (1) Solve a 2nd-order ODE analytically with initial conditions. (2) Apply Euler/RK4 to a 1st-order system and comment on stability. (3) Compute resonance, amplitude, or transmissibility in a forced system. - **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Always check whether the system actually oscillates (i.e., is $\zeta < 1$?) before writing sinusoidal solutions.

topics.title: Interactions of Fundamental Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interactions of Fundamental Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Fundamental Modes

In the context of multi-degree-of-freedom (MDOF) dynamic systems, **fundamental modes** (also called **natural modes of vibration** or **normal modes**) are the set of independent, intrinsic patterns of motion in which every point of the system oscillates sinusoidally at a single characteristic frequency called the **natural frequency** ($\omega_n$) when displaced from equilibrium and released.

Mathematically, for an undamped $N$-DOF linear system, the equations of motion are:

$$[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$$

The fundamental modes are the solutions to the corresponding **generalized eigenvalue problem**:

$$([K] - \omega^2[M])\{\phi\} = \{0\}$$

where:
- $[M]$ = mass matrix (symmetric, positive definite)
- $[K]$ = stiffness matrix (symmetric, positive semi-definite)
- $\omega$ = natural frequency (rad/s)
- $\{\phi\}$ = mode shape vector (eigenvector)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A system with $N$ degrees of freedom possesses exactly $N$ fundamental modes. The $i$-th mode is characterized by its natural frequency $\omega_i$ and mode shape $\{\phi\}_i$. For a 2-DOF system, two fundamental modes exist; for a 3-DOF system, three, and so on.

## 1.2 What Are "Interactions" of Fundamental Modes?

**Modal interaction** refers to the phenomenon where the response of a vibrating system cannot be decomposed into independent contributions from each mode. This occurs under specific physical and mathematical conditions:

| Condition | Effect on Modes |
|-----------|-----------------|
| **Undamped / Proportionally Damped** | Modes are **uncoupled** — each can be analyzed independently |
| **Non-Proportionally Damped** | Modes become **coupled** — equations of motion in modal coordinates remain coupled |
| **Non-Linear Systems** | Strong mode coupling due to stiffness/geometry non-linearities |
| **Gyroscopic / Circulatory Systems** | Asymmetric matrices cause complex modal interactions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Mode Coupling:** A condition where the energy or response of one fundamental mode influences another mode, such that they cannot be analyzed as isolated, independent oscillations.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Two-Coupled-Pendulum Model

Imagine **two pendulums of equal length** hung from a single flexible, lightweight beam (a weakly coupled system).

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** When only Pendulum A is displaced and released, you observe:
> - Initially, Pendulum A swings while Pendulum B remains nearly still
> - Gradually, energy "flows" from A to B (energy transfer via the flexible beam)
> - Pendulum B starts swinging while A gradually slows down
> - Eventually, energy fully transfers back to A — this is called a **beat** or **modal beating** phenomenon
>
> The system actually has **two fundamental modes**:
> - **Mode 1 (Symmetric mode, $\omega_1$):** Both pendulums swing in-phase together
> - **Mode 2 (Asymmetric mode, $\omega_2$):** Pendulums swing out-of-phase (opposite directions)
>
> The beat phenomenon is a classic manifestation of **interaction between two closely-spaced fundamental modes**.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- **Natural frequency unit:** **rad/s** (or Hz: $f = \omega / 2\pi$)
- **Mode shape normalization:** Dimensionless, often normalized so that $\{\phi\}_i^T [M] \{\phi\}_i = 1$ (mass-normalized)
- **Modal overlap factor (MOF):** $MOF = \Delta f / f_n$ — used in statistical energy analysis (SEA) to determine whether modes are "well-separated" or "overlapping"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** When two natural frequencies differ by **less than ~10%**, they are considered **closely spaced**, and strong modal interaction (beats) can be expected under transient excitation.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — Modal Decoupling in MDOF Systems

For an $N$-DOF damped system, the general equation of motion is:

$$[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [C]\{\dot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{F(t)\}$$

### Step-by-Step Logic:

1. **Step 1 — Assume a harmonic solution** $\{x(t)\} = \{\phi\} e^{j\omega t}$ for the undamped free-vibration problem
2. **Step 2 — Substitute** into $[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}$ to obtain the eigenvalue problem
3. **Step 3 — Solve the eigenvalue problem** to get $N$ eigenvalues $(\omega_1^2, \omega_2^2, \ldots, \omega_N^2)$ and $N$ eigenvectors $(\{\phi\}_1, \{\phi\}_2, \ldots, \{\phi\}_N)$
4. **Step 4 — Construct the modal matrix** $[\Phi] = [\{\phi\}_1 \; \{\phi\}_2 \; \cdots \; \{\phi\}_N]$ (an $N \times N$ matrix)
5. **Step 5 — Apply coordinate transformation** $\{x\} = [\Phi]\{q\}$, where $\{q\}$ are the **modal coordinates**
6. **Step 6 — Pre-multiply** by $[\Phi]^T$ to decouple the equations (this works when $[C]$ is proportional to $[M]$ and $[K]$)
7. **Step 7 — Solve each uncoupled modal equation** independently using SDOF theory
8. **Step 8 — Superpose (sum up)** the modal contributions to obtain the total physical response

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why harmonic assumption?** Because we are seeking the natural, free-vibration characteristics — which by definition are sinusoidal.
- **Why construct $[\Phi]$?** Because modal coordinates $\{q\}$ transform the coupled MDOF equations into $N$ independent SDOF equations.
- **Why pre-multiply by $[\Phi]^T$?** Because of the **orthogonality property** of mode shapes: $\{\phi\}_i^T [M] \{\phi\}_j = 0$ and $\{\phi\}_i^T [K] \{\phi\}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$.
- **Why does proportional damping decouple modes?** Because $[C]$ can be expressed as $[C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K]$, inheriting the same orthogonality properties.

## 2.3 Mode Superposition Method (Uncoupled Case)

When damping is **proportional**, each modal coordinate $q_i(t)$ satisfies:

$$\ddot{q}_i + 2\zeta_i \omega_i \dot{q}_i + \omega_i^2 q_i = \frac{Q_i(t)}{M_i}$$

where:
- $M_i = \{\phi\}_i^T [M] \{\phi\}_i$ — **modal mass**
- $K_i = \omega_i^2 M_i$ — **modal stiffness**
- $C_i = 2\zeta_i \omega_i M_i$ — **modal damping coefficient**
- $Q_i(t) = \{\phi\}_i^T \{F(t)\}$ — **modal (generalized) force**

The total physical response is:

$$\{x(t)\} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \{\phi\}_i \, q_i(t)$$

## 2.4 Interaction of Modes — When Does It Occur?

> [!CAUTION]
> **Modes interact when ANY of the following conditions are present:**
> 1. **Non-proportional damping:** $[C]$ cannot be diagonalized by $[\Phi]$, leading to off-diagonal terms in the modal damping matrix
> 2. **Closely spaced natural frequencies:** $\omega_i \approx \omega_j$ for some $i \neq j$ — energy transfer (beats) is significant
> 3. **Multi-harmonic excitation:** Forcing function has frequency content at multiple natural frequencies
> 4. **Non-linearities:** Stiffness non-linearity $k(x)$ or geometric non-linearity couples modes
> 5. **Time-varying or gyroscopic systems:** $[M]$, $[C]$, or $[K]$ depend on time or contain anti-symmetric (skew-symmetric) terms

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $([K] - \omega^2[M])\{\phi\} = \{0\}$ | Generalized eigenvalue problem for natural frequencies |
| 2 | $\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$ | Characteristic (frequency) equation |
| 3 | $\{\phi\}_i^T [M] \{\phi\}_j = 0 \; (i \neq j)$ | Mass orthogonality condition |
| 4 | $\{\phi\}_i^T [K] \{\phi\}_j = 0 \; (i \neq j)$ | Stiffness orthogonality condition |
| 5 | $\{x(t)\} = [\Phi]\{q(t)\}$ | Modal coordinate transformation |
| 6 | $\ddot{q}_i + 2\zeta_i \omega_i \dot{q}_i + \omega_i^2 q_i = Q_i / M_i$ | Decoupled modal equation (proportional damping) |
| 7 | $Q_i(t) = \{\phi\}_i^T \{F(t)\}$ | Generalized modal force |
| 8 | $M_i = \{\phi\}_i^T [M] \{\phi\}_i$ | Modal mass |
| 9 | $\omega_{beat} = \vert \omega_1 - \omega_2 \vert$ | Beat frequency for two closely spaced modes (rad/s) |
| 10 | $[C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K]$ | Rayleigh (proportional) damping form |
| 11 | $\zeta_i = \frac{\alpha}{2\omega_i} + \frac{\beta \omega_i}{2}$ | Modal damping ratio (Rayleigh) |
| 12 | $[C^*] = [\Phi]^T [C] [\Phi]$ | Modal damping matrix (diagonal iff proportional damping) |
| 13 | $MOF = \Delta f / f_n$ | Modal overlap factor (SEA criterion) |
| 14 | $f_i = \omega_i / (2\pi)$ | Conversion to Hz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Modal interaction analysis is the foundation of structural health monitoring (detecting cracks via frequency shifts), aerospace flutter analysis (mode coupling → catastrophic failure), seismic engineering (multi-mode building response), and MEMS resonator design.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Modal Orthogonality (Mass Matrix)

### Given:
For the $i$-th mode: $[K]\{\phi\}_i = \omega_i^2 [M]\{\phi\}_i$

### Step-by-step derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Pre-multiply the } i\text{-th equation by } \{\phi\}_j^T: \quad \{\phi\}_j^T [K]\{\phi\}_i = \omega_i^2 \, \{\phi\}_j^T [M]\{\phi\}_i \\
&\text{Since } [K] \text{ is symmetric: } [K]^T = [K], \\
&\text{then } \{\phi\}_j^T [K] = (\{\phi\}_i^T [K]\{\phi\}_j)^T = ([K]\{\phi\}_j)^T \\
&\text{But } [K]\{\phi\}_j = \omega_j^2 [M]\{\phi\}_j \text{ (by definition of } j\text{-th mode)}, \\
&\text{So: } \{\phi\}_j^T [K]\{\phi\}_i = \omega_j^2 \, \{\phi\}_j^T [M]\{\phi\}_i \\
&\text{Subtracting this from the original: } (\omega_i^2 - \omega_j^2) \{\phi\}_j^T [M]\{\phi\}_i = 0 \\
&\text{Since } \omega_i \neq \omega_j \text{ for } i \neq j: \quad \boxed{\{\phi\}_j^T [M]\{\phi\}_i = 0 \text{ for } i \neq j}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Explanation of logic:
- We exploit the **symmetry** of $[K]$ to "swap" indices $i$ and $j$.
- The pre-multiplication isolates the difference $(\omega_i^2 - \omega_j^2)$.
- Since distinct modes have distinct frequencies, the only solution is the orthogonality condition.
- The same proof yields stiffness orthogonality (since $\omega_i^2 \{\phi\}_j^T [M]\{\phi\}_i = \{\phi\}_j^T [K]\{\phi\}_i$).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Decoupling of Equations (Proportional Damping)

### Given:
$$[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [C]\{\dot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{F(t)\}$$
With $[C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K]$ (Rayleigh damping).

### Step-by-step derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Apply coordinate transformation: } \{x\} = [\Phi]\{q\}, \quad \{\dot{x}\} = [\Phi]\{\dot{q}\}, \quad \{\ddot{x}\} = [\Phi]\{\ddot{q}\} \\
&\text{Substitute into the equation of motion:} \\
&[M][\Phi]\{\ddot{q}\} + [C][\Phi]\{\dot{q}\} + [K][\Phi]\{q\} = \{F(t)\} \\
&\text{Pre-multiply by } [\Phi]^T: \\
&[\Phi]^T [M][\Phi]\{\ddot{q}\} + [\Phi]^T [C][\Phi]\{\dot{q}\} + [\Phi]^T [K][\Phi]\{q\} = [\Phi]^T \{F(t)\} \\
&\text{Now substitute } [C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K]: \\
&[\Phi]^T [C][\Phi] = \alpha \underbrace{[\Phi]^T [M][\Phi]}_{= [M^*]_{\text{diag}}} + \beta \underbrace{[\Phi]^T [K][\Phi]}_{= [K^*]_{\text{diag}}} \\
&\text{Because of orthogonality, } [\Phi]^T [M][\Phi] \text{ and } [\Phi]^T [K][\Phi] \text{ are both diagonal.} \\
&\text{Hence } [\Phi]^T [C][\Phi] \text{ is also diagonal.} \\
&\text{The result is } N \text{ independent SDOF equations:} \\
&\ddot{q}_i + 2\zeta_i \omega_i \dot{q}_i + \omega_i^2 q_i = \frac{Q_i(t)}{M_i^*} \\
&\text{where } M_i^* = \{\phi\}_i^T [M]\{\phi\}_i, \quad K_i^* = \omega_i^2 M_i^*, \quad C_i^* = \{\phi\}_i^T [C]\{\phi\}_i
\end{aligned}
$$

### Explanation of logic:
- The transformation "rotates" the coordinate system into modal space.
- Because all three matrices $[M], [K], [C]$ share the same eigenvectors under proportional damping, they all diagonalize simultaneously.
- This is why **proportional damping is the key condition** for decoupling.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Beat Frequency from Two Closely Spaced Modes

### Given:
Two SDOF equations with frequencies $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$, both excited simultaneously with initial conditions such that the response is a sum of two sinusoids of equal amplitude $A$.

### Step-by-step derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Total displacement: } x(t) = A\cos(\omega_1 t) + A\cos(\omega_2 t) \\
&\text{Apply the trigonometric identity: } \cos\alpha + \cos\beta = 2\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{2}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\alpha+\beta}{2}\right) \\
&\text{So: } x(t) = 2A\cos\!\left(\frac{\omega_1 - \omega_2}{2} t\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2} t\right) \\
&\text{Define average frequency: } \omega_{avg} = \frac{\omega_1 + \omega_2}{2} \\
&\text{Define beat frequency: } \omega_{beat} = \frac{\vert \omega_1 - \omega_2 \vert}{2} \quad \text{(half-difference in rad/s)} \\
&\text{Or in full-period terms: } \omega_{beat,\text{full}} = \vert \omega_1 - \omega_2 \vert \quad \text{(one beat per energy cycle)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Physical interpretation:
- The high-frequency carrier $\omega_{avg}$ is **modulated** by the low-frequency envelope $\omega_{beat}$.
- The amplitude oscillates between 0 and $2A$ periodically — this is the visual signature of modal interaction.
- **Engineering impact:** In structural systems (e.g., bridges, turbine blades), closely spaced modes cause fatigue at locations corresponding to where both modes have large mode shape values simultaneously.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Forced Response with Mode Superposition

### Given:
A 2-DOF system excited by harmonic force $F_1(t) = F_0 \sin(\Omega t)$ at coordinate 1 only.

### Step-by-step derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Modal forces: } Q_i(t) = \{\phi\}_i^T \{F(t)\} = \phi_{1i} F_0 \sin(\Omega t) \\
&\text{Steady-state modal response (assuming proportional damping):} \\
&q_i(t) = \frac{\phi_{1i} F_0 / M_i}{\omega_i^2 - \Omega^2 + j\, 2\zeta_i \omega_i \Omega} \sin(\Omega t) \\
&\text{or, in magnitude form: } \vert q_i \vert = \frac{\phi_{1i} F_0 / M_i}{\sqrt{(\omega_i^2 - \Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta_i \omega_i \Omega)^2}} \\
&\text{Total physical displacement at coordinate } k: \\
&x_k(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{2} \phi_{ki} \, q_i(t)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Resonance condition:
- When $\Omega \approx \omega_i$, mode $i$ dominates the response.
- If $\Omega$ is between $\omega_1$ and $\omega_2$ (with $\omega_1 < \Omega < \omega_2$), the two modal contributions have **opposite phases** — they partially **cancel**, reducing total amplitude.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This phase cancellation is the hallmark of mode interaction under harmonic forcing** — the response is NOT a simple sum of two independent resonances.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Modal Analysis Procedure

The following block diagram captures the complete workflow from physical MDOF system to decoupled modal analysis, including the decision branch that determines whether modes are coupled or uncoupled.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical MDOF System N-DOF] --> B[Assemble Matrices M, C, K]
    B --> C[Form Eigenvalue Problem K - omega squared M]
    C --> D[Solve for Natural Frequencies omega i]
    C --> E[Extract Mode Shapes phi i]
    D --> F[Construct Modal Matrix Phi]
    E --> F
    F --> G{Is Damping Proportional?}
    G -- Yes proportional --> H[Diagonalize M, C, K simultaneously]
    G -- No non proportional --> I[Modal Coupling Retained Off-diagonal terms in C star]
    H --> J[Decoupled SDOF Equations for each mode i]
    I --> K[State Space Formulation Complex Eigenvalues]
    J --> L[Solve each SDOF using Duhamel Integral]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Superpose Modal Contributions x of t equals sum phi i q i of t]
    M --> N[Final Physical Response]
    N --> O{Excitation Frequency close to omega i?}
    O -- Yes resonance --> P[Modal Dominance Single Mode Controls Response]
    O -- No far from resonance --> Q[Multi Mode Interaction Beats and Mode Mixing]
    P --> R[Engineering Interpretation]
    Q --> R
```

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram — Mode Interaction Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Mode1 [Mode 1 omega 1]
        M1[Mass m1]
        K1[Stiffness k1]
        C1[Damping c1]
    end
    subgraph Mode2 [Mode 2 omega 2]
        M2[Mass m2]
        K2[Stiffness k2]
        C2[Damping c2]
    end
    F[External Force F of t] -->|phi 1i| Mode1
    F -->|phi 2i| Mode2
    Mode1 -. energy transfer via non proportional damping or non linearity .-> Mode2
    Mode2 -. back coupling .-> Mode1
    Mode1 --> R1[Modal Response q1 of t]
    Mode2 --> R2[Modal Response q2 of t]
    R1 --> SUM[Superposition x of t]
    R2 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT[Physical Displacement]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — When Do Modes Interact?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[System Type] --> Q1{Is the system linear?}
    Q1 -- No --> NL[Strong Mode Coupling Non linear interactions]
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is damping proportional?}
    Q2 -- No --> NPC[Complex Modal Analysis Required]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Are natural frequencies well separated?}
    Q3 -- No closely spaced --> CS[Beating Phenomena Strong Interaction]
    Q3 -- Yes well separated --> DEC[Decoupled Modes Independent Analysis Valid]
    NL --> END[Engineering Action: Numerical Integration]
    NPC --> END
    CS --> END
    DEC --> END
```

## 4.4 Schematic of a 2-DOF Cantilever Beam Model (Simplified)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Wall Fixed End] -->|k1 spring| M1[Mass m1]
    M1 -->|k2 spring| M2[Mass m2]
    M1 -. c1 damper .-> W
    M2 -. c2 damper .-> M1
    F1[Force F1 at mass 1] --> M1
    F2[Force F2 at mass 2] --> M2
    X1[Displacement x1] --- M1
    X2[Displacement x2] --- M2
```

> [!NOTE]
> This 2-DOF lumped-parameter model is the canonical system used in KTU examinations to illustrate modal interaction. It has two fundamental modes (symmetric and anti-symmetric), and the response depends critically on whether the damping is proportional.
