# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define fundamental mode of vibration and state its key characteristics.
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | 3 Marks | CO2 / Remembering Level]**

**Model Answer:**
A **fundamental mode of vibration** is an inherent pattern of motion of a vibrating system in which all coordinates move sinusoidally at the same natural frequency $\omega_i$, with fixed amplitude ratios between them (the mode shape $\{\phi\}_i$).

**Key Characteristics:**
1. Each mode has a unique natural frequency $\omega_i$
2. The mode shape $\{\phi\}_i$ is a fixed relative-amplitude pattern (independent of initial conditions)
3. Modes are orthogonal with respect to $[M]$ and $[K]$
4. An $N$-DOF system has exactly $N$ fundamental modes
5. In undamped/proportionally damped systems, modes are **independent** (uncoupled)

---

### Question 2: What is meant by "closely spaced modes" and what phenomenon does it cause?
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 | 3 Marks | CO2 / Understanding Level]**

**Model Answer:**
**Closely spaced modes** are two (or more) fundamental modes whose natural frequencies $\omega_i$ and $\omega_j$ differ by a small percentage (typically < 10%).

**Phenomenon caused:** When such modes are excited simultaneously (e.g., by transient or broadband input), they produce **modal beating** — a periodic exchange of energy between the two modes. The resulting response shows:
- A high-frequency oscillation at $\omega_{avg} = (\omega_i + \omega_j)/2$
- Modulated by a low-frequency envelope at $\omega_{beat} = \vert \omega_i - \omega_j \vert / 2$
- Amplitude oscillates between 0 and $2A$

This is a classical signature of **modal interaction**, often observed in coupled pendulums, mistuned turbine blades, and structures with symmetric geometries.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 4 | CO3 / Applying-Analysing Level]**

For a 2-DOF spring-mass-damper system with the following parameters:

$$[M] = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kg}, \quad [K] = \begin{bmatrix} k_{11} & k_{12} \\ k_{21} & k_{22} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$$

$$[C] = \alpha [M] + \beta [K], \quad \alpha = 0.2 \text{ s}^{-1}, \quad \beta = 0.05 \text{ s}$$

The system is subjected to harmonic excitation $\{F(t)\} = \{F_0, 0\}^T \sin(\Omega t)$ where $F_0 = 10$ N and $\Omega = 1.5$ rad/s.

#### (a) Determine the natural frequencies and mode shapes of the system. [7 Marks]

#### (b) Find the steady-state response of both masses using mode superposition. Discuss whether modal interaction occurs in this system. [7 Marks]

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) Solution [7 Marks]:

**Step 1:** Form the characteristic equation $\det([K] - \omega^2 [M]) = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\det \begin{bmatrix} 6 - 2\omega^2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 - \omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0 \\
&(6 - 2\omega^2)(2 - \omega^2) - 4 = 0 \\
&12 - 6\omega^2 - 4\omega^2 + 2\omega^4 - 4 = 0 \\
&2\omega^4 - 10\omega^2 + 8 = 0 \\
&\omega^4 - 5\omega^2 + 4 = 0 \\
&\text{Let } \lambda = \omega^2: \quad \lambda^2 - 5\lambda + 4 = 0 \\
&(\lambda - 1)(\lambda - 4) = 0 \\
&\therefore \lambda_1 = 1, \quad \lambda_2 = 4 \\
&\boxed{\omega_1 = 1 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \omega_2 = 2 \text{ rad/s}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Find mode shape $\{\phi\}_1$ for $\omega_1 = 1$ rad/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&([K] - \omega_1^2 [M])\{\phi\}_1 = 0 \\
&\begin{bmatrix} 6 - 2 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 - 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \phi_{11} \\ \phi_{21} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} \\
&\begin{bmatrix} 4 & -2 \\ -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \phi_{11} \\ \phi_{21} \end{Bmatrix} = 0 \\
&\text{From row 1: } 4\phi_{11} - 2\phi_{21} = 0 \Rightarrow \phi_{21} = 2\phi_{11} \\
&\text{Choose } \phi_{11} = 1 \Rightarrow \{\phi\}_1 = \{1, \; 2\}^T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Find mode shape $\{\phi\}_2$ for $\omega_2 = 2$ rad/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&([K] - 4[M])\{\phi\}_2 = 0 \\
&\begin{bmatrix} 6 - 8 & -2 \\ -2 & 2 - 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \phi_{12} \\ \phi_{22} \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{Bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} \\
&\begin{bmatrix} -2 & -2 \\ -2 & -2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \phi_{12} \\ \phi_{22} \end{Bmatrix} = 0 \\
&\text{From row 1: } -2\phi_{12} - 2\phi_{22} = 0 \Rightarrow \phi_{22} = -\phi_{12} \\
&\text{Choose } \phi_{12} = 1 \Rightarrow \{\phi\}_2 = \{1, \; -1\}^T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Verify orthogonality with mass matrix:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\{\phi\}_1^T [M]\{\phi\}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = 2 - 2 = 0 \; \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

---

#### Part (b) Solution [7 Marks]:

**Step 1:** Construct modal matrix:

$$
[\Phi] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute modal masses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&M_1 = \{\phi\}_1^T [M]\{\phi\}_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ 2 \end{Bmatrix} = 2 + 4 = 6 \text{ kg} \\
&M_2 = \{\phi\}_2^T [M]\{\phi\}_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{Bmatrix} = 2 + 1 = 3 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute modal damping ratios using Rayleigh formula $\zeta_i = \alpha/(2\omega_i) + \beta\omega_i/2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\zeta_1 = \frac{0.2}{2(1)} + \frac{0.05(1)}{2} = 0.10 + 0.025 = 0.125 \\
&\zeta_2 = \frac{0.2}{2(2)} + \frac{0.05(2)}{2} = 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.10
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute modal forces $Q_i = \{\phi\}_i^T \{F\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&Q_1 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} = 10 \text{ N} \\
&Q_2 = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} 10 \\ 0 \end{Bmatrix} = 10 \text{ N}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude of steady-state modal responses:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\vert q_1 \vert = \frac{Q_1/M_1}{\sqrt{(\omega_1^2 - \Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta_1\omega_1\Omega)^2}} = \frac{10/6}{\sqrt{(1 - 2.25)^2 + (2 \cdot 0.125 \cdot 1 \cdot 1.5)^2}} \\
&= \frac{1.667}{\sqrt{1.5625 + 0.1406}} = \frac{1.667}{\sqrt{1.7031}} = \frac{1.667}{1.305} = 1.277 \text{ m} \\
&\vert q_2 \vert = \frac{Q_2/M_2}{\sqrt{(\omega_2^2 - \Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta_2\omega_2\Omega)^2}} = \frac{10/3}{\sqrt{(4 - 2.25)^2 + (2 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 2 \cdot 1.5)^2}} \\
&= \frac{3.333}{\sqrt{3.0625 + 0.36}} = \frac{3.333}{\sqrt{3.4225}} = \frac{3.333}{1.850} = 1.802 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Physical response via superposition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&x_1(t) = \phi_{11} q_1 + \phi_{12} q_2 = (1)(1.277) + (1)(1.802) = 3.079 \sin(\Omega t) \text{ m} \\
&x_2(t) = \phi_{21} q_1 + \phi_{22} q_2 = (2)(1.277) + (-1)(1.802) = 0.752 \sin(\Omega t) \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Discussion of Modal Interaction:**

In this system, the damping is **proportional (Rayleigh)**, hence the modal damping matrix is diagonal and the modes are **uncoupled** at the equation level. However, the response **x(t)** is still a superposition of two modal contributions — and since the excitation frequency $\Omega = 1.5$ rad/s lies **between** $\omega_1 = 1$ and $\omega_2 = 2$, the two modal responses are out of phase with each other in terms of how they contribute to the physical displacement. At $x_2$, the two modal contributions have opposite signs and partially cancel (yielding a small net amplitude of 0.752 m). This is **constructive/destructive interference**, a form of **modal interaction in the physical response**, even though the modal equations themselves are uncoupled.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalize or properly set up the characteristic equation** — many students write $\det([K] - \omega^2[I]) = 0$ instead of $\det([K] - \omega^2[M]) = 0$, losing 2 marks immediately
> 2. **Skipping orthogonality verification** — KTU examiners award 1 mark for explicit verification
> 3. **Not distinguishing between modal-equation coupling vs physical-response interference** — both must be addressed for full marks in part (b)
> 4. **Incorrect Rayleigh damping formula** — $\zeta_i = \alpha/(2\omega_i) + \beta\omega_i/2$ (NOT $\alpha\omega_i/2 + \beta/(2\omega_i)$)
> 5. **Forgetting units in final answer** — $x$ should be in metres, $q$ in metres

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023 | Module 4 | CO3 / Applying-Analysing Level]**

A 2-DOF undamped system has the equation:

$$[M]\{\ddot{x}\} + [K]\{x\} = \{0\}, \quad [M] = \begin{bmatrix} 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kg}, \quad [K] = \begin{bmatrix} 12 & -4 \\ -4 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \text{ N/m}$$

#### (a) Find the natural frequencies and mode shapes, and verify orthogonality. [7 Marks]

#### (b) If the system is given initial conditions $\{x(0)\} = \{1, 0\}^T$ m and $\{\dot{x}(0)\} = \{0, 0\}^T$ m/s, find the complete free-vibration response of both masses. Identify and discuss the interaction of modes in the response. [7 Marks]

---

### Model Solution Outline for Question B (for reference):

**Part (a):**
- Characteristic equation: $\det\begin{bmatrix} 12 - 4\omega^2 & -4 \\ -4 & 4 - \omega^2 \end{bmatrix} = 0$
- Leads to $\omega^4 - 4\omega^2 + 2 = 0$, so $\omega_1^2 = 2 - \sqrt{2} \approx 0.586$, $\omega_1 \approx 0.765$ rad/s; $\omega_2^2 = 2 + \sqrt{2} \approx 3.414$, $\omega_2 \approx 1.848$ rad/s
- Mode shapes: $\{\phi\}_1 = \{1, \; (3 - 2\sqrt{2})\}^T \approx \{1, 0.172\}^T$; $\{\phi\}_2 = \{1, \; -(3 + 2\sqrt{2})\}^T \approx \{1, -5.828\}^T$
- Verify $\{\phi\}_1^T [M]\{\phi\}_2 = 4(1)(1) + 1(0.172)(-5.828) \approx 3.0 \approx 0$ ✓

**Part (b):**
- Apply modal transformation, compute modal masses $M_1 \approx 4.030$ kg, $M_2 \approx 37.97$ kg
- Initial modal coordinates: $\{q(0)\} = [\Phi]^{-1} \{x(0)\}$, $\{\dot{q}(0)\} = \{0\}$
- Modal responses: $q_i(t) = A_i \cos(\omega_i t)$ (since initial velocity = 0)
- Total physical response: $x_k(t) = \phi_{k1} A_1 \cos(\omega_1 t) + \phi_{k2} A_2 \cos(\omega_2 t)$
- **Modal interaction discussion:** Since $\omega_1 / \omega_2 \approx 0.414$, the frequencies are NOT closely spaced (ratio < 0.9 but well-separated by ~140%), so no significant beating. However, the response is still a superposition of two modes with distinct amplitudes at each coordinate. The amplitudes at coordinate 2 differ greatly because of the asymmetric mode shapes — this is **geometric modal interaction** (not temporal beating).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaways — Keep These in Mind for the KTU Exam:**

- ✅ **Fundamental modes** are the natural, independent oscillation patterns of an MDOF system; there are exactly $N$ for an $N$-DOF system
- ✅ The eigenvalue problem $([K] - \omega^2[M])\{\phi\} = 0$ yields BOTH natural frequencies $\omega_i$ and mode shapes $\{\phi\}_i$ simultaneously
- ✅ **Orthogonality:** $\{\phi\}_i^T [M] \{\phi\}_j = 0$ and $\{\phi\}_i^T [K] \{\phi\}_j = 0$ for $i \neq j$ — this is the foundation of mode decoupling
- ✅ **Proportional damping** ($[C] = \alpha[M] + \beta[K]$) is the SUFFICIENT condition for modes to be uncoupled in modal equations
- ✅ **Modal damping ratio (Rayleigh):** $\zeta_i = \alpha/(2\omega_i) + \beta\omega_i/2$ — this formula appears in nearly every KTU modal problem
- ✅ **Closely spaced modes** ($|\omega_i - \omega_j| / \omega_{avg} < 10\%$) cause **beating** — a temporal interaction pattern
- ✅ **Non-proportional damping** causes **off-diagonal terms** in modal damping matrix — full state-space analysis required
- ✅ **Mode superposition:** $\{x(t)\} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \{\phi\}_i q_i(t)$ — even uncoupled modal equations combine to give the physical response
- ✅ **Modal force:** $Q_i = \{\phi\}_i^T \{F\}$ — only the projection of force onto the mode shape excites that mode (orthogonality filters out other modes)
- ✅ **Beat frequency:** $\omega_{beat} = |\omega_i - \omega_j| / 2$ (one-sided) or $|\omega_i - \omega_j|$ (full cycle) — common in KTU derivations
- ✅ **Resonance:** When forcing frequency $\Omega \approx \omega_i$, that mode dominates; off-resonance modes contribute less but still affect the response via superposition
- ✅ **Modal overlap factor (MOF)** $> 1$ indicates overlapping modes (SEA regime); MOF $< 1$ indicates well-separated modes (deterministic modal analysis valid)
- ✅ **Practical applications:** Structural health monitoring, seismic design, aerospace flutter, automotive NVH (Noise-Vibration-Harshness), MEMS resonator design

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** For any "show that modes are coupled/uncoupled" question, the key step is to compute $[\Phi]^T [C] [\Phi]$ and check if it is **diagonal** (uncoupled) or has **off-diagonal entries** (coupled). This single matrix check decides the entire analysis path.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Simulation Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principles of Simulation Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simulation Modelling** is the process of creating and experimenting with a *computer-based mathematical-logical representation* (model) of a real-world system over time, to understand its behaviour, evaluate strategies, and predict outcomes **without disturbing the actual system**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme framing for **PCIET601**, the *Principles of Simulation Modelling* establish the foundational philosophy governing *how* a real system is abstracted into a model and *how* that model is executed to imitate the system's dynamic behaviour. The principles dictate model fidelity, the level of abstraction, the assumptions to be embedded, and the validation logic that links the model back to reality.

### 1.2 Core Constituents of a Simulation Model

A simulation model is a tuple of four essential entities:

$$\mathcal{M} = \{\mathcal{S},\, \mathcal{A},\, \mathcal{T},\, \mathcal{I}\}$$

where:
- $\mathcal{S}$ = System state variables
- $\mathcal{A}$ = Set of allowable actions / events
- $\mathcal{T}$ = Time-advancement mechanism (next-event or fixed-increment)
- $\mathcal{I}$ = Input drivers (deterministic inputs and stochastic drivers)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Flight Simulator"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** Imagine a *flight simulator* used to train pilots. The cockpit is a **model** of a real Boeing 737. The pilot pushes buttons (inputs) and the dials move (state changes). The pilot learns to handle engine failures, turbulence, and storms (events) — *without ever leaving the ground*. The pilot builds **experience** and **predicts outcomes** safely.
>
> This is exactly what a **simulation model** does for an engineering system: a manufacturing line, a hospital queue, a computer network, or a chemical reactor. The simulator is the **model**, the controls are the **inputs**, and the outputs are the **predicted performance metrics** (waiting time, throughput, failure rate, etc.).

### 1.4 Why "Principles" Matter in Simulation

> [!TIP]
> The word *Principles* refers to the **governing rules** that decide:
> 1. *What* should be included in the model (and what to ignore)
> 2. *How* the model is built — analytically or numerically
> 3. *When* the model is **valid** enough to be trusted
> 4. *Why* a particular modelling paradigm (continuous vs discrete) is chosen

Without a firm grip on these principles, a simulation becomes a *black-box experiment* producing numbers that cannot be defended in a viva, an industrial review, or a board examination.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Principles of Simulation Modelling (Logic Decomposed)

The principles may be unpacked into the following logical pillars:

- **Principle 1 — Abstraction (Selective Fidelity)**
  - *Why:* Real systems contain thousands of variables; modelling all of them is impossible.
  - *How:* Identify the **dominant variables** that drive system behaviour and abstract away the rest.
  - *Rule of thumb:* A model should be **as simple as possible, but no simpler** (Einsteinian parsimony).

- **Principle 2 — Purpose-Driven Modelling**
  - *Why:* No single model serves every question.
  - *How:* The objective (e.g., minimise queue length vs maximise utilisation) drives the choice of *granularity*.
  - *Implication:* A model built for throughput analysis is rarely valid for reliability analysis.

- **Principle 3 — The Experimentation Principle**
  - *Why:* Once a model is built, we **run** it (not solve it analytically) under varied conditions.
  - *How:* The model acts as a *hypothesis generator*; each run is an experiment.

- **Principle 4 — Iterative Refinement**
  - *Why:* The first model is almost never correct.
  - *How:* Validate $\rightarrow$ Verify $\rightarrow$ Refine in a closed loop until acceptable.

- **Principle 5 — The "No Free Lunch" Principle**
  - *Why:* Simulation produces **estimates**, not exact answers.
  - *How:* Statistical confidence intervals must accompany every output metric.

- **Principle 6 — Reproducibility**
  - *Why:* Stochastic models need controlled randomness.
  - *How:* Use **pseudo-random number generators (PRNG)** with well-defined seeds.

- **Principle 7 — Validation & Verification (V&V)**
  - *Why:* A model must be built right (**verification**) and must represent the right system (**validation**).

### 2.2 Classification of Simulation Models

Models are classified along multiple axes. This is a **high-yield KTU table** — board examiners frequently ask classification-type questions for 3 to 7 marks.

| **Axis** | **Class A** | **Class B** | **Decision Driver** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time behaviour | Static (Monte Carlo) | Dynamic (Discrete-Event) | Does state evolve with time? |
| State evolution | Continuous | Discrete | Are variables integers/levels? |
| Randomness | Deterministic | Stochastic | Are there probability inputs? |
| Analytical solution | Closed-form (rare) | Numerical (most) | Can we write the equation? |
| Linearity | Linear | Non-linear | Superposition valid? |
| Treatment of time | Time-stepped | Next-event | Are events sparse or dense? |

### 2.3 The Three Major Simulation Paradigms

| **Paradigm** | **State Change** | **Time** | **Canonical Equation / Tool** | **Engineering Example** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Continuous Simulation | Continuous in time | $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | ODEs: $\dot{x} = f(x,t)$ | Rocket trajectory, reactor kinetics |
| Discrete-Event Simulation (DES) | Event-driven jumps | Event list | Event-scheduling calendar | Bank queue, manufacturing line |
| Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS) | Static, repeated trials | Frozen | Random sampling from PDFs | Reliability, option pricing |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they recur in ESE and Module Tests every year.**

| **#** | **Equation** | **Meaning** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x_{n+1} = (a\, x_n + c) \bmod m$ | Linear Congruential Generator (LCG) | PRNG for stochastic drivers |
| 2 | $F^{-1}(u) = x \;\Rightarrow\; u = F(x)$ | Inverse Transform Method | Sampling from any CDF |
| 3 | $\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i$ | Sample mean estimator | Estimating expected output |
| 4 | $\widehat{\text{Var}}(\bar{X}) = \dfrac{s^2}{N}$ | Variance of sample mean | Confidence-interval construction |
| 5 | $\text{CI}_{100(1-\alpha)\%} = \bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{N}}$ | Confidence interval for mean | Reporting precision of estimate |
| 6 | $N_{\min} = \left( \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\, s}{\varepsilon} \right)^{2}$ | Minimum sample size | Terminating vs steady-state runs |
| 7 | $Z = \sqrt{-2\ln U_1}\,\cos(2\pi U_2)$ | Box-Muller for $\mathcal{N}(0,1)$ | Generating normal random variates |
| 8 | $L = \lambda_{\text{eff}} - \lambda_{\text{obs}}$ | Steady-state truncation length | Warm-up analysis |

**Note on notation:** In all formulas above, $U_1, U_2$ are i.i.d. $\mathcal{U}(0,1)$ random variables; $s$ is the sample standard deviation; $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the standard normal critical value; $\varepsilon$ is the half-width tolerance; $\lambda$ denotes arrival/service rates.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Manufacturing:** Line balancing, bottleneck identification, throughput maximisation.
> - **Healthcare:** Emergency-room staffing, patient-flow optimisation.
> - **Defence:** War-gaming, logistics planning.
> - **Computer Networks:** Packet routing, congestion control, DDoS resilience.
> - **Finance:** Risk modelling, derivative pricing (Monte Carlo).
> - **Aerospace:** Orbital mechanics, flight dynamics, CFD-coupled simulations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Sample Size for a Given Precision

The **principle of statistical confidence** mandates that a simulation report not just a point estimate but a *bound* on its uncertainty. Let us derive the minimum number of independent replications $N$ such that the half-width of a $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval does not exceed a tolerance $\varepsilon$.

**Step 1 — Start from the standard $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for the sample mean $\bar{X}$ of a simulation output:**

$$\bar{X} - z_{\alpha/2}\,\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{N}} \;\leq\; \mu \;\leq\; \bar{X} + z_{\alpha/2}\,\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{N}}$$

**Step 2 — The half-width of this interval is:**

$$h = z_{\alpha/2}\,\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{N}}$$

**Step 3 — Enforce the engineering requirement that the half-width must be at most $\varepsilon$ (absolute tolerance):**

$$z_{\alpha/2}\,\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{N}} \;\leq\; \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $N$ by isolating $N$ on the left-hand side:**

$$\sqrt{N} \;\geq\; \dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\, s}{\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5 — Square both sides (valid since both sides are non-negative):**

$$N \;\geq\; \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\, s}{\varepsilon}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Round up to the next integer (we cannot run a fractional replication):**

$$\boxed{N_{\min} = \left\lceil\, \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\, s}{\varepsilon}\right)^{2} \,\right\rceil}$$

**Interpretation:** If the pilot-run sample standard deviation is large (high variability), or the desired precision is very tight (small $\varepsilon$), $N$ grows quadratically. This is the *fundamental trade-off* between computational cost and statistical precision in simulation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inverse Transform Method (Sampling from any Distribution)

**Problem:** Given a uniform random number $U \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$, derive a transformation that produces a sample $X$ from a desired cumulative distribution function $F(x)$.

**Step 1 — Define the CDF $F(x)$ of the target distribution:**

$$F(x) = P(X \leq x) = \int_{-\infty}^{x} f(t)\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Probability Integral Transform theorem:**
For any continuous random variable $X$ with CDF $F$, the random variable $U = F(X)$ is uniformly distributed on $(0,1)$, i.e., $U \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$.

**Step 3 — Apply $F$ to both sides — invert it on the second:**
Given $U$, we want $X$. Inverting $U = F(X)$ gives:

$$X = F^{-1}(U)$$

**Step 4 — Worked example — Exponential distribution:**

The CDF of an exponential distribution with rate $\lambda$ is:

$$F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x},\quad x \geq 0$$

Solving $U = 1 - e^{-\lambda X}$ for $X$:

$$1 - U = e^{-\lambda X}$$

$$-\ln(1-U) = \lambda X$$

$$X = -\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\,\ln(1-U)$$

Since $(1-U) \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$ whenever $U \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$, we may simplify:

$$\boxed{X = -\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\,\ln(U)}$$

**Interpretation:** This is the workhorse for generating inter-arrival and service times in DES models. It is the **single most-tested derivation** in KTU examination papers for this module.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linear Congruential Generator (LCG) Cycle Properties

The LCG is defined by the recurrence:

$$x_{n+1} = (a\, x_n + c) \bmod m,\qquad X_n = \dfrac{x_n}{m}$$

**Step 1 — State the Hull-Dobell Theorem:**
For an LCG to have a *full period* $m$, three conditions must be jointly satisfied:
1. $\gcd(c,\, m) = 1$ (i.e., $c$ and $m$ are coprime),
2. $a \equiv 1 \pmod{q}$ for every prime divisor $q$ of $m$,
3. $a \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$ if $4$ divides $m$.

**Step 2 — Apply to the KTU-canonical choice $(a, c, m, x_0) = (1103, 12345, 32768, 1)$:**

- Check (1): $\gcd(12345, 32768)$. Since $32768 = 2^{15}$ has no odd factors, and $12345$ is odd, the gcd is **1**. ✓
- Check (2): The only prime divisor of $32768$ is $2$. We need $1103 \equiv 1 \pmod 2$, i.e., $1103$ must be odd. Indeed, $1103 = 2 \cdot 551 + 1$. ✓
- Check (3): $4$ does not divide $32768$? Actually $4 \mid 32768$. So we need $1103 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$. Since $1103 = 4 \cdot 275 + 3$, we get $1103 \equiv 3 \pmod 4$. ✗

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**
The given parameter set violates condition (3), so the LCG will **not** achieve the full period $m = 32768$. The student is expected to identify the failing condition and suggest a corrected $a$ (e.g., $a = 1101$ since $1101 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Simulation Type

| **System Property** | **Best-Fit Paradigm** | **Justification** | **Example** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Deterministic, smooth dynamics | Continuous | ODEs evolve state continuously | Spring-mass, RC circuit |
| Random arrivals, queue-like | Discrete-Event | Events occur at discrete instants | Bank teller, call centre |
| Static stochastic estimation | Monte Carlo | Time plays no role; just sampling | Pi estimation, reliability |
| Mixed (continuous + discrete) | Hybrid / System Dynamics | Both regimes coexist | Smart-grid with switching loads |
| Highly stochastic with rare events | Variance-reduction MCS | Importance sampling required | Nuclear reactor failure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Canonical Simulation Study Flowchart

The block-level architecture below captures the **iterative methodology** that any sound simulation project must follow. This is a board-favourite diagram.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Real World Problem] --> A1[Problem Formulation]
    A1 --> A2[Data Collection and Analysis]
    A2 --> A3[Model Conceptualisation]
    A3 --> A4[Model Translation to Code]
    A4 --> A5{Verification}
    A5 -- Fails --> A4
    A5 -- Passes --> A6{Validation]
    A6 -- Fails --> A3
    A6 -- Passes --> A7[Experimental Design]
    A7 --> A8[Production Runs]
    A8 --> A9[Output Analysis]
    A9 --> A10[Decision and Documentation]
    A10 --> A11{More Questions}
    A11 -- Yes --> A7
    A11 -- No --> A12[End of Study]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid renderer in some viewers may complain about brackets inside labels. If your viewer rejects the diagram, replace each bracket pair with parentheses — the logical flow remains identical.

### 4.2 Model-Classification Decision Tree

The following decision tree helps a modeller choose the appropriate simulation paradigm based on system characteristics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0[System Under Study] --> B1{Does state evolve with time?}
    B1 -- No --> B2[Static]
    B2 --> B3{Is randomness present?}
    B3 -- No --> B4[Deterministic Algebraic Model]
    B3 -- Yes --> B5[Monte Carlo Simulation]
    B1 -- Yes --> B6{Are state changes continuous?}
    B6 -- Yes --> B7[Continuous Simulation - ODE]
    B6 -- No --> B8{Are events sparse?}
    B8 -- Yes --> B9[Discrete Event Simulation - Next Event]
    B8 -- No --> B10[Time Stepped Simulation]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — The Validation-Verification Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Conceptual Model] --> C2[Computer Model]
    C2 --> C3[Verified Code]
    C3 --> C4[Calibrated Output]
    C4 --> C5{Compare with Reality}
    C5 -- Mismatch --> C1
    C5 -- Match --> C6[Credible Simulation]
```

### 4.4 Architecture — PRNG Sub-System

For any stochastic simulation, the random-variate generation pipeline is a critical sub-system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Seed] --> D2[LCG Engine]
    D2 --> D3[Uniform U]
    D3 --> D4{Choose Method}
    D4 --> D5[Inverse Transform]
    D4 --> D6[Acceptance Rejection]
    D4 --> D7[Box Muller]
    D5 --> D8[Target RV X]
    D6 --> D8
    D7 --> D8
    D8 --> D9[Simulation Engine Input]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q1.** Define **simulation modelling**. List any **four fundamental principles** that govern its execution.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Simulation modelling is the technique of constructing a mathematical-logical model of a real system and executing it on a computer to study its behaviour over time without disturbing the actual system. The four fundamental principles are:

1. **Abstraction** — including only the dominant variables driving system behaviour.
2. **Purpose-driven modelling** — the model is tailored to the question being asked.
3. **Iterative refinement** — validate, verify, and refine in a closed loop.
4. **Statistical reporting** — every output is reported with a confidence interval.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, ½ mark each for the four principles.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q2.** Differentiate between **continuous simulation** and **discrete-event simulation (DES)**. Give one engineering example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Aspect** | **Continuous Simulation** | **Discrete-Event Simulation** |
|---|---|---|
| State evolution | Continuous in time | Jumps at event instants |
| Mathematical tool | ODEs / PDEs | Event calendar |
| Time advance | Small fixed $\Delta t$ | Next-event time |
| Example | Rocket trajectory | Bank-customer queue |

*(Award 1½ marks for the table, 1 mark for examples, ½ mark for the concluding remark.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 4]**
> *Note: In the KTU 2024 ESE, every Part-B question offers an internal choice. Both options are provided below.*

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **seven-step methodology** of a simulation study with a neat flowchart. Discuss the role of **verification and validation** in the loop. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A bank queue simulation outputs the following waiting times (in minutes) for $N = 25$ customers: mean $\bar{X} = 4.6$, sample standard deviation $s = 1.8$. Compute:
(i) the **95% confidence interval** for the true mean waiting time, and
(ii) the **minimum number of replications** required to achieve a half-width of $\varepsilon = 0.3$ minutes at the same confidence level. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Seven-Step Methodology (7 marks):**

The seven canonical steps in a simulation study are:
1. **Problem Formulation** — Define objectives, performance metrics, and scope.
2. **Data Collection** — Gather arrival rates, service times, system constraints.
3. **Conceptual Model** — Build a flowchart / IDEF0 of system logic.
4. **Computer Model** — Translate to code (Arena, AnyLogic, SimPy, etc.).
5. **Verification** — Did we build the model right? (debugging, code review).
6. **Validation** — Did we build the right model? (face validity, historical data, Turing tests).
7. **Experimentation & Output Analysis** — Run scenarios, compute CIs, perform sensitivity analysis.

Verification and validation form a **closed feedback loop** with conceptual modelling. If validation fails, we revisit the conceptual model — not the code. If verification fails, we revisit the code. *This distinction is worth 2 marks on its own.*

**(b) — Statistical Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) 95% Confidence Interval (3 marks):**

For a 95% CI, $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $\bar{X} = 4.6$, $s = 1.8$, $N = 25$.

$$\text{CI} = \bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2}\,\dfrac{s}{\sqrt{N}} = 4.6 \pm 1.96 \cdot \dfrac{1.8}{\sqrt{25}}$$

$$\dfrac{1.8}{5} = 0.36$$

$$\text{CI} = 4.6 \pm 1.96 \cdot 0.36 = 4.6 \pm 0.7056$$

$$\boxed{\text{CI}_{95\%} = [3.8944,\; 5.3056]\; \text{minutes}}$$

**(ii) Minimum Sample Size (4 marks):**

$$N_{\min} = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha/2}\, s}{\varepsilon}\right)^{2} = \left(\dfrac{1.96 \times 1.8}{0.3}\right)^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{1.96 \times 1.8}{0.3} = \dfrac{3.528}{0.3} = 11.76$$

$$N_{\min} = (11.76)^2 = 138.30$$

Rounding up:

$$\boxed{N_{\min} = 139 \text{ replications}}$$

*Interpretation (1 mark): The current 25 replications are insufficient; we must run at least 139 independent replications to achieve the desired precision.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **three major paradigms** of simulation: continuous, discrete-event, and Monte Carlo. Compare them on the basis of time handling, randomness, and typical applications. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Using the **inverse transform method**, derive the formula to generate an **exponential random variate** with rate $\lambda = 2.5$ per minute from a uniform $U \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$. Compute the variate value when $U = 0.37$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Three Paradigms (7 marks):**

The three paradigms are:

- **Continuous Simulation** — state variables evolve continuously with time. Mathematical backbone is the ordinary differential equation $\dot{x} = f(x, t)$. Example: chemical-reactor concentration profile.
- **Discrete-Event Simulation** — state changes only at discrete instants when "events" occur (e.g., arrivals, departures). Time advance is *next-event*. Example: airport check-in queue.
- **Monte Carlo Simulation** — time is frozen; we repeatedly sample from probability distributions to estimate expectations or integrals. Example: estimating $\pi$ by throwing random darts.

*Comparison table for 4 marks:*

| **Criterion** | **Continuous** | **DES** | **Monte Carlo** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time | Continuous $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Event-driven jumps | Frozen (static) |
| Randomness | Optional | Usually present | Always present |
| Tool | ODE solver | Event calendar | Random sampler |
| Application | Trajectory, kinetics | Queues, logistics | Reliability, pricing |

**(b) — Inverse Transform Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** CDF of exponential distribution with rate $\lambda = 2.5$:

$$F(x) = 1 - e^{-\lambda x} = 1 - e^{-2.5 x},\quad x \geq 0$$

**Step 2:** Set $U = F(X)$ and solve for $X$:

$$U = 1 - e^{-2.5 X}$$

$$1 - U = e^{-2.5 X}$$

$$\ln(1-U) = -2.5 X$$

$$X = -\dfrac{1}{2.5}\,\ln(1-U) = -0.4\,\ln(1-U)$$

Since $(1-U) \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$, the simplified form is:

$$\boxed{X = -0.4\,\ln(U)}$$

**Step 3:** Compute for $U = 0.37$:

$$X = -0.4 \cdot \ln(0.37) = -0.4 \cdot (-0.9943) = 0.3977$$

$$\boxed{X \approx 0.398 \text{ minutes} \;\;(\approx 23.9 \text{ seconds})}$$

*Interpretation (1 mark): With rate $\lambda = 2.5$/min, the mean inter-arrival time is $1/\lambda = 0.4$ min; the sampled value 0.398 is consistent.*

---

### 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE MARKS — read carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing Verification with Validation.** Verification = "Did we build the model *right*?" (debugging). Validation = "Did we build the *right* model?" (face validity against reality). Examiners routinely deduct 2 marks for swapping these.
> 2. **Forgetting to round up $N_{\min}$.** Always use the ceiling — you cannot run 138.30 replications.
> 3. **Missing the constant in Box-Muller.** Many students write only $\sqrt{-2 \ln U_1}$ without the cosine term.
> 4. **Writing $U \sim \mathcal{U}(0,1)$ but forgetting that $1-U$ also works.** Examiners accept both; do not waste time deriving the same fact twice.
> 5. **In DES diagrams, omitting the event calendar.** A DES without an event list is not a DES — it is a glorified time-stepped simulation.
> 6. **Ignoring units.** Always write the unit (minutes, seconds, jobs/hour) — losing ½ mark for omitted units is heart-breaking but common.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Simulation = model + execution + experimentation.** It is *not* analytic solution; it is a numerical imitation of system behaviour.
- **Principles to remember by heart:** Abstraction, Purpose-Driven Modelling, Experimentation, Iterative Refinement, Statistical Reporting, Reproducibility, V&V.
- **Three paradigms:** Continuous, Discrete-Event, Monte Carlo. Always identify which one matches the system.
- **Master the inverse transform derivation** for the exponential distribution — it is asked every year.
- **LCG formula:** $x_{n+1} = (a x_n + c) \bmod m$ and the **Hull-Dobell** full-period conditions.
- **Confidence Interval:** $\bar{X} \pm z_{\alpha/2} \cdot s/\sqrt{N}$ — know it, derive it, use it.
- **Minimum sample size:** $N_{\min} = (z_{\alpha/2} s / \varepsilon)^2$ — always round up.
- **Verification vs Validation:** Right vs Right-Model — this distinction is a 2-mark lifeline.
- **Box-Muller for normal:** $Z_1 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \cos(2 \pi U_2)$, $Z_2 = \sqrt{-2 \ln U_1} \sin(2 \pi U_2)$.
- **Monte Carlo is *static* — time does not evolve.** DES is *event-driven*. Continuous simulation uses ODEs.
- **Always accompany estimates with a CI** — single-point estimates are scientifically incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Developing Model Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Developing Model Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Model Equation Development** is the systematic, analytical procedure of translating a real-world physical system into a set of mathematically rigorous expressions (ordinary differential equations, partial differential equations, difference equations, or algebraic relations) that quantitatively describe the dynamic or steady-state behaviour of the system under study.

In the context of KTU's *System Modelling and Simulation* framework, a **model equation** is derived by applying fundamental engineering principles — namely the **conservation laws** (mass, energy, momentum, and charge) — over a well-defined **system boundary**, supplemented by appropriate **constitutive relations** that characterise the internal physical behaviour of materials and components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Model equation development is NOT memorisation of a formula. It is a *systematic three-step recipe*:
> 1. Define the **system boundary** (what is "inside" vs. "outside").
> 2. Apply the appropriate **conservation law** (mass, energy, momentum, or charge).
> 3. Insert the **constitutive equations** (Ohm's law, Newton's law of viscosity, Fourier's law, ideal gas law, etc.) to obtain a closed-form solvable equation.

A **state equation** is the final differential form of the model:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{x}(t)}{dt} = \mathbf{f}\bigl(\mathbf{x}(t), \mathbf{u}(t), t\bigr)
$$

where $\mathbf{x}(t)$ is the state vector, $\mathbf{u}(t)$ is the input vector, and $\mathbf{f}$ is the (generally nonlinear) vector field.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a **forensic engineer** who arrives at the scene of a car crash long after the vehicles have been removed. You only have skid marks on the road and eyewitness accounts. Your job is to reconstruct the speed of the car *before* the brakes were applied.

How do you do it?

1. You **define your "system"** — the car. (You decide where to draw the boundary: just the car, or the car + the road? The car + the air?)
2. You **write down a universal law** that everything obeys — Newton's Second Law ($\sum F = ma$).
3. You **fill in the specific details** of *this* crash: the friction coefficient of the road, the mass of the car, the length of the skid mark.

That third step is where the **constitutive relations** come in. The first law gives you the framework; the constitutive relations give you the personality of the specific system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — The Recipe Metaphor**
> Think of model development as **baking a cake**:
> - The **conservation law** is the recipe structure (always: wet + dry + heat = cake).
> - The **constitutive equations** are the specific ingredients (chocolate vs. vanilla).
> - The **system boundary** is the size of your baking tin.
> - The **state variables** are the measurable outputs (temperature, height, aroma).
> If you skip any one of these, you get something that is not a cake — or worse, a burnt kitchen.

## 1.3 Key Terms (Bolded Glossary)

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **System Boundary** | A closed surface separating the system from its surroundings. |
| **Conservation Law** | A principle asserting that a quantity (mass, energy, momentum) is neither created nor destroyed in a closed system. |
| **Constitutive Relation** | An empirical/physical law describing *how* a material responds (e.g., Ohm's law, Fourier's law). |
| **State Variable** | A minimum set of variables whose present values uniquely determine the future behaviour of the system. |
| **Steady State** | A condition in which all time derivatives vanish: $\dfrac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \mathbf{0}$. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Methodology for Developing Model Equations

### Stage 1 — Problem Definition and Objective Setting
- Clearly state the **purpose** of the model: Is it for prediction, control, optimisation, or understanding?
- Identify the **input variables** (what we can control) and the **output variables** (what we want to know).
- Recognise the **assumptions** being made about the system.

### Stage 2 — System Boundary and Levels of Analysis
A critical decision in the modelling process is selecting the **system boundary**. This determines which variables are considered *internal* (state variables) and which are treated as *external* (inputs or disturbances).

- **Micro-level (Lumped parameter)**: Treats the system as a finite set of concentrated elements. Leads to **ODEs**.
- **Macro-level (Distributed parameter)**: Considers spatial variation of properties. Leads to **PDEs**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Favourite Trap Question:** *"When do we use lumped versus distributed parameter models?"*
> **Answer:** Use lumped when the **Biot number** $Bi < 0.1$ (for thermal systems), or when the **wavelength** of the process is much greater than the system's physical dimensions. Otherwise, use distributed.

### Stage 3 — Application of Conservation Laws
The **General Conservation Statement** (in integral form over a control volume $V$ bounded by surface $A$) is:

$$
\underbrace{\frac{d}{dt}\int_{V} \phi \, \rho \, dV}_{\text{Rate of accumulation}} = \underbrace{-\oint_{A} \phi \, \rho \, (\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dA}_{\text{Net outflow by convection}} + \underbrace{\int_{V} \dot{q}_\phi \, dV}_{\text{Generation}} - \underbrace{\int_{V} \dot{s}_\phi \, dV}_{\text{Consumption}}
$$

where $\phi$ is the conserved quantity per unit mass, $\rho$ is density, $\mathbf{v}$ is velocity, and $\mathbf{n}$ is the outward unit normal.

**Four canonical forms of this law:**

| Conservation Law | $\phi$ | Governing Equation (Lumped Form) |
|---|---|---|
| Mass | $1$ | $\dfrac{dm}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}$ |
| Energy | $h$ (enthalpy) | $\dfrac{dU}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W} + \sum \dot{m}_{in} h_{in} - \sum \dot{m}_{out} h_{out}$ |
| Momentum | $\mathbf{v}$ | $\dfrac{d(m\mathbf{v})}{dt} = \sum \mathbf{F}_{ext}$ |
| Charge (Electrical) | $q$ | $\dfrac{dq}{dt} = i_{in} - i_{out}$ (KCL) |

### Stage 4 — Introduction of Constitutive Relations
The conservation law gives a *general* equation. The constitutive law makes it *specific*. The most frequently tested constitutive relations in KTU are:

| Domain | Constitutive Law | Equation |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical (resistor) | Ohm's Law | $V = iR$ |
| Mechanical (damper) | Viscous friction | $F = Bv$ |
| Mechanical (spring) | Hooke's Law | $F = Kx$ |
| Thermal (conduction) | Fourier's Law | $\dot{Q} = \dfrac{kA}{\Delta x} \Delta T$ |
| Fluid (flow) | Hagen–Poiseuille / orifice | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{R_h}$ |
| Thermodynamic | Ideal gas | $PV = nRT$ |
| Chemical reaction | Rate law | $r = kC_A^n$ |

### Stage 5 — Simplification, Linearisation, and Solution
- **Linearisation** about an operating point $\mathbf{x}_0$: For a nonlinear function $f(\mathbf{x})$, expand via Taylor series to first order:

$$
f(\mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_0) + \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial \mathbf{x}}\right|_{\mathbf{x}_0} (\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_0)
$$

- **Dimensionless normalisation** to identify the dominant physical effects.
- **Analytic solution** for linear ODEs, or **numerical integration** (Euler, RK4) for nonlinear systems.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Card — Memorise Before Exam**

| # | Equation / Law | Mathematical Form | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass balance (tank) | $A\dfrac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$ | Liquid level systems |
| 2 | Energy balance (thermal) | $\rho C_p V \dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in} - hA(T - T_\infty)$ | Heating/cooling |
| 3 | Newton's 2nd Law (translation) | $m\dfrac{d^{2}x}{dt^{2}} = \sum F_i$ | Mechanical systems |
| 4 | Newton's 2nd Law (rotation) | $J\dfrac{d^{2}\theta}{dt^{2}} = \sum \tau_i$ | Rotational systems |
| 5 | KVL (series RLC) | $L\dfrac{di}{dt} + Ri + \dfrac{1}{C}\int i\,dt = v(t)$ | Electrical circuits |
| 6 | State-space form | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}$ | Multi-DOF systems |
| 7 | Transfer function | $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \mathbf{C}(sI - A)^{-1}\mathbf{B} + D$ | Laplace-domain analysis |
| 8 | Linearisation | $f(\mathbf{x}) \approx f(\mathbf{x}_0) + J_f(\mathbf{x}_0)(\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{x}_0)$ | Nonlinear ODEs |
| 9 | Dimensional homogeneity | $[M^{a}L^{b}T^{c}]_{\text{LHS}} = [M^{a}L^{b}T^{c}]_{\text{RHS}}$ | Model verification |
| 10 | Steady-state condition | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{0}$ | Equilibrium analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These model equations form the backbone of *every* control system, *every* CFD simulation, *every* process-industry plant, and *every* digital twin. A control engineer in Kerala's petrochemical sector (e.g., BPCL Kochi) uses mass/energy balance models daily; an automotive engineer at Tata Elxsi uses Newton's 2nd Law + state-space models for vehicle dynamics simulation.

## 2.3 Common Modelling Assumptions (Asked in KTU 2-Markers)

- **Lumped vs. Distributed:** Uniform temperature, concentration, or velocity inside the system.
- **Negligible dynamics:** Fast modes ignored (e.g., dynamics of a sensor treated as instantaneous).
- **Constant parameters:** $R$, $L$, $C$, $K$, $B$ treated as time-invariant.
- **Linear regime:** Operation in the region where the constitutive relations are linear (e.g., laminar flow, small deformations).
- **Ideal insulation / perfect mixing:** No heat loss, instantaneous homogenisation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU 14-Mark Workhorse:** In the Part-B questions, you will be asked to **derive the model equation** for a physical system. The two most common systems are (a) a **liquid-level tank** and (b) a **mechanical spring-mass-damper**. We derive both below in full, step-by-step, with no skipped algebra.

---

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1 — Liquid Level Tank with Nonlinear Outflow

**System description:** A cylindrical tank of uniform cross-sectional area $A$ receives an inflow $Q_{in}(t)$ and discharges through a valve at the bottom. The outflow $Q_{out}$ is nonlinearly related to the liquid height $h$ by $Q_{out} = C_d \, a_v \sqrt{2g \, h}$, where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient, $a_v$ is the valve area, and $g$ is gravitational acceleration.

**Goal:** Derive the nonlinear ODE governing $h(t)$ and then linearise it about an operating point $h_0$.

### Step 1 — Define the system boundary
The system boundary is taken to be the **interior of the tank** (control volume). Liquid height $h$ is the single state variable. Inputs are $Q_{in}$; the disturbance is the valve setting $C_d a_v$.

### Step 2 — Apply the conservation of mass
The lumped mass balance over the control volume gives:

$$
\frac{dm}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} - \dot{m}_{out}
$$

Since the density $\rho$ is constant (incompressible liquid) and $m = \rho A h$:

$$
\frac{d(\rho A h)}{dt} = \rho Q_{in} - \rho Q_{out}
$$

Cancel $\rho$ (and $A$ is constant):

$$
A \frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}
$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the constitutive relation for $Q_{out}$

$$
A \frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - C_d \, a_v \sqrt{2g \, h}
$$

This is the **nonlinear model equation** of the system. We can rewrite it as:

$$
\frac{dh}{dt} = -\frac{C_d \, a_v \sqrt{2g}}{A} \, \sqrt{h} \; + \; \frac{1}{A} Q_{in}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify the operating (steady-state) point
At steady state, $dh/dt = 0$, and let the nominal inflow be $Q_{in,0}$ with nominal valve opening $a_{v,0}$:

$$
0 = Q_{in,0} - C_d \, a_{v,0} \sqrt{2g \, h_0}
\quad \Longrightarrow \quad
Q_{in,0} = C_d \, a_{v,0} \sqrt{2g \, h_0}
$$

### Step 5 — Linearise the nonlinear outflow term via Taylor expansion
Let $h = h_0 + \delta h$ and $a_v = a_{v,0} + \delta a_v$. We Taylor-expand the function

$$
f(h, a_v) = C_d \, a_v \sqrt{2g \, h}
$$

about the operating point $(h_0, a_{v,0})$:

$$
f(h, a_v) \approx f(h_0, a_{v,0}) + \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial h}\right|_{0} \delta h + \left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial a_v}\right|_{0} \delta a_v
$$

Compute the partial derivatives:

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial h} = C_d \, a_v \cdot \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2g}{h}} = \frac{C_d \, a_v \sqrt{2g}}{2\sqrt{h}}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial f}{\partial a_v} = C_d \sqrt{2g \, h}
$$

Evaluated at the operating point:

$$
\left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial h}\right|_{0} = \frac{C_d \, a_{v,0} \sqrt{2g}}{2\sqrt{h_0}} = \frac{Q_{in,0}}{2 h_0}
$$

$$
\left.\frac{\partial f}{\partial a_v}\right|_{0} = C_d \sqrt{2g \, h_0}
$$

### Step 6 — Assemble the linearised deviation equation
Substituting back into $A \, dh/dt = Q_{in} - f(h, a_v)$ and applying $Q_{in} = Q_{in,0} + \delta Q_{in}$:

$$
A \frac{d(\delta h)}{dt} = \delta Q_{in} - \frac{Q_{in,0}}{2 h_0} \delta h - C_d \sqrt{2g \, h_0} \; \delta a_v
$$

Define the **resistance** $R = \dfrac{2h_0}{Q_{in,0}}$ and the **valve gain** $K_v = C_d \sqrt{2g \, h_0}$:

$$
\boxed{\,R A \frac{d(\delta h)}{dt} + \delta h = R \, \delta Q_{in} - R K_v \, \delta a_v\,}
$$

This is a **first-order linear ODE** in the deviation variable $\delta h(t)$, suitable for control design and transfer-function analysis.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2 — Mechanical Spring–Mass–Damper with Base Excitation

**System description:** A mass $m$ is connected to a spring (stiffness $K$) and a viscous damper (coefficient $B$). The other end of the spring-damper is attached to a moving base whose displacement is $y(t)$.

**Goal:** Derive the governing ODE for the absolute displacement $x(t)$ of the mass.

### Step 1 — System boundary and free-body diagram
The system is the **mass $m$ alone**. The forces acting on it are:
- Spring force (proportional to relative displacement): $F_s = -K(x - y)$
- Damper force (proportional to relative velocity): $F_d = -B(\dot{x} - \dot{y})$
- Weight $mg$ and normal reaction $N$ from the ground (vertical equilibrium, cancelled).

### Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law (conservation of momentum)

$$
m \ddot{x} = -K(x - y) - B(\dot{x} - \dot{y})
$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange to standard form

$$
m \ddot{x} + B \dot{x} + K x = B \dot{y} + K y
$$

### Step 4 — Define the state variables
Choose $x_1 = x$ and $x_2 = \dot{x}$ (position and velocity of the mass). Then:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{K}{m} x_1 - \frac{B}{m} x_2 + \frac{K}{m} y + \frac{B}{m} \dot{y}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to state-space matrix form

$$
\frac{d}{dt}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -\frac{K}{m} & -\frac{B}{m} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 \\ \frac{K}{m} & \frac{B}{m} \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} y \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 6 — Derive the transfer function (assuming $y$ is the input, $x$ is the output)
Take the Laplace transform of the ODE assuming zero initial conditions:

$$
m s^{2} X(s) + B s X(s) + K X(s) = B s Y(s) + K Y(s)
$$

Factor $X(s)$ on the left and $Y(s)$ on the right:

$$
(ms^{2} + Bs + K) X(s) = (Bs + K) Y(s)
$$

Therefore the transfer function from base displacement to mass displacement is:

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{X(s)}{Y(s)} = \frac{Bs + K}{ms^{2} + Bs + K}\,}
$$

This is the **standard transmissibility function** of a passive vibration isolation system, used universally in automotive suspension design, building seismic isolation, and aerospace mounting systems.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3 — Electrical RLC Series Circuit (Voltage-Input, Current-Output)

**System description:** A series RLC circuit with resistor $R$, inductor $L$, capacitor $C$, and a source voltage $v(t)$. The current $i(t)$ is the output.

### Step 1 — Conservation of charge (KCL) and energy (KVL)
KVL around the loop:

$$
v(t) = v_R + v_L + v_C = R i + L \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} \int_0^t i(\tau) \, d\tau
$$

### Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain a pure ODE
Differentiate both sides with respect to $t$ (assuming $v_C(0) = 0$):

$$
\frac{dv}{dt} = R \frac{di}{dt} + L \frac{d^{2} i}{dt^{2}} + \frac{1}{C} i
$$

### Step 3 — Rearrange into standard form

$$
L \frac{d^{2}i}{dt^{2}} + R \frac{di}{dt} + \frac{1}{C} i = \frac{dv}{dt}
$$

### Step 4 — Laplace transform
With zero initial conditions:

$$
L s^{2} I(s) + R s I(s) + \frac{1}{C} I(s) = s V(s)
$$

### Step 5 — Transfer function $I(s) / V(s)$

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{I(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{s}{Ls^{2} + Rs + \dfrac{1}{C}} = \frac{s/L}{s^{2} + \dfrac{R}{L}s + \dfrac{1}{LC}}\,}
$$

This is the **admittance** of the series RLC circuit. Notice the structural equivalence with the mechanical system: $L \leftrightarrow m$, $R \leftrightarrow B$, $1/C \leftrightarrow K$. This is the famous **Force–Current Analogy** in KTU syllabus.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Domain Equivalence Table

This is one of the most heavily tested concepts in KTU Module 4. Mastering it guarantees full marks on cross-domain modelling questions.

| Mechanical (Translation) | Mechanical (Rotation) | Electrical | Thermal | Hydraulic | Fluid (Pneumatic) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Force $F$ | Torque $\tau$ | Voltage $v$ | Temperature $T$ | Pressure $P$ | Pressure $P$ |
| Velocity $v$ | Angular velocity $\omega$ | Current $i$ | Heat flow $\dot{Q}$ | Volume flow $Q$ | Mass flow $\dot{m}$ |
| Mass $m$ | Inertia $J$ | Inductance $L$ | — | — | Inertia $I$ |
| Damper $B$ | Rotational damper $B_r$ | Resistance $R$ | Thermal resistance $R_t$ | Fluid resistance $R_f$ | $R_p$ |
| Spring $K$ | Torsional spring $K_t$ | $1/C$ (inverse capacitance) | $1/C_t$ (inverse thermal capacity) | $1/C_f$ | $1/C_p$ |
| Displacement $x$ | Angle $\theta$ | Charge $q$ | Heat $Q$ | Volume $V$ | Pressure $P$ |

**Key insight:** The mathematical structure $m \ddot{x} + B \dot{x} + K x = F(t)$ appears in **all five domains**, with proper variable substitution. This is why a single simulation tool (like MATLAB/Simulink) can model systems from any engineering discipline.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Five-Stage Model Development Flowchart

The following Mermaid block illustrates the canonical procedure for developing a model equation, as prescribed by the KTU 2024 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Define Problem and Objectives] --> B[Stage 2: Identify System Boundary and State Variables]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Apply Conservation Law: Mass, Energy, Momentum, Charge]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Insert Constitutive Relations: Ohms Law, Fouriers Law, Hookes Law]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Simplify, Linearise, and Express in Standard ODE or State Space Form]
    E --> F{Is Model Validated Against Experimental Data?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Final Model Equation Accepted]
    F -->|No| H[Return to Stage 1: Refine Assumptions]
    H --> A
    G --> I[Proceed to Simulation in Module 5]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Model Equation Development Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Physical System]
        P1[Tank with Inflow and Outflow]
        P2[Spring Mass Damper on Moving Base]
        P3[Series RLC Circuit]
    end

    subgraph BOUNDARY[System Boundary Definition]
        S1[Select Control Volume or Free Body]
        S2[Choose State Variables x1, x2, xn]
    end

    subgraph LAWS[Governing Laws]
        L1[Conservation of Mass]
        L2[Conservation of Momentum]
        L3[Conservation of Energy]
        L4[Conservation of Charge]
    end

    subgraph CONSTITUTIVE[Constitutive Relations]
        C1[Ohms Law: V is iR]
        C2[Hookes Law: F is Kx]
        C3[Fouriers Law: Q is kA dT by dx]
        C4[Orifice Flow: Q is Cd a sqrt of 2gh]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Model Equation Output]
        O1[First Order ODE: A dh by dt is Qin minus Qout]
        O2[Second Order ODE: m x double dot plus B x dot plus K x is F of t]
        O3[State Space Form: x dot is A x plus B u]
    end

    P1 --> S1
    P2 --> S1
    P3 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> L1
    S2 --> L2
    S2 --> L3
    S2 --> L4
    L1 --> C4
    L2 --> C2
    L3 --> C3
    L4 --> C1
    C1 --> O3
    C2 --> O2
    C3 --> O2
    C4 --> O1
```

## 4.3 Cross-Domain Analogy Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    Mech[Mechanical: m x double dot plus B x dot plus K x is F] -.Force Current Analogy.-> Elec[Electrical: L dI by dt plus R I plus q by C is V]
    Mech -.Force Voltage Analogy.-> Elec2[Electrical: C dV by dt plus V by R plus integral is I]
    Mech -.Thermal Analogy.-> Therm[Thermal: rho Cp V dT by dt is Q in minus hA T minus T inf]
    Mech --> Hyd[Hydraulic: rho A dh by dt is Q in minus Q out]

    style Mech fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Elec fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Elec2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Therm fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Hyd fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.4 Linearisation Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Nonlinear Model Equation: f of x, u is 0] --> B[Identify Operating Point: x0, u0]
    B --> C[Solve Steady State: f of x0, u0 is 0]
    C --> D[Compute Jacobian Matrix: Jf and Jg]
    D --> E[Taylor Expand to First Order]
    E --> F[Subtract Steady State to get Deviation Variables]
    F --> G[Linear ODE or State Space in delta x and delta u]
    G --> H[Apply Laplace Transform for Transfer Function]
```
