# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**Differentiate between lumped parameter and distributed parameter models. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **lumped parameter model** assumes that the properties of the system (temperature, concentration, velocity, etc.) are spatially uniform within the system. The system is described by a finite number of state variables, leading to **ordinary differential equations (ODEs)**.

A **distributed parameter model** accounts for the spatial variation of system properties. The model is described by **partial differential equations (PDEs)** involving both time and space derivatives.

| Feature | Lumped Parameter | Distributed Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical form | ODE | PDE |
| Spatial variation | Neglected | Explicitly modelled |
| Example | A well-stirred tank at uniform temperature | Heat conduction along a long insulated rod |
| Example | An RLC electrical circuit | A long transmission line |
| Example | A single mass on a spring | A flexible cantilever beam |

**Example:** A small liquid tank with vigorous stirring → lumped. A long pipe carrying hot oil with significant temperature drop along the length → distributed.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**List the four conservation laws used in developing model equations. State the general form of each in words.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Conservation of Mass:** The rate of accumulation of mass inside a control volume equals the net mass inflow minus the net mass outflow plus any mass generated by chemical reactions.
2. **Conservation of Energy (First Law of Thermodynamics):** The rate of change of stored energy inside a system equals the net heat added to the system minus the net work done by the system plus the net energy carried in by mass flow.
3. **Conservation of Momentum (Newton's Second Law):** The time rate of change of linear momentum of a system equals the sum of all external forces acting on the system.
4. **Conservation of Charge (Kirchhoff's Current Law):** The algebraic sum of currents entering any node in an electrical network is zero; equivalently, the rate of accumulation of charge in a lumped element equals the net current flowing into it.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Format

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 4 Internal Choice Pattern:** Question A and Question B must be attempted on the basis of the same Module 4 content. Below we present two completely independent alternatives. In the KTU exam you will be given a similar choice.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024]

A cylindrical tank of uniform cross-sectional area $A = 2 \, \text{m}^{2}$ is fed by an inlet flow rate $Q_{in}(t)$. The outlet flow rate through a valve at the bottom obeys the nonlinear relation $Q_{out} = C_d \, a_v \sqrt{2 g h(t)}$, where $C_d = 0.6$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^{2}$, $a_v$ is the valve opening area, and $h(t)$ is the liquid height.

#### (a) **Derive the nonlinear model equation governing $h(t)$ and find the steady-state operating point when $Q_{in,0} = 0.1 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$ and $a_{v,0} = 0.01 \, \text{m}^{2}$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass balance (lumped form):** With the system boundary taken as the tank interior, accumulation of mass per unit time equals inflow minus outflow:

$$
A \frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the constitutive orifice relation:**

$$
A \frac{dh}{dt} = Q_{in} - C_d \, a_v \sqrt{2g \, h}
$$

This is the **nonlinear model equation**.

**Step 3 — Steady state:** Set $dh/dt = 0$ and use the given numerical values:

$$
0 = Q_{in,0} - C_d \, a_{v,0} \sqrt{2 g h_0}
$$

$$
0.1 = 0.6 \times 0.01 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times h_0}
$$

$$
0.1 = 0.006 \times \sqrt{19.62 \, h_0}
$$

$$
\sqrt{19.62 \, h_0} = \frac{0.1}{0.006} = 16.6667
$$

$$
19.62 \, h_0 = (16.6667)^{2} = 277.78
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h_0 \approx 14.16 \, \text{m}\,}
$$

#### (b) **Linearise the nonlinear equation about this operating point and derive the deviation equation. Also compute the time constant of the linearised system.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define deviation variables:** Let $h = h_0 + \delta h$ and $Q_{in} = Q_{in,0} + \delta Q_{in}$.

**Step 2 — Linearise the outflow term** $f(h) = C_d \, a_v \sqrt{2g \, h}$ about $h_0$:

$$
f(h) \approx f(h_0) + \left.\frac{df}{dh}\right|_{h_0} \delta h
$$

where

$$
\frac{df}{dh} = \frac{C_d \, a_v \sqrt{2g}}{2\sqrt{h}}
$$

At the operating point, using $Q_{in,0} = C_d \, a_{v,0}\sqrt{2g \, h_0}$:

$$
\left.\frac{df}{dh}\right|_{h_0} = \frac{Q_{in,0}}{2 h_0} = \frac{0.1}{2 \times 14.16} = 0.00353 \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the original ODE and subtract the steady-state equation:**

$$
A \frac{d(\delta h)}{dt} = \delta Q_{in} - \frac{Q_{in,0}}{2 h_0} \delta h
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into standard first-order form:**

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{2 A h_0}{Q_{in,0}} \frac{d(\delta h)}{dt} + \delta h = \frac{2 h_0}{Q_{in,0}} \delta Q_{in}\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the time constant:**

$$
\tau = \frac{2 A h_0}{Q_{in,0}} = \frac{2 \times 2 \times 14.16}{0.1} = 566.4 \, \text{s} \approx 9.44 \, \text{min}
$$

The system responds slowly to inlet perturbations because the tank is large.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, June 2024]

A mechanical system consists of a mass $m = 10 \, \text{kg}$ connected to a wall through a parallel combination of a spring of stiffness $K = 400 \, \text{N/m}$ and a viscous damper of coefficient $B = 40 \, \text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}$. The mass is also subjected to an applied force $F(t)$.

#### (a) **Derive the governing differential equation for the displacement $x(t)$ of the mass. Express it in standard second-order form and identify the natural frequency $\omega_n$ and damping ratio $\zeta$.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Free-body diagram:** The mass $m$ experiences:
- Spring restoring force: $-Kx$
- Damper viscous force: $-B\dot{x}$
- Applied external force: $F(t)$

**Step 2 — Newton's second law** (conservation of momentum):

$$
m \ddot{x} = -Kx - B\dot{x} + F(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Standard form** (divide by $m$):

$$
\ddot{x} + \frac{B}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{K}{m} x = \frac{1}{m} F(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Identify canonical parameters:**

$$
\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{K}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{400}{10}} = \sqrt{40} \approx 6.32 \, \text{rad/s}
$$

$$
\zeta = \frac{B}{2\sqrt{mK}} = \frac{40}{2 \times \sqrt{10 \times 400}} = \frac{40}{2 \times \sqrt{4000}} = \frac{40}{2 \times 63.25} = \frac{40}{126.5} \approx 0.316
$$

The system is **underdamped** since $\zeta < 1$, and will exhibit damped oscillations at frequency $\omega_d = \omega_n \sqrt{1 - \zeta^{2}} \approx 6.0 \, \text{rad/s}$.

#### (b) **Derive the transfer function $X(s)/F(s)$ and the state-space representation of the system. Comment on the stability of the system using the Routh–Hurwitz criterion.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Transfer function:** Take the Laplace transform of the ODE with zero initial conditions:

$$
m s^{2} X(s) + B s X(s) + K X(s) = F(s)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\frac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{1}{m s^{2} + B s + K} = \frac{1}{10 s^{2} + 40 s + 400}\,}
$$

In standard second-order form, dividing by $m$:

$$
G(s) = \frac{1/m}{s^{2} + 2\zeta \omega_n s + \omega_n^{2}} = \frac{0.1}{s^{2} + 4s + 40}
$$

**Step 2 — State-space representation:** Choose $x_1 = x$ (position) and $x_2 = \dot{x}$ (velocity). The state vector is $\mathbf{x} = [x_1 \; x_2]^{T}$, the input is $u = F$, and the output is $y = x_1$.

The state equations are:

$$
\dot{x}_1 = x_2
$$

$$
\dot{x}_2 = -\frac{K}{m} x_1 - \frac{B}{m} x_2 + \frac{1}{m} u
$$

In matrix form:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ -40 & -4 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
+
\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix}
u, \quad
y = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} x_1 \\ x_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 3 — Stability via Routh–Hurwitz:** The characteristic polynomial is $10 s^{2} + 40 s + 400 = 0$, or equivalently $s^{2} + 4s + 40 = 0$.

The Routh array is:

| Row | Col 1 | Col 2 |
|---|---|---|
| $s^{2}$ | $1$ | $40$ |
| $s^{1}$ | $4$ | $0$ |
| $s^{0}$ | $40$ | |

All elements in the first column are **positive** ($1, 4, 40 > 0$). By the Routh–Hurwitz criterion, the system is **asymptotically stable**. The roots are $s = -2 \pm j\sqrt{36} = -2 \pm j 6$, which lie in the **left half-plane**, confirming stability.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Forgetting to divide the nonlinear outflow term correctly when linearising. Students often write $Q_{out} \approx C_d a_{v,0} \sqrt{2g h_0} + \dfrac{C_d a_{v,0} \sqrt{2g}}{2\sqrt{h_0}} \delta h$ — correct, but they forget to **subtract the steady-state equation** afterwards, leaving an extra constant term that breaks the linearity.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Confusing **lumped** and **distributed** parameter assumptions. If the problem says "uniform temperature," do NOT introduce spatial derivatives $\partial T / \partial x$ in your equation.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting **units** in the final answer. The time constant $\tau$ must be in seconds, not in the numerical value alone.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–1 mark):** Writing the state-space output equation $y = Cx + Du$ incorrectly. For a position-output system, the correct $C$ matrix is $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$, not $\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$.
> - **Common Mistake 5 (–1 mark):** Not stating the **assumptions** explicitly at the start. Examiners award up to 1 mark for a clear "Assumptions" line (e.g., "Incompressible liquid, constant density, well-mixed tank").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam**

- **Model equation development = Boundary + Conservation Law + Constitutive Relation.** Master this three-step recipe.

- **Four conservation laws:** Mass, Energy, Momentum, Charge. Each has a canonical *lumped* and *distributed* form.

- **Linearisation** is mandatory when the system contains nonlinear terms like $\sqrt{h}$, $h^{2}$, $e^{-x}$, or $v \cdot v$ (drag). Use the **first-order Taylor expansion** about the operating point.

- **Five canonical constitutive laws** to memorise: Ohm ($V = iR$), Fourier ($\dot Q = kA\,dT/dx$), Hooke ($F = Kx$), Newton-viscous ($F = Bv$), Ideal gas ($PV = nRT$).

- **State variables** must be a *minimum* set. For an $n$-th order ODE you need exactly $n$ state variables. Choose position + velocity for 2nd-order mechanical; current + charge (or current + capacitor voltage) for 2nd-order electrical.

- **Cross-domain analogy** is a guaranteed 14-mark question. Memorise: $L \leftrightarrow m$, $R \leftrightarrow B$, $1/C \leftrightarrow K$, $i \leftrightarrow v$ (velocity), $v \leftrightarrow F$ (force).

- **Steady-state condition:** Set all time derivatives to zero and solve for the operating point $(x_0, u_0)$.

- **Dimensional homogeneity** check: every term in your final ODE must have the **same physical units**. This is the fastest way to catch algebra errors.

- **Stability via Routh–Hurwitz:** For a 2nd-order system $as^{2} + bs + c = 0$, stability requires $a > 0$, $b > 0$, and $c > 0$.

- **Transfer function** is the ratio of Laplace-transformed output to input, assuming **zero initial conditions**. The denominator is the **characteristic polynomial**, whose roots are the system **poles**.

- **Assumptions list** is worth 1 mark by itself. Always start a 14-mark derivation with a bullet list: lumped/distributed, constant parameters, linear regime, ideal mixing, etc.

- **Verification step:** Substitute your derived ODE back into the conservation law symbolically to confirm both sides balance dimensionally and algebraically.

- **Common pitfall:** When asked to "develop the model equation," students often skip directly to the answer. The KTU rubric explicitly allocates marks for (i) stating assumptions, (ii) defining the boundary, (iii) writing the conservation law, and (iv) inserting constitutive relations. Missing any one of these costs 1–2 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Algorithms for Euler Integration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Algorithms for Euler Integration ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Euler Integration** refers to a family of first-order numerical procedures used to approximate the solution of an **Initial Value Problem (IVP)** of an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) of the form: $$\frac{dy}{dt} = f(t, y), \quad y(t_0) = y_0$$ The method constructs a discrete trajectory by stepping forward in time using the slope of the solution at the current point. It is the simplest and most historically significant member of the **Runge-Kutta** family, derived directly by truncating the Taylor series after the first derivative term. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 4):** Students are expected to master the **algorithm, derivation, error analysis, and stability properties** of all three principal variants — Forward (Explicit) Euler, Backward (Implicit) Euler, and the Modified (Improved) Euler / Heun's Method. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blind Hiker" Imagine you are **blindfolded on a mountainside**, and you want to reach the village at the bottom of the valley. You cannot see the slope ahead, but you can feel the **gradient beneath your feet at your current position**. Euler Integration is exactly this strategy: - You stand at your current position $(t_n, y_n)$. - You feel the local slope $f(t_n, y_n)$. - You take a small, carefully measured step of size $h$ in the direction of the slope. - You repeat this process until you reach your destination. The smaller the step $h$ is, the more accurate your descent — but the more steps (and effort) it takes. This trade-off between **accuracy and computational cost** is the central theme of numerical integration. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The fundamental premise of Euler Integration is that the slope $f(t, y)$ is **approximately constant** over a small time interval $\Delta t = h$. This assumption is what introduces the truncation error. ### 1.3 Physical & Engineering Context In continuous-time system simulation, real-world processes such as **RC circuits, mechanical spring-mass-damper systems, population growth models, and chemical reaction kinetics** are described by ODEs. Closed-form analytical solutions exist only for a narrow class of ODEs. Euler Integration provides a **computational bridge** that allows digital computers to simulate these systems iteratively. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Euler Integration is the conceptual ancestor of every modern ODE solver used in MATLAB (`ode1`), Simulink (default fixed-step solver), SPICE (circuit simulation), and ANSYS (thermal/structural solvers). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Geometric Intuition Behind the Method For an ODE $y' = f(t, y)$, the **true solution curve** $y(t)$ passing through $(t_n, y_n)$ has a tangent line. If we approximate the curve by its tangent over a short interval $[t_n, t_{n+1}]$, we obtain: $$y(t_{n+1}) \approx y(t_n) + h \cdot y'(t_n) = y_n + h \cdot f(t_n, y_n)$$ This is the geometric foundation of the Forward Euler method. ### 2.2 Taxonomy of Euler Integration Algorithms | **Algorithm Variant** | **Type** | **Order of Accuracy** | **Stability** | **Required Iterations** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Forward Euler (Explicit) | One-step, Explicit | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ local, $\mathcal{O}(h)$ global | Conditionally stable | 1 per step | | Backward Euler (Implicit) | One-step, Implicit | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ local, $\mathcal{O}(h)$ global | Unconditionally stable (A-stable) | 1 (with solver) | | Modified / Improved Euler (Heun's) | Two-stage Predictor-Corrector | $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$ local, $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ global | Conditionally stable | 2 per step | | Euler-Cromer (Semi-implicit) | One-step, Semi-implicit | $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ global | Improved for oscillatory systems | 1 per step | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description** | |---|---|---| | Standard IVP Form | $y' = f(t, y), \quad y(t_0) = y_0$ | First-order ODE with initial condition | | Step Size | $h = (t_{final} - t_0) / N$ | Discretization increment | | Forward Euler Update | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(t_n, y_n)$ | Uses slope at current point | | Backward Euler Update | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1})$ | Uses slope at *future* point (implicit) | | Heun's Predictor | $\tilde{y}_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(t_n, y_n)$ | Initial Euler guess | | Heun's Corrector | $y_{n+1} = y_n + \dfrac{h}{2} \bigl[ f(t_n, y_n) + f(t_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1}) \bigr]$ | Average of two slopes | | Euler-Cromer Update | $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(t_{n+1}, y_n)$ | Future time, current state | | Local Truncation Error | $\tau_n = \mathcal{O}(h^2)$ | Per-step error | | Global Accumulation Error | $E_N = \mathcal{O}(h)$ | Total error over $N$ steps | | Stability Region (Forward) | $\vert 1 + h\lambda \vert \leq 1$ | Condition in complex $\lambda h$-plane | | Stability Region (Backward) | $\vert 1 - h\lambda \vert^{-1} \leq 1$ | Always satisfied (for $\text{Re}(\lambda) \leq 0$) | | Taylor Series Truncation | $y(t+h) = y(t) + h y'(t) + \frac{h^2}{2} y''(t) + \ldots$ | Drops terms of order $\geq 2$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stability Rule of Thumb:** For a stiff ODE or a system with eigenvalue $\lambda < 0$, the **Forward Euler** method requires $h \leq \dfrac{2}{\vert \lambda \vert}$ to remain stable. The **Backward Euler** method imposes *no such restriction* on $h$ (this is the definition of **A-stability**). ### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Modern Simulation - **Power Electronics:** Forward Euler is widely used in real-time digital simulators (RTDS) for converter switching, where small step sizes (microseconds) ensure accuracy. - **Aerospace Control:** Backward Euler is preferred for stiff systems like rocket propulsion, where fast and slow dynamics coexist. - **Biomechanics:** Heun's method is often used in musculoskeletal simulation (OpenSim) for better accuracy at moderate step sizes. - **Climate Modelling:** Modified Euler variants form the baseline for atmospheric time-stepping schemes. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Forward Euler from Taylor Series Expansion We begin with the exact Taylor series expansion of the analytical solution $y(t)$ about the known point $t_n$: $$y(t_{n+1}) = y(t_n) + h \, y'(t_n) + \frac{h^2}{2!} y''(t_n) + \frac{h^3}{3!} y'''(t_n) + \ldots + \frac{h^k}{k!} y^{(k)}(t_n) + \ldots$$ Since the ODE states that $y'(t) = f(t, y)$, we substitute and obtain: $$y(t_{n+1}) = y(t_n) + h \, f(t_n, y(t_n)) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f'(t_n, y_n) + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$ **Step 1:** Drop all terms of order $h^2$ and higher. This is the **truncation assumption**. **Step 2:** Replace the exact values $y(t_n)$ with the numerical approximations $y_n$: $$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \, f(t_n, y_n)$$ **Step 3:** This recursive formula is the **Forward Euler Algorithm**. The error introduced by neglecting the $h^2$ term and beyond is termed the **Local Truncation Error (LTE)**: $$\tau_{n+1} = \frac{h^2}{2} y''(\xi_n), \quad \text{for some } \xi_n \in [t_n, t_{n+1}]$$ Since $y''(\xi_n)$ is bounded, the LTE is $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$. The **Global Error** after $N$ steps is $\mathcal{O}(h)$, because errors accumulate linearly over $N = (t_f - t_0)/h$ steps. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Backward Euler Algorithm The Backward Euler formula uses the slope evaluated at the **future** point: $$y_{n+1} = y_n + h \, f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1})$$ **Step 1:** Note that the unknown $y_{n+1}$ appears on **both sides** of the equation. This is why it is called an **implicit** method. **Step 2:** For a general nonlinear $f(t, y)$, this requires solving a nonlinear equation at each step, typically via **Newton-Raphson iteration**: $$y_{n+1}^{(k+1)} = y_{n+1}^{(k)} - \frac{F(y_{n+1}^{(k)})}{F'(y_{n+1}^{(k)})}$$ where $F(y_{n+1}) \equiv y_{n+1} - y_n - h \, f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1}) = 0$. **Step 3:** For the linear case $f(t, y) = \lambda y + g(t)$, an explicit solution exists: $$y_{n+1} - h \lambda y_{n+1} = y_n + h g(t_{n+1})$$ $$y_{n+1} = \frac{y_n + h g(t_{n+1})}{1 - h \lambda}$$ **Step 4:** The stability function is $R(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - z}$, where $z = h\lambda$. For $\text{Re}(z) > 0$, we have $\vert R(z) \vert < 1$, meaning the method is **unconditionally stable** for the decay of any linear system. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Modified Euler (Heun's Method) Heun's method is a **Predictor-Corrector** approach that improves accuracy by using the **average slope** over the interval. **Step 1 (Predictor):** Use Forward Euler to estimate the value at $t_{n+1}$: $$\tilde{y}_{n+1} = y_n + h \, f(t_n, y_n)$$ **Step 2:** Compute the slope at the predicted future point: $$k_2 = f(t_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})$$ **Step 3 (Corrector):** Use the average of the slope at the start and the slope at the predicted end: $$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2} \bigl[ f(t_n, y_n) + f(t_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1}) \bigr]$$ $$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2} (k_1 + k_2)$$ **Step 4 (Error Order):** The local truncation error of Heun's method is $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$, and the global error is $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$. This makes it equivalent to a second-order Runge-Kutta method (specifically, the explicit midpoint and Ralston variants differ subtly). **Verification via Taylor Expansion:** $$y(t_{n+1}) = y(t_n) + h f + \frac{h^2}{2}(f_t + f_y f) + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$ Heun's corrector evaluates: $$\frac{1}{2}(k_1 + k_2) = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[ f + f + h(f_t + f_y f) + \mathcal{O}(h^2) \bigr] = f + \frac{h}{2}(f_t + f_y f) + \mathcal{O}(h^2)$$ Multiplying by $h$ and adding $y_n$ recovers the $h^2$ term exactly, confirming the $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$ local error. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Heun's Method) **Problem:** Solve $y' = -2y + t$, with $y(0) = 1$, over $[0, 1]$ using $h = 0.25$ via Heun's Method. **Step 1 — Setup parameters:** $$f(t, y) = -2y + t, \quad t_0 = 0, \quad y_0 = 1, \quad h = 0.25, \quad N = 4$$ **Step 2 — Iteration 1 (n = 0):** $$k_1 = f(0, 1) = -2(1) + 0 = -2$$ $$\tilde{y}_1 = 1 + (0.25)(-2) = 0.5$$ $$k_2 = f(0.25, 0.5) = -2(0.5) + 0.25 = -0.75$$ $$y_1 = 1 + \frac{0.25}{2}(-2 + (-0.75)) = 1 + 0.125(-2.75) = 1 - 0.34375 = 0.65625$$ **Step 3 — Iteration 2 (n = 1):** $$k_1 = f(0.25, 0.65625) = -2(0.65625) + 0.25 = -1.0625$$ $$\tilde{y}_2 = 0.65625 + (0.25)(-1.0625) = 0.390625$$ $$k_2 = f(0.5, 0.390625) = -2(0.390625) + 0.5 = -0.28125$$ $$y_2 = 0.65625 + 0.125(-1.0625 + (-0.28125)) = 0.65625 - 0.167969 = 0.488281$$ **Step 4 — Summary of Results:** | $n$ | $t_n$ | $y_n$ (Heun) | $y(t_n)$ (Exact) | Absolute Error | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0.00 | 1.000000 | 1.000000 | 0.000000 | | 1 | 0.25 | 0.656250 | 0.679570 | 0.023320 | | 2 | 0.50 | 0.488281 | 0.521954 | 0.033673 | | 3 | 0.75 | 0.416016 | 0.438935 | 0.022919 | | 4 | 1.00 | 0.394043 | 0.397585 | 0.003542 | The exact analytical solution is $y(t) = \dfrac{4e^{-2t} + 2t - 1}{4}$, confirming the $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ global accuracy of Heun's method. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Forward Euler Integration Algorithm ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA([START]) --> nodeB[Read Initial Conditions
t0, y0, h, N] nodeB --> nodeC[Initialize n = 0] nodeC --> nodeD[Compute Slope
f of tn and yn] nodeD --> nodeE[Apply Update Rule
y n plus 1 equals yn plus h times f] nodeE --> nodeF[Advance Time
t n plus 1 equals tn plus h] nodeF --> nodeG{Is n less than N minus 1} nodeG -- YES --> nodeD nodeG -- NO --> nodeH[Store or Plot Result
Array of tn and yn] nodeH --> nodeI([END]) ``` ### 4.2 Comparative Flow — Forward vs Backward vs Modified Euler ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SF[FORWARD EULER EXPLICIT] fA[Current State yn] --> fB[Evaluate f at tn yn] fB --> fC[Update yn plus 1 directly] end subgraph SB[BACKWARD EULER IMPLICIT] bA[Current State yn] --> bB[Form Equation
y n plus 1 minus h f equals yn] bB --> bC[Solve for y n plus 1
Newton Raphson] bC --> bD[Accept Converged Value] end subgraph SM[MODIFIED EULER HEUNS] mA[Current State yn] --> mB[Predictor
Compute y tilde n plus 1] mB --> mC[Evaluate f at t n plus 1 and y tilde] mC --> mD[Corrector
Average of two slopes] mD --> mE[Accept y n plus 1] end SF -- uses only past info --> OUT1[Single Function Call per Step] SB -- requires solver --> OUT2[Iteration per Step but A Stable] SM -- two evaluations --> OUT3[Higher Accuracy at Same h] ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Architecture of the Integration Pipeline in a Simulation Engine ```mermaid flowchart TD inp[Continuous Time Model
differential equation] --> dis[Discretization Block
choose step size h] dis --> alg[Algorithm Selector
Forward or Backward or Heun] alg --> step[Single Step Computation
apply update rule] step --> err[Error Estimator Block
compare to tolerance] err -- tolerance violated --> hAdj[Step Size Adapter
reduce h and retry] hAdj --> step err -- tolerance satisfied --> acc[Accumulator
store tn and yn] acc --> stop{Did we reach final time} stop -- NO --> alg stop -- YES --> out[Output Solution Trajectory] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]** *Define Euler Integration. Differentiate between Forward Euler and Backward Euler methods in terms of stability and computational complexity. (3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** Euler Integration is a first-order numerical procedure for solving Initial Value Problems of the form $y' = f(t, y)$ with $y(t_0) = y_0$. It uses the recursive update $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n)$ derived from truncating the Taylor series after the linear term. **Differences:** | **Aspect** | **Forward Euler** | **Backward Euler** | |---|---|---| | Slope Evaluation | At current point $t_n$ | At future point $t_{n+1}$ | | Type | Explicit | Implicit | | Stability | Conditionally stable | Unconditionally stable (A-stable) | | Computation | Direct, one evaluation per step | Requires solving nonlinear equation | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** *What is the Local Truncation Error of the Forward Euler method? Show that the global error is of order $\mathcal{O}(h)$. (3 Marks)* **Model Answer:** The Local Truncation Error (LTE) is the error introduced in a single step, assuming the previous value is exact. From the Taylor expansion: $$y(t_{n+1}) = y(t_n) + h f(t_n, y_n) + \frac{h^2}{2} y''(\xi_n)$$ The Forward Euler update is $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n)$, so the LTE is: $$\tau_{n+1} = \frac{h^2}{2} y''(\xi_n) = \mathcal{O}(h^2)$$ Over $N = (t_f - t_0)/h$ steps, errors accumulate: $$E_N = N \cdot \mathcal{O}(h^2) = \frac{t_f - t_0}{h} \cdot \mathcal{O}(h^2) = \mathcal{O}(h)$$ This proves the global error is of order $\mathcal{O}(h)$, establishing Forward Euler as a **first-order method**. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) --- **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a)** Derive the Forward Euler algorithm from the Taylor series expansion of $y(t)$ about $t_n$. Clearly state the truncation assumption and identify the local truncation error. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** Starting from the Taylor series of $y(t)$ about $t_n$: $$y(t_n + h) = y(t_n) + h y'(t_n) + \frac{h^2}{2!} y''(t_n) + \frac{h^3}{3!} y'''(t_n) + \ldots$$ Since $y'(t) = f(t, y)$: $$y(t_{n+1}) = y(t_n) + h f(t_n, y_n) + \frac{h^2}{2!} f'(t_n, y_n) + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$ **Truncation Assumption:** We drop all terms of order $h^2$ and higher, retaining only: $$y(t_{n+1}) \approx y(t_n) + h f(t_n, y_n)$$ **Discretization:** Replace exact values with numerical approximations $y_n \approx y(t_n)$: $$y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n) \quad \text{...(Forward Euler Formula)}$$ **Local Truncation Error:** By comparing the exact Taylor expansion with the numerical formula, the LTE is: $$\tau_{n+1} = y(t_{n+1}) - y_{n+1} = \frac{h^2}{2} y''(\xi_n) = \mathcal{O}(h^2)$$ for some $\xi_n \in (t_n, t_{n+1})$ by the Mean Value Theorem. **Global Error Accumulation:** Since the method takes $N$ steps where $N h = T$ (total integration interval), the global error is bounded by: $$E_N \leq C \cdot N \cdot h^2 = C \cdot T \cdot h = \mathcal{O}(h)$$ This confirms the method is of **first-order global accuracy**. --- **(b)** Solve the IVP $y' = -2y + e^{-t}$, $y(0) = 1$ on $[0, 0.5]$ using Forward Euler with $h = 0.1$. Compare with the exact solution at $t = 0.5$. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Setup:** $f(t, y) = -2y + e^{-t}$, $t_0 = 0$, $y_0 = 1$, $h = 0.1$, $N = 5$. **Step 2 — Exact solution:** The ODE is linear: $y' + 2y = e^{-t}$. Using integrating factor $e^{2t}$: $$\frac{d}{dt}(e^{2t} y) = e^{2t} \cdot e^{-t} = e^{t}$$ $$e^{2t} y = e^{t} + C \implies y(t) = e^{-t} + C e^{-2t}$$ With $y(0) = 1$: $1 = 1 + C \Rightarrow C = 0$. So $y(t) = e^{-t}$. Exact value at $t = 0.5$: $y(0.5) = e^{-0.5} \approx 0.60653$. **Step 3 — Forward Euler Iterations:** | $n$ | $t_n$ | $y_n$ | $f(t_n, y_n) = -2y_n + e^{-t_n}$ | $y_{n+1} = y_n + 0.1 \cdot f$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0.0 | 1.000000 | $-2.000000 + 1.000000 = -1.000000$ | 0.900000 | | 1 | 0.1 | 0.900000 | $-1.800000 + 0.904837 = -0.895163$ | 0.810484 | | 2 | 0.2 | 0.810484 | $-1.620967 + 0.818731 = -0.802236$ | 0.730260 | | 3 | 0.3 | 0.730260 | $-1.460521 + 0.740818 = -0.719703$ | 0.658290 | | 4 | 0.4 | 0.658290 | $-1.316579 + 0.670320 = -0.646259$ | 0.593664 | | 5 | 0.5 | 0.593664 | — | — | **Step 4 — Error Analysis:** $$y_{numeric}(0.5) = 0.593664, \quad y_{exact}(0.5) = 0.606531$$ $$\text{Absolute Error} = \vert 0.593664 - 0.606531 \vert = 0.012867 \approx 1.29\%$$ **Observation:** The numerical solution **underestimates** the true value because Forward Euler introduces a consistent negative bias for decaying exponentials (the slope at the start of each interval is steeper than the average slope). --- **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a predictor-corrector formulation, derive the Modified Euler (Heun's) method. State and justify its order of accuracy. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** Heun's method is a **two-stage predictor-corrector** algorithm that improves upon the Forward Euler by using the average of two slopes. **Stage 1 — Predictor (Euler Estimate):** $$\tilde{y}_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n)$$ This is a tentative estimate of the solution at the next time point, computed using the slope at the start of the interval. **Stage 2 — Slope at Predicted Point:** $$k_2 = f(t_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})$$ **Stage 3 — Corrector (Trapezoidal Averaging):** $$y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2} \bigl[ f(t_n, y_n) + f(t_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1}) \bigr]$$ This uses the **average of the initial slope and the slope at the predicted endpoint**, which approximates the integral of $f$ over $[t_n, t_{n+1}]$ more accurately than Forward Euler. **Order of Accuracy Derivation:** Expanding the corrector using Taylor series: $$f(t_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1}) = f(t_n, y_n) + h \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y} f \right)_{(t_n, y_n)} + \mathcal{O}(h^2)$$ Substituting into the corrector: $$\frac{f(t_n, y_n) + f(t_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})}{2} = f + \frac{h}{2}(f_t + f_y f) + \mathcal{O}(h^2)$$ Multiplying by $h$ and adding $y_n$: $$y_{n+1} = y_n + h f + \frac{h^2}{2}(f_t + f_y f) + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$ Comparing with the exact Taylor expansion: $$y(t_{n+1}) = y(t_n) + h f + \frac{h^2}{2}(f_t + f_y f) + \mathcal{O}(h^3)$$ The numerical formula matches the exact expansion up to $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$, hence the **Local Truncation Error is $\mathcal{O}(h^3)$** and the **Global Error is $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$**, making it a **second-order method**. --- **(b)** A first-order thermal system is modelled by the ODE $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = -0.5(T - 25) + 10\sin(t)$, with $T(0) = 100$°C. Using Heun's method with $h = 0.2$, compute $T$ at $t = 0.4$ and $t = 0.6$. *(7 Marks)* **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Setup:** $f(t, T) = -0.5(T - 25) + 10\sin(t) = -0.5T + 12.5 + 10\sin(t)$, $T_0 = 100$, $h = 0.2$. **Step 2 — Iteration 1 (n = 0, $t_0 = 0$):** $$k_1 = f(0, 100) = -0.5(100) + 12.5 + 10\sin(0) = -50 + 12.5 + 0 = -37.5$$ $$\tilde{T}_1 = 100 + 0.2 \times (-37.5) = 92.5$$ $$k_2 = f(0.2, 92.5) = -0.5(92.5) + 12.5 + 10\sin(0.2) = -46.25 + 12.5 + 1.9867 = -31.7633$$ $$T_1 = 100 + 0.1 \times (-37.5 + (-31.7633)) = 100 - 6.92633 = 93.07367$$ **Step 3 — Iteration 2 (n = 1, $t_1 = 0.2$):** $$k_1 = f(0.2, 93.07367) = -0.5(93.07367) + 12.5 + 10\sin(0.2) = -46.5368 + 12.5 + 1.9867 = -32.0501$$ $$\tilde{T}_2 = 93.07367 + 0.2 \times (-32.0501) = 86.66365$$ $$k_2 = f(0.4, 86.66365) = -0.5(86.66365) + 12.5 + 10\sin(0.4) = -43.3318 + 12.5 + 3.8942 = -26.9376$$ $$T_2 = 93.07367 + 0.1 \times (-32.0501 + (-26.9376)) = 93.07367 - 5.89877 = 87.17490$$ **Step 4 — Final Results:** $$T(0.4) \approx 93.07°C, \quad T(0.6) \approx 87.17°C$$ **Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:** The system starts at 100°C and is being driven by both a **Newton cooling term** toward 25°C and a **sinusoidal forcing** of amplitude 10°C. The numerical trajectory shows the temperature dropping rapidly and then beginning to oscillate, consistent with the physical behaviour of a forced thermal system. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign Error in Heun's Corrector:** Writing $y_{n+1} = y_n + h \cdot f(t_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})$ *instead of* the average $\frac{h}{2}(k_1 + k_2)$. The averaging is the heart of the second-order accuracy. > 2. **Confusing LTE and Global Error:** The LTE is per-step ($\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ for Forward Euler) while the Global Error is total ($\mathcal{O}(h)$). Many students write "error is $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$" without specifying the type. > 3. **Forgetting to Iterate in Backward Euler:** Backward Euler is implicit; for a linear ODE, an explicit formula exists, but for nonlinear ODEs, students must mention the Newton-Raphson solver. A direct substitution without iteration loses 2–3 marks. > 4. **Wrong Step Count:** $N = (t_f - t_0)/h$, *not* $(t_f - t_0) \cdot h$. This single error can cascade and make all subsequent iterations wrong. > 5. **Not Reporting Units:** In engineering applications (temperature, voltage), omitting units like °C or V loses 0.5–1 mark in the KTU valuation key. > 6. **Skipping the Truncation Assumption:** When deriving the Forward Euler formula, students must explicitly state "we drop all terms of order $h^2$ and higher" — this is mandatory for full marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** > - Forward Euler: $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n)$ — explicit, $\mathcal{O}(h)$ global error. > - Backward Euler: $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_{n+1}, y_{n+1})$ — implicit, **A-stable**, requires solver. > - Modified Euler (Heun's): $y_{n+1} = y_n + \frac{h}{2}[f(t_n, y_n) + f(t_{n+1}, \tilde{y}_{n+1})]$ — predictor-corrector, $\mathcal{O}(h^2)$ global error. > - Euler-Cromer: $y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_{n+1}, y_n)$ — semi-implicit, energy-preserving for oscillators. > - **Stability Region (Forward Euler):** $\vert 1 + h\lambda \vert \leq 1$ — restricts $h \leq 2/\vert \lambda \vert$ for real $\lambda < 0$. > - **Stability Region (Backward Euler):** Always inside the unit disk for $\text{Re}(\lambda) \leq 0$ — **A-stable**. > - **Local Truncation Error** $\propto h^{p+1}$ where $p$ is the method order. > - **Global Accumulation Error** $\propto h^p$. > - The **order** of the method = power of $h$ in the **global** error. > - For the **RK2 family**, Heun's, Midpoint, and Ralston are three distinct variants with different coefficients but identical order. > - All Euler methods are **one-step** — they do not require the history of previous points. > - In **MATLAB/Simulink**, the default fixed-step solver is Forward Euler; the default variable-step solver is `ode45` (Dormand-Prince RK45), which is a higher-order descendant of the Euler concept. > - **Engineering Memory Aid:** "**E**uler = **E**asy, **E**xplicit, **E**rror-prone" (forward) vs "**B**ackward = **B**etter stability, **B**ut needs iteration".

topics.title: Hand Simulation of System Dynamics Models
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hand Simulation of System Dynamics Models

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hand Simulation** (also called *Manual Simulation* or *Manual Numerical Integration*) is the disciplined, pencil-and-paper process of iteratively computing the future state of a system described by a set of continuous-time differential equations, by converting them into discrete-time difference equations and stepping forward in time one increment ($\Delta t$) at a time.

In the context of **System Dynamics** — a modelling philosophy pioneered by **Jay W. Forrester** of MIT in the 1950s — a system is described by three primary structural elements:

| Element | Mathematical Role | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Stock (Level)** | State variable $L(t)$; accumulator | Water in a tank, inventory in a godown, population |
| **Flow (Rate)** | Rate variable $R(t)$; $dL/dt$ | Inflow tap, sales rate, birth rate |
| **Auxiliary** | Intermediate computed signal | Valve opening, current price |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hand simulation is essential because most real-world system dynamics models are **non-linear, time-varying, and analytically unsolvable**. Forcing a student to compute even 10–20 steps by hand builds the *intuition* for what computer simulators (Vensim, Stella, AnyLogic, MATLAB/Simulink) do internally.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Water"

Imagine a bucket with water flowing in through a tap and out through a hole at the bottom.

- The **water level** is the *stock* — you can see how much is in the bucket at any moment.
- The **tap rate** is an *inflow* — it adds water.
- The **drain rate** is an *outflow* — it removes water.
- The **net rate of change** is $\text{inflow} - \text{outflow}$.

To predict the water level in 5 minutes *without* a computer, you would:
1. Note the current level.
2. Estimate the inflow and outflow *right now* (e.g., 2 L/min in, 1.5 L/min out → net +0.5 L/min).
3. Multiply by a small time step (say $\Delta t = 0.5$ min) → level rises by 0.25 L.
4. Update the level, then repeat for the next 0.5 min, and so on.

That iterative, step-by-step procedure **is** hand simulation.

> [!NOTE]
> A **Causal Loop Diagram (CLD)** shows the qualitative feedback structure (positive/negative loops), while a **Stock-Flow Diagram** is the quantitative counterpart used for hand simulation. Both were formalised by Forrester.

### 1.3 Why Hand Simulation is Still Taught (even in the age of supercomputers)

1. **Conceptual Clarity** — forces the student to understand *time* as a discrete, marching variable.
2. **Validation** — the simulation result of any software should match hand calculation for a sanity check.
3. **Exam Relevance** — KTU university and internal examinations regularly ask for 5–10 step hand-simulated tables.
4. **Stability Awareness** — students discover first-hand what happens when $\Delta t$ is chosen too large (the model *blows up*).

> [!TIP]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework maps this topic to **CO4: Apply numerical integration techniques to simulate dynamic systems manually** — typically tested under Bloom's Level 3 (*Apply*) and Level 4 (*Analyse*).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Stage Workflow of Hand Simulation

**Stage 1 — Model Formulation**
Write the system as a set of coupled first-order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs):

$$\frac{dx_i}{dt} = f_i(x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n, t, \text{parameters})$$

**Stage 2 — Discretisation (Continuous → Discrete)**
Replace the differential $dx/dt$ with a forward finite difference over a chosen time step $\Delta t$:

$$\frac{dx}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{x(t+\Delta t) - x(t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Stage 3 — Iterative Marching (Euler's Method)**
Starting from the known initial condition $x(0)$, compute successive values using:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + f(x_k, t_k)\cdot \Delta t$$

This is **Euler's Forward (Explicit) Method** — the workhorse of hand simulation.

### 2.2 Why Euler's Method is Preferred for Hand Work

- **Simplicity** — needs only addition and multiplication; no derivatives of $f$ (unlike Runge-Kutta).
- **Transparency** — each step shows *exactly* how the new state depends on the current state.
- **Pedagogical Insight** — exposes the effect of $\Delta t$ on accuracy and stability.

> [!WARNING]
> Euler's method is **conditionally stable**. If $\Delta t$ exceeds a model-specific critical value, the hand-simulated values will oscillate or diverge to infinity — even though the true solution is well-behaved. This is the single most common reason students "get a wrong answer" in KTU viva-voce.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | State (Stock) update (Euler) | $L_{k+1} = L_k + (R^{\text{in}}_k - R^{\text{out}}_k)\cdot \Delta t$ | [Stock unit] | Fundamental hand-simulation equation |
| 2 | Forward Euler ODE | $x_{k+1} = x_k + f(x_k, t_k)\cdot \Delta t$ | [x unit] | First-order accurate, $O(\Delta t)$ |
| 3 | Backward Euler ODE | $x_{k+1} = x_k + f(x_{k+1}, t_{k+1})\cdot \Delta t$ | [x unit] | Implicit; needs algebraic solve |
| 4 | Local Truncation Error (LTE) | $\varepsilon_k = \tfrac{1}{2}x''(\xi_k)\,\Delta t^2$ | [x unit] | Bounded by step size squared |
| 5 | Global Error | $E_n \leq \dfrac{e^{Lt}-1}{L}\cdot O(\Delta t)$ | [x unit] | $L$ = Lipschitz constant of $f$ |
| 6 | Critical step (logistic) | $\Delta t_c \approx 2/r$ | [time] | Above this, Euler diverges |
| 7 | Critical step (damped osc.) | $\Delta t_c \approx 2\zeta/\omega_n$ | [time] | $\zeta,\omega_n$ from $\ddot{x}+2\zeta\omega_n\dot{x}+\omega_n^2 x=0$ |
| 8 | Average rate over interval | $\bar{R} \approx \dfrac{R_k + R_{k+1}}{2}$ | [flow unit] | Used in improved (Heun's) scheme |
| 9 | Steady-state condition | $dL/dt = 0 \Rightarrow R^{\text{in}} = R^{\text{out}}$ | — | Goal-seeking feedback systems |
| 10 | RK2 (Midpoint) | $k_1 = f(x_k);\ k_2 = f(x_k+k_1\Delta t/2);\ x_{k+1}=x_k+k_2\Delta t$ | [x unit] | Optional 2nd-order upgrade |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU **does not** expect students to derive LTE formulas in the exam, but examiners *love* to award 1–2 bonus marks if you **state the stability condition** for the chosen $\Delta t$ in words.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Control** — predicting tank level, temperature, pressure responses before tuning a PID controller.
- **Population Biology** — disease spread (SIR), fishery management.
- **Economics & Supply Chain** — bullwhip effect, inventory oscillations.
- **Mechanical Systems** — spring-mass-damper transient response.
- **Pharmacokinetics** — drug concentration in blood plasma over time.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Forward-Euler Update Rule

We begin with a generic first-order ODE describing a stock $L(t)$ driven by a net flow $F(t)$:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = F(t)$$

**Step 1 — Replace the derivative with a forward finite difference.**

The exact mathematical definition of the derivative is:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0}\frac{L(t+\Delta t) - L(t)}{\Delta t}$$

For a *finite* (non-zero) $\Delta t$, we approximate:

$$\frac{dL}{dt} \approx \frac{L(t+\Delta t) - L(t)}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the original ODE.**

$$\frac{L(t+\Delta t) - L(t)}{\Delta t} \approx F(t)$$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $L(t+\Delta t)$.**

$$L(t+\Delta t) - L(t) \approx F(t)\cdot \Delta t$$

$$L(t+\Delta t) \approx L(t) + F(t)\cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Discretise time as $t = k\cdot \Delta t$ and write the iterative rule.**

$$\boxed{\,L_{k+1} = L_k + F_k \cdot \Delta t\,}$$

This is the **master hand-simulation equation** that KTU expects every student to memorise.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Exponential Population Growth (Foundation Example)

**Model statement.** A bacterial culture grows according to:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = r\,N, \qquad N(0)=N_0,\quad r = 0.5 \text{ per hour}$$

Hand-simulate for 4 hours with $\Delta t = 1$ h, starting from $N_0 = 100$.

**Step 1 — Discretise the ODE.**

From Section 3.1, the update rule with $L \to N$ and $F \to rN$ is:

$$N_{k+1} = N_k + (r\,N_k)\,\Delta t$$

Substitute $r=0.5$, $\Delta t = 1$:

$$N_{k+1} = N_k + 0.5\,N_k = 1.5\,N_k$$

**Step 2 — Build the iteration table by hand.**

| Step $k$ | Time $t_k$ (h) | $N_k$ (cells) | $F_k = 0.5 N_k$ (cells/h) | $\Delta N = F_k \cdot \Delta t$ | Updated $N_{k+1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 100 | 50 | +50 | 150 |
| 1 | 1 | 150 | 75 | +75 | 225 |
| 2 | 2 | 225 | 112.5 | +112.5 | 337.5 |
| 3 | 3 | 337.5 | 168.75 | +168.75 | 506.25 |
| 4 | 4 | 506.25 | — | — | — |

**Step 3 — Compare with analytical (true) solution.**

The exact solution is $N(t) = N_0 e^{rt} = 100 e^{0.5t}$.

| $t$ (h) | Exact $N(t)$ | Hand-simulated $N_k$ | Absolute Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 100.00 | 100.00 | 0.00 |
| 1 | 164.87 | 150.00 | 14.87 |
| 2 | 271.83 | 225.00 | 46.83 |
| 3 | 448.17 | 337.50 | 110.67 |
| 4 | 738.91 | 506.25 | 232.66 |

> [!NOTE]
> The hand-simulated value **systematically underestimates** the true exponential because Euler's method uses the *current* rate, ignoring the *future* growth that will occur during the step. The error grows with $t$ — this is the classic behaviour of $O(\Delta t)$ methods.

---

### 3.3 Advanced Worked Example — Logistic Growth (S-Shaped Curve, KTU Favourite)

**Model statement.** A fish population in a lake obeys logistic dynamics:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = r\,N\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$$

Given $r = 0.4$ /yr, $K = 1000$ fish, $N(0) = 50$ fish. Hand-simulate for 5 years with $\Delta t = 1$ yr.

**Step 1 — Discretise using the generic Euler update.**

$$N_{k+1} = N_k + F_k\cdot \Delta t, \qquad F_k = r\,N_k\left(1 - \frac{N_k}{K}\right)$$

Substituting numerical values ($r\Delta t = 0.4$, $1/K = 0.001$):

$$N_{k+1} = N_k + 0.4\,N_k\left(1 - 0.001\,N_k\right)$$

**Step 2 — Hand-compute step by step.**

**Iteration $k=0$:**

$$F_0 = 0.4 \times 50 \times (1 - 0.001 \times 50) = 0.4 \times 50 \times 0.95 = 19.0$$

$$N_1 = 50 + 19.0 = 69.0 \text{ fish}$$

**Iteration $k=1$:**

$$F_1 = 0.4 \times 69 \times (1 - 0.001 \times 69) = 0.4 \times 69 \times 0.931 = 25.7$$

$$N_2 = 69.0 + 25.7 = 94.7 \text{ fish}$$

**Iteration $k=2$:**

$$F_2 = 0.4 \times 94.7 \times (1 - 0.001 \times 94.7) = 0.4 \times 94.7 \times 0.9053 = 34.3$$

$$N_3 = 94.7 + 34.3 = 129.0 \text{ fish}$$

**Iteration $k=3$:**

$$F_3 = 0.4 \times 129.0 \times (1 - 0.001 \times 129.0) = 0.4 \times 129.0 \times 0.871 = 44.95$$

$$N_4 = 129.0 + 44.95 = 173.95 \text{ fish}$$

**Iteration $k=4$:**

$$F_4 = 0.4 \times 173.95 \times (1 - 0.001 \times 173.95) = 0.4 \times 173.95 \times 0.826 = 57.47$$

$$N_5 = 173.95 + 57.47 = 231.42 \text{ fish}$$

**Iteration $k=5$:**

$$F_5 = 0.4 \times 231.42 \times (1 - 0.001 \times 231.42) = 0.4 \times 231.42 \times 0.7686 = 71.16$$

$$N_6 = 231.42 + 71.16 = 302.58 \text{ fish}$$

**Step 3 — Tabulate the complete hand-simulation.**

| Step $k$ | $t_k$ (yr) | $N_k$ (fish) | $F_k = rN_k(1-N_k/K)$ (fish/yr) | $N_{k+1}$ (fish) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 50.00 | 19.00 | 69.00 |
| 1 | 1 | 69.00 | 25.70 | 94.70 |
| 2 | 2 | 94.70 | 34.30 | 129.00 |
| 3 | 3 | 129.00 | 44.95 | 173.95 |
| 4 | 4 | 173.95 | 57.47 | 231.42 |
| 5 | 5 | 231.42 | 71.16 | 302.58 |

**Step 4 — Interpret physically.**

The population is in the **exponential growth phase** (low $N/K$ ratio). The growth rate $F_k$ increases because more fish reproduce. Eventually, as $N \to K = 1000$, the term $(1 - N/K) \to 0$ and the curve flattens — this **S-shaped (sigmoidal) saturation** cannot be captured by the simpler exponential model of Section 3.2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Choice of $\Delta t$ in the logistic model:** The stability condition is $\Delta t < 2/r = 2/0.4 = 5$ years. Our choice $\Delta t = 1$ yr is comfortably within the stable region, guaranteeing physically meaningful results.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — First-Order Inventory System with Replenishment Feedback (KTU Board Favourite)

**Model statement.** A warehouse stocks a product. The manager follows a *target-adjustment* policy: production rate is proportional to the gap between desired and actual inventory. Demand is constant.

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \alpha\,(I_{\text{target}} - I) - D$$

Given $I_{\text{target}} = 500$ units, $D = 20$ units/day, $\alpha = 0.1$ /day, $I(0) = 100$ units. Hand-simulate for 10 days, $\Delta t = 1$ day.

**Step 1 — Identify the structure.**

- Stock: $I$ (inventory)
- Inflow (Production): $P = \alpha(I_{\text{target}} - I) = 0.1(500 - I)$
- Outflow (Demand): $D = 20$
- Net flow: $F = P - D = 0.1(500 - I) - 20$

**Step 2 — Discretise.**

$$I_{k+1} = I_k + \big[0.1(500 - I_k) - 20\big]\cdot \Delta t$$

With $\Delta t = 1$:

$$I_{k+1} = I_k + 0.1(500 - I_k) - 20 = I_k + 50 - 0.1 I_k - 20 = 0.9 I_k + 30$$

**Step 3 — Hand-compute.**

| $k$ | $t_k$ (day) | $I_k$ (units) | $P_k = 0.1(500 - I_k)$ (units/day) | $D_k$ | $F_k = P_k - D_k$ | $I_{k+1}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 100.00 | 40.00 | 20 | +20.00 | 120.00 |
| 1 | 1 | 120.00 | 38.00 | 20 | +18.00 | 138.00 |
| 2 | 2 | 138.00 | 36.20 | 20 | +16.20 | 154.20 |
| 3 | 3 | 154.20 | 34.58 | 20 | +14.58 | 168.78 |
| 4 | 4 | 168.78 | 33.12 | 20 | +13.12 | 181.90 |
| 5 | 5 | 181.90 | 31.81 | 20 | +11.81 | 193.71 |
| 6 | 6 | 193.71 | 30.63 | 20 | +10.63 | 204.34 |
| 7 | 7 | 204.34 | 29.57 | 20 | +9.57 | 213.91 |
| 8 | 8 | 213.91 | 28.61 | 20 | +8.61 | 222.52 |
| 9 | 9 | 222.52 | 27.75 | 20 | +7.75 | 230.27 |
| 10 | 10 | 230.27 | — | — | — | — |

**Step 4 — Identify the equilibrium (steady state).**

At steady state, $F = 0$:

$$0.1(500 - I^*) = 20 \;\Rightarrow\; 500 - I^* = 200 \;\Rightarrow\; I^* = 300 \text{ units}$$

The hand-simulated values are approaching 300 from below — a **goal-seeking negative feedback loop** in action.

> [!NOTE]
> This example is the textbook KTU Part B question pattern. Marks are awarded for: (i) writing the correct discretised equation [4], (ii) showing 5–10 hand-computed rows [6], (iii) identifying the steady state [2], and (iv) interpreting the feedback nature [2].

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causal Loop Diagram (CLD) — Logistic Population Model

The qualitative feedback structure of the logistic model shows a *reinforcing* loop dominated by a *balancing* loop as $N$ grows toward $K$.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA["Birth Rate rN"] -- increases --> nodeB["Population N"]
    nodeB -- increases --> nodeC["Net Flow rN(1-N/K)"]
    nodeA -- drives --> nodeC
    nodeC -- increases --> nodeB
    nodeB -- increases --> nodeD["Carrying Pressure N over K"]
    nodeD -- decreases --> nodeE["Growth Factor 1 minus N over K"]
    nodeE -- decreases --> nodeC
```

**Loop Interpretation:**
- **Reinforcing Loop (R):** More population $\to$ more births $\to$ more population. Dominates when $N \ll K$.
- **Balancing Loop (B):** More population $\to$ more crowding $\to$ lower growth factor $\to$ fewer births. Dominates as $N \to K$.

### 4.2 Stock-Flow Diagram — Inventory System with Replenishment

The quantitative Forrester-style diagram used to derive the state equation for hand simulation.

```mermaid
graph TD
    stockA["Stock I = Inventory Level"] -- outflow --> flowA["Outflow D = 20 units per day Demand"]
    flowB["Inflow P = alpha times I target minus I Replenishment"] -- inflow --> stockA
    auxA["Aux Signal I target minus I Gap"] -- drives --> flowB
    constA["Constant I target = 500 units"] -- defines --> auxA
    stockA -- feeds back --> auxA
    constB["Constant alpha = 0.1 per day Adjustment Rate"] -- scales --> flowB
```

### 4.3 Hand-Simulation Algorithm Flowchart

The complete decision procedure a student follows during the KTU exam.

```mermaid
graph TD
    stepA["Step 1 Read state equation dx over dt equals f of x t"] --> stepB["Step 2 Choose time step delta t"]
    stepB --> stepC["Step 3 Verify stability delta t less than 2 over r"]
    stepC --> stepD{"Stable?"}
    stepD -- No --> stepE["Reduce delta t and return to Step 2"]
    stepD -- Yes --> stepF["Step 4 Initialise x0 and t0"]
    stepF --> stepG["Step 5 Loop Evaluate f xk tk"]
    stepG --> stepH["Step 6 Update xk+1 equals xk plus f times delta t"]
    stepH --> stepI["Step 7 Advance time tk+1 equals tk plus delta t"]
    stepI --> stepJ{"Reached final time?"}
    stepJ -- No --> stepG
    stepJ -- Yes --> stepK["Step 8 Tabulate xk and tk and interpret"]
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inside a Hand-Simulator

Mapping the interactions between the human, the model, and the numerical integrator.

```mermaid
graph LR
    blockA["Human Modeller"] -- writes --> blockB["State Equation f of x t"]
    blockB -- feeds --> blockC["Numerical Integrator Euler Engine"]
    blockD["Initial Condition x0"] -- feeds --> blockC
    blockE["Time Step delta t"] -- configures --> blockC
    blockC -- outputs --> blockF["Sequence x0 x1 x2 ... xN"]
    blockF -- feeds --> blockG["Tabulation and Plot"]
    blockG -- interpreted by --> blockA
```
