## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
Define **Hand Simulation** in the context of system dynamics. Why is it preferred over analytical solution for most real-world system dynamics models?

**Model Answer (for reference, 3 marks):**

*Hand simulation* is the manual, iterative numerical evaluation of a system dynamics model by converting the governing continuous-time differential equations into discrete-time difference equations and marching forward in time, one step ($\Delta t$) at a time, using a method such as **Euler's forward integration**. [1.5 marks]

It is preferred over analytical methods because real-world system dynamics models (logistic growth, SIR epidemic, bullwhip supply chain, predator–prey) are typically **non-linear, time-varying, and coupled** — closed-form analytical solutions either do not exist or are mathematically intractable. Hand simulation only requires basic arithmetic operations at each step and reveals the *transient trajectory* of the system, which is the primary engineering interest. [1.5 marks]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
State the **Forward Euler update rule** for a first-order ODE $dx/dt = f(x,t)$. Mention one advantage and one limitation of using this method for hand simulation.

**Model Answer (for reference, 3 marks):**

The Forward Euler update rule is:

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + f(x_k, t_k)\cdot \Delta t \quad \text{where} \quad t_k = k\cdot \Delta t$$

[1 mark for equation, 0.5 for the meaning of subscripts]

*Advantage:* It is **conceptually simple** — uses only the current state, requires no derivatives of $f$, and each step is transparent and auditable. [0.75 mark]

*Limitation:* It is **only first-order accurate** ($O(\Delta t)$) and is **conditionally stable** — if $\Delta t$ is chosen too large, the simulated trajectory diverges from the true solution or oscillates spuriously. [0.75 mark]

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

A chemical reactor obeys the first-order dynamics:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k\,C, \qquad C(0) = C_0 = 100 \text{ mol/L}, \quad k = 0.3 \text{ /min}$$

**(a)** Discretise the governing equation using the **Forward Euler method** and derive the iterative update rule. Choose a suitable time step $\Delta t = 1$ min and state the **stability condition**. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Hand-simulate the concentration for the **first 6 time steps** ($t = 0$ to $t = 5$ min), build a complete table of $t_k$, $C_k$, flow $F_k$, and updated $C_{k+1}$. Compare the final hand-simulated value with the **exact analytical solution** $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ at $t = 5$ min and compute the **percentage error**. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Discretisation & Stability (7 marks):**

The state equation is $dC/dt = -kC$. Following the derivation in Section 3.1, we replace $dC/dt$ with the forward finite difference: **[1 mark]**

$$\frac{C_{k+1} - C_k}{\Delta t} = -k\,C_k$$

Solving for $C_{k+1}$: **[2 marks]**

$$\boxed{\,C_{k+1} = C_k - k\,C_k\,\Delta t = C_k(1 - k\,\Delta t)\,}$$

Substituting $k = 0.3$, $\Delta t = 1$: **[1 mark]**

$$C_{k+1} = 0.7\,C_k$$

**Stability condition:** For Forward Euler applied to a linear ODE of the form $dx/dt = \lambda x$ (here $\lambda = -k = -0.3$), the iteration $x_{k+1} = (1+\lambda \Delta t) x_k$ is stable only if the **amplification factor magnitude** is less than 1: **[2 marks]**

$$|1 + \lambda \Delta t| < 1 \;\Rightarrow\; |1 - 0.3 \times 1| = 0.7 < 1 \;\checkmark$$

The condition $|1 - k\Delta t| < 1$ is satisfied, so $\Delta t = 1$ min is **stable** for this problem. (General stability bound: $\Delta t < 2/k = 2/0.3 \approx 6.67$ min.) **[1 mark]**

---

**Part (b) — Hand Simulation Table & Error (7 marks):**

Starting from $C_0 = 100$ mol/L, apply $C_{k+1} = 0.7\,C_k$ at each step: **[5 marks for table]**

| $k$ | $t_k$ (min) | $C_k$ (mol/L) | $F_k = -k C_k$ (mol/L/min) | $\Delta C = F_k \cdot \Delta t$ | $C_{k+1}$ (mol/L) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 100.00 | $-30.00$ | $-30.00$ | 70.00 |
| 1 | 1 | 70.00 | $-21.00$ | $-21.00$ | 49.00 |
| 2 | 2 | 49.00 | $-14.70$ | $-14.70$ | 34.30 |
| 3 | 3 | 34.30 | $-10.29$ | $-10.29$ | 24.01 |
| 4 | 4 | 24.01 | $-7.20$ | $-7.20$ | 16.81 |
| 5 | 5 | 16.81 | $-5.04$ | $-5.04$ | 11.77 |

**Comparison with exact solution at $t = 5$ min:** **[2 marks]**

$$C_{\text{exact}}(5) = 100 \cdot e^{-0.3 \times 5} = 100 \cdot e^{-1.5} = 100 \times 0.22313 = 22.313 \text{ mol/L}$$

**Percentage error:**

$$\%\text{Error} = \frac{|C_{\text{exact}} - C_{\text{hand}}|}{|C_{\text{exact}}|} \times 100 = \frac{|22.313 - 16.81|}{22.313} \times 100 \approx 24.66\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A common mistake is to compute the percentage error as $(22.313 - 16.81)/16.81 \times 100 \approx 32.7\%$ — this uses the *hand-simulated* value as the denominator. The KTU convention (and correct practice) is to use the *true/exact* value as the denominator. Also, students often forget the absolute value bars — required when the hand-simulated value exceeds the exact value (which happens for *decaying* models with large $\Delta t$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

A fish hatchery follows the **logistic growth model**:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = r\,N\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$$

Given $r = 0.5$ /yr, $K = 800$ fish, $N(0) = 40$ fish.

**(a)** Derive the **Forward Euler update equation** for this model. State the **stability condition** for $\Delta t$ and recommend a suitable step size. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Perform a **hand simulation for 6 steps** using the recommended $\Delta t$. Tabulate $N_k$, $F_k$, and $N_{k+1}$. Identify the **long-term steady-state population** $N^*$ and explain how the hand-simulated values converge toward it. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Update Rule & Stability (7 marks):**

Apply the Forward Euler discretisation (Section 3.1) to the logistic ODE: **[1 mark]**

$$\frac{N_{k+1} - N_k}{\Delta t} = r\,N_k\left(1 - \frac{N_k}{K}\right)$$

Solve for $N_{k+1}$: **[2 marks]**

$$\boxed{\,N_{k+1} = N_k + r\,N_k\left(1 - \frac{N_k}{K}\right)\Delta t\,}$$

Substituting numerical values $r = 0.5$, $K = 800$, $\Delta t = 1$: **[1 mark]**

$$N_{k+1} = N_k + 0.5\,N_k\left(1 - \frac{N_k}{800}\right) = N_k + 0.5\,N_k - 0.000625\,N_k^2$$

$$N_{k+1} = 1.5\,N_k - 0.000625\,N_k^2$$

**Stability analysis:** For Euler's method on a logistic system, the **linearised** critical step size is $\Delta t_c \approx 2/r$: **[2 marks]**

$$\Delta t_c = \frac{2}{r} = \frac{2}{0.5} = 4 \text{ years}$$

Therefore, any $\Delta t < 4$ yr is stable. A **recommended choice** is $\Delta t = 1$ yr — well within the safe region, gives good accuracy, and is a round number for hand computation. **[1 mark]**

---

**Part (b) — Six-Step Hand Simulation (7 marks):**

**Iteration $k=0$:** **[1 mark]**

$$F_0 = 0.5 \times 40 \times (1 - 40/800) = 0.5 \times 40 \times 0.95 = 19.0$$

$$N_1 = 40 + 19.0 = 59.0$$

**Iteration $k=1$:** **[0.5 mark]**

$$F_1 = 0.5 \times 59 \times (1 - 59/800) = 0.5 \times 59 \times 0.9263 = 27.32$$

$$N_2 = 59.0 + 27.32 = 86.32$$

**Iteration $k=2$:** **[0.5 mark]**

$$F_2 = 0.5 \times 86.32 \times (1 - 86.32/800) = 0.5 \times 86.32 \times 0.8921 = 38.50$$

$$N_3 = 86.32 + 38.50 = 124.82$$

**Iteration $k=3$:** **[0.5 mark]**

$$F_3 = 0.5 \times 124.82 \times (1 - 124.82/800) = 0.5 \times 124.82 \times 0.8440 = 52.68$$

$$N_4 = 124.82 + 52.68 = 177.50$$

**Iteration $k=4$:** **[0.5 mark]**

$$F_4 = 0.5 \times 177.50 \times (1 - 177.50/800) = 0.5 \times 177.50 \times 0.7781 = 69.07$$

$$N_5 = 177.50 + 69.07 = 246.57$$

**Iteration $k=5$:** **[0.5 mark]**

$$F_5 = 0.5 \times 246.57 \times (1 - 246.57/800) = 0.5 \times 246.57 \times 0.6918 = 85.30$$

$$N_6 = 246.57 + 85.30 = 331.87$$

**Tabulated Result (full marks for table):** **[2 marks]**

| $k$ | $t_k$ (yr) | $N_k$ (fish) | $F_k$ (fish/yr) | $N_{k+1}$ (fish) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 40.00 | 19.00 | 59.00 |
| 1 | 1 | 59.00 | 27.32 | 86.32 |
| 2 | 2 | 86.32 | 38.50 | 124.82 |
| 3 | 3 | 124.82 | 52.68 | 177.50 |
| 4 | 4 | 177.50 | 69.07 | 246.57 |
| 5 | 5 | 246.57 | 85.30 | 331.87 |

**Steady-state identification:** **[1 mark]**

Setting $dN/dt = 0$ gives two equilibria — the **trivial** $N^* = 0$ and the **non-trivial** $N^* = K = 800$ fish. The hand-simulated values (40 → 59 → 86 → … → 332) are clearly moving toward the non-trivial equilibrium of 800, confirming the **goal-seeking** nature of the negative feedback loop. The growth rate $F_k$ is currently still increasing, indicating the system is in the **exponential phase** and has not yet begun to saturate.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **General KTU Valuation Pitfalls (Cross-Question):**
> 1. **Equation without derivation** — stating the Euler rule without showing the *discretisation step* loses 1–2 marks. Always show the finite-difference substitution.
> 2. **Arithmetic in flow column** — examiners check one row at random. A single miscalculated $F_k$ propagates and **zeroes the entire subsequent column**.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — the flow column *must* carry units (e.g., fish/yr, mol/L/min). Missing units = −0.5 to −1 mark.
> 4. **No steady-state discussion** — even if not asked, mentioning $N^* = K$ or $I^* = 300$ shows CO4 mastery and earns a "value-add" mark.
> 5. **Picking unstable $\Delta t$** — if you choose $\Delta t > 2/r$ for the logistic or $\Delta t > 2/k$ for the exponential and don't justify, you lose the "stability" marks entirely.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Hand Simulation of System Dynamics Models**

- **Hand simulation = manual discrete-time marching** of a continuous ODE using finite differences.
- **Master equation to memorise:** $L_{k+1} = L_k + F_k \cdot \Delta t$, where $F_k$ is the net flow evaluated at the *current* step.
- **Three pillars of a system dynamics model:** *Stock* (state), *Flow* (rate), *Auxiliary* (intermediate).
- **Forward Euler** is the default hand-simulation method — simple, transparent, but only $O(\Delta t)$ accurate and **conditionally stable**.
- **Stability condition (linearised):** $\Delta t < 2 / \vert \lambda \vert$ where $\lambda$ is the largest-magnitude eigenvalue (≈ $r$ for growth, $k$ for decay, $\zeta \omega_n$ for damped oscillations).
- **Smaller $\Delta t$ → higher accuracy** but more hand-computation work; KTU exam typically expects 5–10 rows.
- **Always present a complete table** with columns: $k$, $t_k$, state $L_k$, flow $F_k$, $\Delta L$, updated $L_{k+1}$. Missing columns = lost marks.
- **Always carry units** in every numerical cell — they are the easiest marks to earn.
- **Compare with the analytical (exact) solution** wherever possible and quote the **percentage error** using the *exact* value as the denominator.
- **Identify the steady state** by setting $F = 0$ and discuss whether the hand-simulated trajectory is *goal-seeking* (negative feedback) or *explosive* (positive feedback).
- **Causal Loop Diagrams** give the qualitative structure; **Stock-Flow Diagrams** give the quantitative basis for hand simulation.
- **Common KTU examples to practise:** exponential growth/decay, logistic growth, first-order inventory with replenishment, damped spring-mass-damper, RC circuit charging/discharging.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** the hand-simulated value *always* lags the true solution (for growth) or *overshoots* it (for decay) when $\Delta t$ is large — never reverse-engineer $\Delta t$ to match the exact answer; that defeats the purpose of simulation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: S-Shaped Growth - Epidemics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** S-Shaped Growth - Epidemics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is S-Shaped Growth?

**S-shaped growth**, also known as **sigmoidal growth** or **logistic growth**, is a fundamental pattern in system dynamics where a population or quantity grows slowly at first, then accelerates rapidly through an exponential-like phase, and finally decelerates as it approaches a saturation ceiling (the **carrying capacity**). The resulting curve, when plotted over time, distinctly resembles the letter "S".

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> S-Shaped growth is a dynamic system behavior governed by the **logistic differential equation**:
> $$\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$$
> where the growth rate is proportional to both the current population $N$ and the **remaining available capacity** $(1 - N/K)$. The system is characterized by an **inflection point** at $N = K/2$, where the curve transitions from concave-up (accelerating) to concave-down (decelerating).

## 1.2 What are Epidemics in the Context of System Modeling?

In system modelling, an **epidemic** is the rapid spread of an infectious disease through a population over a short period, with the number of cases exceeding normal expectancy. From a modelling perspective, epidemics are dynamic systems involving:

- **Transmission mechanisms** (how the disease spreads)
- **Population state transitions** (from healthy to sick, sick to recovered)
- **Threshold phenomena** (whether the disease dies out or becomes an outbreak)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The Empty Restaurant Story):**
> Imagine a small restaurant that can comfortably seat **100 people** (carrying capacity $K = 100$).
> - **Day 1:** Only 1 customer arrives. Word-of-mouth is slow, so growth is sluggish.
> - **Month 3:** A food blogger reviews it. Suddenly 50 customers are coming daily — this is the steep middle of the S-curve.
> - **Month 6:** The restaurant is full (100 people), and no more can be seated — the curve flattens, even though demand is still high.
> 
> This exact pattern — slow start, explosive middle, saturation — is **S-shaped growth**.

## 1.3 Why S-Shaped Growth Matters in Epidemics

Epidemic curves are textbook examples of S-shaped behavior when viewed at the **cumulative infection level**. As a disease spreads through a susceptible population:

1. Initially, few are infected (slow start).
2. As infected individuals contact more susceptibles, transmission accelerates (steep rise).
3. As the pool of susceptibles depletes (herd immunity or behavior change), growth slows and saturates (flattening).

The **SIR Model** (Susceptible–Infected–Recovered) by **Kermack and McKendrick (1927)** is the foundational mathematical framework connecting S-shaped growth to epidemic dynamics.

## 1.4 Key Terminology and Constants

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| **Carrying Capacity** | $K$ | Maximum sustainable population size |
| **Intrinsic Growth Rate** | $r$ | Per capita growth rate in absence of limits |
| **Inflection Point** | $N = K/2$ | Point of maximum growth rate |
| **Basic Reproduction Number** | $R_0$ | Avg. number of secondary infections from one case |
| **Infection Rate** | $\beta$ | Effective contact rate (per capita transmission) |
| **Recovery Rate** | $\gamma$ | Rate at which infected individuals recover |
| **Total Population** | $N$ | $S + I + R$ (assumed constant) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 KTU scheme for PCIET601 emphasizes both the **mathematical structure** of the logistic model AND its **extension to compartmental epidemic models**. Students are expected to derive, simulate mentally, and interpret threshold behavior in $R_0$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logistic Model — Step-by-Step Logic

The S-shaped curve emerges naturally from a single first-order nonlinear ODE. The logic unfolds as follows:

1. **Unconstrained Malthusian Growth (Exponential Phase):**
   - When resources are unlimited, the population grows exponentially: $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN$
   - Solution: $N(t) = N_0 e^{rt}$ (J-shaped curve)

2. **Introduce Environmental Resistance:**
   - Real systems have a **finite carrying capacity** $K$
   - As $N \to K$, the available "room to grow" shrinks
   - The fractional remaining capacity is $\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$

3. **Combine the Two Effects (Multiplicative Coupling):**
   - Growth rate = (intrinsic potential) × (environmental resistance)
   - This gives the **logistic equation**:
   $$\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$$

4. **Interpret the Two Limiting Cases:**
   - If $N \ll K$: term $(1 - N/K) \approx 1$, exponential growth dominates
   - If $N \to K$: term $(1 - N/K) \to 0$, growth halts
   - If $N = K/2$: growth rate is at its **maximum** $\frac{rK}{4}$

## 2.2 Analytical Solution of the Logistic Equation

The logistic ODE is a **separable** equation. Its closed-form solution is:

$$N(t) = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right) e^{-rt}}$$

This explicit function traces the entire S-curve from $t = 0$ to $t \to \infty$.

## 2.3 Extension to Epidemics — The Kermack-McKendrick SIR Model

The SIR model partitions a fixed population $N$ into three mutually exclusive compartments:

- $S(t)$ = **Susceptibles** (can catch the disease)
- $I(t)$ = **Infecteds** (currently infectious)
- $R(t)$ = **Removed/Recovered** (immune or deceased)

The governing equations are:

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I$$

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$$

with the conservation law $S + I + R = N$ (closed population).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the transmission term $\beta SI$?**
> - The number of **effective contacts** is proportional to the density of susceptibles $S$ AND the density of infectives $I$.
> - This is a **mass-action** assumption (borrowed from chemical kinetics).
> - The parameter $\beta$ encodes both contact frequency and transmission probability.

## 2.4 The Epidemic Threshold (Herd Immunity)

A pivotal result in epidemic theory: an outbreak occurs **if and only if**:

$$R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma} > 1$$

- If $R_0 > 1$: epidemic grows (S-curve in cumulative cases)
- If $R_0 < 1$: disease dies out exponentially
- If $R_0 = 1$: **endemic equilibrium** (threshold condition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Final Epidemic Size Equation:**
> The total fraction of the population that ever gets infected, $z = 1 - S_\infty/N$, satisfies the transcendental equation:
> $$1 - z = e^{-R_0 z}$$
> This has no closed-form solution but is readily solved numerically — a classic exam question.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---------|---------|-------------|-------|
| **Logistic ODE** | $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \dfrac{N}{K}\right)$ | Core S-shaped growth model | $N$/time |
| **Logistic Solution** | $N(t) = \dfrac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K-N_0}{N_0}\right)e^{-rt}}$ | Explicit time-domain form | $N$ |
| **Inflection Point** | $N^* = \dfrac{K}{2}$ | Maximum growth rate location | $N$ |
| **Max Growth Rate** | $\left.\dfrac{dN}{dt}\right\vert_{\max} = \dfrac{rK}{4}$ | Peak slope of S-curve | $N$/time |
| **Time to Inflection** | $t^* = \dfrac{1}{r}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K-N_0}{N_0}\right)$ | When half of $K$ is reached | time |
| **SIR Dynamics** | $\dfrac{dS}{dt}=-\beta SI,\ \dfrac{dI}{dt}=\beta SI - \gamma I$ | Epidemic state transitions | per time |
| **Basic Reproduction** | $R_0 = \dfrac{\beta N}{\gamma}$ | Threshold parameter | dimensionless |
| **Herd Immunity Threshold** | $H = 1 - \dfrac{1}{R_0}$ | Fraction to vaccinate | dimensionless |
| **Final Size Eq.** | $1 - z = e^{-R_0 z}$ | Cumulative infected fraction | dimensionless |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering & Public Health Utility

- **Public Health Policy:** Predicting vaccination thresholds ($H = 1 - 1/R_0$) for diseases like measles, COVID-19, and influenza.
- **Ecology:** Modeling fish populations, endangered species recovery, invasive species control.
- **Technology Adoption:** Bass diffusion model (Rogers' curve) for product market penetration — same S-shape.
- **Chemical Engineering:** Autocatalytic reaction kinetics, fermentation processes (biomass growth in bioreactors).
- **Marketing Analytics:** Forecasting social media user growth, viral content spread.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in System Modelling and Simulation:** S-shaped growth is one of the few universal system archetypes. Whether modelling biology, epidemiology, or sociology, the same differential equation structure applies — a beautiful example of **isomorphism across disciplines**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Form Solution of the Logistic Equation

**Starting Point:**
$$\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Separate variables:**
We rearrange so that all $N$-terms are on one side and $t$-terms on the other:
$$\frac{dN}{N\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)} = r\, dt$$

**Step 2 — Use partial fractions on the left side:**
We decompose:
$$\frac{1}{N\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)} = \frac{1}{N} + \frac{1}{K - N}$$

*Verification:* Combining fractions gives:
$$\frac{(K-N) + N}{N(K-N)} = \frac{K}{N(K-N)} = \frac{1}{N(1 - N/K)} \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Integrate both sides:**
$$\int \left(\frac{1}{N} + \frac{1}{K-N}\right) dN = \int r\, dt$$

$$\ln\vert N \vert - \ln\vert K - N \vert = rt + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Combine the logarithms:**
$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{K-N}\right) = rt + C_1$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate both sides:**
$$\frac{N}{K-N} = e^{rt + C_1} = C_2 e^{rt}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the initial condition $N(0) = N_0$:**
At $t = 0$:
$$\frac{N_0}{K - N_0} = C_2 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_2 = \frac{N_0}{K - N_0}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute back and solve for $N(t)$:**
$$\frac{N}{K - N} = \frac{N_0}{K - N_0} e^{rt}$$

Cross-multiplying:
$$N(K - N_0) = N_0(K - N) e^{rt}$$

$$NK - N N_0 = N_0 K e^{rt} - N_0 N e^{rt}$$

$$N K + N N_0 e^{rt} = N_0 K e^{rt} + N N_0$$

$$N(K + N_0 e^{rt}) = N_0 K e^{rt} + N_0 N_0$$

Wait — let's cleanly collect:
$$NK - N_0 K e^{rt} = N N_0 - N N_0 e^{rt}$$

$$K(N - N_0 e^{rt}) = N N_0 (1 - e^{rt})$$

This route is messy. A cleaner method: solve for $N$ via:
$$N = (K - N) \cdot \frac{N_0}{K - N_0} e^{rt}$$

Let $A = \dfrac{N_0}{K - N_0} e^{rt}$. Then $N = A(K - N)$, so $N = AK - AN$, hence:
$$N(1 + A) = AK \quad \Longrightarrow \quad N = \frac{AK}{1 + A}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $A$ back:**
$$N(t) = \frac{K \cdot \dfrac{N_0}{K-N_0} e^{rt}}{1 + \dfrac{N_0}{K-N_0} e^{rt}}$$

**Step 9 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(K - N_0)e^{-rt}$:**
$$\boxed{N(t) = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right) e^{-rt}}}$$

This is the standard closed-form S-shaped solution. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equilibrium Points of the Logistic Model

**Step 1 — Set the time derivative to zero:**
$$rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Identify the two equilibrium candidates:**
- $N_1^* = 0$ (extinction / trivial equilibrium)
- $N_2^* = K$ (carrying capacity / non-trivial equilibrium)

**Step 3 — Linear stability analysis via $f(N) = rN(1 - N/K)$:**
The Jacobian (1-D derivative) is:
$$f'(N) = r - \frac{2rN}{K}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate at each equilibrium:**
- At $N = 0$: $f'(0) = r > 0$ → **unstable** (population grows away from zero if $N_0 > 0$)
- At $N = K$: $f'(K) = r - 2r = -r < 0$ → **stable** (perturbations decay back to $K$)

**Conclusion:** The carrying capacity $K$ is a globally attracting stable equilibrium for any $N_0 > 0$. This explains why the S-curve always saturates at $K$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Epidemic Threshold Theorem

**Step 1 — Start with the SIR system (focus on $\dfrac{dI}{dt}$):**
$$\frac{dI}{dt} = I(\beta S - \gamma)$$

**Step 2 — Observe that an epidemic requires $\dfrac{dI}{dt} > 0$:**
This holds if and only if:
$$\beta S - \gamma > 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad S > \frac{\gamma}{\beta}$$

**Step 3 — Define the basic reproduction number $R_0$:**
At the start of an epidemic, almost everyone is susceptible: $S(0) \approx N$. So:
$$R_0 \equiv \frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$$

**Step 4 — State the threshold condition:**
An epidemic occurs when the initial susceptible pool exceeds the critical value:
$$S(0) > \frac{\gamma}{\beta} = \frac{N}{R_0}$$

Equivalently, in dimensionless form:
$$R_0 \cdot \frac{S(0)}{N} > 1$$

**Step 5 — For a fully susceptible initial population ($S(0) = N$):**
$$R_0 > 1 \quad \text{(epidemic grows)}$$
$$R_0 < 1 \quad \text{(disease dies out)}$$

**Step 6 — Herd immunity threshold derivation:**
To stop an outbreak via vaccination, we need to reduce susceptibles below $\gamma/\beta$. The fraction $H$ to vaccinate satisfies:
$$(1 - H)N = \frac{\gamma}{\beta} = \frac{N}{R_0}$$

$$1 - H = \frac{1}{R_0} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \boxed{H = 1 - \frac{1}{R_0}}$$

For example, if $R_0 = 4$ (measles-like), $H = 75\%$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: The Final Size Equation

**Step 1 — Divide the $S$ and $I$ equations to eliminate time:**
$$\frac{dS}{dI} = \frac{-\beta SI}{\beta SI - \gamma I} = \frac{-\beta S}{\beta S - \gamma} = \frac{-S}{S - \gamma/\beta}$$

**Step 2 — Separate variables:**
$$\frac{S - \gamma/\beta}{-S} dS = dI \quad \text{(after algebra)}$$

Equivalently:
$$\frac{dS}{S} - \frac{\gamma/\beta}{S} \cdot \frac{dS}{S \cdot \text{adjustment}} \cdots$$

A cleaner approach uses the substitution $u = S/N$ and integrates from $t=0$ to $t=\infty$:

**Step 3 — Integrate $dI/dt$ over all time:**
$$\int_0^{\infty} \frac{dI}{dt} dt = I(\infty) - I(0) = -I_0 \quad \text{(since } I_\infty = 0\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Use the conservation law $S + I + R = N$ and the recovered equation:**
At the end of the epidemic, $R(\infty) = N - S(\infty)$ and $I(\infty) = 0$.

**Step 5 — Integrate the $R$ equation:**
$$R(\infty) - R(0) = \gamma \int_0^{\infty} I(t) dt$$

**Step 6 — Combine with phase-plane analysis:**
After careful algebra, one obtains the celebrated final-size relation:
$$\boxed{1 - \frac{S_\infty}{N} = e^{-R_0 \cdot (1 - S_\infty/N)}}$$

Letting $z = 1 - S_\infty/N$ be the final attack rate:
$$1 - z = e^{-R_0 z}$$

This transcendental equation is solved numerically (e.g., Newton's method) for any given $R_0$. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical example:** For $R_0 = 2$, the equation $1 - z = e^{-2z}$ has solution $z \approx 0.797$, meaning **~80% of the population** will be infected before the epidemic ends.

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Time to Inflection Point (Logistic Curve)

**Step 1 — The inflection point occurs when $N = K/2$.**

**Step 2 — Set $N(t^*) = K/2$ in the closed-form solution:**
$$\frac{K}{2} = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right) e^{-rt^*}}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**
$$1 + \left(\frac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right) e^{-rt^*} = 2$$

$$\left(\frac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right) e^{-rt^*} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t^*$:**
$$e^{-rt^*} = \frac{N_0}{K - N_0}$$

$$-rt^* = \ln\left(\frac{N_0}{K - N_0}\right) = -\ln\left(\frac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right)$$

$$\boxed{t^* = \frac{1}{r}\ln\left(\frac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right)}$$

For $N_0 = K/100$ and $r = 0.5$/year, $t^* \approx 9.2$ years.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Dynamics Block Diagram — Logistic Growth

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **causal feedback structure** of logistic S-shaped growth. Note that I avoid using reserved keywords as standalone node names and use alphanumeric IDs prefixed with letters.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Population N] --> B[Growth Engine]
    B --> C{dN/dt = rN}
    A --> D[Capacity Check]
    D --> E[Remaining Room 1 - N/K]
    E --> F[Multiplicative Coupling]
    C --> F
    F --> G[Net Growth Signal]
    G --> H[Integration Block]
    H --> A
    H --> I[S-Shaped Trajectory Output]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style I fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Population N** (cyan) feeds into both the growth engine and the capacity check.
- The **multiplicative coupling** (green) is the heart of logistic behavior — both factors must be high for rapid growth.
- The **integration block** accumulates the net growth signal, producing the **S-curve output** (pink).

## 4.2 Compartmental Flow Diagram — SIR Epidemic Model

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Susceptible S] -->|beta S I| I[Infected I]
    I -->|gamma I| R[Recovered R]
    
    S2[Susceptible Pool] -.->|depletes over time| S
    I2[Infected Pool] -.->|peaks at epidemic apex| I
    R2[Recovered Pool] -.->|accumulates monotonically| R
    
    style S fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style I fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
    style R fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Interpretation:**
- Solid arrows show the **state transitions** in the SIR model.
- Dotted boxes show the **dynamic behavior** of each compartment.
- The susceptible pool **depletes**, infected pool **rises then falls** (single peak), and recovered pool **monotonically accumulates**.

## 4.3 State Transition Flowchart — S-Curve Phases

```mermaid
graph TB
    P1[Phase 1: Lag Phase] --> P2[Phase 2: Exponential Acceleration]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3: Inflection Point K/2]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4: Deceleration]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5: Saturation at K]
    
    P1 -.->|Slow start| P1
    P2 -.->|Maximum slope nearby| P3
    P3 -.->|Curve bends| P4
    P4 -.->|Asymptotic approach| P5
    
    style P1 fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style P2 fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17
    style P3 fill:#f44336,stroke:#b71c1c
    style P4 fill:#c5e1a5,stroke:#33691e
    style P5 fill:#4caf50,stroke:#1b5e20
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Phase 1 (Orange):** Slow start, system gathering momentum.
- **Phase 2 (Yellow):** Rapid acceleration, exponential-like growth.
- **Phase 3 (Red):** Inflection point at $K/2$, maximum growth rate.
- **Phase 4 (Light Green):** Deceleration as resources dwindle.
- **Phase 5 (Green):** Saturation/plateau at carrying capacity $K$.

## 4.4 Feedback Loop Diagram — Epidemic Dynamics

```mermaid
graph LR
    SUS[Susceptible S] -->|Contact and Infection| INF[Infected I]
    INF -->|Recovery| REC[Recovered R]
    REC -->|No longer susceptible| SUS
    INF -->|Infects Susceptibles| SUS
    SUS -->|Provides New Infections| INF
    
    style SUS fill:#90caf9,stroke:#1565c0
    style INF fill:#ef9a9a,stroke:#c62828
    style REC fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#2e7d32
```

**Interpretation:**
- This diagram highlights the **reinforcing loop**: more infecteds → more new infections → even more infecteds.
- The **balancing loop** is the depletion of susceptibles and the growth of recovered (immune) individuals.
- The interplay between these two loops produces the S-shaped **cumulative infection curve**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define S-shaped growth. State the logistic differential equation and identify the meaning of each parameter.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

S-shaped growth, also called **sigmoidal or logistic growth**, is a dynamic system pattern where a quantity grows slowly initially, accelerates rapidly in the middle, and finally decelerates to approach a saturation limit (the **carrying capacity**). The resulting plot over time resembles the letter "S".

The governing differential equation is:
$$\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$$

Parameter meanings:
- $N$ = current population/state variable
- $r$ = **intrinsic growth rate** (per capita growth rate in unlimited environment)
- $K$ = **carrying capacity** (maximum sustainable population)
- $(1 - N/K)$ = **environmental resistance factor**, representing remaining room to grow

The inflection point occurs at $N = K/2$, where growth is fastest.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the basic reproduction number $R_0$ in epidemic modelling? State the threshold condition for an epidemic to occur.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **basic reproduction number $R_0$** is defined as the **average number of secondary infections produced by a single infected individual in a fully susceptible population**. Mathematically:
$$R_0 = \frac{\beta N}{\gamma}$$

where $\beta$ is the transmission rate, $N$ is the total population, and $\gamma$ is the recovery rate.

**Threshold condition:**
- If $R_0 > 1$: Each infected person infects more than one other on average → **epidemic grows** (S-curve behavior in cumulative cases).
- If $R_0 < 1$: Each infected person infects fewer than one other on average → **epidemic dies out**.
- If $R_0 = 1$: **Endemic equilibrium** (just sustains itself).

The **herd immunity threshold** to stop an outbreak via vaccination is $H = 1 - 1/R_0$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**a) Derive the analytical solution of the logistic differential equation $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = rN(1 - N/K)$ starting from separation of variables. Show all integration steps clearly.** **(7 marks)**

**b) A disease in a closed population of $N = 1000$ people has $R_0 = 3$ and recovery rate $\gamma = 0.2$/day. Compute (i) the transmission rate $\beta$, (ii) the herd immunity threshold percentage, and (iii) the maximum number of people simultaneously infected if the infection starts with $I_0 = 1$.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

We solve:
$$\frac{dN}{dt} = rN\left(1 - \frac{N}{K}\right)$$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Separate variables:
$$\frac{dN}{N(1 - N/K)} = r\, dt$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Partial fraction decomposition:
$$\frac{1}{N(1 - N/K)} = \frac{1}{N} + \frac{1}{K - N}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Integrate both sides:
$$\int \left(\frac{1}{N} + \frac{1}{K-N}\right) dN = \int r\, dt$$
$$\ln \vert N \vert - \ln \vert K - N \vert = rt + C_1$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Combine and exponentiate:
$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{K-N}\right) = rt + C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{N}{K-N} = C_2 e^{rt}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply initial condition $N(0) = N_0$:
$$C_2 = \frac{N_0}{K - N_0}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Solve for $N$ explicitly:
$$\frac{N}{K-N} = \frac{N_0}{K-N_0} e^{rt}$$
$$N = \frac{K N_0 e^{rt}}{K - N_0 + N_0 e^{rt}}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Multiply numerator and denominator by $e^{-rt}$ and simplify to obtain the standard form:
$$\boxed{N(t) = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right) e^{-rt}}}$$

**Verification:** As $t \to \infty$, $e^{-rt} \to 0$, so $N(t) \to K$. At $t = 0$, $N(0) = K/[1 + (K-N_0)/N_0] = K \cdot N_0/K = N_0$. ✓

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 1000$, $R_0 = 3$, $\gamma = 0.2$/day, $I_0 = 1$.

**(i) Transmission rate $\beta$ (2 marks):**

From $R_0 = \beta N / \gamma$:
$$\beta = \frac{R_0 \cdot \gamma}{N} = \frac{3 \times 0.2}{1000} = \frac{0.6}{1000} = 6 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per person per day}$$

**(ii) Herd immunity threshold (2 marks):**
$$H = 1 - \frac{1}{R_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{3} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 66.67\%$$

So **at least 667 people** must be vaccinated/immune to halt the outbreak.

**(iii) Maximum simultaneously infected (3 marks):**

In the SIR model, $I(t)$ peaks when $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = 0$, i.e., when $\beta S = \gamma$, or:
$$S^* = \frac{\gamma}{\beta} = \frac{0.2}{6 \times 10^{-4}} = 333.33 \text{ people}$$

Using the final-size equation with $R_0 = 3$: $1 - z = e^{-3z}$, solving numerically gives $z \approx 0.940$ (94% of population eventually infected).

At peak infection: $R^* = N - S^* - I^*$. Approximating for early epidemic where $R \approx 0$ at peak:
$$I_{\max} \approx N - S^* = 1000 - 333 = 667 \text{ people}$$

**Simultaneously infected peak $\approx$ 667 people** (occurs when $S = 333$).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**a) Explain the SIR model for epidemic spread. Write the governing differential equations, identify the parameters, and describe the role of the basic reproduction number $R_0$.** **(7 marks)**

**b) Derive the threshold condition and the herd immunity formula $H = 1 - 1/R_0$. Also, derive the final size equation $1 - z = e^{-R_0 z}$ and estimate the final attack rate for $R_0 = 5$.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**The SIR Model (7 marks total):**

The **Kermack-McKendrick SIR model (1927)** partitions a closed, fixed population of size $N$ into three compartments:

- $S(t)$: number of **Susceptibles** (healthy, can be infected)
- $I(t)$: number of **Infecteds** (currently infectious)
- $R(t)$: number of **Recovered/Removed** (immune or deceased)

**Governing ODEs (3 marks):**

$$\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I \quad \text{(susceptibles decrease due to new infections)}$$

$$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta S I - \gamma I \quad \text{(infecteds grow then decline)}$$

$$\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I \quad \text{(recoveries accumulate)}$$

**Parameter meanings (2 marks):**
- $\beta$: transmission rate (effective contact rate per susceptible per infected per unit time)
- $\gamma$: recovery rate (per infected per unit time, so $1/\gamma$ is the mean infectious period)
- **Conservation:** $S + I + R = N$ (constant)

**Role of $R_0$ (2 marks):**
$R_0 = \beta N / \gamma$ represents the **average secondary infections** from one primary case in a fully susceptible population. It is the **bifurcation parameter** that determines whether the disease spreads ($R_0 > 1$) or dies out ($R_0 < 1$).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Threshold condition derivation (3 marks):**

From $\dfrac{dI}{dt} = I(\beta S - \gamma)$:
- For an outbreak: $\dfrac{dI}{dt} > 0 \Rightarrow \beta S > \gamma \Rightarrow S > \gamma/\beta$
- Initially $S(0) \approx N$, so the condition becomes $\beta N > \gamma$, i.e., $R_0 > 1$.

**Herd immunity (2 marks):**

To prevent outbreaks, we must reduce susceptibles below $\gamma/\beta$ via vaccination:
$$(1 - H) N = \frac{\gamma}{\beta} = \frac{N}{R_0}$$
$$\boxed{H = 1 - \frac{1}{R_0}}$$

**Final size equation (2 marks):**

Dividing $dS/dI$ and integrating, one obtains:
$$\frac{S_\infty}{N} = e^{-R_0(1 - S_\infty/N)} = e^{-R_0 z}$$

Rearranging with $z = 1 - S_\infty/N$:
$$\boxed{1 - z = e^{-R_0 z}}$$

**For $R_0 = 5$:** Solve $1 - z = e^{-5z}$ numerically (Newton's method).

Trial: $z = 0.99$: LHS = $0.01$, RHS = $e^{-4.95} = 0.0071$ → too small
Trial: $z = 0.98$: LHS = $0.02$, RHS = $e^{-4.9} = 0.0074$ → still too small
Trial: $z = 0.95$: LHS = $0.05$, RHS = $e^{-4.75} = 0.0087$ → LHS bigger
Trial: $z = 0.96$: LHS = $0.04$, RHS = $e^{-4.8} = 0.0082$ → LHS bigger
Trial: $z = 0.97$: LHS = $0.03$, RHS = $e^{-4.85} = 0.0078$ → LHS bigger
Refining near $z = 0.985$: LHS = $0.015$, RHS = $e^{-4.925} = 0.00724$ → still LHS bigger
Newton iteration converges to: $z \approx 0.9984$ — almost **100% infection** when $R_0 = 5$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $R_0$ with $r$:** $R_0$ is a dimensionless ratio in epidemiology, $r$ is the logistic growth rate. Don't mix them!
> 2. **Forgetting the conservation law $S + I + R = N$** when solving SIR. Always use it to reduce the system from 3 to 2 ODEs.
> 3. **Sign errors in the recovery term:** $\dfrac{dI}{dt}$ has $-\gamma I$ (loss), $\dfrac{dR}{dt}$ has $+\gamma I$ (gain). Reverse these and your peak infection calculation collapses.
> 4. **Not stating the partial fraction step explicitly** in logistic derivation — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for skipping algebra.
> 5. **Misinterpreting the final-size equation:** It applies to a **single wave** of an epidemic in a closed population, not to ongoing endemic transmission.
> 6. **Herd immunity formula is for a homogeneously mixed population** — stratification (age, geography) changes the actual required coverage.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **S-shaped growth** arises from $\frac{dN}{dt} = rN(1 - N/K)$ — a balance of **exponential potential** and **environmental resistance**.
- ✅ **Closed-form solution:** $N(t) = \dfrac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K-N_0}{N_0}\right)e^{-rt}}$ — always saturates to $K$.
- ✅ **Inflection point** at $N = K/2$ and time $t^* = \dfrac{1}{r}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K-N_0}{N_0}\right)$.
- ✅ **Max growth rate:** $\dfrac{rK}{4}$ — the steepest slope of the S-curve.
- ✅ **SIR model** has 3 compartments and 2 independent ODEs (using conservation $S+I+R = N$).
- ✅ **Basic reproduction number:** $R_0 = \beta N/\gamma$ — epidemic occurs iff $R_0 > 1$.
- ✅ **Herd immunity threshold:** $H = 1 - 1/R_0$ — fraction of population to vaccinate.
- ✅ **Final size equation:** $1 - z = e^{-R_0 z}$ — solved numerically, gives total attack rate.
- ✅ **Two equilibria** of the logistic model: $N = 0$ (unstable) and $N = K$ (stable).
- ✅ **Real-world uses:** epidemiology, ecology, technology adoption (Bass/Rogers model), fermentation, viral marketing.
- ✅ **Exam tip:** Always draw the S-curve with labeled axes ($N$ vs $t$) and mark the inflection point — visuals score marks.
- ✅ **Numericals to practice:** Given $r$ and $K$, find $t^*$; given $R_0$ and $\gamma$, find $\beta$ and $H$; given $R_0$, solve final size equation numerically.
- ✅ **Crossover to KTU Module 5:** System dynamics modelling and feedback loops build directly on these compartmental structures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Innovation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCIET601 - SYSTEM MODELLING AND SIMULATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Innovation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **System Modelling and Simulation (PCIET601)**, **Innovation** refers to the systematic process of creating, designing, modeling, and implementing novel ideas, products, services, or processes that deliver measurable value within a socio-technical system. From a systems engineering perspective, innovation is treated as a **dynamic, time-dependent phenomenon** that can be quantitatively analyzed, predicted, and optimized using mathematical and computational simulation models.

According to **Joseph Schumpeter's theory of economic development**, innovation is the engine of creative destruction — replacing existing systems, processes, or products with fundamentally new approaches. In modern KTU-aligned simulation studies, innovation is operationalized through three measurable dimensions:

- **Novelty** — the degree to which an idea deviates from existing baselines.
- **Value Creation** — the quantifiable economic, social, or technical benefit generated.
- **Adoption Velocity** — the rate at which target users/entities embrace the innovation, which is the primary metric in diffusion-based simulation models.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Innovation in system simulation is the modeling, analysis, and prediction of how a new entity (product, technology, or process) propagates through a population over time, governed by parameters of **internal influence (innovation coefficient)** and **external/social influence (imitation coefficient)**.

## 1.2 Types of Innovation — The KTU Classification

For the 2024 scheme, innovation is classified into the following canonical categories, each with distinct simulation characteristics:

| Type | Definition | Simulation Behavior |
|------|------------|---------------------|
| **Incremental Innovation** | Minor, continuous improvements to existing systems | Low-amplitude, high-frequency S-curve segments |
| **Radical Innovation** | Fundamental, paradigm-shifting breakthroughs | Discontinuous jump; may trigger new S-curve |
| **Disruptive Innovation** | Initially inferior but rapidly improving technology that displaces incumbents | Cross-cutting trajectory intersecting established S-curves |
| **Architectural Innovation** | Reconfiguration of existing components into new structures | Re-arrangement of system topology |
| **Open Innovation** | Distributed innovation across organizational boundaries | Multi-agent network diffusion |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Smartphone Analogy**
>
> Imagine a college campus. When the first student gets a new smartphone, very few others care. That one student is the **innovator**. Then their close friends see it, want it, and buy it (these are the **early adopters** influenced by direct contact — **internal influence**). Soon, it becomes a social norm, and almost everyone on campus owns one. The adoption looks like an **S-shaped curve** climbing slowly at first, then exploding, then tapering off as the last holdouts finally buy in.
>
> This exact pattern is what the **Bass Diffusion Model** simulates — the mathematical engine of innovation modeling in KTU Module 4.

## 1.4 Core Physical / Mathematical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics Used in Innovation Simulation:**
> - **Market Potential (m)** — Total addressable population of adopters (dimensionless count)
> - **Coefficient of Innovation (p)** — Typical range: **0.001 to 0.03** (external/independent influence)
> - **Coefficient of Imitation (q)** — Typical range: **0.3 to 0.5** (internal/social/word-of-mouth influence)
> - **Peak Adoption Time (t*)** — Time at which the adoption rate is maximum
> - **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** — Standard **1 to 9** scale used in innovation systems engineering
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Bass Diffusion Model — Foundation of Innovation Simulation

The **Bass Diffusion Model (1969)** is the cornerstone mathematical framework for simulating innovation adoption. It treats the adoption process as a **continuous-time stochastic process** where the probability of an individual adopting at time *t* depends on two factors:

1. **External Influence (p):** The effect of mass media, advertising, and independent decision-making.
2. **Internal Influence (q):** The effect of word-of-mouth, social contagion, and imitation.

### 2.1.1 Bass Model — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1:** At time *t*, let *N(t)* represent the **cumulative number of adopters** who have already adopted the innovation.
- **Step 2:** The remaining non-adopters are given by *m − N(t)*, where *m* is the market potential.
- **Step 3:** Each non-adopter adopts with a probability that increases linearly with the fraction of the population that has already adopted.
- **Step 4:** The hazard rate (instantaneous probability of adoption) is:
  $$ h(t) = p + q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m} $$
- **Step 5:** The rate of new adoptions is the product of the hazard rate and the remaining non-adopters:
  $$ \frac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left[p + q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m}\right] \cdot \left[m - N(t)\right] $$
- **Step 6:** The closed-form solution of this Riccati differential equation is:
  $$ N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} \cdot e^{-(p+q)t}} $$
- **Step 7:** The peak adoption time occurs when the second derivative of *N(t)* equals zero:
  $$ t^* = \frac{\ln(q/p)}{p+q} $$
- **Step 8:** The peak adoption rate (maximum sales velocity) is:
  $$ \frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t=t^*} = m \cdot \frac{(p+q)^2}{4q} $$

### 2.2 The S-Curve of Innovation

Innovation diffusion universally follows an **S-shaped (sigmoidal) curve**, characterized by three distinct phases:

- **Introduction Phase:** Slow uptake, dominated by innovators (low *N(t)*).
- **Growth Phase:** Exponential acceleration due to imitation effects (*q* dominates).
- **Maturity/Saturation Phase:** Deceleration as the market saturates (*N(t) → m*).

### 2.3 Technology Readiness Levels (TRL) — Innovation Maturity Metric

> [!NOTE]
> The **TRL scale** is the standard 9-level metric used in systems engineering to classify the maturity of an innovation. It is a critical concept in KTU Module 4 for simulating the lifecycle of technological innovations.

| TRL | Level Name | Simulation Implication |
|-----|------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Basic Principles Observed | Conceptual model only |
| 2 | Technology Concept Formulated | Initial parameters identified |
| 3 | Experimental Proof of Concept | Proof-of-concept simulation |
| 4 | Technology Validated in Lab | Component-level simulation |
| 5 | Technology Validated in Relevant Environment | Subsystem simulation |
| 6 | Demonstrated in Relevant Environment | Prototype simulation |
| 7 | System Prototype Demonstration | Full-system simulation |
| 8 | System Complete and Qualified | Operational simulation |
| 9 | System Proven in Operational Environment | Market diffusion simulation (Bass Model) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for KTU ESE 2024. Every parameter, boundary condition, and unit is examinable.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $h(t) = p + q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m}$ | Hazard rate of adoption at time *t* | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\frac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left[p + q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m}\right] \cdot [m - N(t)]$ | Bass Model ODE for adoption rate | adopters/time |
| 3 | $N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} \cdot e^{-(p+q)t}}$ | Closed-form cumulative adopters | adopters |
| 4 | $t^* = \frac{\ln(q/p)}{p+q}$ | Peak adoption time | time units |
| 5 | $\frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t^*} = m \cdot \frac{(p+q)^2}{4q}$ | Maximum adoption rate at peak | adopters/time |
| 6 | $N(t^*) = m \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{p}{2q}\right)$ | Cumulative adopters at peak time | adopters |
| 7 | $L(t) = p \cdot (m - N(t))$ | Sales due to innovators (external) | adopters/time |
| 8 | $I(t) = q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m} \cdot (m - N(t))$ | Sales due to imitators (internal) | adopters/time |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Bass Diffusion Model is the workhorse of innovation simulation in:

- **Consumer Electronics:** Forecasting smartphone adoption (Apple, Samsung).
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Predicting the diffusion of a new drug across hospitals.
- **Electric Vehicles:** Simulating market penetration of EVs (p ≈ 0.003, q ≈ 0.38 historically).
- **Renewable Energy:** Modeling solar panel adoption in residential markets.
- **Software Products:** Predicting user growth for SaaS platforms.
- **Process Innovation in Manufacturing:** Simulating Six Sigma or Lean adoption across factory floors.

> [!NOTE]
> The **innovation coefficient p** reflects how strongly mass-media-driven, **autonomous adoption** is. The **imitation coefficient q** reflects the **social contagion effect** — when q >> p, the innovation is said to be "viral" or "highly contagious," producing the explosive middle phase of the S-curve.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Bass Model Closed-Form Solution

We will derive the closed-form solution of the Bass Model ODE step-by-step. This is a **high-yield derivation** for KTU 2024 ESE.

### 3.1.1 Starting Equation

The Bass Model is given by the first-order nonlinear ODE:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = \left[p + q \cdot \frac{N}{m}\right] \cdot [m - N] $$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Transformation

**Step 1: Expand the right-hand side.**

Let us denote $N$ for $N(t)$ for brevity. Expanding:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = p \cdot m - p \cdot N + q \cdot N - \frac{q \cdot N^2}{m} $$

**Step 2: Group constant and N terms.**

Combining the linear terms in *N*:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = pm + (q - p) \cdot N - \frac{q}{m} \cdot N^2 $$

**Step 3: Recognize the Riccati structure.**

This is a **Bernoulli-Riccati equation** in *N* with a quadratic nonlinearity. We make the substitution:

$$ N(t) = m \cdot f(t) $$

where $f(t)$ is the **fraction of market adopted** at time *t* (dimensionless, $0 \leq f \leq 1$).

**Step 4: Substitute and simplify.**

The derivative becomes $\frac{dN}{dt} = m \cdot \frac{df}{dt}$. Substituting:

$$ m \cdot \frac{df}{dt} = pm + (q-p) \cdot m f - \frac{q}{m} \cdot m^2 f^2 $$

$$ m \cdot \frac{df}{dt} = pm + (q-p) \cdot m f - qm f^2 $$

**Step 5: Divide through by m.**

$$ \frac{df}{dt} = p + (q-p) \cdot f - q f^2 $$

$$ \frac{df}{dt} = p + (q-p) f - q f^2 $$

**Step 6: Recognize the logistic-like form.**

Rearranging:

$$ \frac{df}{dt} = -q \left[f^2 - \frac{(q-p)}{q} f - \frac{p}{q}\right] $$

$$ \frac{df}{dt} = -q \left[f^2 - \left(1 - \frac{p}{q}\right) f - \frac{p}{q}\right] $$

**Step 7: Solve the separable ODE.**

Separating variables:

$$ \frac{df}{f^2 - (1 - p/q) f - p/q} = -q \, dt $$

The denominator factors using the quadratic formula. The roots of $f^2 - (1 - p/q) f - p/q = 0$ are:

$$ f = \frac{(1 - p/q) \pm \sqrt{(1 - p/q)^2 + 4p/q}}{2} $$

**Step 8: Define the discriminant.**

Let $r_1$ and $r_2$ be the two real roots with $r_1 < 0 < r_2$:

$$ r_1 = \frac{(1 - p/q) - \sqrt{(1 - p/q)^2 + 4p/q}}{2} $$

$$ r_2 = \frac{(1 - p/q) + \sqrt{(1 - p/q)^2 + 4p/q}}{2} $$

**Step 9: Integrate both sides.**

Using partial fractions, the integral evaluates to:

$$ \int \frac{df}{(f - r_1)(f - r_2)} = \frac{1}{r_2 - r_1} \ln \left| \frac{f - r_1}{f - r_2} \right| $$

**Step 10: Apply initial condition $f(0) = 0$.**

$$ \frac{1}{r_2 - r_1} \ln \left| \frac{-r_1}{-r_2} \right| = 0 $$

$$ \ln \left| \frac{r_1}{r_2} \right| = 0 \Rightarrow \text{this gives a constant of integration} $$

**Step 11: Solve for f(t).**

After algebraic manipulation (exponentiating and isolating *f*):

$$ \frac{f - r_1}{f - r_2} = \frac{r_1}{r_2} \cdot e^{(r_2 - r_1) q t} $$

Let $C = r_1 / r_2$. Cross-multiplying:

$$ f - r_1 = (f - r_2) \cdot C \cdot e^{(r_2 - r_1) q t} $$

$$ f \left[1 - C \cdot e^{(r_2 - r_1) q t}\right] = r_1 - r_2 \cdot C \cdot e^{(r_2 - r_1) q t} $$

$$ f(t) = \frac{r_1 - r_2 \cdot C \cdot e^{(r_2 - r_1) q t}}{1 - C \cdot e^{(r_2 - r_1) q t}} $$

**Step 12: Substitute back to obtain N(t).**

Using $C = r_1 / r_2$ and the relation $(r_2 - r_1) = \sqrt{(1 - p/q)^2 + 4p/q} = (p+q)/q$:

$$ f(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}} $$

**Step 13: Final closed-form solution.**

Multiplying by *m* to recover *N(t)*:

$$ \boxed{N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical Bass Diffusion closed-form solution** — a guaranteed high-yield derivation in KTU ESE 2024. Note the elegant symmetry: the entire dynamics are governed by a single composite rate constant $(p+q)$, often called the **diffusion rate parameter**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Peak Adoption Time $t^*$

**Step 1: Compute the second derivative of N(t).**

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = \left[p + \frac{q N}{m}\right] (m - N) $$

$$ \frac{d^2 N}{dt^2} = \frac{d}{dt}\left[pm + (q-p)N - \frac{q N^2}{m}\right] $$

$$ \frac{d^2 N}{dt^2} = (q-p) \frac{dN}{dt} - \frac{2q N}{m} \frac{dN}{dt} $$

**Step 2: Set $d^2N/dt^2 = 0$ for inflection point.**

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} \left[(q-p) - \frac{2q N}{m}\right] = 0 $$

Since $dN/dt \neq 0$ during active diffusion:

$$ (q - p) - \frac{2q N^*}{m} = 0 $$

**Step 3: Solve for N* (cumulative adopters at peak).**

$$ N^* = \frac{m(q - p)}{2q} = m \left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{p}{2q}\right] $$

**Step 4: Find t* by inverting the closed-form solution.**

At $t = t^*$, the closed-form gives:

$$ N^* = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t^*}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t^*}} $$

Setting $u = e^{-(p+q)t^*}$ and solving:

$$ \frac{1 - u}{1 + \frac{q}{p} u} = \frac{1}{2} - \frac{p}{2q} = \frac{q - p}{2q} $$

After cross-multiplication and simplification:

$$ u = \frac{p}{q} $$

$$ e^{-(p+q)t^*} = \frac{p}{q} $$

**Step 5: Take natural log of both sides.**

$$ -(p+q) t^* = \ln(p/q) $$

$$ \boxed{t^* = \frac{\ln(q/p)}{p+q}} $$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A new electric scooter is launched with market potential $m = 100{,}000$ units. The innovation coefficient is $p = 0.02$ and the imitation coefficient is $q = 0.4$. Compute (a) cumulative adoption at $t = 5$ years, (b) peak adoption time, (c) peak adoption rate.

**Solution:**

**(a) Cumulative adoption at t = 5:**

$$ N(5) = 100000 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(0.02 + 0.4) \times 5}}{1 + \frac{0.4}{0.02} e^{-(0.42) \times 5}} $$

$$ N(5) = 100000 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-2.1}}{1 + 20 \cdot e^{-2.1}} $$

$$ e^{-2.1} \approx 0.12246 $$

$$ N(5) = 100000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.12246}{1 + 20 \times 0.12246} = 100000 \cdot \frac{0.87754}{1 + 2.4492} $$

$$ N(5) = 100000 \cdot \frac{0.87754}{3.4492} = 100000 \cdot 0.25440 $$

$$ \boxed{N(5) \approx 25{,}440 \text{ units}} $$

**(b) Peak adoption time:**

$$ t^* = \frac{\ln(q/p)}{p+q} = \frac{\ln(0.4/0.02)}{0.02 + 0.4} = \frac{\ln(20)}{0.42} = \frac{2.9957}{0.42} $$

$$ \boxed{t^* \approx 7.13 \text{ years}} $$

**(c) Peak adoption rate:**

$$ \frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t^*} = m \cdot \frac{(p+q)^2}{4q} = 100000 \cdot \frac{(0.42)^2}{4 \times 0.4} $$

$$ = 100000 \cdot \frac{0.1764}{1.6} = 100000 \cdot 0.11025 $$

$$ \boxed{\frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t^*} \approx 11{,}025 \text{ units/year}} $$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Innovation Diffusion Process Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the complete block-level functional architecture of the Bass Diffusion simulation process, mapping how parameters interact across the innovation lifecycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Innovation Concept] --> B[Identify Market Potential m]
    B --> C[Estimate Innovation Coefficient p]
    C --> D[Estimate Imitation Coefficient q]
    D --> E[Initialize N of 0 equals 0]
    E --> F[Compute Hazard Rate h of t]
    F --> G{p t greater than 0 check External Influence}
    G -->|Yes| H[Add Innovator Adoption Term p times m minus N]
    H --> I[Add Imitator Adoption Term q times N divided by m times m minus N]
    I --> J[Compute dN by dt Total Adoption Rate]
    J --> K[Integrate to get N of t]
    K --> L{Time t less than t star}
    L -->|Yes| M[Growth Phase: dN by dt Increasing]
    L -->|No| N[Saturation Phase: dN by dt Decreasing]
    M --> O[Update N of t plus dt]
    N --> O
    O --> P{N of t approx equals m check Saturation}
    P -->|No| F
    P -->|Yes| Q[Output: Cumulative Adoption Curve]
    Q --> R[Compute Peak Time t star]
    R --> S[Compute Peak Rate dN by dt at t star]
    S --> T[End Simulation]
```

## 4.2 Innovation Types Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Innovation Taxonomy] --> B[Incremental]
    A --> C[Radical]
    A --> D[Disruptive]
    A --> E[Architectural]
    B --> B1[Continuous improvement of existing S-curve]
    C --> C1[Jump to new S-curve with step change]
    D --> D1[Initially inferior but rapidly improving]
    D1 --> D2[Displaces incumbent technology]
    E --> E1[Reuses components in new configuration]
    B1 --> F[S-curve Continuum]
    C1 --> F
    D2 --> F
    E1 --> F
```

## 4.3 S-Curve Schematic of Innovation Adoption

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase1["Introduction Phase"]
        S1[Innovators: p-driven, slow uptake]
    end
    subgraph Phase2["Growth Phase"]
        S2[Early Majority: q-driven, exponential rise]
    end
    subgraph Phase3["Maturity Phase"]
        S3[Late Majority: saturation, deceleration]
    end
    subgraph Phase4["Saturation Phase"]
        S4[Laggards: N approaches m, plateau]
    end
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> S4
    S4 --> T{Time t}
    S1 --> T
    S2 --> T
    S3 --> T
```

## 4.4 System Dynamics Feedback Loop of Innovation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Adopters] -->|Word of Mouth| R2[Social Influence]
    R2 -->|Increases| R3[Adoption Rate]
    R3 -->|Increases| R1
    R4[Mass Media] -->|External Influence| R3
    R5[Remaining Market] -->|Decreases| R3
    R3 -->|Decreases| R5
    R1 -->|Reduces| R5
    R5 -->|Limits| R3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The Bass Model is fundamentally a **positive feedback loop** (R1 → R2 → R3 → R1) that is bounded by a **negative feedback loop** (R5 limiting R3) — this is the mathematical reason the diffusion curve is sigmoidal, not exponential.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the Bass Diffusion Model. Explain the significance of the innovation coefficient *p* and the imitation coefficient *q*.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Bass Diffusion Model** is a mathematical model that predicts the adoption pattern of a new product or innovation over time. It is expressed as:

$$ \frac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left[p + q \cdot \frac{N(t)}{m}\right] \cdot [m - N(t)] $$

- **Innovation Coefficient (p):** Represents the **external influence** such as mass media, advertising, and independent consumer decision-making. It is the probability of adoption by an individual who has had no contact with previous adopters. Typical range: **0.001 to 0.03**.
- **Imitation Coefficient (q):** Represents the **internal influence** such as word-of-mouth and social contagion. It captures the probability of adoption by an individual who is influenced by existing adopters. Typical range: **0.3 to 0.5**.
- When **q >> p**, the innovation exhibits viral, socially-driven adoption (explosive S-curve).
- When **p >> q**, the innovation is mass-media-driven with weak social contagion.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between radical, incremental, and disruptive innovation with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Incremental** | Continuous, marginal improvements to an existing product | Annual iPhone updates with better camera |
| **Radical** | Fundamental breakthrough creating a new paradigm | Introduction of the internet in the 1990s |
| **Disruptive** | Initially inferior technology that improves rapidly to displace incumbents | Netflix displacing Blockbuster; digital cameras displacing film |

- **Incremental** innovations extend the existing S-curve.
- **Radical** innovations spawn a new S-curve with discontinuous jump.
- **Disruptive** innovations begin below existing performance and cross-cut through the incumbent's S-curve from below.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the closed-form solution of the Bass Diffusion Model. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a new smartphone model, the market potential is $m = 500{,}000$ units, the innovation coefficient is $p = 0.015$, and the imitation coefficient is $q = 0.45$. Calculate: **(i)** the peak adoption time $t^*$, **(ii)** the cumulative adopters at peak time, and **(iii)** the peak adoption rate. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Bass Model Closed-Form Solution: (7 Marks)**

Starting from the Bass ODE:

$$ \frac{dN}{dt} = \left[p + \frac{q N}{m}\right](m - N) \quad \text{...(1)} $$

**Step 1: Substitute $N = m f$, where $f$ is the fraction of market adopted.**

$$ m \frac{df}{dt} = \left[p + q f\right] m (1 - f) = m \left[p(1-f) + q f (1-f)\right] $$

**Step 2: Divide by m.**

$$ \frac{df}{dt} = p(1 - f) + q f (1 - f) $$

$$ \frac{df}{dt} = p - pf + qf - qf^2 = p + (q-p)f - qf^2 $$

**Step 3: Factor the quadratic form.**

$$ \frac{df}{dt} = -q\left[f^2 - \left(\frac{q-p}{q}\right) f - \frac{p}{q}\right] \quad \text{...(2)} $$

**Step 4: Define the discriminant.**

$$ \Delta = \left(\frac{q-p}{q}\right)^2 + 4 \cdot \frac{p}{q} = \frac{(q-p)^2 + 4pq}{q^2} = \frac{(p+q)^2}{q^2} $$

**Step 5: The two roots are $r_1$ and $r_2$ with $r_2 - r_1 = (p+q)/q$.**

**Step 6: Separate variables and integrate.**

$$ \int \frac{df}{(f-r_1)(f-r_2)} = -q \int dt + C $$

**Step 7: Apply initial condition $f(0) = 0$.**

$$ \frac{1}{r_2 - r_1} \ln\left|\frac{-r_1}{-r_2}\right| = C \Rightarrow C = \frac{1}{r_2 - r_1} \ln\left|\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right| $$

**Step 8: Solve for f(t).**

$$ \frac{1}{r_2 - r_1} \ln\left|\frac{(f-r_1) r_2}{(f-r_2) r_1}\right| = -q t $$

$$ \frac{f - r_1}{f - r_2} = \frac{r_1}{r_2} e^{-(p+q)t} \quad \text{...(since } r_2 - r_1 = (p+q)/q\text{)} $$

**Step 9: After algebraic simplification:**

$$ f(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}} $$

**Step 10: Final answer in terms of N(t).**

$$ \boxed{N(t) = m \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}} $$

**(b) Numerical Solution: (7 Marks)**

Given: $m = 500{,}000$, $p = 0.015$, $q = 0.45$.

**(i) Peak adoption time:**

$$ t^* = \frac{\ln(q/p)}{p+q} = \frac{\ln(0.45/0.015)}{0.015 + 0.45} = \frac{\ln(30)}{0.465} $$

$$ \ln(30) \approx 3.4012 $$

$$ t^* = \frac{3.4012}{0.465} \approx 7.31 \text{ time units} $$

**(ii) Cumulative adopters at peak:**

$$ N(t^*) = m \left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{p}{2q}\right] = 500000 \left[\frac{1}{2} - \frac{0.015}{2 \times 0.45}\right] $$

$$ = 500000 \left[0.5 - 0.01667\right] = 500000 \times 0.48333 $$

$$ N(t^*) \approx 241{,}665 \text{ units} $$

**(iii) Peak adoption rate:**

$$ \frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t^*} = m \cdot \frac{(p+q)^2}{4q} = 500000 \cdot \frac{(0.465)^2}{4 \times 0.45} $$

$$ = 500000 \cdot \frac{0.2162}{1.8} = 500000 \times 0.1201 $$

$$ \frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t^*} \approx 60{,}060 \text{ units/time unit} $$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of Technology Readiness Levels (TRL) with all nine levels. Discuss how TRL maps onto the Bass Diffusion Model phases. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simulation study of a new solar inverter technology shows $m = 200{,}000$ adopters, $p = 0.01$, $q = 0.35$. Using the Bass Model, compute the cumulative adoption at $t = 3$ years and $t = 6$ years. Comment on which phase the innovation is in at each time point. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) TRL Concept and Mapping to Bass Model: (7 Marks)**

The **Technology Readiness Level (TRL)** is a 9-level scale developed by NASA and adopted globally to assess the maturity of an innovation:

| TRL | Level | Description | Bass Model Phase |
|-----|-------|-------------|------------------|
| 1 | Basic Principles | Fundamental research | Pre-innovation (outside Bass) |
| 2 | Concept Formulated | Application identified | Pre-launch |
| 3 | Proof of Concept | Analytical/experimental POC | Pre-launch |
| 4 | Lab Validation | Component testing | TRL 4 — Pre-introduction |
| 5 | Relevant Environment | Subsystem integration | TRL 5 — Innovator phase begins |
| 6 | Demonstrated | Prototype in operational env. | TRL 6 — Early Adopters |
| 7 | System Prototype | Full-scale prototype | TRL 7 — Growth phase |
| 8 | System Qualified | Actual system completed | TRL 8 — Maturity onset |
| 9 | Proven in Operations | Real-world deployment | TRL 9 — Saturation phase |

**Mapping Summary:**
- **TRL 1–4** correspond to the pre-Bass phase where $N(t) = 0$.
- **TRL 5–6** maps to the **introduction phase** (innovator-driven, *p*-dominated).
- **TRL 7–8** maps to the **growth phase** (imitator-driven, *q*-dominated).
- **TRL 9** maps to the **maturity/saturation phase** ($N(t) \to m$).

**(b) Numerical Solution: (7 Marks)**

Given: $m = 200{,}000$, $p = 0.01$, $q = 0.35$.

The composite rate: $p + q = 0.36$.
The ratio: $q/p = 35$.

The closed-form:

$$ N(t) = 200000 \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-0.36 t}}{1 + 35 \cdot e^{-0.36 t}} $$

**At $t = 3$ years:**

$$ e^{-0.36 \times 3} = e^{-1.08} \approx 0.3396 $$

$$ N(3) = 200000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.3396}{1 + 35 \times 0.3396} = 200000 \cdot \frac{0.6604}{1 + 11.886} $$

$$ N(3) = 200000 \cdot \frac{0.6604}{12.886} = 200000 \cdot 0.05125 $$

$$ N(3) \approx 10{,}250 \text{ adopters} $$

**At $t = 6$ years:**

$$ e^{-0.36 \times 6} = e^{-2.16} \approx 0.1153 $$

$$ N(6) = 200000 \cdot \frac{1 - 0.1153}{1 + 35 \times 0.1153} = 200000 \cdot \frac{0.8847}{1 + 4.0355} $$

$$ N(6) = 200000 \cdot \frac{0.8847}{5.0355} = 200000 \cdot 0.1757 $$

$$ N(6) \approx 35{,}140 \text{ adopters} $$

**Phase Identification:**

Peak time:
$$ t^* = \frac{\ln(35)}{0.36} = \frac{3.5553}{0.36} \approx 9.88 \text{ years} $$

- At **$t = 3$ years**: We are well before the peak (9.88 years), so the innovation is in the **Introduction/early Growth phase**, dominated by innovators and early adopters. The adoption rate is still low but accelerating.
- At **$t = 6$ years**: Still in the **Growth phase** (accelerating adoption), approaching but not yet at the inflection point. Social contagion (imitation) is starting to dominate over independent adoption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in $t^*$ formula:** A very common mistake is writing $t^* = \frac{\ln(p/q)}{p+q}$ (with inverted ratio). Always verify that $q > p$ is required for $t^* > 0$.
> 2. **Confusing $N(t)$ with $dN/dt$:** Students often report the cumulative adoption as the "rate." Remember, $N(t)$ is the **cumulative** count and $dN/dt$ is the **instantaneous rate**.
> 3. **Missing units in final answer:** Always state the units (adopters, adopters/time, years).
> 4. **Forgetting the boundary condition $f(0) = 0$:** In the derivation, this is essential; missing it leads to a different constant of integration and wrong closed-form.
> 5. **Misidentifying phases:** The peak time $t^*$ is the **inflection point** of the S-curve, not the saturation point. Saturation occurs as $t \to \infty$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE 2024 — Module 4: Innovation**

- **Bass Diffusion Model** is the core mathematical engine of innovation simulation in this module — know the ODE and closed-form solution by heart.
- **Three critical parameters:** $m$ (market potential), $p$ (innovation coefficient), $q$ (imitation coefficient).
- **Typical ranges:** $p \in [0.001, 0.03]$, $q \in [0.3, 0.5]$.
- **The composite rate $(p+q)$** governs the speed of diffusion — higher values mean faster market saturation.
- **Peak time formula:** $t^* = \frac{\ln(q/p)}{p+q}$ — requires $q > p$ for a positive peak time.
- **Peak rate formula:** $\frac{dN}{dt}\bigg|_{t^*} = m \cdot \frac{(p+q)^2}{4q}$.
- **S-Curve phases:** Introduction (slow, $p$-driven) → Growth (fast, $q$-driven) → Maturity (decelerating) → Saturation ($N \to m$).
- **Five innovation types:** Incremental, Radical, Disruptive, Architectural, Open.
- **Technology Readiness Levels (TRL):** 9-level scale from basic research (1) to operational deployment (9).
- **Boundary condition:** $N(0) = 0$, i.e., no adopters at the time of launch.
- **Limiting behavior:** $\lim_{t \to \infty} N(t) = m$ (full market saturation).
- **Disruptive innovation** starts at low performance and rises steeply to displace incumbents.
- **Open innovation** involves distributed knowledge flows across organizational boundaries — modeled via multi-agent simulations.
- The Bass Model assumes a **homogeneous market** — extensions (e.g., **Generalized Bass Model**) incorporate marketing effort, price, and competition.
- **Real-world applications:** EV adoption, pharmaceutical diffusion, SaaS growth, smartphone penetration, renewable energy rollout.
- **Examinable derivations:** Closed-form solution, peak time, peak rate, and the Riccati-to-logistic transformation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 4
